1737        If you have a dedicated database server with 1GB or more of RAM, a
1738        reasonable starting value for <varname>shared_buffers</varname> is 25%
1739        of the memory in your system.  There are some workloads where even
1740        larger settings for <varname>shared_buffers</varname> are effective, but
1741        because <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> also relies on the
1742        operating system cache, it is unlikely that an allocation of more than
1743        40% of RAM to <varname>shared_buffers</varname> will work better than a
1744        smaller amount.  Larger settings for <varname>shared_buffers</varname>
1745        usually require a corresponding increase in
1746        <varname>max_wal_size</varname>, in order to spread out the
1747        process of writing large quantities of new or changed data over a
1748        longer period of time.
1749       </para>
1750
1751       <para>
1752        On systems with less than 1GB of RAM, a smaller percentage of RAM is
1753        appropriate, so as to leave adequate space for the operating system.
1754       </para>
1755
1756      </listitem>
1757     </varlistentry>
1758
1759     <varlistentry id="guc-huge-pages" xreflabel="huge_pages">
1760      <term><varname>huge_pages</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
1761      <indexterm>
1762       <primary><varname>huge_pages</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
1763      </indexterm>
1764      </term>
1765      <listitem>
1766       <para>
1767        Controls whether huge pages are requested for the main shared memory
1768        area. Valid values are <literal>try</literal> (the default),
1769        <literal>on</literal>, and <literal>off</literal>.
1770        This parameter can only be set at server start. With
1771        <varname>huge_pages</varname> set to <literal>try</literal>, the
1772        server will try to request huge pages, but fall back to the default if
1773        that fails. With <literal>on</literal>, failure to request huge pages
1774        will prevent the server from starting up. With <literal>off</literal>,
1775        huge pages will not be requested. The actual state of huge pages is
1776        indicated by the server variable
1777        <xref linkend="guc-huge-pages-status"/>.
1778       </para>
1779
1780       <para>
1781        At present, this setting is supported only on Linux and Windows. The
1782        setting is ignored on other systems when set to
1783        <literal>try</literal>.  On Linux, it is only supported when
1784        <varname>shared_memory_type</varname> is set to <literal>mmap</literal>
1785        (the default).
1786       </para>
1787
1788       <para>
1789        The use of huge pages results in smaller page tables and less CPU time
1790        spent on memory management, increasing performance. For more details about
1791        using huge pages on Linux, see <xref linkend="linux-huge-pages"/>.
1792       </para>
1793
1794       <para>
1795        Huge pages are known as large pages on Windows.  To use them, you need to
1796        assign the user right <quote>Lock pages in memory</quote> to the Windows user account
1797        that runs <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>.
1798        You can use Windows Group Policy tool (gpedit.msc) to assign the user right
1799        <quote>Lock pages in memory</quote>.
1800        To start the database server on the command prompt as a standalone process,
1801        not as a Windows service, the command prompt must be run as an administrator or
1802        User Access Control (UAC) must be disabled. When the UAC is enabled, the normal
1803        command prompt revokes the user right <quote>Lock pages in memory</quote> when started.
1804       </para>
1805
1806       <para>
1807        Note that this setting only affects the main shared memory area.
1808        Operating systems such as Linux, FreeBSD, and Illumos can also use
1809        huge pages (also known as <quote>super</quote> pages or
1810        <quote>large</quote> pages) automatically for normal memory
1811        allocation, without an explicit request from
1812        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>. On Linux, this is called
1813        <quote>transparent huge pages</quote><indexterm><primary>transparent
1814        huge pages</primary></indexterm> (THP). That feature has been known to
1815        cause performance degradation with
1816        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> for some users on some Linux
1817        versions, so its use is currently discouraged (unlike explicit use of
1818        <varname>huge_pages</varname>).
1819       </para>
1820      </listitem>
1821     </varlistentry>
1822
1823     <varlistentry id="guc-huge-page-size" xreflabel="huge_page_size">
1824      <term><varname>huge_page_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
1825      <indexterm>
1826       <primary><varname>huge_page_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
1827      </indexterm>
1828      </term>
1829      <listitem>
1830       <para>
1831        Controls the size of huge pages, when they are enabled with
1832        <xref linkend="guc-huge-pages"/>.
1833        The default is zero (<literal>0</literal>).
1834        When set to <literal>0</literal>, the default huge page size on the
1835        system will be used. This parameter can only be set at server start.
1836       </para>
1837       <para>
1838        Some commonly available page sizes on modern 64 bit server architectures include:
1839        <literal>2MB</literal> and <literal>1GB</literal> (Intel and AMD), <literal>16MB</literal> and
1840        <literal>16GB</literal> (IBM POWER), and <literal>64kB</literal>, <literal>2MB</literal>,
1841        <literal>32MB</literal> and <literal>1GB</literal> (ARM). For more information
1842        about usage and support, see <xref linkend="linux-huge-pages"/>.
1843       </para>
1844       <para>
1845        Non-default settings are currently supported only on Linux.
1846       </para>
1847      </listitem>
1848     </varlistentry>
1849
1850     <varlistentry id="guc-temp-buffers" xreflabel="temp_buffers">
1851      <term><varname>temp_buffers</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
1852      <indexterm>
1853       <primary><varname>temp_buffers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
1854      </indexterm>
1855      </term>
1856      <listitem>
1857       <para>
1858        Sets the maximum amount of memory used for temporary buffers within
1859        each database session.  These are session-local buffers used only
1860        for access to temporary tables.
1861        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
1862        that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
1863        The default is eight megabytes (<literal>8MB</literal>).
1864        (If <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> is not 8kB, the default value scales
1865        proportionally to it.)
1866        This setting can be changed within individual
1867        sessions, but only before the first use of temporary tables
1868        within the session; subsequent attempts to change the value will
1869        have no effect on that session.
1870       </para>
1871
1872       <para>
1873        A session will allocate temporary buffers as needed up to the limit
1874        given by <varname>temp_buffers</varname>.  The cost of setting a large
1875        value in sessions that do not actually need many temporary
1876        buffers is only a buffer descriptor, or about 64 bytes, per
1877        increment in <varname>temp_buffers</varname>.  However if a buffer is
1878        actually used an additional 8192 bytes will be consumed for it
1879        (or in general, <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes).
1880       </para>
1881      </listitem>
1882     </varlistentry>
1883
1884     <varlistentry id="guc-max-prepared-transactions" xreflabel="max_prepared_transactions">
1885      <term><varname>max_prepared_transactions</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
1886      <indexterm>
1887       <primary><varname>max_prepared_transactions</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
1888      </indexterm>
1889      </term>
1890      <listitem>
1891       <para>
1892        Sets the maximum number of transactions that can be in the
1893        <quote>prepared</quote> state simultaneously (see <xref
1894        linkend="sql-prepare-transaction"/>).
1895        Setting this parameter to zero (which is the default)
1896        disables the prepared-transaction feature.
1897        This parameter can only be set at server start.
1898       </para>
1899
1900       <para>
1901        If you are not planning to use prepared transactions, this parameter
1902        should be set to zero to prevent accidental creation of prepared
1903        transactions.  If you are using prepared transactions, you will
1904        probably want <varname>max_prepared_transactions</varname> to be at
1905        least as large as <xref linkend="guc-max-connections"/>, so that every
1906        session can have a prepared transaction pending.
1907       </para>
1908
1909       <para>
1910        When running a standby server, you must set this parameter to the
1911        same or higher value than on the primary server. Otherwise, queries
1912        will not be allowed in the standby server.
1913       </para>
1914      </listitem>
1915     </varlistentry>
1916
1917     <varlistentry id="guc-work-mem" xreflabel="work_mem">
1918      <term><varname>work_mem</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
1919      <indexterm>
1920       <primary><varname>work_mem</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
1921      </indexterm>
1922      </term>
1923      <listitem>
1924       <para>
1925        Sets the base maximum amount of memory to be used by a query operation
1926        (such as a sort or hash table) before writing to temporary disk files.
1927        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as kilobytes.
1928        The default value is four megabytes (<literal>4MB</literal>).
1929        Note that a complex query might perform several sort and hash
1930        operations at the same time, with each operation generally being
1931        allowed to use as much memory as this value specifies before
1932        it starts
1933        to write data into temporary files.  Also, several running
1934        sessions could be doing such operations concurrently.
1935        Therefore, the total memory used could be many times the value
1936        of <varname>work_mem</varname>; it is necessary to keep this
1937        fact in mind when choosing the value.  Sort operations are used
1938        for <literal>ORDER BY</literal>, <literal>DISTINCT</literal>,
1939        and merge joins.
1940        Hash tables are used in hash joins, hash-based aggregation, memoize
1941        nodes and hash-based processing of <literal>IN</literal> subqueries.
1942       </para>
1943       <para>
1944        Hash-based operations are generally more sensitive to memory
1945        availability than equivalent sort-based operations.  The
1946        memory limit for a hash table is computed by multiplying
1947        <varname>work_mem</varname> by
1948        <varname>hash_mem_multiplier</varname>.  This makes it
1949        possible for hash-based operations to use an amount of memory
1950        that exceeds the usual <varname>work_mem</varname> base
1951        amount.
1952       </para>
1953      </listitem>
1954     </varlistentry>
1955
1956     <varlistentry id="guc-hash-mem-multiplier" xreflabel="hash_mem_multiplier">
1957      <term><varname>hash_mem_multiplier</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
1958      <indexterm>
1959       <primary><varname>hash_mem_multiplier</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
1960      </indexterm>
1961      </term>
1962      <listitem>
1963       <para>
1964        Used to compute the maximum amount of memory that hash-based
1965        operations can use.  The final limit is determined by
1966        multiplying <varname>work_mem</varname> by
1967        <varname>hash_mem_multiplier</varname>.  The default value is
1968        2.0, which makes hash-based operations use twice the usual
1969        <varname>work_mem</varname> base amount.
1970       </para>
1971       <para>
1972        Consider increasing <varname>hash_mem_multiplier</varname> in
1973        environments where spilling by query operations is a regular
1974        occurrence, especially when simply increasing
1975        <varname>work_mem</varname> results in memory pressure (memory
1976        pressure typically takes the form of intermittent out of
1977        memory errors).  The default setting of 2.0 is often effective with
1978        mixed workloads.  Higher settings in the range of 2.0 - 8.0 or
1979        more may be effective in environments where
1980        <varname>work_mem</varname> has already been increased to 40MB
1981        or more.
1982       </para>
1983      </listitem>
1984     </varlistentry>
1985
1986     <varlistentry id="guc-maintenance-work-mem" xreflabel="maintenance_work_mem">
1987      <term><varname>maintenance_work_mem</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
1988      <indexterm>
1989       <primary><varname>maintenance_work_mem</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
1990      </indexterm>
1991      </term>
1992      <listitem>
1993       <para>
1994        Specifies the maximum amount of memory to be used by maintenance
1995        operations, such as <command>VACUUM</command>, <command>CREATE
1996        INDEX</command>, and <command>ALTER TABLE ADD FOREIGN KEY</command>.
1997        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as kilobytes.
1998        It defaults
1999        to 64 megabytes (<literal>64MB</literal>).  Since only one of these
2000        operations can be executed at a time by a database session, and
2001        an installation normally doesn't have many of them running
2002        concurrently, it's safe to set this value significantly larger
2003        than <varname>work_mem</varname>.  Larger settings might improve
2004        performance for vacuuming and for restoring database dumps.
2005       </para>
2006       <para>
2007        Note that when autovacuum runs, up to
2008        <xref linkend="guc-autovacuum-max-workers"/> times this memory
2009        may be allocated, so be careful not to set the default value
2010        too high.  It may be useful to control for this by separately
2011        setting <xref linkend="guc-autovacuum-work-mem"/>.
2012       </para>
2013      </listitem>
2014     </varlistentry>
2015
2016     <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-work-mem" xreflabel="autovacuum_work_mem">
2017      <term><varname>autovacuum_work_mem</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2018      <indexterm>
2019       <primary><varname>autovacuum_work_mem</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2020      </indexterm>
2021      </term>
2022      <listitem>
2023       <para>
2024        Specifies the maximum amount of memory to be used by each
2025        autovacuum worker process.
2026        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as kilobytes.
2027        It defaults to -1, indicating that
2028        the value of <xref linkend="guc-maintenance-work-mem"/> should
2029        be used instead.  The setting has no effect on the behavior of
2030        <command>VACUUM</command> when run in other contexts.
2031        This parameter can only be set in the
2032        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command
2033        line.
2034       </para>
2035      </listitem>
2036     </varlistentry>
2037
2038     <varlistentry id="guc-vacuum-buffer-usage-limit" xreflabel="vacuum_buffer_usage_limit">
2039      <term>
2040       <varname>vacuum_buffer_usage_limit</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2041       <indexterm>
2042        <primary><varname>vacuum_buffer_usage_limit</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2043       </indexterm>
2044      </term>
2045      <listitem>
2046       <para>
2047        Specifies the size of the
2048        <glossterm linkend="glossary-buffer-access-strategy">Buffer Access Strategy</glossterm>
2049        used by the <command>VACUUM</command> and <command>ANALYZE</command>
2050        commands.  A setting of <literal>0</literal> will allow the operation
2051        to use any number of <varname>shared_buffers</varname>.  Otherwise
2052        valid sizes range from <literal>128 kB</literal> to
2053        <literal>16 GB</literal>.  If the specified size would exceed 1/8 the
2054        size of <varname>shared_buffers</varname>, the size is silently capped
2055        to that value.  The default value is <literal>2MB</literal>.  If
2056        this value is specified without units, it is taken as kilobytes.  This
2057        parameter can be set at any time.  It can be overridden for
2058        <xref linkend="sql-vacuum"/> and <xref linkend="sql-analyze"/>
2059        when passing the <option>BUFFER_USAGE_LIMIT</option> option.  Higher
2060        settings can allow <command>VACUUM</command> and
2061        <command>ANALYZE</command> to run more quickly, but having too large a
2062        setting may cause too many other useful pages to be evicted from
2063        shared buffers.
2064       </para>
2065      </listitem>
2066     </varlistentry>
2067
2068     <varlistentry id="guc-logical-decoding-work-mem" xreflabel="logical_decoding_work_mem">
2069      <term><varname>logical_decoding_work_mem</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2070      <indexterm>
2071       <primary><varname>logical_decoding_work_mem</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2072      </indexterm>
2073      </term>
2074      <listitem>
2075       <para>
2076        Specifies the maximum amount of memory to be used by logical decoding,
2077        before some of the decoded changes are written to local disk. This
2078        limits the amount of memory used by logical streaming replication
2079        connections. It defaults to 64 megabytes (<literal>64MB</literal>).
2080        Since each replication connection only uses a single buffer of this size,
2081        and an installation normally doesn't have many such connections
2082        concurrently (as limited by <varname>max_wal_senders</varname>), it's
2083        safe to set this value significantly higher than <varname>work_mem</varname>,
2084        reducing the amount of decoded changes written to disk.
2085       </para>
2086      </listitem>
2087     </varlistentry>
2088
2089     <varlistentry id="guc-commit-timestamp-buffers" xreflabel="commit_timestamp_buffers">
2090      <term><varname>commit_timestamp_buffers</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2091      <indexterm>
2092       <primary><varname>commit_timestamp_buffers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2093      </indexterm>
2094      </term>
2095      <listitem>
2096       <para>
2097        Specifies the amount of memory to use to cache the contents of
2098        <literal>pg_commit_ts</literal> (see
2099        <xref linkend="pgdata-contents-table"/>).
2100        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
2101        that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
2102        The default value is <literal>0</literal>, which requests
2103        <varname>shared_buffers</varname>/512 up to 1024 blocks,
2104        but not fewer than 16 blocks.
2105        This parameter can only be set at server start.
2106       </para>
2107      </listitem>
2108     </varlistentry>
2109
2110     <varlistentry id="guc-multixact-member-buffers" xreflabel="multixact_member_buffers">
2111      <term><varname>multixact_member_buffers</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2112      <indexterm>
2113       <primary><varname>multixact_member_buffers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2114      </indexterm>
2115      </term>
2116      <listitem>
2117       <para>
2118        Specifies the amount of shared memory to use to cache the contents
2119        of <literal>pg_multixact/members</literal> (see
2120        <xref linkend="pgdata-contents-table"/>).
2121        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
2122        that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
2123        The default value is <literal>32</literal>.
2124        This parameter can only be set at server start.
2125       </para>
2126      </listitem>
2127     </varlistentry>
2128
2129     <varlistentry id="guc-multixact-offset-buffers" xreflabel="multixact_offset_buffers">
2130      <term><varname>multixact_offset_buffers</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2131      <indexterm>
2132       <primary><varname>multixact_offset_buffers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2133      </indexterm>
2134      </term>
2135      <listitem>
2136       <para>
2137        Specifies the amount of shared memory to use to cache the contents
2138        of <literal>pg_multixact/offsets</literal> (see
2139        <xref linkend="pgdata-contents-table"/>).
2140        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
2141        that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
2142        The default value is <literal>16</literal>.
2143        This parameter can only be set at server start.
2144       </para>
2145      </listitem>
2146     </varlistentry>
2147
2148     <varlistentry id="guc-notify-buffers" xreflabel="notify_buffers">
2149      <term><varname>notify_buffers</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2150      <indexterm>
2151       <primary><varname>notify_buffers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2152      </indexterm>
2153      </term>
2154      <listitem>
2155       <para>
2156        Specifies the amount of shared memory to use to cache the contents
2157        of <literal>pg_notify</literal> (see
2158        <xref linkend="pgdata-contents-table"/>).
2159        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
2160        that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
2161        The default value is <literal>16</literal>.
2162        This parameter can only be set at server start.
2163       </para>
2164      </listitem>
2165     </varlistentry>
2166
2167     <varlistentry id="guc-serializable-buffers" xreflabel="serializable_buffers">
2168      <term><varname>serializable_buffers</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2169      <indexterm>
2170       <primary><varname>serializable_buffers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2171      </indexterm>
2172      </term>
2173      <listitem>
2174       <para>
2175        Specifies the amount of shared memory to use to cache the contents
2176        of <literal>pg_serial</literal> (see
2177        <xref linkend="pgdata-contents-table"/>).
2178        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
2179        that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
2180        The default value is <literal>32</literal>.
2181        This parameter can only be set at server start.
2182       </para>
2183      </listitem>
2184     </varlistentry>
2185
2186     <varlistentry id="guc-subtransaction-buffers" xreflabel="subtransaction_buffers">
2187      <term><varname>subtransaction_buffers</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2188      <indexterm>
2189       <primary><varname>subtransaction_buffers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2190      </indexterm>
2191      </term>
2192      <listitem>
2193       <para>
2194        Specifies the amount of shared memory to use to cache the contents
2195        of <literal>pg_subtrans</literal> (see
2196        <xref linkend="pgdata-contents-table"/>).
2197        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
2198        that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
2199        The default value is <literal>0</literal>, which requests
2200        <varname>shared_buffers</varname>/512 up to 1024 blocks,
2201        but not fewer than 16 blocks.
2202        This parameter can only be set at server start.
2203       </para>
2204      </listitem>
2205     </varlistentry>
2206
2207     <varlistentry id="guc-transaction-buffers" xreflabel="transaction_buffers">
2208      <term><varname>transaction_buffers</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2209      <indexterm>
2210       <primary><varname>transaction_buffers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2211      </indexterm>
2212      </term>
2213      <listitem>
2214       <para>
2215        Specifies the amount of shared memory to use to cache the contents
2216        of <literal>pg_xact</literal> (see
2217        <xref linkend="pgdata-contents-table"/>).
2218        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
2219        that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
2220        The default value is <literal>0</literal>, which requests
2221        <varname>shared_buffers</varname>/512 up to 1024 blocks,
2222        but not fewer than 16 blocks.
2223        This parameter can only be set at server start.
2224       </para>
2225      </listitem>
2226     </varlistentry>
2227
2228     <varlistentry id="guc-max-stack-depth" xreflabel="max_stack_depth">
2229      <term><varname>max_stack_depth</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2230      <indexterm>
2231       <primary><varname>max_stack_depth</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2232      </indexterm>
2233      </term>
2234      <listitem>
2235       <para>
2236        Specifies the maximum safe depth of the server's execution stack.
2237        The ideal setting for this parameter is the actual stack size limit
2238        enforced by the kernel (as set by <literal>ulimit -s</literal> or local
2239        equivalent), less a safety margin of a megabyte or so.  The safety
2240        margin is needed because the stack depth is not checked in every
2241        routine in the server, but only in key potentially-recursive routines.
2242        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as kilobytes.
2243        The default setting is two megabytes (<literal>2MB</literal>), which
2244        is conservatively small and unlikely to risk crashes.  However,
2245        it might be too small to allow execution of complex functions.
2246        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
2247        privilege can change this setting.
2248       </para>
2249
2250       <para>
2251        Setting <varname>max_stack_depth</varname> higher than
2252        the actual kernel limit will mean that a runaway recursive function
2253        can crash an individual backend process.  On platforms where
2254        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> can determine the kernel limit,
2255        the server will not allow this variable to be set to an unsafe
2256        value.  However, not all platforms provide the information,
2257        so caution is recommended in selecting a value.
2258       </para>
2259      </listitem>
2260     </varlistentry>
2261
2262     <varlistentry id="guc-shared-memory-type" xreflabel="shared_memory_type">
2263      <term><varname>shared_memory_type</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
2264      <indexterm>
2265       <primary><varname>shared_memory_type</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2266      </indexterm>
2267      </term>
2268      <listitem>
2269       <para>
2270        Specifies the shared memory implementation that the server
2271        should use for the main shared memory region that holds
2272        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>'s shared buffers and other
2273        shared data.  Possible values are <literal>mmap</literal> (for
2274        anonymous shared memory allocated using <function>mmap</function>),
2275        <literal>sysv</literal> (for System V shared memory allocated via
2276        <function>shmget</function>) and <literal>windows</literal> (for Windows
2277        shared memory).  Not all values are supported on all platforms; the
2278        first supported option is the default for that platform.  The use of
2279        the <literal>sysv</literal> option, which is not the default on any
2280        platform, is generally discouraged because it typically requires
2281        non-default kernel settings to allow for large allocations (see <xref
2282        linkend="sysvipc"/>).
2283        This parameter can only be set at server start.
2284       </para>
2285      </listitem>
2286     </varlistentry>
2287
2288     <varlistentry id="guc-dynamic-shared-memory-type" xreflabel="dynamic_shared_memory_type">
2289      <term><varname>dynamic_shared_memory_type</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
2290      <indexterm>
2291       <primary><varname>dynamic_shared_memory_type</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2292      </indexterm>
2293      </term>
2294      <listitem>
2295       <para>
2296        Specifies the dynamic shared memory implementation that the server
2297        should use.  Possible values are <literal>posix</literal> (for POSIX shared
2298        memory allocated using <literal>shm_open</literal>), <literal>sysv</literal>
2299        (for System V shared memory allocated via <literal>shmget</literal>),
2300        <literal>windows</literal> (for Windows shared memory),
2301        and <literal>mmap</literal> (to simulate shared memory using
2302        memory-mapped files stored in the data directory).
2303        Not all values are supported on all platforms; the first supported
2304        option is usually the default for that platform.  The use of the
2305        <literal>mmap</literal> option, which is not the default on any platform,
2306        is generally discouraged because the operating system may write
2307        modified pages back to disk repeatedly, increasing system I/O load;
2308        however, it may be useful for debugging, when the
2309        <literal>pg_dynshmem</literal> directory is stored on a RAM disk, or when
2310        other shared memory facilities are not available.
2311        This parameter can only be set at server start.
2312       </para>
2313      </listitem>
2314     </varlistentry>
2315
2316     <varlistentry id="guc-min-dynamic-shared-memory" xreflabel="min_dynamic_shared_memory">
2317      <term><varname>min_dynamic_shared_memory</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2318      <indexterm>
2319       <primary><varname>min_dynamic_shared_memory</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2320      </indexterm>
2321      </term>
2322      <listitem>
2323       <para>
2324        Specifies the amount of memory that should be allocated at server
2325        startup for use by parallel queries.  When this memory region is
2326        insufficient or exhausted by concurrent queries, new parallel queries
2327        try to allocate extra shared memory temporarily from the operating
2328        system using the method configured with
2329        <varname>dynamic_shared_memory_type</varname>, which may be slower due
2330        to memory management overheads.  Memory that is allocated at startup
2331        with <varname>min_dynamic_shared_memory</varname> is affected by
2332        the <varname>huge_pages</varname> setting on operating systems where
2333        that is supported, and may be more likely to benefit from larger pages
2334        on operating systems where that is managed automatically.
2335        The default value is <literal>0</literal> (none). This parameter can
2336        only be set at server start.
2337       </para>
2338      </listitem>
2339     </varlistentry>
2340
2341     </variablelist>
2342     </sect2>
2343
2344     <sect2 id="runtime-config-resource-disk">
2345     <title>Disk</title>
2346
2347     <variablelist>
2348     <varlistentry id="guc-temp-file-limit" xreflabel="temp_file_limit">
2349      <term><varname>temp_file_limit</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2350      <indexterm>
2351       <primary><varname>temp_file_limit</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2352      </indexterm>
2353      </term>
2354      <listitem>
2355       <para>
2356        Specifies the maximum amount of disk space that a process can use
2357        for temporary files, such as sort and hash temporary files, or the
2358        storage file for a held cursor.  A transaction attempting to exceed
2359        this limit will be canceled.
2360        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as kilobytes.
2361        <literal>-1</literal> (the default) means no limit.
2362        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
2363        privilege can change this setting.
2364       </para>
2365       <para>
2366        This setting constrains the total space used at any instant by all
2367        temporary files used by a given <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> process.
2368        It should be noted that disk space used for explicit temporary
2369        tables, as opposed to temporary files used behind-the-scenes in query
2370        execution, does <emphasis>not</emphasis> count against this limit.
2371       </para>
2372      </listitem>
2373     </varlistentry>
2374
2375     <varlistentry id="guc-file-copy-method" xreflabel="file_copy_method">
2376      <term><varname>file_copy_method</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
2377      <indexterm>
2378       <primary><varname>file_copy_method</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2379      </indexterm>
2380      </term>
2381      <listitem>
2382       <para>
2383        Specifies the method used to copy files.
2384        Possible values are <literal>COPY</literal> (default) and
2385        <literal>CLONE</literal> (if operating support is available).
2386       </para>
2387
2388       <para>
2389       This parameter affects:
2390       </para>
2391       <itemizedlist>
2392        <listitem>
2393        <para>
2394         <literal><command>CREATE DATABASE ... STRATEGY=FILE_COPY</command></literal>
2395        </para>
2396        </listitem>
2397        <listitem>
2398        <para>
2399         <command>ALTER DATABASE ... SET TABLESPACE ...</command>
2400        </para>
2401        </listitem>
2402       </itemizedlist>
2403
2404       <para>
2405        <literal>CLONE</literal> uses the <function>copy_file_range()</function>
2406        (Linux, FreeBSD) or <function>copyfile</function>
2407        (macOS) system calls, giving the kernel the opportunity to share disk
2408        blocks or push work down to lower layers on some file systems.
2409       </para>
2410      </listitem>
2411     </varlistentry>
2412
2413     <varlistentry id="guc-file-extend-method" xreflabel="file_extend_method">
2414      <term><varname>file_extend_method</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
2415      <indexterm>
2416       <primary><varname>file_extend_method</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2417      </indexterm>
2418      </term>
2419      <listitem>
2420       <para>
2421        Specifies the method used to extend data files during bulk operations
2422        such as <command>COPY</command>.  The first available option is used as
2423        the default, depending on the operating system:
2424        <itemizedlist>
2425         <listitem>
2426          <para>
2427           <literal>posix_fallocate</literal> (Unix) uses the standard POSIX
2428            interface for allocating disk space, but is missing on some systems.
2429            If it is present but the underlying file system doesn't support it,
2430            this option silently falls back to <literal>write_zeros</literal>.
2431            Current versions of BTRFS are known to disable compression when
2432            this option is used.
2433            This is the default on systems that have the function.
2434           </para>
2435         </listitem>
2436         <listitem>
2437          <para>
2438           <literal>write_zeros</literal> extends files by writing out blocks
2439            of zero bytes.  This is the default on systems that don't have the
2440            function <function>posix_fallocate</function>.
2441          </para>
2442         </listitem>
2443        </itemizedlist>
2444        The <literal>write_zeros</literal> method is always used when data
2445        files are extended by 8 blocks or fewer.
2446       </para>
2447      </listitem>
2448     </varlistentry>
2449
2450     <varlistentry id="guc-max-notify-queue-pages" xreflabel="max_notify_queue_pages">
2451      <term><varname>max_notify_queue_pages</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2452      <indexterm>
2453       <primary><varname>max_notify_queue_pages</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2454      </indexterm>
2455      </term>
2456      <listitem>
2457       <para>
2458        Specifies the maximum amount of allocated pages for
2459        <xref linkend="sql-notify"/> / <xref linkend="sql-listen"/> queue.
2460        The default value is 1048576. For 8 KB pages it allows to consume
2461        up to 8 GB of disk space.
2462        This parameter can only be set at server start.
2463       </para>
2464      </listitem>
2465     </varlistentry>
2466
2467     </variablelist>
2468     </sect2>
2469
2470     <sect2 id="runtime-config-resource-kernel">
2471     <title>Kernel Resource Usage</title>
2472
2473     <variablelist>
2474     <varlistentry id="guc-max-files-per-process" xreflabel="max_files_per_process">
2475      <term><varname>max_files_per_process</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2476      <indexterm>
2477       <primary><varname>max_files_per_process</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2478      </indexterm>
2479      </term>
2480      <listitem>
2481       <para>
2482        Sets the maximum number of open files each server subprocess is
2483        allowed to open simultaneously;  files already opened in the
2484        postmaster are not counted toward this limit. The default is one
2485        thousand files.
2486       </para>
2487       <para>
2488        If the kernel is enforcing
2489        a safe per-process limit, you don't need to worry about this setting.
2490        But on some platforms (notably, most BSD systems), the kernel will
2491        allow individual processes to open many more files than the system
2492        can actually support if many processes all try to open
2493        that many files. If you find yourself seeing <quote>Too many open
2494        files</quote> failures, try reducing this setting.
2495        This parameter can only be set at server start.
2496       </para>
2497      </listitem>
2498     </varlistentry>
2499     </variablelist>
2500    </sect2>
2501
2502
2503    <sect2 id="runtime-config-resource-background-writer">
2504     <title>Background Writer</title>
2505
2506     <para>
2507      There is a separate server
2508      process called the <firstterm>background writer</firstterm>, whose function
2509      is to issue writes of <quote>dirty</quote> (new or modified) shared
2510      buffers.  When the number of clean shared buffers appears to be
2511      insufficient, the background writer writes some dirty buffers to the
2512      file system and marks them as clean.  This reduces the likelihood
2513      that server processes handling user queries will be unable to find
2514      clean buffers and have to write dirty buffers themselves.
2515      However, the background writer does cause a net overall
2516      increase in I/O load, because while a repeatedly-dirtied page might
2517      otherwise be written only once per checkpoint interval, the
2518      background writer might write it several times as it is dirtied
2519      in the same interval.  The parameters discussed in this subsection
2520      can be used to tune the behavior for local needs.
2521     </para>
2522
2523     <variablelist>
2524      <varlistentry id="guc-bgwriter-delay" xreflabel="bgwriter_delay">
2525       <term><varname>bgwriter_delay</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2526       <indexterm>
2527        <primary><varname>bgwriter_delay</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2528       </indexterm>
2529       </term>
2530       <listitem>
2531        <para>
2532         Specifies the delay between activity rounds for the
2533         background writer.  In each round the writer issues writes
2534         for some number of dirty buffers (controllable by the
2535         following parameters).  It then sleeps for
2536         the length of <varname>bgwriter_delay</varname>, and repeats.
2537         When there are no dirty buffers in the
2538         buffer pool, though, it goes into a longer sleep regardless of
2539         <varname>bgwriter_delay</varname>.
2540         If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
2541         The default value is 200
2542         milliseconds (<literal>200ms</literal>). Note that on some systems, the
2543         effective resolution of sleep delays is 10 milliseconds; setting
2544         <varname>bgwriter_delay</varname> to a value that is not a multiple of 10
2545         might have the same results as setting it to the next higher multiple
2546         of 10.  This parameter can only be set in the
2547         <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command line.
2548        </para>
2549       </listitem>
2550      </varlistentry>
2551
2552      <varlistentry id="guc-bgwriter-lru-maxpages" xreflabel="bgwriter_lru_maxpages">
2553       <term><varname>bgwriter_lru_maxpages</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2554       <indexterm>
2555        <primary><varname>bgwriter_lru_maxpages</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2556       </indexterm>
2557       </term>
2558       <listitem>
2559        <para>
2560         In each round, no more than this many buffers will be written
2561         by the background writer.  Setting this to zero disables
2562         background writing.  (Note that checkpoints, which are managed by
2563         a separate, dedicated auxiliary process, are unaffected.)
2564         The default value is 100 buffers.
2565         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
2566         file or on the server command line.
2567        </para>
2568       </listitem>
2569      </varlistentry>
2570
2571      <varlistentry id="guc-bgwriter-lru-multiplier" xreflabel="bgwriter_lru_multiplier">
2572       <term><varname>bgwriter_lru_multiplier</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
2573       <indexterm>
2574        <primary><varname>bgwriter_lru_multiplier</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2575       </indexterm>
2576       </term>
2577       <listitem>
2578        <para>
2579         The number of dirty buffers written in each round is based on the
2580         number of new buffers that have been needed by server processes
2581         during recent rounds.  The average recent need is multiplied by
2582         <varname>bgwriter_lru_multiplier</varname> to arrive at an estimate of the
2583         number of buffers that will be needed during the next round.  Dirty
2584         buffers are written until there are that many clean, reusable buffers
2585         available.  (However, no more than <varname>bgwriter_lru_maxpages</varname>
2586         buffers will be written per round.)
2587         Thus, a setting of 1.0 represents a <quote>just in time</quote> policy
2588         of writing exactly the number of buffers predicted to be needed.
2589         Larger values provide some cushion against spikes in demand,
2590         while smaller values intentionally leave writes to be done by
2591         server processes.
2592         The default is 2.0.
2593         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
2594         file or on the server command line.
2595        </para>
2596       </listitem>
2597      </varlistentry>
2598
2599      <varlistentry id="guc-bgwriter-flush-after" xreflabel="bgwriter_flush_after">
2600       <term><varname>bgwriter_flush_after</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2601       <indexterm>
2602        <primary><varname>bgwriter_flush_after</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2603       </indexterm>
2604       </term>
2605       <listitem>
2606        <para>
2607         Whenever more than this amount of data has
2608         been written by the background writer, attempt to force the OS to issue these
2609         writes to the underlying storage.  Doing so will limit the amount of
2610         dirty data in the kernel's page cache, reducing the likelihood of
2611         stalls when an <function>fsync</function> is issued at the end of a checkpoint, or when
2612         the OS writes data back in larger batches in the background.  Often
2613         that will result in greatly reduced transaction latency, but there
2614         also are some cases, especially with workloads that are bigger than
2615         <xref linkend="guc-shared-buffers"/>, but smaller than the OS's page
2616         cache, where performance might degrade.  This setting may have no
2617         effect on some platforms.
2618         If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
2619         that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
2620         The valid range is between
2621         <literal>0</literal>, which disables forced writeback, and
2622         <literal>2MB</literal>.  The default is <literal>512kB</literal> on Linux,
2623         <literal>0</literal> elsewhere.  (If <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> is not 8kB,
2624         the default and maximum values scale proportionally to it.)
2625         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
2626         file or on the server command line.
2627        </para>
2628       </listitem>
2629      </varlistentry>
2630     </variablelist>
2631
2632     <para>
2633      Smaller values of <varname>bgwriter_lru_maxpages</varname> and
2634      <varname>bgwriter_lru_multiplier</varname> reduce the extra I/O load
2635      caused by the background writer, but make it more likely that server
2636      processes will have to issue writes for themselves, delaying interactive
2637      queries.
2638     </para>
2639    </sect2>
2640
2641    <sect2 id="runtime-config-resource-io">
2642     <title>I/O</title>
2643
2644     <variablelist>
2645      <varlistentry id="guc-backend-flush-after" xreflabel="backend_flush_after">
2646       <term><varname>backend_flush_after</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2647       <indexterm>
2648        <primary><varname>backend_flush_after</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2649       </indexterm>
2650       </term>
2651       <listitem>
2652        <para>
2653         Whenever more than this amount of data has
2654         been written by a single backend, attempt to force the OS to issue
2655         these writes to the underlying storage.  Doing so will limit the
2656         amount of dirty data in the kernel's page cache, reducing the
2657         likelihood of stalls when an <function>fsync</function> is issued at the end of a
2658         checkpoint, or when the OS writes data back in larger batches in the
2659         background.  Often that will result in greatly reduced transaction
2660         latency, but there also are some cases, especially with workloads
2661         that are bigger than <xref linkend="guc-shared-buffers"/>, but smaller
2662         than the OS's page cache, where performance might degrade.  This
2663         setting may have no effect on some platforms.
2664         If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
2665         that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
2666         The valid range is
2667         between <literal>0</literal>, which disables forced writeback,
2668         and <literal>2MB</literal>.  The default is <literal>0</literal>, i.e., no
2669         forced writeback.  (If <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> is not 8kB,
2670         the maximum value scales proportionally to it.)
2671        </para>
2672       </listitem>
2673      </varlistentry>
2674
2675      <varlistentry id="guc-effective-io-concurrency" xreflabel="effective_io_concurrency">
2676       <term><varname>effective_io_concurrency</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2677       <indexterm>
2678        <primary><varname>effective_io_concurrency</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2679       </indexterm>
2680       </term>
2681       <listitem>
2682        <para>
2683         Sets the number of concurrent storage I/O operations that
2684         <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> expects can be executed
2685         simultaneously.  Raising this value will increase the number of I/O
2686         operations that any individual <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
2687         session attempts to initiate in parallel.  The allowed range is
2688         <literal>1</literal> to <literal>1000</literal>, or
2689         <literal>0</literal> to disable issuance of asynchronous I/O requests.
2690         The default is <literal>16</literal>.
2691        </para>
2692
2693        <para>
2694         Higher values will have the most impact on higher latency storage
2695         where queries otherwise experience noticeable I/O stalls and on
2696         devices with high IOPs. Unnecessarily high values may increase I/O
2697         latency for all queries on the system.
2698        </para>
2699
2700        <para>
2701         On systems with prefetch advice support,
2702         <varname>effective_io_concurrency</varname> also controls the
2703         prefetch distance.
2704        </para>
2705
2706        <para>
2707         This value can be overridden for tables in a particular tablespace by
2708         setting the tablespace parameter of the same name (see <xref
2709         linkend="sql-altertablespace"/>).
2710        </para>
2711       </listitem>
2712      </varlistentry>
2713
2714      <varlistentry id="guc-maintenance-io-concurrency" xreflabel="maintenance_io_concurrency">
2715       <term><varname>maintenance_io_concurrency</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2716       <indexterm>
2717        <primary><varname>maintenance_io_concurrency</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2718       </indexterm>
2719       </term>
2720       <listitem>
2721        <para>
2722         Similar to <varname>effective_io_concurrency</varname>, but used
2723         for maintenance work that is done on behalf of many client sessions.
2724        </para>
2725        <para>
2726         The default is <literal>16</literal>.  This value can be overridden
2727         for tables in a particular tablespace by setting the tablespace
2728         parameter of the same name (see <xref
2729         linkend="sql-altertablespace"/>).
2730        </para>
2731       </listitem>
2732      </varlistentry>
2733
2734      <varlistentry id="guc-io-max-combine-limit" xreflabel="io_max_combine_limit">
2735       <term><varname>io_max_combine_limit</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2736       <indexterm>
2737        <primary><varname>io_max_combine_limit</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2738       </indexterm>
2739       </term>
2740       <listitem>
2741        <para>
2742         Controls the largest I/O size in operations that combine I/O, and silently
2743         limits the user-settable parameter <varname>io_combine_limit</varname>.
2744         This parameter can only be set at server start.
2745         If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
2746         that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
2747         The maximum possible size depends on the operating system and block
2748         size, but is typically 1MB on Unix and 128kB on Windows.
2749         The default is 128kB.
2750        </para>
2751       </listitem>
2752      </varlistentry>
2753
2754      <varlistentry id="guc-io-combine-limit" xreflabel="io_combine_limit">
2755       <term><varname>io_combine_limit</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2756       <indexterm>
2757        <primary><varname>io_combine_limit</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2758       </indexterm>
2759       </term>
2760       <listitem>
2761        <para>
2762         Controls the largest I/O size in operations that combine I/O.  If set
2763         higher than the <varname>io_max_combine_limit</varname> parameter, the
2764         lower value will silently be used instead, so both may need to be raised
2765         to increase the I/O size.
2766         If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
2767         that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
2768         The maximum possible size depends on the operating system and block
2769         size, but is typically 1MB on Unix and 128kB on Windows.
2770         The default is 128kB.
2771        </para>
2772       </listitem>
2773      </varlistentry>
2774
2775      <varlistentry id="guc-io-max-concurrency" xreflabel="io_max_concurrency">
2776       <term><varname>io_max_concurrency</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2777       <indexterm>
2778        <primary><varname>io_max_concurrency</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2779       </indexterm>
2780       </term>
2781       <listitem>
2782        <para>
2783         Controls the maximum number of I/O operations that one process can
2784         execute simultaneously.
2785        </para>
2786        <para>
2787         The default setting of <literal>-1</literal> selects a number based
2788         on <xref linkend="guc-shared-buffers"/> and the maximum number of
2789         processes (<xref linkend="guc-max-connections"/>, <xref
2790         linkend="guc-autovacuum-worker-slots"/>, <xref
2791         linkend="guc-max-worker-processes"/> and <xref
2792         linkend="guc-max-wal-senders"/>), but not more than
2793         <literal>64</literal>.
2794        </para>
2795        <para>
2796         This parameter can only be set at server start.
2797        </para>
2798       </listitem>
2799      </varlistentry>
2800
2801      <varlistentry id="guc-io-method" xreflabel="io_method">
2802       <term><varname>io_method</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
2803       <indexterm>
2804        <primary><varname>io_method</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2805       </indexterm>
2806       </term>
2807       <listitem>
2808        <para>
2809         Selects the method for executing asynchronous I/O.
2810         Possible values are:
2811         <itemizedlist>
2812          <listitem>
2813           <para>
2814            <literal>worker</literal> (execute asynchronous I/O using worker processes)
2815           </para>
2816          </listitem>
2817          <listitem>
2818           <para>
2819            <literal>io_uring</literal> (execute asynchronous I/O using
2820            io_uring, requires a build with
2821            <link linkend="configure-option-with-liburing"><option>--with-liburing</option></link> /
2822            <link linkend="configure-with-liburing-meson"><option>-Dliburing</option></link>)
2823           </para>
2824          </listitem>
2825          <listitem>
2826           <para>
2827            <literal>sync</literal> (execute asynchronous-eligible I/O synchronously)
2828           </para>
2829          </listitem>
2830         </itemizedlist>
2831         The default is <literal>worker</literal>.
2832        </para>
2833        <para>
2834         This parameter can only be set at server start.
2835        </para>
2836       </listitem>
2837      </varlistentry>
2838
2839      <varlistentry id="guc-io-workers" xreflabel="io_workers">
2840       <term><varname>io_workers</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2841       <indexterm>
2842        <primary><varname>io_workers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2843       </indexterm>
2844       </term>
2845       <listitem>
2846        <para>
2847         Selects the number of I/O worker processes to use. The default is
2848         3. This parameter can only be set in the
2849         <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command
2850         line.
2851        </para>
2852        <para>
2853         Only has an effect if <xref linkend="guc-io-method"/> is set to
2854         <literal>worker</literal>.
2855        </para>
2856       </listitem>
2857      </varlistentry>
2858     </variablelist>
2859    </sect2>
2860
2861    <sect2 id="runtime-config-resource-worker-processes">
2862     <title>Worker Processes</title>
2863
2864     <variablelist>
2865      <varlistentry id="guc-max-worker-processes" xreflabel="max_worker_processes">
2866       <term><varname>max_worker_processes</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2867       <indexterm>
2868        <primary><varname>max_worker_processes</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2869       </indexterm>
2870       </term>
2871       <listitem>
2872        <para>
2873         Sets the maximum number of background processes that the cluster
2874         can support.  This parameter can only be set at server start.  The
2875         default is 8.
2876        </para>
2877
2878        <para>
2879         When running a standby server, you must set this parameter to the
2880         same or higher value than on the primary server. Otherwise, queries
2881         will not be allowed in the standby server.
2882        </para>
2883
2884        <para>
2885         When changing this value, consider also adjusting
2886         <xref linkend="guc-max-parallel-workers"/>,
2887         <xref linkend="guc-max-parallel-maintenance-workers"/>, and
2888         <xref linkend="guc-max-parallel-workers-per-gather"/>.
2889        </para>
2890       </listitem>
2891      </varlistentry>
2892
2893      <varlistentry id="guc-max-parallel-workers-per-gather" xreflabel="max_parallel_workers_per_gather">
2894       <term><varname>max_parallel_workers_per_gather</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2895       <indexterm>
2896        <primary><varname>max_parallel_workers_per_gather</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2897       </indexterm>
2898       </term>
2899       <listitem>
2900        <para>
2901         Sets the maximum number of workers that can be started by a single
2902         <literal>Gather</literal> or <literal>Gather Merge</literal> node.
2903         Parallel workers are taken from the pool of processes established by
2904         <xref linkend="guc-max-worker-processes"/>, limited by
2905         <xref linkend="guc-max-parallel-workers"/>.  Note that the requested
2906         number of workers may not actually be available at run time.  If this
2907         occurs, the plan will run with fewer workers than expected, which may
2908         be inefficient.  The default value is 2.  Setting this value to 0
2909         disables parallel query execution.
2910        </para>
2911
2912        <para>
2913         Note that parallel queries may consume very substantially more
2914         resources than non-parallel queries, because each worker process is
2915         a completely separate process which has roughly the same impact on the
2916         system as an additional user session.  This should be taken into
2917         account when choosing a value for this setting, as well as when
2918         configuring other settings that control resource utilization, such
2919         as <xref linkend="guc-work-mem"/>.  Resource limits such as
2920         <varname>work_mem</varname> are applied individually to each worker,
2921         which means the total utilization may be much higher across all
2922         processes than it would normally be for any single process.
2923         For example, a parallel query using 4 workers may use up to 5 times
2924         as much CPU time, memory, I/O bandwidth, and so forth as a query which
2925         uses no workers at all.
2926        </para>
2927
2928        <para>
2929         For more information on parallel query, see
2930         <xref linkend="parallel-query"/>.
2931        </para>
2932       </listitem>
2933      </varlistentry>
2934
2935      <varlistentry id="guc-max-parallel-maintenance-workers" xreflabel="max_parallel_maintenance_workers">
2936       <term><varname>max_parallel_maintenance_workers</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2937       <indexterm>
2938        <primary><varname>max_parallel_maintenance_workers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2939       </indexterm>
2940       </term>
2941       <listitem>
2942        <para>
2943         Sets the maximum number of parallel workers that can be
2944         started by a single utility command.  Currently, the parallel
2945         utility commands that support the use of parallel workers are
2946         <command>CREATE INDEX</command> when building a B-tree,
2947         GIN, or BRIN index,
2948         and <command>VACUUM</command> without <literal>FULL</literal>
2949         option.  Parallel workers are taken from the pool of processes
2950         established by <xref linkend="guc-max-worker-processes"/>, limited
2951         by <xref linkend="guc-max-parallel-workers"/>.  Note that the requested
2952         number of workers may not actually be available at run time.
2953         If this occurs, the utility operation will run with fewer
2954         workers than expected.  The default value is 2.  Setting this
2955         value to 0 disables the use of parallel workers by utility
2956         commands.
2957        </para>
2958
2959        <para>
2960         Note that parallel utility commands should not consume
2961         substantially more memory than equivalent non-parallel
2962         operations.  This strategy differs from that of parallel
2963         query, where resource limits generally apply per worker
2964         process.  Parallel utility commands treat the resource limit
2965         <varname>maintenance_work_mem</varname> as a limit to be applied to
2966         the entire utility command, regardless of the number of
2967         parallel worker processes.  However, parallel utility
2968         commands may still consume substantially more CPU resources
2969         and I/O bandwidth.
2970        </para>
2971       </listitem>
2972      </varlistentry>
2973
2974      <varlistentry id="guc-max-parallel-workers" xreflabel="max_parallel_workers">
2975       <term><varname>max_parallel_workers</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
2976       <indexterm>
2977        <primary><varname>max_parallel_workers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
2978       </indexterm>
2979       </term>
2980       <listitem>
2981        <para>
2982         Sets the maximum number of workers that the cluster can support for
2983         parallel operations.  The default value is 8.  When increasing or
2984         decreasing this value, consider also adjusting
2985         <xref linkend="guc-max-parallel-maintenance-workers"/> and
2986         <xref linkend="guc-max-parallel-workers-per-gather"/>.
2987         Also, note that a setting for this value which is higher than
2988         <xref linkend="guc-max-worker-processes"/> will have no effect,
2989         since parallel workers are taken from the pool of worker processes
2990         established by that setting.
2991        </para>
2992       </listitem>
2993      </varlistentry>
2994
2995      <varlistentry id="guc-parallel-leader-participation" xreflabel="parallel_leader_participation">
2996       <term>
2997       <varname>parallel_leader_participation</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
2998       <indexterm>
2999        <primary><varname>parallel_leader_participation</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3000       </indexterm>
3001       </term>
3002       <listitem>
3003        <para>
3004         Allows the leader process to execute the query plan under
3005         <literal>Gather</literal> and <literal>Gather Merge</literal> nodes
3006         instead of waiting for worker processes.  The default is
3007         <literal>on</literal>.  Setting this value to <literal>off</literal>
3008         reduces the likelihood that workers will become blocked because the
3009         leader is not reading tuples fast enough, but requires the leader
3010         process to wait for worker processes to start up before the first
3011         tuples can be produced.  The degree to which the leader can help or
3012         hinder performance depends on the plan type, number of workers and
3013         query duration.
3014        </para>
3015       </listitem>
3016      </varlistentry>
3017     </variablelist>
3018    </sect2>
3019   </sect1>
3020
3021   <sect1 id="runtime-config-wal">
3022    <title>Write Ahead Log</title>
3023
3024   <para>
3025    For additional information on tuning these settings,
3026    see <xref linkend="wal-configuration"/>.
3027   </para>
3028
3029    <sect2 id="runtime-config-wal-settings">
3030     <title>Settings</title>
3031     <variablelist>
3032
3033     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-level" xreflabel="wal_level">
3034      <term><varname>wal_level</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
3035      <indexterm>
3036       <primary><varname>wal_level</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3037      </indexterm>
3038      </term>
3039      <listitem>
3040       <para>
3041        <varname>wal_level</varname> determines how much information is written to
3042        the WAL. The default value is <literal>replica</literal>, which writes enough
3043        data to support WAL archiving and replication, including running
3044        read-only queries on a standby server. <literal>minimal</literal> removes all
3045        logging except the information required to recover from a crash or
3046        immediate shutdown.  Finally,
3047        <literal>logical</literal> adds information necessary to support logical
3048        decoding.  Each level includes the information logged at all lower
3049        levels.  This parameter can only be set at server start.
3050       </para>
3051       <para>
3052        The <literal>minimal</literal> level generates the least WAL
3053        volume.  It logs no row information for permanent relations
3054        in transactions that create or
3055        rewrite them.  This can make operations much faster (see
3056        <xref linkend="populate-pitr"/>).  Operations that initiate this
3057        optimization include:
3058        <simplelist>
3059         <member><command>ALTER ... SET TABLESPACE</command></member>
3060         <member><command>CLUSTER</command></member>
3061         <member><command>CREATE TABLE</command></member>
3062         <member><command>REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW</command>
3063         (without <option>CONCURRENTLY</option>)</member>
3064         <member><command>REINDEX</command></member>
3065         <member><command>TRUNCATE</command></member>
3066        </simplelist>
3067        However, minimal WAL does not contain sufficient information for
3068        point-in-time recovery, so <literal>replica</literal> or
3069        higher must be used to enable continuous archiving
3070        (<xref linkend="guc-archive-mode"/>) and streaming binary replication.
3071        In fact, the server will not even start in this mode if
3072        <varname>max_wal_senders</varname> is non-zero.
3073        Note that changing <varname>wal_level</varname> to
3074        <literal>minimal</literal> makes previous base backups unusable
3075        for point-in-time recovery and standby servers.
3076       </para>
3077       <para>
3078        In <literal>logical</literal> level, the same information is logged as
3079        with <literal>replica</literal>, plus information needed to
3080        extract logical change sets from the WAL. Using a level of
3081        <literal>logical</literal> will increase the WAL volume, particularly if many
3082        tables are configured for <literal>REPLICA IDENTITY FULL</literal> and
3083        many <command>UPDATE</command> and <command>DELETE</command> statements are
3084        executed.
3085       </para>
3086       <para>
3087        In releases prior to 9.6, this parameter also allowed the
3088        values <literal>archive</literal> and <literal>hot_standby</literal>.
3089        These are still accepted but mapped to <literal>replica</literal>.
3090       </para>
3091      </listitem>
3092     </varlistentry>
3093
3094     <varlistentry id="guc-fsync" xreflabel="fsync">
3095      <term><varname>fsync</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
3096      <indexterm>
3097       <primary><varname>fsync</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3098      </indexterm>
3099      </term>
3100      <listitem>
3101       <para>
3102        If this parameter is on, the <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> server
3103        will try to make sure that updates are physically written to
3104        disk, by issuing <function>fsync()</function> system calls or various
3105        equivalent methods (see <xref linkend="guc-wal-sync-method"/>).
3106        This ensures that the database cluster can recover to a
3107        consistent state after an operating system or hardware crash.
3108       </para>
3109
3110       <para>
3111        While turning off <varname>fsync</varname> is often a performance
3112        benefit, this can result in unrecoverable data corruption in
3113        the event of a power failure or system crash.  Thus it
3114        is only advisable to turn off <varname>fsync</varname> if
3115        you can easily recreate your entire database from external
3116        data.
3117       </para>
3118
3119       <para>
3120        Examples of safe circumstances for turning off
3121        <varname>fsync</varname> include the initial loading of a new
3122        database cluster from a backup file, using a database cluster
3123        for processing a batch of data after which the database
3124        will be thrown away and recreated,
3125        or for a read-only database clone which
3126        gets recreated frequently and is not used for failover.  High
3127        quality hardware alone is not a sufficient justification for
3128        turning off <varname>fsync</varname>.
3129       </para>
3130
3131       <para>
3132        For reliable recovery when changing <varname>fsync</varname>
3133        off to on, it is necessary to force all modified buffers in the
3134        kernel to durable storage.  This can be done while the cluster
3135        is shutdown or while <varname>fsync</varname> is on by running <command>initdb
3136        --sync-only</command>, running <command>sync</command>, unmounting the
3137        file system, or rebooting the server.
3138       </para>
3139
3140       <para>
3141        In many situations, turning off <xref linkend="guc-synchronous-commit"/>
3142        for noncritical transactions can provide much of the potential
3143        performance benefit of turning off <varname>fsync</varname>, without
3144        the attendant risks of data corruption.
3145       </para>
3146
3147       <para>
3148        <varname>fsync</varname> can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
3149        file or on the server command line.
3150        If you turn this parameter off, also consider turning off
3151        <xref linkend="guc-full-page-writes"/>.
3152       </para>
3153      </listitem>
3154     </varlistentry>
3155
3156     <varlistentry id="guc-synchronous-commit" xreflabel="synchronous_commit">
3157      <term><varname>synchronous_commit</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
3158      <indexterm>
3159       <primary><varname>synchronous_commit</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3160      </indexterm>
3161      </term>
3162      <listitem>
3163       <para>
3164        Specifies how much WAL processing must complete before
3165        the database server returns a <quote>success</quote>
3166        indication to the client.  Valid values are
3167        <literal>remote_apply</literal>, <literal>on</literal>
3168        (the default), <literal>remote_write</literal>,
3169        <literal>local</literal>, and <literal>off</literal>.
3170       </para>
3171
3172       <para>
3173        If <varname>synchronous_standby_names</varname> is empty,
3174        the only meaningful settings are <literal>on</literal> and
3175        <literal>off</literal>;  <literal>remote_apply</literal>,
3176        <literal>remote_write</literal> and <literal>local</literal>
3177        all provide the same local synchronization level
3178        as <literal>on</literal>.  The local behavior of all
3179        non-<literal>off</literal> modes is to wait for local flush of WAL
3180        to disk.  In <literal>off</literal> mode, there is no waiting,
3181        so there can be a delay between when success is reported to the
3182        client and when the transaction is later guaranteed to be safe
3183        against a server crash.  (The maximum
3184        delay is three times <xref linkend="guc-wal-writer-delay"/>.)  Unlike
3185        <xref linkend="guc-fsync"/>, setting this parameter to <literal>off</literal>
3186        does not create any risk of database inconsistency: an operating
3187        system or database crash might
3188        result in some recent allegedly-committed transactions being lost, but
3189        the database state will be just the same as if those transactions had
3190        been aborted cleanly.  So, turning <varname>synchronous_commit</varname> off
3191        can be a useful alternative when performance is more important than
3192        exact certainty about the durability of a transaction.  For more
3193        discussion see <xref linkend="wal-async-commit"/>.
3194       </para>
3195
3196       <para>
3197        If <xref linkend="guc-synchronous-standby-names"/> is non-empty,
3198        <varname>synchronous_commit</varname> also controls whether
3199        transaction commits will wait for their WAL records to be
3200        processed on the standby server(s).
3201       </para>
3202
3203       <para>
3204        When set to <literal>remote_apply</literal>, commits will wait
3205        until replies from the current synchronous standby(s) indicate they
3206        have received the commit record of the transaction and applied
3207        it, so that it has become visible to queries on the standby(s),
3208        and also written to durable storage on the standbys.  This will
3209        cause much larger commit delays than previous settings since
3210        it waits for WAL replay.  When set to <literal>on</literal>,
3211        commits wait until replies
3212        from the current synchronous standby(s) indicate they have received
3213        the commit record of the transaction and flushed it to durable storage.  This
3214        ensures the transaction will not be lost unless both the primary and
3215        all synchronous standbys suffer corruption of their database storage.
3216        When set to <literal>remote_write</literal>, commits will wait until replies
3217        from the current synchronous standby(s) indicate they have
3218        received the commit record of the transaction and written it to
3219        their file systems. This setting ensures data preservation if a standby instance of
3220        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> crashes, but not if the standby
3221        suffers an operating-system-level crash because the data has not
3222        necessarily reached durable storage on the standby.
3223        The setting <literal>local</literal> causes commits to wait for
3224        local flush to disk, but not for replication.  This is usually not
3225        desirable when synchronous replication is in use, but is provided for
3226        completeness.
3227       </para>
3228
3229       <para>
3230        This parameter can be changed at any time; the behavior for any
3231        one transaction is determined by the setting in effect when it
3232        commits.  It is therefore possible, and useful, to have some
3233        transactions commit synchronously and others asynchronously.
3234        For example, to make a single multistatement transaction commit
3235        asynchronously when the default is the opposite, issue <command>SET
3236        LOCAL synchronous_commit TO OFF</command> within the transaction.
3237       </para>
3238
3239       <para>
3240        <xref linkend="synchronous-commit-matrix"/> summarizes the
3241        capabilities of the <varname>synchronous_commit</varname> settings.
3242       </para>
3243
3244       <table id="synchronous-commit-matrix">
3245        <title>synchronous_commit Modes</title>
3246        <tgroup cols="5">
3247         <colspec colname="col1" colwidth="1.5*"/>
3248         <colspec colname="col2" colwidth="1*"/>
3249         <colspec colname="col3" colwidth="1*"/>
3250         <colspec colname="col4" colwidth="1*"/>
3251         <colspec colname="col5" colwidth="1*"/>
3252         <thead>
3253          <row>
3254           <entry>synchronous_commit setting</entry>
3255           <entry>local durable commit</entry>
3256           <entry>standby durable commit after PG crash</entry>
3257           <entry>standby durable commit after OS crash</entry>
3258           <entry>standby query consistency</entry>
3259          </row>
3260         </thead>
3261
3262         <tbody>
3263
3264          <row>
3265           <entry>remote_apply</entry>
3266           <entry align="center">&bull;</entry>
3267           <entry align="center">&bull;</entry>
3268           <entry align="center">&bull;</entry>
3269           <entry align="center">&bull;</entry>
3270          </row>
3271
3272          <row>
3273           <entry>on</entry>
3274           <entry align="center">&bull;</entry>
3275           <entry align="center">&bull;</entry>
3276           <entry align="center">&bull;</entry>
3277           <entry align="center"></entry>
3278          </row>
3279
3280          <row>
3281           <entry>remote_write</entry>
3282           <entry align="center">&bull;</entry>
3283           <entry align="center">&bull;</entry>
3284           <entry align="center"></entry>
3285           <entry align="center"></entry>
3286          </row>
3287
3288          <row>
3289           <entry>local</entry>
3290           <entry align="center">&bull;</entry>
3291           <entry align="center"></entry>
3292           <entry align="center"></entry>
3293           <entry align="center"></entry>
3294          </row>
3295
3296          <row>
3297           <entry>off</entry>
3298           <entry align="center"></entry>
3299           <entry align="center"></entry>
3300           <entry align="center"></entry>
3301           <entry align="center"></entry>
3302          </row>
3303
3304         </tbody>
3305        </tgroup>
3306       </table>
3307
3308      </listitem>
3309     </varlistentry>
3310
3311     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-sync-method" xreflabel="wal_sync_method">
3312      <term><varname>wal_sync_method</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
3313      <indexterm>
3314       <primary><varname>wal_sync_method</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3315      </indexterm>
3316      </term>
3317      <listitem>
3318       <para>
3319        Method used for forcing WAL updates out to disk.
3320        If <varname>fsync</varname> is off then this setting is irrelevant,
3321        since WAL file updates will not be forced out at all.
3322        Possible values are:
3323       </para>
3324       <itemizedlist>
3325        <listitem>
3326        <para>
3327         <literal>open_datasync</literal> (write WAL files with <function>open()</function> option <symbol>O_DSYNC</symbol>)
3328        </para>
3329        </listitem>
3330        <listitem>
3331        <para>
3332         <literal>fdatasync</literal> (call <function>fdatasync()</function> at each commit)
3333        </para>
3334        </listitem>
3335        <listitem>
3336        <para>
3337         <literal>fsync</literal> (call <function>fsync()</function> at each commit)
3338        </para>
3339        </listitem>
3340        <listitem>
3341        <para>
