3342         <literal>fsync_writethrough</literal> (call <function>fsync()</function> at each commit, forcing write-through of any disk write cache)
3343        </para>
3344        </listitem>
3345        <listitem>
3346        <para>
3347         <literal>open_sync</literal> (write WAL files with <function>open()</function> option <symbol>O_SYNC</symbol>)
3348        </para>
3349        </listitem>
3350       </itemizedlist>
3351       <para>
3352        Not all of these choices are available on all platforms.
3353        The default is the first method in the above list that is supported
3354        by the platform, except that <literal>fdatasync</literal> is the default on
3355        Linux and FreeBSD.  The default is not necessarily ideal; it might be
3356        necessary to change this setting or other aspects of your system
3357        configuration in order to create a crash-safe configuration or
3358        achieve optimal performance.
3359        These aspects are discussed in <xref linkend="wal-reliability"/>.
3360        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
3361        file or on the server command line.
3362       </para>
3363      </listitem>
3364     </varlistentry>
3365
3366     <varlistentry id="guc-full-page-writes" xreflabel="full_page_writes">
3367      <term><varname>full_page_writes</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
3368      <indexterm>
3369       <primary><varname>full_page_writes</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3370      </indexterm>
3371      </term>
3372      <listitem>
3373       <para>
3374        When this parameter is on, the <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> server
3375        writes the entire content of each disk page to WAL during the
3376        first modification of that page after a checkpoint.
3377        This is needed because
3378        a page write that is in process during an operating system crash might
3379        be only partially completed, leading to an on-disk page
3380        that contains a mix of old and new data.  The row-level change data
3381        normally stored in WAL will not be enough to completely restore
3382        such a page during post-crash recovery.  Storing the full page image
3383        guarantees that the page can be correctly restored, but at the price
3384        of increasing the amount of data that must be written to WAL.
3385        (Because WAL replay always starts from a checkpoint, it is sufficient
3386        to do this during the first change of each page after a checkpoint.
3387        Therefore, one way to reduce the cost of full-page writes is to
3388        increase the checkpoint interval parameters.)
3389       </para>
3390
3391       <para>
3392        Turning this parameter off speeds normal operation, but
3393        might lead to either unrecoverable data corruption, or silent
3394        data corruption, after a system failure. The risks are similar to turning off
3395        <varname>fsync</varname>, though smaller, and it should be turned off
3396        only based on the same circumstances recommended for that parameter.
3397       </para>
3398
3399       <para>
3400        Turning off this parameter does not affect use of
3401        WAL archiving for point-in-time recovery (PITR)
3402        (see <xref linkend="continuous-archiving"/>).
3403       </para>
3404
3405       <para>
3406        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
3407        file or on the server command line.
3408        The default is <literal>on</literal>.
3409       </para>
3410      </listitem>
3411     </varlistentry>
3412
3413     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-log-hints" xreflabel="wal_log_hints">
3414      <term><varname>wal_log_hints</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
3415      <indexterm>
3416       <primary><varname>wal_log_hints</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3417      </indexterm>
3418      </term>
3419      <listitem>
3420       <para>
3421        When this parameter is <literal>on</literal>, the <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
3422        server writes the entire content of each disk page to WAL during the
3423        first modification of that page after a checkpoint, even for
3424        non-critical modifications of so-called hint bits.
3425       </para>
3426
3427       <para>
3428        If data checksums are enabled, hint bit updates are always WAL-logged
3429        and this setting is ignored. You can use this setting to test how much
3430        extra WAL-logging would occur if your database had data checksums
3431        enabled.
3432       </para>
3433
3434       <para>
3435        This parameter can only be set at server start. The default value is <literal>off</literal>.
3436       </para>
3437      </listitem>
3438     </varlistentry>
3439
3440     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-compression" xreflabel="wal_compression">
3441      <term><varname>wal_compression</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
3442      <indexterm>
3443       <primary><varname>wal_compression</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3444      </indexterm>
3445      </term>
3446      <listitem>
3447       <para>
3448        This parameter enables compression of WAL using the specified
3449        compression method.
3450        When enabled, the <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
3451        server compresses full page images written to WAL (e.g. when
3452        <xref linkend="guc-full-page-writes"/> is on, during a base backup,
3453        etc.).
3454        A compressed page image will be decompressed during WAL replay.
3455        The supported methods are <literal>pglz</literal>,
3456        <literal>lz4</literal> (if <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
3457        was compiled with <option>--with-lz4</option>) and
3458        <literal>zstd</literal> (if <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
3459        was compiled with <option>--with-zstd</option>).
3460        The default value is <literal>off</literal>.
3461        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
3462        privilege can change this setting.
3463       </para>
3464
3465       <para>
3466        Enabling compression can reduce the WAL volume without
3467        increasing the risk of unrecoverable data corruption,
3468        but at the cost of some extra CPU spent on the compression during
3469        WAL logging and on the decompression during WAL replay.
3470       </para>
3471      </listitem>
3472     </varlistentry>
3473
3474     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-init-zero" xreflabel="wal_init_zero">
3475      <term><varname>wal_init_zero</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
3476      <indexterm>
3477       <primary><varname>wal_init_zero</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3478      </indexterm>
3479      </term>
3480      <listitem>
3481       <para>
3482        If set to <literal>on</literal> (the default), this option causes new
3483        WAL files to be filled with zeroes.  On some file systems, this ensures
3484        that space is allocated before we need to write WAL records.  However,
3485        <firstterm>Copy-On-Write</firstterm> (COW) file systems may not benefit
3486        from this technique, so the option is given to skip the unnecessary
3487        work.  If set to <literal>off</literal>, only the final byte is written
3488        when the file is created so that it has the expected size.
3489       </para>
3490      </listitem>
3491     </varlistentry>
3492
3493     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-recycle" xreflabel="wal_recycle">
3494      <term><varname>wal_recycle</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
3495      <indexterm>
3496       <primary><varname>wal_recycle</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3497      </indexterm>
3498      </term>
3499      <listitem>
3500       <para>
3501        If set to <literal>on</literal> (the default), this option causes WAL
3502        files to be recycled by renaming them, avoiding the need to create new
3503        ones.  On COW file systems, it may be faster to create new ones, so the
3504        option is given to disable this behavior.
3505       </para>
3506      </listitem>
3507     </varlistentry>
3508
3509     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-buffers" xreflabel="wal_buffers">
3510      <term><varname>wal_buffers</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
3511      <indexterm>
3512       <primary><varname>wal_buffers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3513      </indexterm>
3514      </term>
3515      <listitem>
3516       <para>
3517        The amount of shared memory used for WAL data that has not yet been
3518        written to disk.  The default setting of -1 selects a size equal to
3519        1/32nd (about 3%) of <xref linkend="guc-shared-buffers"/>, but not less
3520        than <literal>64kB</literal> nor more than the size of one WAL
3521        segment, typically <literal>16MB</literal>.  This value can be set
3522        manually if the automatic choice is too large or too small,
3523        but any positive value less than <literal>32kB</literal> will be
3524        treated as <literal>32kB</literal>.
3525        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as WAL blocks,
3526        that is <symbol>XLOG_BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
3527        This parameter can only be set at server start.
3528       </para>
3529
3530       <para>
3531        The contents of the WAL buffers are written out to disk at every
3532        transaction commit, so extremely large values are unlikely to
3533        provide a significant benefit.  However, setting this value to at
3534        least a few megabytes can improve write performance on a busy
3535        server where many clients are committing at once.  The auto-tuning
3536        selected by the default setting of -1 should give reasonable
3537        results in most cases.
3538       </para>
3539
3540      </listitem>
3541     </varlistentry>
3542
3543     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-writer-delay" xreflabel="wal_writer_delay">
3544      <term><varname>wal_writer_delay</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
3545      <indexterm>
3546       <primary><varname>wal_writer_delay</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3547      </indexterm>
3548      </term>
3549      <listitem>
3550      <para>
3551        Specifies how often the WAL writer flushes WAL, in time terms.
3552        After flushing WAL the writer sleeps for the length of time given
3553        by <varname>wal_writer_delay</varname>, unless woken up sooner
3554        by an asynchronously committing transaction. If the last flush
3555        happened less than <varname>wal_writer_delay</varname> ago and less
3556        than <varname>wal_writer_flush_after</varname> worth of WAL has been
3557        produced since, then WAL is only written to the operating system, not
3558        flushed to disk.
3559        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
3560        The default value is 200 milliseconds (<literal>200ms</literal>).  Note that
3561        on some systems, the effective resolution of sleep delays is 10
3562        milliseconds; setting <varname>wal_writer_delay</varname> to a value that is
3563        not a multiple of 10 might have the same results as setting it to the
3564        next higher multiple of 10. This parameter can only be set in the
3565        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command line.
3566       </para>
3567      </listitem>
3568     </varlistentry>
3569
3570     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-writer-flush-after" xreflabel="wal_writer_flush_after">
3571      <term><varname>wal_writer_flush_after</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
3572      <indexterm>
3573       <primary><varname>wal_writer_flush_after</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3574      </indexterm>
3575      </term>
3576      <listitem>
3577      <para>
3578        Specifies how often the WAL writer flushes WAL, in volume terms.
3579        If the last flush happened less
3580        than <varname>wal_writer_delay</varname> ago and less
3581        than <varname>wal_writer_flush_after</varname> worth of WAL has been
3582        produced since, then WAL is only written to the operating system, not
3583        flushed to disk.  If <varname>wal_writer_flush_after</varname> is set
3584        to <literal>0</literal> then WAL data is always flushed immediately.
3585        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as WAL blocks,
3586        that is <symbol>XLOG_BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
3587        The default is <literal>1MB</literal>.
3588        This parameter can only be set in the
3589        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command line.
3590       </para>
3591      </listitem>
3592     </varlistentry>
3593
3594     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-skip-threshold" xreflabel="wal_skip_threshold">
3595      <term><varname>wal_skip_threshold</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
3596      <indexterm>
3597       <primary><varname>wal_skip_threshold</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3598      </indexterm>
3599      </term>
3600      <listitem>
3601       <para>
3602        When <varname>wal_level</varname> is <literal>minimal</literal> and a
3603        transaction commits after creating or rewriting a permanent relation,
3604        this setting determines how to persist the new data.  If the data is
3605        smaller than this setting, write it to the WAL log; otherwise, use an
3606        fsync of affected files.  Depending on the properties of your storage,
3607        raising or lowering this value might help if such commits are slowing
3608        concurrent transactions.  If this value is specified without units, it
3609        is taken as kilobytes.  The default is two megabytes
3610        (<literal>2MB</literal>).
3611       </para>
3612      </listitem>
3613     </varlistentry>
3614
3615     <varlistentry id="guc-commit-delay" xreflabel="commit_delay">
3616      <term><varname>commit_delay</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
3617      <indexterm>
3618       <primary><varname>commit_delay</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3619      </indexterm>
3620      </term>
3621      <listitem>
3622       <para>
3623        Setting <varname>commit_delay</varname> adds a time delay
3624        before a WAL flush is initiated.  This can improve
3625        group commit throughput by allowing a larger number of transactions
3626        to commit via a single WAL flush, if system load is high enough
3627        that additional transactions become ready to commit within the
3628        given interval.  However, it also increases latency by up to the
3629        <varname>commit_delay</varname> for each WAL
3630        flush.  Because the delay is just wasted if no other transactions
3631        become ready to commit, a delay is only performed if at least
3632        <varname>commit_siblings</varname> other transactions are active
3633        when a flush is about to be initiated.  Also, no delays are
3634        performed if <varname>fsync</varname> is disabled.
3635        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as microseconds.
3636        The default <varname>commit_delay</varname> is zero (no delay).
3637        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
3638        privilege can change this setting.
3639       </para>
3640       <para>
3641        In <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> releases prior to 9.3,
3642        <varname>commit_delay</varname> behaved differently and was much
3643        less effective: it affected only commits, rather than all WAL flushes,
3644        and waited for the entire configured delay even if the WAL flush
3645        was completed sooner.  Beginning in <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> 9.3,
3646        the first process that becomes ready to flush waits for the configured
3647        interval, while subsequent processes wait only until the leader
3648        completes the flush operation.
3649       </para>
3650      </listitem>
3651     </varlistentry>
3652
3653     <varlistentry id="guc-commit-siblings" xreflabel="commit_siblings">
3654      <term><varname>commit_siblings</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
3655      <indexterm>
3656       <primary><varname>commit_siblings</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3657      </indexterm>
3658      </term>
3659      <listitem>
3660       <para>
3661        Minimum number of concurrent open transactions to require
3662        before performing the <varname>commit_delay</varname> delay. A larger
3663        value makes it more probable that at least one other
3664        transaction will become ready to commit during the delay
3665        interval. The default is five transactions.
3666       </para>
3667      </listitem>
3668     </varlistentry>
3669
3670     </variablelist>
3671     </sect2>
3672     <sect2 id="runtime-config-wal-checkpoints">
3673     <title>Checkpoints</title>
3674
3675    <variablelist>
3676     <varlistentry id="guc-checkpoint-timeout" xreflabel="checkpoint_timeout">
3677      <term><varname>checkpoint_timeout</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
3678      <indexterm>
3679       <primary><varname>checkpoint_timeout</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3680      </indexterm>
3681      </term>
3682      <listitem>
3683       <para>
3684        Maximum time between automatic WAL checkpoints.
3685        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as seconds.
3686        The valid range is between 30 seconds and one day.
3687        The default is five minutes (<literal>5min</literal>).
3688        Increasing this parameter can increase the amount of time needed
3689        for crash recovery.
3690        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
3691        file or on the server command line.
3692       </para>
3693      </listitem>
3694     </varlistentry>
3695
3696     <varlistentry id="guc-checkpoint-completion-target" xreflabel="checkpoint_completion_target">
3697      <term><varname>checkpoint_completion_target</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
3698      <indexterm>
3699       <primary><varname>checkpoint_completion_target</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3700      </indexterm>
3701      </term>
3702      <listitem>
3703       <para>
3704        Specifies the target of checkpoint completion, as a fraction of
3705        total time between checkpoints. The default is 0.9, which spreads the
3706        checkpoint across almost all of the available interval, providing fairly
3707        consistent I/O load while also leaving some time for checkpoint
3708        completion overhead.  Reducing this parameter is not recommended because
3709        it causes the checkpoint to complete faster.  This results in a higher
3710        rate of I/O during the checkpoint followed by a period of less I/O between
3711        the checkpoint completion and the next scheduled checkpoint.  This
3712        parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file
3713        or on the server command line.
3714       </para>
3715      </listitem>
3716     </varlistentry>
3717
3718     <varlistentry id="guc-checkpoint-flush-after" xreflabel="checkpoint_flush_after">
3719      <term><varname>checkpoint_flush_after</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
3720      <indexterm>
3721       <primary><varname>checkpoint_flush_after</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3722      </indexterm>
3723      </term>
3724      <listitem>
3725       <para>
3726        Whenever more than this amount of data has been
3727        written while performing a checkpoint, attempt to force the
3728        OS to issue these writes to the underlying storage.  Doing so will
3729        limit the amount of dirty data in the kernel's page cache, reducing
3730        the likelihood of stalls when an <function>fsync</function> is issued at the end of the
3731        checkpoint, or when the OS writes data back in larger batches in the
3732        background.  Often that will result in greatly reduced transaction
3733        latency, but there also are some cases, especially with workloads
3734        that are bigger than <xref linkend="guc-shared-buffers"/>, but smaller
3735        than the OS's page cache, where performance might degrade.  This
3736        setting may have no effect on some platforms.
3737        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
3738        that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
3739        The valid range is
3740        between <literal>0</literal>, which disables forced writeback,
3741        and <literal>2MB</literal>.  The default is <literal>256kB</literal> on
3742        Linux, <literal>0</literal> elsewhere.  (If <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> is not
3743        8kB, the default and maximum values scale proportionally to it.)
3744        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
3745        file or on the server command line.
3746       </para>
3747      </listitem>
3748     </varlistentry>
3749
3750     <varlistentry id="guc-checkpoint-warning" xreflabel="checkpoint_warning">
3751      <term><varname>checkpoint_warning</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
3752      <indexterm>
3753       <primary><varname>checkpoint_warning</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3754      </indexterm>
3755      </term>
3756      <listitem>
3757       <para>
3758        Write a message to the server log if checkpoints caused by
3759        the filling of WAL segment files happen closer together
3760        than this amount of time (which suggests that
3761        <varname>max_wal_size</varname> ought to be raised).
3762        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as seconds.
3763        The default is 30 seconds (<literal>30s</literal>).
3764        Zero disables the warning.
3765        No warnings will be generated if <varname>checkpoint_timeout</varname>
3766        is less than <varname>checkpoint_warning</varname>.
3767        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
3768        file or on the server command line.
3769       </para>
3770      </listitem>
3771     </varlistentry>
3772
3773     <varlistentry id="guc-max-wal-size" xreflabel="max_wal_size">
3774      <term><varname>max_wal_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
3775      <indexterm>
3776       <primary><varname>max_wal_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3777      </indexterm>
3778      </term>
3779      <listitem>
3780       <para>
3781        Maximum size to let the WAL grow during automatic
3782        checkpoints. This is a soft limit; WAL size can exceed
3783        <varname>max_wal_size</varname> under special circumstances, such as
3784        heavy load, a failing <varname>archive_command</varname> or <varname>archive_library</varname>, or a high
3785        <varname>wal_keep_size</varname> setting.
3786        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as megabytes.
3787        The default is 1 GB.
3788        Increasing this parameter can increase the amount of time needed for
3789        crash recovery.
3790        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
3791        file or on the server command line.
3792       </para>
3793      </listitem>
3794     </varlistentry>
3795
3796     <varlistentry id="guc-min-wal-size" xreflabel="min_wal_size">
3797      <term><varname>min_wal_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
3798      <indexterm>
3799       <primary><varname>min_wal_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3800      </indexterm>
3801      </term>
3802      <listitem>
3803       <para>
3804        As long as WAL disk usage stays below this setting, old WAL files are
3805        always recycled for future use at a checkpoint, rather than removed.
3806        This can be used to ensure that enough WAL space is reserved to
3807        handle spikes in WAL usage, for example when running large batch
3808        jobs.
3809        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as megabytes.
3810        The default is 80 MB.
3811        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
3812        file or on the server command line.
3813       </para>
3814      </listitem>
3815     </varlistentry>
3816
3817     </variablelist>
3818     </sect2>
3819     <sect2 id="runtime-config-wal-archiving">
3820     <title>Archiving</title>
3821
3822    <variablelist>
3823     <varlistentry id="guc-archive-mode" xreflabel="archive_mode">
3824      <term><varname>archive_mode</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
3825      <indexterm>
3826       <primary><varname>archive_mode</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3827      </indexterm>
3828      </term>
3829      <listitem>
3830       <para>
3831        When <varname>archive_mode</varname> is enabled, completed WAL segments
3832        are sent to archive storage by setting
3833        <xref linkend="guc-archive-command"/> or
3834        <xref linkend="guc-archive-library"/>. In addition to <literal>off</literal>,
3835        to disable, there are two modes: <literal>on</literal>, and
3836        <literal>always</literal>. During normal operation, there is no
3837        difference between the two modes, but when set to <literal>always</literal>
3838        the WAL archiver is enabled also during archive recovery or standby
3839        mode. In <literal>always</literal> mode, all files restored from the archive
3840        or streamed with streaming replication will be archived (again). See
3841        <xref linkend="continuous-archiving-in-standby"/> for details.
3842       </para>
3843       <para>
3844        <varname>archive_mode</varname> is a separate setting from
3845        <varname>archive_command</varname> and
3846        <varname>archive_library</varname> so that
3847        <varname>archive_command</varname> and
3848        <varname>archive_library</varname> can be changed without leaving
3849        archiving mode.
3850        This parameter can only be set at server start.
3851        <varname>archive_mode</varname> cannot be enabled when
3852        <varname>wal_level</varname> is set to <literal>minimal</literal>.
3853       </para>
3854      </listitem>
3855     </varlistentry>
3856
3857     <varlistentry id="guc-archive-command" xreflabel="archive_command">
3858      <term><varname>archive_command</varname> (<type>string</type>)
3859      <indexterm>
3860       <primary><varname>archive_command</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3861      </indexterm>
3862      </term>
3863      <listitem>
3864       <para>
3865        The local shell command to execute to archive a completed WAL file
3866        segment.  Any <literal>%p</literal> in the string is
3867        replaced by the path name of the file to archive, and any
3868        <literal>%f</literal> is replaced by only the file name.
3869        (The path name is relative to the working directory of the server,
3870        i.e., the cluster's data directory.)
3871        Use <literal>%%</literal> to embed an actual <literal>%</literal> character in the
3872        command.  It is important for the command to return a zero
3873        exit status only if it succeeds. For more information see
3874        <xref linkend="backup-archiving-wal"/>.
3875       </para>
3876       <para>
3877        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
3878        file or on the server command line.  It is only used if
3879        <varname>archive_mode</varname> was enabled at server start and
3880        <varname>archive_library</varname> is set to an empty string.  If both
3881        <varname>archive_command</varname> and <varname>archive_library</varname>
3882        are set, an error will be raised.
3883        If <varname>archive_command</varname> is an empty string (the default) while
3884        <varname>archive_mode</varname> is enabled (and <varname>archive_library</varname>
3885        is set to an empty string), WAL archiving is temporarily
3886        disabled, but the server continues to accumulate WAL segment files in
3887        the expectation that a command will soon be provided.  Setting
3888        <varname>archive_command</varname> to a command that does nothing but
3889        return true, e.g., <literal>/bin/true</literal> (<literal>REM</literal> on
3890        Windows), effectively disables
3891        archiving, but also breaks the chain of WAL files needed for
3892        archive recovery, so it should only be used in unusual circumstances.
3893       </para>
3894      </listitem>
3895     </varlistentry>
3896
3897     <varlistentry id="guc-archive-library" xreflabel="archive_library">
3898      <term><varname>archive_library</varname> (<type>string</type>)
3899      <indexterm>
3900       <primary><varname>archive_library</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3901      </indexterm>
3902      </term>
3903      <listitem>
3904       <para>
3905        The library to use for archiving completed WAL file segments.  If set to
3906        an empty string (the default), archiving via shell is enabled, and
3907        <xref linkend="guc-archive-command"/> is used.  If both
3908        <varname>archive_command</varname> and <varname>archive_library</varname>
3909        are set, an error will be raised.  Otherwise, the specified
3910        shared library is used for archiving. The WAL archiver process is
3911        restarted by the postmaster when this parameter changes. For more
3912        information, see <xref linkend="backup-archiving-wal"/> and
3913        <xref linkend="archive-modules"/>.
3914       </para>
3915       <para>
3916        This parameter can only be set in the
3917        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command line.
3918       </para>
3919      </listitem>
3920     </varlistentry>
3921
3922     <varlistentry id="guc-archive-timeout" xreflabel="archive_timeout">
3923      <term><varname>archive_timeout</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
3924      <indexterm>
3925       <primary><varname>archive_timeout</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3926      </indexterm>
3927      </term>
3928      <listitem>
3929       <para>
3930        The <xref linkend="guc-archive-command"/> or <xref linkend="guc-archive-library"/> is only invoked for
3931        completed WAL segments. Hence, if your server generates little WAL
3932        traffic (or has slack periods where it does so), there could be a
3933        long delay between the completion of a transaction and its safe
3934        recording in archive storage.  To limit how old unarchived
3935        data can be, you can set <varname>archive_timeout</varname> to force the
3936        server to switch to a new WAL segment file periodically.  When this
3937        parameter is greater than zero, the server will switch to a new
3938        segment file whenever this amount of time has elapsed since the last
3939        segment file switch, and there has been any database activity,
3940        including a single checkpoint (checkpoints are skipped if there is
3941        no database activity).  Note that archived files that are closed
3942        early due to a forced switch are still the same length as completely
3943        full files.  Therefore, it is unwise to use a very short
3944        <varname>archive_timeout</varname> &mdash; it will bloat your archive
3945        storage.  <varname>archive_timeout</varname> settings of a minute or so are
3946        usually reasonable.  You should consider using streaming replication,
3947        instead of archiving, if you want data to be copied off the primary
3948        server more quickly than that.
3949        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as seconds.
3950        This parameter can only be set in the
3951        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command line.
3952       </para>
3953      </listitem>
3954     </varlistentry>
3955
3956     </variablelist>
3957    </sect2>
3958
3959   <sect2 id="runtime-config-wal-recovery">
3960
3961    <title>Recovery</title>
3962
3963     <indexterm>
3964      <primary>configuration</primary>
3965      <secondary>of recovery</secondary>
3966      <tertiary>general settings</tertiary>
3967     </indexterm>
3968
3969    <para>
3970     This section describes the settings that apply to recovery in general,
3971     affecting crash recovery, streaming replication and archive-based
3972     replication.
3973    </para>
3974
3975
3976    <variablelist>
3977     <varlistentry id="guc-recovery-prefetch" xreflabel="recovery_prefetch">
3978      <term><varname>recovery_prefetch</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
3979      <indexterm>
3980       <primary><varname>recovery_prefetch</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
3981      </indexterm>
3982      </term>
3983      <listitem>
3984       <para>
3985        Whether to try to prefetch blocks that are referenced in the WAL that
3986        are not yet in the buffer pool, during recovery.  Valid values are
3987        <literal>off</literal>, <literal>on</literal> and
3988        <literal>try</literal> (the default).  The setting
3989        <literal>try</literal> enables
3990        prefetching only if the operating system provides support for issuing
3991        read-ahead advice.
3992       </para>
3993       <para>
3994        Prefetching blocks that will soon be needed can reduce I/O wait times
3995        during recovery with some workloads.
3996        See also the <xref linkend="guc-wal-decode-buffer-size"/> and
3997        <xref linkend="guc-maintenance-io-concurrency"/> settings, which limit
3998        prefetching activity.
3999       </para>
4000      </listitem>
4001     </varlistentry>
4002
4003     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-decode-buffer-size" xreflabel="wal_decode_buffer_size">
4004      <term><varname>wal_decode_buffer_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
4005      <indexterm>
4006       <primary><varname>wal_decode_buffer_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4007      </indexterm>
4008      </term>
4009      <listitem>
4010       <para>
4011        A limit on how far ahead the server can look in the WAL, to find
4012        blocks to prefetch.  If this value is specified without units, it is
4013        taken as bytes.
4014        The default is 512kB.
4015        This parameter can only be set at server start.
4016       </para>
4017      </listitem>
4018     </varlistentry>
4019
4020    </variablelist>
4021   </sect2>
4022
4023  <sect2 id="runtime-config-wal-archive-recovery">
4024
4025    <title>Archive Recovery</title>
4026
4027    <indexterm>
4028     <primary>configuration</primary>
4029     <secondary>of recovery</secondary>
4030     <tertiary>of a standby server</tertiary>
4031    </indexterm>
4032
4033    <para>
4034     This section describes the settings that apply only for the duration of
4035     the recovery.  They must be reset for any subsequent recovery you wish to
4036     perform.
4037    </para>
4038
4039    <para>
4040     <quote>Recovery</quote> covers using the server as a standby or for
4041     executing a targeted recovery.  Typically, standby mode would be used to
4042     provide high availability and/or read scalability, whereas a targeted
4043     recovery is used to recover from data loss.
4044    </para>
4045
4046    <para>
4047     To start the server in standby mode, create a file called
4048     <filename>standby.signal</filename><indexterm><primary>standby.signal</primary></indexterm>
4049     in the data directory.  The server will enter recovery and will not stop
4050     recovery when the end of archived WAL is reached, but will keep trying to
4051     continue recovery by connecting to the sending server as specified by the
4052     <varname>primary_conninfo</varname> setting and/or by fetching new WAL
4053     segments using <varname>restore_command</varname>.  For this mode, the
4054     parameters from this section and <xref
4055     linkend="runtime-config-replication-standby"/> are of interest.
4056     Parameters from <xref linkend="runtime-config-wal-recovery-target"/> will
4057     also be applied but are typically not useful in this mode.
4058    </para>
4059
4060    <para>
4061     To start the server in targeted recovery mode, create a file called
4062     <filename>recovery.signal</filename><indexterm><primary>recovery.signal</primary></indexterm>
4063     in the data directory.  If both <filename>standby.signal</filename> and
4064     <filename>recovery.signal</filename> files are created, standby mode
4065     takes precedence.  Targeted recovery mode ends when the archived WAL is
4066     fully replayed, or when <varname>recovery_target</varname> is reached.
4067     In this mode, the parameters from both this section and <xref
4068     linkend="runtime-config-wal-recovery-target"/> will be used.
4069    </para>
4070
4071    <variablelist>
4072     <varlistentry id="guc-restore-command" xreflabel="restore_command">
4073      <term><varname>restore_command</varname> (<type>string</type>)
4074      <indexterm>
4075        <primary><varname>restore_command</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4076      </indexterm>
4077      </term>
4078      <listitem>
4079       <para>
4080        The local shell command to execute to retrieve an archived segment of
4081        the WAL file series. This parameter is required for archive recovery,
4082        but optional for streaming replication.
4083        Any <literal>%f</literal> in the string is
4084        replaced by the name of the file to retrieve from the archive,
4085        and any <literal>%p</literal> is replaced by the copy destination path name
4086        on the server.
4087        (The path name is relative to the current working directory,
4088        i.e., the cluster's data directory.)
4089        Any <literal>%r</literal> is replaced by the name of the file containing the
4090        last valid restart point. That is the earliest file that must be kept
4091        to allow a restore to be restartable, so this information can be used
4092        to truncate the archive to just the minimum required to support
4093        restarting from the current restore. <literal>%r</literal> is typically only
4094        used by warm-standby configurations
4095        (see <xref linkend="warm-standby"/>).
4096        Write <literal>%%</literal> to embed an actual <literal>%</literal> character.
4097       </para>
4098
4099       <para>
4100        It is important for the command to return a zero exit status
4101        only if it succeeds.  The command <emphasis>will</emphasis> be asked for file
4102        names that are not present in the archive; it must return nonzero
4103        when so asked.  Examples:
4104<programlisting>
4105restore_command = 'cp /mnt/server/archivedir/%f "%p"'
4106restore_command = 'copy "C:\\server\\archivedir\\%f" "%p"'  # Windows
4107</programlisting>
4108        An exception is that if the command was terminated by a signal (other
4109        than <systemitem>SIGTERM</systemitem>, which is used as part of a
4110        database server shutdown) or an error by the shell (such as command
4111        not found), then recovery will abort and the server will not start up.
4112       </para>
4113
4114       <para>
4115        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
4116        file or on the server command line.
4117       </para>
4118      </listitem>
4119     </varlistentry>
4120
4121     <varlistentry id="guc-archive-cleanup-command" xreflabel="archive_cleanup_command">
4122      <term><varname>archive_cleanup_command</varname> (<type>string</type>)
4123      <indexterm>
4124        <primary><varname>archive_cleanup_command</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4125      </indexterm>
4126      </term>
4127      <listitem>
4128       <para>
4129        This optional parameter specifies a shell command that will be executed
4130        at every restartpoint.  The purpose of
4131        <varname>archive_cleanup_command</varname> is to provide a mechanism for
4132        cleaning up old archived WAL files that are no longer needed by the
4133        standby server.
4134        Any <literal>%r</literal> is replaced by the name of the file containing the
4135        last valid restart point.
4136        That is the earliest file that must be <emphasis>kept</emphasis> to allow a
4137        restore to be restartable, and so all files earlier than <literal>%r</literal>
4138        may be safely removed.
4139        This information can be used to truncate the archive to just the
4140        minimum required to support restart from the current restore.
4141        The <xref linkend="pgarchivecleanup"/> module
4142        is often used in <varname>archive_cleanup_command</varname> for
4143        single-standby configurations, for example:
4144<programlisting>archive_cleanup_command = 'pg_archivecleanup /mnt/server/archivedir %r'</programlisting>
4145        Note however that if multiple standby servers are restoring from the
4146        same archive directory, you will need to ensure that you do not delete
4147        WAL files until they are no longer needed by any of the servers.
4148        <varname>archive_cleanup_command</varname> would typically be used in a
4149        warm-standby configuration (see <xref linkend="warm-standby"/>).
4150        Write <literal>%%</literal> to embed an actual <literal>%</literal> character in the
4151        command.
4152       </para>
4153       <para>
4154        If the command returns a nonzero exit status then a warning log
4155        message will be written.  An exception is that if the command was
4156        terminated by a signal or an error by the shell (such as command not
4157        found), a fatal error will be raised.
4158       </para>
4159       <para>
4160        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
4161        file or on the server command line.
4162       </para>
4163      </listitem>
4164     </varlistentry>
4165
4166     <varlistentry id="guc-recovery-end-command" xreflabel="recovery_end_command">
4167      <term><varname>recovery_end_command</varname> (<type>string</type>)
4168      <indexterm>
4169        <primary><varname>recovery_end_command</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4170      </indexterm>
4171      </term>
4172      <listitem>
4173       <para>
4174        This parameter specifies a shell command that will be executed once only
4175        at the end of recovery. This parameter is optional. The purpose of the
4176        <varname>recovery_end_command</varname> is to provide a mechanism for cleanup
4177        following replication or recovery.
4178        Any <literal>%r</literal> is replaced by the name of the file containing the
4179        last valid restart point, like in <xref linkend="guc-archive-cleanup-command"/>.
4180       </para>
4181       <para>
4182        If the command returns a nonzero exit status then a warning log
4183        message will be written and the database will proceed to start up
4184        anyway.  An exception is that if the command was terminated by a
4185        signal or an error by the shell (such as command not found), the
4186        database will not proceed with startup.
4187       </para>
4188       <para>
4189        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
4190        file or on the server command line.
4191       </para>
4192      </listitem>
4193     </varlistentry>
4194
4195    </variablelist>
4196
4197  </sect2>
4198
4199  <sect2 id="runtime-config-wal-recovery-target">
4200
4201    <title>Recovery Target</title>
4202
4203     <para>
4204      By default, recovery will recover to the end of the WAL log. The
4205      following parameters can be used to specify an earlier stopping point.
4206      At most one of <varname>recovery_target</varname>,
4207      <varname>recovery_target_lsn</varname>, <varname>recovery_target_name</varname>,
4208      <varname>recovery_target_time</varname>, or <varname>recovery_target_xid</varname>
4209      can be used; if more than one of these is specified in the configuration
4210      file, an error will be raised.
4211      These parameters can only be set at server start.
4212     </para>
4213
4214     <variablelist>
4215     <varlistentry id="guc-recovery-target" xreflabel="recovery_target">
4216      <term><varname>recovery_target</varname><literal> = 'immediate'</literal>
4217      <indexterm>
4218        <primary><varname>recovery_target</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4219      </indexterm>
4220      </term>
4221      <listitem>
4222       <para>
4223        This parameter specifies that recovery should end as soon as a
4224        consistent state is reached, i.e., as early as possible. When restoring
4225        from an online backup, this means the point where taking the backup
4226        ended.
4227       </para>
4228       <para>
4229        Technically, this is a string parameter, but <literal>'immediate'</literal>
4230        is currently the only allowed value.
4231       </para>
4232      </listitem>
4233     </varlistentry>
4234
4235     <varlistentry id="guc-recovery-target-name" xreflabel="recovery_target_name">
4236      <term><varname>recovery_target_name</varname> (<type>string</type>)
4237      <indexterm>
4238        <primary><varname>recovery_target_name</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4239      </indexterm>
4240      </term>
4241      <listitem>
4242       <para>
4243        This parameter specifies the named restore point (created with
4244        <function>pg_create_restore_point()</function>) to which recovery will proceed.
4245       </para>
4246      </listitem>
4247     </varlistentry>
4248
4249     <varlistentry id="guc-recovery-target-time" xreflabel="recovery_target_time">
4250      <term><varname>recovery_target_time</varname> (<type>timestamp</type>)
4251      <indexterm>
4252        <primary><varname>recovery_target_time</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4253      </indexterm>
4254      </term>
4255      <listitem>
4256       <para>
4257        This parameter specifies the time stamp up to which recovery
4258        will proceed.
4259        The precise stopping point is also influenced by
4260        <xref linkend="guc-recovery-target-inclusive"/>.
4261       </para>
4262
4263       <para>
4264        The value of this parameter is a time stamp in the same format
4265        accepted by the <type>timestamp with time zone</type> data type,
4266        except that you cannot use a time zone abbreviation (unless the
4267        <xref linkend="guc-timezone-abbreviations"/> variable has been set
4268        earlier in the configuration file).  Preferred style is to use a
4269        numeric offset from UTC, or you can write a full time zone name,
4270        e.g., <literal>Europe/Helsinki</literal> not <literal>EEST</literal>.
4271       </para>
4272      </listitem>
4273     </varlistentry>
4274
4275     <varlistentry id="guc-recovery-target-xid" xreflabel="recovery_target_xid">
4276      <term><varname>recovery_target_xid</varname> (<type>string</type>)
4277      <indexterm>
4278        <primary><varname>recovery_target_xid</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4279      </indexterm>
4280      </term>
4281      <listitem>
4282       <para>
4283        This parameter specifies the transaction ID up to which recovery
4284        will proceed. Keep in mind
4285        that while transaction IDs are assigned sequentially at transaction
4286        start, transactions can complete in a different numeric order.
4287        The transactions that will be recovered are those that committed
4288        before (and optionally including) the specified one.
4289        The precise stopping point is also influenced by
4290        <xref linkend="guc-recovery-target-inclusive"/>.
4291       </para>
4292      </listitem>
4293     </varlistentry>
4294
4295     <varlistentry id="guc-recovery-target-lsn" xreflabel="recovery_target_lsn">
4296      <term><varname>recovery_target_lsn</varname> (<type>pg_lsn</type>)
4297      <indexterm>
4298        <primary><varname>recovery_target_lsn</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4299      </indexterm>
4300      </term>
4301      <listitem>
4302       <para>
4303        This parameter specifies the LSN of the write-ahead log location up
4304        to which recovery will proceed. The precise stopping point is also
4305        influenced by <xref linkend="guc-recovery-target-inclusive"/>. This
4306        parameter is parsed using the system data type
4307        <link linkend="datatype-pg-lsn"><type>pg_lsn</type></link>.
4308       </para>
4309      </listitem>
4310     </varlistentry>
4311     </variablelist>
4312
4313     <para>
4314       The following options further specify the recovery target, and affect
4315       what happens when the target is reached:
4316     </para>
4317
4318     <variablelist>
4319     <varlistentry id="guc-recovery-target-inclusive"
4320                   xreflabel="recovery_target_inclusive">
4321      <term><varname>recovery_target_inclusive</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
4322      <indexterm>
4323        <primary><varname>recovery_target_inclusive</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4324      </indexterm>
4325      </term>
4326      <listitem>
4327       <para>
4328        Specifies whether to stop just after the specified recovery target
4329        (<literal>on</literal>), or just before the recovery target
4330        (<literal>off</literal>).
4331        Applies when <xref linkend="guc-recovery-target-lsn"/>,
4332        <xref linkend="guc-recovery-target-time"/>, or
4333        <xref linkend="guc-recovery-target-xid"/> is specified.
4334        This setting controls whether transactions
4335        having exactly the target WAL location (LSN), commit time, or transaction ID, respectively, will
4336        be included in the recovery.  Default is <literal>on</literal>.
4337       </para>
4338      </listitem>
4339     </varlistentry>
4340
4341     <varlistentry id="guc-recovery-target-timeline"
4342                   xreflabel="recovery_target_timeline">
4343      <term><varname>recovery_target_timeline</varname> (<type>string</type>)
4344      <indexterm>
4345        <primary><varname>recovery_target_timeline</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4346      </indexterm>
4347      </term>
4348      <listitem>
4349       <para>
4350        Specifies recovering into a particular timeline.  The value can be a
4351        numeric timeline ID or a special value.  The value
4352        <literal>current</literal> recovers along the same timeline that was
4353        current when the base backup was taken.  The
4354        value <literal>latest</literal> recovers
4355        to the latest timeline found in the archive, which is useful in
4356        a standby server.  <literal>latest</literal> is the default.
4357       </para>
4358
4359       <para>
4360        To specify a timeline ID in hexadecimal (for example, if extracted
4361        from a WAL file name or history file), prefix it with a
4362        <literal>0x</literal>.  For instance, if the WAL file name is
4363        <filename>00000011000000A10000004F</filename>, then the timeline ID is
4364        <literal>0x11</literal> (or 17 decimal).
4365       </para>
4366
4367       <para>
4368        You usually only need to set this parameter
4369        in complex re-recovery situations, where you need to return to
4370        a state that itself was reached after a point-in-time recovery.
4371        See <xref linkend="backup-timelines"/> for discussion.
4372       </para>
4373      </listitem>
4374     </varlistentry>
4375
4376     <varlistentry id="guc-recovery-target-action"
4377                   xreflabel="recovery_target_action">
4378      <term><varname>recovery_target_action</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
4379      <indexterm>
4380        <primary><varname>recovery_target_action</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4381      </indexterm>
4382      </term>
4383      <listitem>
4384       <para>
4385        Specifies what action the server should take once the recovery target is
4386        reached. The default is <literal>pause</literal>, which means recovery will
4387        be paused. <literal>promote</literal> means the recovery process will finish
4388        and the server will start to accept connections.
4389        Finally <literal>shutdown</literal> will stop the server after reaching the
4390        recovery target.
4391       </para>
4392       <para>
4393        The intended use of the <literal>pause</literal> setting is to allow queries
4394        to be executed against the database to check if this recovery target
4395        is the most desirable point for recovery.
4396        The paused state can be resumed by
4397        using <function>pg_wal_replay_resume()</function> (see
4398        <xref linkend="functions-recovery-control-table"/>), which then
4399        causes recovery to end. If this recovery target is not the
4400        desired stopping point, then shut down the server, change the
4401        recovery target settings to a later target and restart to
4402        continue recovery.
4403       </para>
4404       <para>
4405        The <literal>shutdown</literal> setting is useful to have the instance ready
4406        at the exact replay point desired.  The instance will still be able to
4407        replay more WAL records (and in fact will have to replay WAL records
4408        since the last checkpoint next time it is started).
4409       </para>
4410       <para>
4411        Note that because <filename>recovery.signal</filename> will not be
4412        removed when <varname>recovery_target_action</varname> is set to <literal>shutdown</literal>,
4413        any subsequent start will end with immediate shutdown unless the
4414        configuration is changed or the <filename>recovery.signal</filename>
4415        file is removed manually.
4416       </para>
4417       <para>
4418        This setting has no effect if no recovery target is set.
4419        If <xref linkend="guc-hot-standby"/> is not enabled, a setting of
4420        <literal>pause</literal> will act the same as <literal>shutdown</literal>.
4421        If the recovery target is reached while a promotion is ongoing,
4422        a setting of <literal>pause</literal> will act the same as
4423        <literal>promote</literal>.
4424       </para>
4425       <para>
4426        In any case, if a recovery target is configured but the archive
4427        recovery ends before the target is reached, the server will shut down
4428        with a fatal error.
4429       </para>
4430      </listitem>
4431     </varlistentry>
4432
4433    </variablelist>
4434   </sect2>
4435
4436   <sect2 id="runtime-config-wal-summarization">
4437    <title>WAL Summarization</title>
4438
4439    <para>
4440     These settings control WAL summarization, a feature which must be
4441     enabled in order to perform an
4442     <link linkend="backup-incremental-backup">incremental backup</link>.
4443    </para>
4444
4445    <variablelist>
4446     <varlistentry id="guc-summarize-wal" xreflabel="summarize_wal">
4447      <term><varname>summarize_wal</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
4448      <indexterm>
4449       <primary><varname>summarize_wal</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4450      </indexterm>
4451      </term>
4452     <listitem>
4453      <para>
4454       Enables the WAL summarizer process. Note that WAL summarization can
4455       be enabled either on a primary or on a standby.  This parameter can only
4456       be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server
4457       command line.  The default is <literal>off</literal>.
4458      </para>
4459      <para>
4460       The server cannot be started with <literal>summarize_wal=on</literal>
4461       if <literal>wal_level</literal> is set to <literal>minimal</literal>. If
4462       <literal>summarize_wal=on</literal> is configured after server startup
4463       while <literal>wal_level=minimal</literal>, the summarizer will run
4464       but refuse to generate summary files for any WAL generated with
4465       <literal>wal_level=minimal</literal>.
4466      </para>
4467      </listitem>
4468     </varlistentry>
4469
4470     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-summary-keep-time" xreflabel="wal_summary_keep_time">
4471      <term><varname>wal_summary_keep_time</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
4472      <indexterm>
4473       <primary><varname>wal_summary_keep_time</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4474      </indexterm>
4475      </term>
4476     <listitem>
4477      <para>
4478       Configures the amount of time after which the WAL summarizer
4479       automatically removes old WAL summaries. The file timestamp is used to
4480       determine which files are old enough to remove. Typically, you should set
4481       this comfortably higher than the time that could pass between a backup
4482       and a later incremental backup that depends on it.  WAL summaries must
4483       be available for the entire range of WAL records between the preceding
4484       backup and the new one being taken; if not, the incremental backup will
4485       fail. If this parameter is set to zero, WAL summaries will not be
4486       automatically deleted, but it is safe to manually remove files that you
4487       know will not be required for future incremental backups.
4488       This parameter can only be set in the
4489       <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command line.
4490       If this value is specified without units, it is taken as minutes.
4491       The default is 10 days. If <literal>summarize_wal = off</literal>,
4492       existing WAL summaries will not be removed regardless of the value of
4493       this parameter, because the WAL summarizer will not run.
4494      </para>
4495      </listitem>
4496     </varlistentry>
4497
4498    </variablelist>
4499
4500   </sect2>
4501
4502   </sect1>
4503
4504   <sect1 id="runtime-config-replication">
4505    <title>Replication</title>
4506
4507    <para>
4508     These settings control the behavior of the built-in
4509     <firstterm>streaming replication</firstterm> feature (see
4510     <xref linkend="streaming-replication"/>), and the built-in
4511     <firstterm>logical replication</firstterm> feature (see
4512     <xref linkend="logical-replication"/>).
4513    </para>
4514
4515    <para>
4516     For <emphasis>streaming replication</emphasis>, servers will be either a
4517     primary or a standby server.  Primaries can send data, while standbys
4518     are always receivers of replicated data.  When cascading replication
4519     (see <xref linkend="cascading-replication"/>) is used, standby servers
4520     can also be senders, as well as receivers.
4521     Parameters are mainly for sending and standby servers, though some
4522     parameters have meaning only on the primary server.  Settings may vary
4523     across the cluster without problems if that is required.
4524    </para>
4525
4526    <para>
4527     For <emphasis>logical replication</emphasis>, <firstterm>publishers</firstterm>
4528     (servers that do <link linkend="sql-createpublication"><command>CREATE PUBLICATION</command></link>)
4529     replicate data to <firstterm>subscribers</firstterm>
4530     (servers that do <link linkend="sql-createsubscription"><command>CREATE SUBSCRIPTION</command></link>).
4531     Servers can also be publishers and subscribers at the same time. Note,
4532     the following sections refer to publishers as "senders". For more details
4533     about logical replication configuration settings refer to
4534     <xref linkend="logical-replication-config"/>.
4535    </para>
4536
4537    <sect2 id="runtime-config-replication-sender">
4538     <title>Sending Servers</title>
4539
4540     <para>
4541      These parameters can be set on any server that is
4542      to send replication data to one or more standby servers.
4543      The primary is always a sending server, so these parameters must
4544      always be set on the primary.
4545      The role and meaning of these parameters does not change after a
4546      standby becomes the primary.
4547     </para>
4548
4549     <variablelist>
4550      <varlistentry id="guc-max-wal-senders" xreflabel="max_wal_senders">
4551       <term><varname>max_wal_senders</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
4552       <indexterm>
4553        <primary><varname>max_wal_senders</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4554       </indexterm>
4555       </term>
4556       <listitem>
4557       <para>
4558        Specifies the maximum number of concurrent connections from standby
4559        servers or streaming base backup clients (i.e., the maximum number of
4560        simultaneously running WAL sender processes). The default is
4561        <literal>10</literal>.  The value <literal>0</literal> means
4562        replication is disabled.  Abrupt disconnection of a streaming client might
4563        leave an orphaned connection slot behind until a timeout is reached,
4564        so this parameter should be set slightly higher than the maximum
4565        number of expected clients so disconnected clients can immediately
4566        reconnect.  This parameter can only be set at server start.  Also,
4567        <varname>wal_level</varname> must be set to
4568        <literal>replica</literal> or higher to allow connections from standby
4569        servers.
4570       </para>
4571
4572       <para>
4573         When running a standby server, you must set this parameter to the
4574         same or higher value than on the primary server. Otherwise, queries
4575         will not be allowed in the standby server.
4576        </para>
4577       </listitem>
4578      </varlistentry>
4579
4580      <varlistentry id="guc-max-replication-slots" xreflabel="max_replication_slots">
4581       <term><varname>max_replication_slots</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
4582       <indexterm>
4583        <primary><varname>max_replication_slots</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4584       </indexterm>
4585       </term>
4586       <listitem>
4587        <para>
4588         Specifies the maximum number of replication slots
4589         (see <xref linkend="streaming-replication-slots"/>) that the server
4590         can support. The default is 10.  This parameter can only be set at
4591         server start.
4592         Setting it to a lower value than the number of currently
4593         existing replication slots will prevent the server from starting.
4594         Also, <varname>wal_level</varname> must be set
4595         to <literal>replica</literal> or higher to allow replication slots to
4596         be used.
4597        </para>
4598       </listitem>
4599      </varlistentry>
4600
4601      <varlistentry id="guc-wal-keep-size" xreflabel="wal_keep_size">
4602       <term><varname>wal_keep_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
4603       <indexterm>
4604        <primary><varname>wal_keep_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4605       </indexterm>
4606       </term>
4607       <listitem>
4608       <para>
4609        Specifies the minimum size of past WAL files kept in the
4610        <filename>pg_wal</filename>
4611        directory, in case a standby server needs to fetch them for streaming
4612        replication. If a standby
4613        server connected to the sending server falls behind by more than
4614        <varname>wal_keep_size</varname> megabytes, the sending server might
4615        remove a WAL segment still needed by the standby, in which case the
4616        replication connection will be terminated.  Downstream connections
4617        will also eventually fail as a result.  (However, the standby
4618        server can recover by fetching the segment from archive, if WAL
4619        archiving is in use.)
4620       </para>
4621
4622       <para>
4623        This sets only the minimum size of segments retained in
4624        <filename>pg_wal</filename>; the system might need to retain more segments
4625        for WAL archival or to recover from a checkpoint. If
4626        <varname>wal_keep_size</varname> is zero (the default), the system
4627        doesn't keep any extra segments for standby purposes, so the number
4628        of old WAL segments available to standby servers is a function of
4629        the location of the previous checkpoint and status of WAL
4630        archiving.
4631        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as megabytes.
4632        This parameter can only be set in the
4633        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command line.
4634       </para>
4635       </listitem>
4636      </varlistentry>
4637
4638      <varlistentry id="guc-max-slot-wal-keep-size" xreflabel="max_slot_wal_keep_size">
4639       <term><varname>max_slot_wal_keep_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
4640       <indexterm>
4641        <primary><varname>max_slot_wal_keep_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4642       </indexterm>
4643       </term>
4644       <listitem>
4645       <para>
4646        Specify the maximum size of WAL files
4647        that <link linkend="streaming-replication-slots">replication
4648        slots</link> are allowed to retain in the <filename>pg_wal</filename>
4649        directory at checkpoint time.
4650        If <varname>max_slot_wal_keep_size</varname> is -1 (the default),
4651        replication slots may retain an unlimited amount of WAL files.  Otherwise, if
4652        restart_lsn of a replication slot falls behind the current LSN by more
4653        than the given size, the standby using the slot may no longer be able
4654        to continue replication due to removal of required WAL files. You
4655        can see the WAL availability of replication slots
4656        in <link linkend="view-pg-replication-slots">pg_replication_slots</link>.
4657        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as megabytes.
4658        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
4659        file or on the server command line.
4660       </para>
4661       </listitem>
4662      </varlistentry>
4663
4664     <varlistentry id="guc-idle-replication-slot-timeout" xreflabel="idle_replication_slot_timeout">
4665      <term><varname>idle_replication_slot_timeout</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
4666      <indexterm>
4667       <primary><varname>idle_replication_slot_timeout</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4668      </indexterm>
4669      </term>
4670      <listitem>
4671       <para>
4672        Invalidate replication slots that have remained inactive (not used by
4673        a <link linkend="protocol-replication">replication connection</link>)
4674        for longer than this duration.
4675        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as seconds.
4676        A value of zero (the default) disables the idle timeout
4677        invalidation mechanism.  This parameter can only be set in the
4678        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command
4679        line.
4680       </para>
4681
4682       <para>
4683        Slot invalidation due to idle timeout occurs during checkpoint.
4684        Because checkpoints happen at <varname>checkpoint_timeout</varname>
4685        intervals, there can be some lag between when the
4686        <varname>idle_replication_slot_timeout</varname> was exceeded and when
4687        the slot invalidation is triggered at the next checkpoint.
4688        To avoid such lags, users can force a checkpoint to promptly invalidate
4689        inactive slots. The duration of slot inactivity is calculated using the
4690        slot's <link linkend="view-pg-replication-slots">pg_replication_slots</link>.<structfield>inactive_since</structfield>
4691        value.
4692       </para>
4693
4694       <para>
4695        Note that the idle timeout invalidation mechanism is not applicable
4696        for slots that do not reserve WAL or for slots on the standby server
4697        that are being synced from the primary server (i.e., standby slots
4698        having <link linkend="view-pg-replication-slots">pg_replication_slots</link>.<structfield>synced</structfield>
4699        value <literal>true</literal>). Synced slots are always considered to
4700        be inactive because they don't perform logical decoding to produce
4701        changes.
4702       </para>
4703      </listitem>
4704     </varlistentry>
4705
4706     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-sender-timeout" xreflabel="wal_sender_timeout">
4707      <term><varname>wal_sender_timeout</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
4708      <indexterm>
4709       <primary><varname>wal_sender_timeout</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4710      </indexterm>
4711      </term>
4712      <listitem>
4713       <para>
4714        Terminate replication connections that are inactive for longer
4715        than this amount of time. This is useful for
4716        the sending server to detect a standby crash or network outage.
4717        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
4718        The default value is 60 seconds.
4719        A value of zero disables the timeout mechanism.
4720       </para>
4721       <para>
4722        With a cluster distributed across multiple geographic
4723        locations, using different values per location brings more flexibility
4724        in the cluster management. A smaller value is useful for faster
4725        failure detection with a standby having a low-latency network
4726        connection, and a larger value helps in judging better the health
4727        of a standby if located on a remote location, with a high-latency
4728        network connection.
4729       </para>
4730      </listitem>
4731     </varlistentry>
4732
4733     <varlistentry id="guc-track-commit-timestamp" xreflabel="track_commit_timestamp">
4734      <term><varname>track_commit_timestamp</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
4735      <indexterm>
4736       <primary><varname>track_commit_timestamp</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4737      </indexterm>
4738      </term>
4739      <listitem>
4740       <para>
4741        Record commit time of transactions.
4742        This parameter can only be set at server start.
4743        The default value is <literal>off</literal>.
4744       </para>
4745      </listitem>
4746     </varlistentry>
4747
4748     </variablelist>
4749    </sect2>
4750
4751    <sect2 id="runtime-config-replication-primary">
4752     <title>Primary Server</title>
4753
4754     <para>
4755      These parameters can be set on the primary server that is
4756      to send replication data to one or more standby servers.
4757      Note that in addition to these parameters,
4758      <xref linkend="guc-wal-level"/> must be set appropriately on the primary
4759      server, and optionally WAL archiving can be enabled as
4760      well (see <xref linkend="runtime-config-wal-archiving"/>).
4761      The values of these parameters on standby servers are irrelevant,
4762      although you may wish to set them there in preparation for the
4763      possibility of a standby becoming the primary.
4764     </para>
4765
4766    <variablelist>
4767
4768     <varlistentry id="guc-synchronous-standby-names" xreflabel="synchronous_standby_names">
4769      <term><varname>synchronous_standby_names</varname> (<type>string</type>)
4770      <indexterm>
4771       <primary><varname>synchronous_standby_names</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4772      </indexterm>
4773      </term>
4774      <listitem>
4775       <para>
4776        Specifies a list of standby servers that can support
4777        <firstterm>synchronous replication</firstterm>, as described in
4778        <xref linkend="synchronous-replication"/>.
4779        There will be one or more active synchronous standbys;
4780        transactions waiting for commit will be allowed to proceed after
4781        these standby servers confirm receipt of their data.
4782        The synchronous standbys will be those whose names appear
4783        in this list, and
4784        that are both currently connected and streaming data in real-time
4785        (as shown by a state of <literal>streaming</literal> in the
4786        <link linkend="monitoring-pg-stat-replication-view">
4787        <structname>pg_stat_replication</structname></link> view).
4788        Specifying more than one synchronous standby can allow for very high
4789        availability and protection against data loss.
4790       </para>
4791       <para>
4792        The name of a standby server for this purpose is the
4793        <varname>application_name</varname> setting of the standby, as set in the
4794        standby's connection information.  In case of a physical replication
4795        standby, this should be set in the <varname>primary_conninfo</varname>
4796        setting; the default is the setting of <xref linkend="guc-cluster-name"/>
4797        if set, else <literal>walreceiver</literal>.
4798        For logical replication, this can be set in the connection
4799        information of the subscription, and it defaults to the
4800        subscription name.  For other replication stream consumers,
4801        consult their documentation.
4802       </para>
4803       <para>
4804        This parameter specifies a list of standby servers using
4805        either of the following syntaxes:
4806<synopsis>
4807[FIRST] <replaceable class="parameter">num_sync</replaceable> ( <replaceable class="parameter">standby_name</replaceable> [, ...] )
4808ANY <replaceable class="parameter">num_sync</replaceable> ( <replaceable class="parameter">standby_name</replaceable> [, ...] )
4809<replaceable class="parameter">standby_name</replaceable> [, ...]
4810</synopsis>
4811        where <replaceable class="parameter">num_sync</replaceable> is
4812        the number of synchronous standbys that transactions need to
4813        wait for replies from,
4814        and <replaceable class="parameter">standby_name</replaceable>
4815        is the name of a standby server.
4816        <replaceable class="parameter">num_sync</replaceable>
4817        must be an integer value greater than zero.
4818        <literal>FIRST</literal> and <literal>ANY</literal> specify the method to choose
4819        synchronous standbys from the listed servers.
4820       </para>
4821       <para>
4822        The keyword <literal>FIRST</literal>, coupled with
4823        <replaceable class="parameter">num_sync</replaceable>, specifies a
4824        priority-based synchronous replication and makes transaction commits
4825        wait until their WAL records are replicated to
4826        <replaceable class="parameter">num_sync</replaceable> synchronous
4827        standbys chosen based on their priorities. For example, a setting of
4828        <literal>FIRST 3 (s1, s2, s3, s4)</literal> will cause each commit to wait for
4829        replies from three higher-priority standbys chosen from standby servers
4830        <literal>s1</literal>, <literal>s2</literal>, <literal>s3</literal> and <literal>s4</literal>.
4831        The standbys whose names appear earlier in the list are given higher
4832        priority and will be considered as synchronous. Other standby servers
4833        appearing later in this list represent potential synchronous standbys.
4834        If any of the current synchronous standbys disconnects for whatever
4835        reason, it will be replaced immediately with the next-highest-priority
4836        standby. The keyword <literal>FIRST</literal> is optional.
4837       </para>
4838       <para>
4839        The keyword <literal>ANY</literal>, coupled with
4840        <replaceable class="parameter">num_sync</replaceable>, specifies a
4841        quorum-based synchronous replication and makes transaction commits
4842        wait until their WAL records are replicated to <emphasis>at least</emphasis>
4843        <replaceable class="parameter">num_sync</replaceable> listed standbys.
4844        For example, a setting of <literal>ANY 3 (s1, s2, s3, s4)</literal> will cause
4845        each commit to proceed as soon as at least any three standbys of
4846        <literal>s1</literal>, <literal>s2</literal>, <literal>s3</literal> and <literal>s4</literal>
4847        reply.
4848       </para>
4849       <para>
4850        <literal>FIRST</literal> and <literal>ANY</literal> are case-insensitive. If these
4851        keywords are used as the name of a standby server,
4852        its <replaceable class="parameter">standby_name</replaceable> must
4853        be double-quoted.
4854       </para>
4855       <para>
4856        The third syntax was used before <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
4857        version 9.6 and is still supported. It's the same as the first syntax
4858        with <literal>FIRST</literal> and
4859        <replaceable class="parameter">num_sync</replaceable> equal to 1.
4860        For example, <literal>FIRST 1 (s1, s2)</literal> and <literal>s1, s2</literal> have
4861        the same meaning: either <literal>s1</literal> or <literal>s2</literal> is chosen
4862        as a synchronous standby.
4863       </para>
4864       <para>
4865        The special entry <literal>*</literal> matches any standby name.
4866       </para>
4867       <para>
4868        There is no mechanism to enforce uniqueness of standby names.  In case
4869        of duplicates one of the matching standbys will be considered as
4870        higher priority, though exactly which one is indeterminate.
4871       </para>
4872       <note>
4873        <para>
4874         Each <replaceable class="parameter">standby_name</replaceable>
4875         should have the form of a valid SQL identifier, unless it
4876         is <literal>*</literal>.  You can use double-quoting if necessary.  But note
4877         that <replaceable class="parameter">standby_name</replaceable>s are
4878         compared to standby application names case-insensitively, whether
4879         double-quoted or not.
4880        </para>
4881       </note>
4882       <para>
4883        If no synchronous standby names are specified here, then synchronous
4884        replication is not enabled and transaction commits will not wait for
4885        replication.  This is the default configuration.  Even when
4886        synchronous replication is enabled, individual transactions can be
4887        configured not to wait for replication by setting the
4888        <xref linkend="guc-synchronous-commit"/> parameter to
4889        <literal>local</literal> or <literal>off</literal>.
4890       </para>
4891       <para>
4892        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
4893        file or on the server command line.
4894       </para>
4895      </listitem>
4896
4897     </varlistentry>
4898     <varlistentry id="guc-synchronized-standby-slots" xreflabel="synchronized_standby_slots">
4899      <term><varname>synchronized_standby_slots</varname> (<type>string</type>)
4900      <indexterm>
4901       <primary><varname>synchronized_standby_slots</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4902      </indexterm>
4903      </term>
4904      <listitem>
4905       <para>
4906        A comma-separated list of streaming replication standby server slot names
4907        that logical WAL sender processes will wait for. Logical WAL sender processes
