4908        will send decoded changes to plugins only after the specified replication
4909        slots confirm receiving WAL. This guarantees that logical replication
4910        failover slots do not consume changes until those changes are received
4911        and flushed to corresponding physical standbys. If a
4912        logical replication connection is meant to switch to a physical standby
4913        after the standby is promoted, the physical replication slot for the
4914        standby should be listed here. Note that logical replication will not
4915        proceed if the slots specified in the
4916        <varname>synchronized_standby_slots</varname> do not exist or are invalidated.
4917        Additionally, the replication management functions
4918        <link linkend="pg-replication-slot-advance">
4919        <function>pg_replication_slot_advance</function></link>,
4920        <link linkend="pg-logical-slot-get-changes">
4921        <function>pg_logical_slot_get_changes</function></link>, and
4922        <link linkend="pg-logical-slot-peek-changes">
4923        <function>pg_logical_slot_peek_changes</function></link>,
4924        when used with logical failover slots, will block until all
4925        physical slots specified in <varname>synchronized_standby_slots</varname> have
4926        confirmed WAL receipt.
4927       </para>
4928       <para>
4929        The standbys corresponding to the physical replication slots in
4930        <varname>synchronized_standby_slots</varname> must configure
4931        <literal>sync_replication_slots = true</literal> so they can receive
4932        logical failover slot changes from the primary.
4933       </para>
4934      </listitem>
4935     </varlistentry>
4936
4937     </variablelist>
4938    </sect2>
4939
4940    <sect2 id="runtime-config-replication-standby">
4941     <title>Standby Servers</title>
4942
4943     <para>
4944      These settings control the behavior of a
4945      <link linkend="standby-server-operation">standby server</link>
4946      that is
4947      to receive replication data.  Their values on the primary server
4948      are irrelevant.
4949     </para>
4950
4951    <variablelist>
4952
4953       <varlistentry id="guc-primary-conninfo" xreflabel="primary_conninfo">
4954        <term><varname>primary_conninfo</varname> (<type>string</type>)
4955        <indexterm>
4956          <primary><varname>primary_conninfo</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
4957        </indexterm>
4958        </term>
4959        <listitem>
4960         <para>
4961          Specifies a connection string to be used for the standby server
4962          to connect with a sending server. This string is in the format
4963          described in <xref linkend="libpq-connstring"/>. If any option is
4964          unspecified in this string, then the corresponding environment
4965          variable (see <xref linkend="libpq-envars"/>) is checked. If the
4966          environment variable is not set either, then
4967          defaults are used.
4968         </para>
4969         <para>
4970          The connection string should specify the host name (or address)
4971          of the sending server, as well as the port number if it is not
4972          the same as the standby server's default.
4973          Also specify a user name corresponding to a suitably-privileged role
4974          on the sending server (see
4975          <xref linkend="streaming-replication-authentication"/>).
4976          A password needs to be provided too, if the sender demands password
4977          authentication.  It can be provided in the
4978          <varname>primary_conninfo</varname> string, or in a separate
4979          <filename>~/.pgpass</filename> file on the standby server (use
4980          <literal>replication</literal> as the database name).
4981         </para>
4982         <para>
4983          For replication slot synchronization (see
4984          <xref linkend="logicaldecoding-replication-slots-synchronization"/>),
4985          it is also necessary to specify a valid <literal>dbname</literal>
4986          in the <varname>primary_conninfo</varname> string. This will only be
4987          used for slot synchronization. It is ignored for streaming.
4988         </para>
4989         <para>
4990          This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
4991          file or on the server command line.
4992          If this parameter is changed while the WAL receiver process is
4993          running, that process is signaled to shut down and expected to
4994          restart with the new setting (except if <varname>primary_conninfo</varname>
4995          is an empty string).
4996          This setting has no effect if the server is not in standby mode.
4997         </para>
4998        </listitem>
4999       </varlistentry>
5000       <varlistentry id="guc-primary-slot-name" xreflabel="primary_slot_name">
5001        <term><varname>primary_slot_name</varname> (<type>string</type>)
5002        <indexterm>
5003          <primary><varname>primary_slot_name</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5004        </indexterm>
5005        </term>
5006        <listitem>
5007         <para>
5008          Optionally specifies an existing replication slot to be used when
5009          connecting to the sending server via streaming replication to control
5010          resource removal on the upstream node
5011          (see <xref linkend="streaming-replication-slots"/>).
5012          This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
5013          file or on the server command line.
5014          If this parameter is changed while the WAL receiver process is running,
5015          that process is signaled to shut down and expected to restart with the
5016          new setting.
5017          This setting has no effect if <varname>primary_conninfo</varname> is not
5018          set or the server is not in standby mode.
5019         </para>
5020        </listitem>
5021       </varlistentry>
5022
5023     <varlistentry id="guc-hot-standby" xreflabel="hot_standby">
5024      <term><varname>hot_standby</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5025      <indexterm>
5026       <primary><varname>hot_standby</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5027      </indexterm>
5028      </term>
5029      <listitem>
5030       <para>
5031        Specifies whether or not you can connect and run queries during
5032        recovery, as described in <xref linkend="hot-standby"/>.
5033        The default value is <literal>on</literal>.
5034        This parameter can only be set at server start. It only has effect
5035        during archive recovery or in standby mode.
5036       </para>
5037      </listitem>
5038     </varlistentry>
5039
5040     <varlistentry id="guc-max-standby-archive-delay" xreflabel="max_standby_archive_delay">
5041      <term><varname>max_standby_archive_delay</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
5042      <indexterm>
5043       <primary><varname>max_standby_archive_delay</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5044      </indexterm>
5045      </term>
5046      <listitem>
5047       <para>
5048        When hot standby is active, this parameter determines how long the
5049        standby server should wait before canceling standby queries that
5050        conflict with about-to-be-applied WAL entries, as described in
5051        <xref linkend="hot-standby-conflict"/>.
5052        <varname>max_standby_archive_delay</varname> applies when WAL data is
5053        being read from WAL archive (and is therefore not current).
5054        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
5055        The default is 30 seconds.
5056        A value of -1 allows the standby to wait forever for conflicting
5057        queries to complete.
5058        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
5059        file or on the server command line.
5060       </para>
5061       <para>
5062        Note that <varname>max_standby_archive_delay</varname> is not the same as the
5063        maximum length of time a query can run before cancellation; rather it
5064        is the maximum total time allowed to apply any one WAL segment's data.
5065        Thus, if one query has resulted in significant delay earlier in the
5066        WAL segment, subsequent conflicting queries will have much less grace
5067        time.
5068       </para>
5069      </listitem>
5070     </varlistentry>
5071
5072     <varlistentry id="guc-max-standby-streaming-delay" xreflabel="max_standby_streaming_delay">
5073      <term><varname>max_standby_streaming_delay</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
5074      <indexterm>
5075       <primary><varname>max_standby_streaming_delay</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5076      </indexterm>
5077      </term>
5078      <listitem>
5079       <para>
5080        When hot standby is active, this parameter determines how long the
5081        standby server should wait before canceling standby queries that
5082        conflict with about-to-be-applied WAL entries, as described in
5083        <xref linkend="hot-standby-conflict"/>.
5084        <varname>max_standby_streaming_delay</varname> applies when WAL data is
5085        being received via streaming replication.
5086        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
5087        The default is 30 seconds.
5088        A value of -1 allows the standby to wait forever for conflicting
5089        queries to complete.
5090        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
5091        file or on the server command line.
5092       </para>
5093       <para>
5094        Note that <varname>max_standby_streaming_delay</varname> is not the same as
5095        the maximum length of time a query can run before cancellation; rather
5096        it is the maximum total time allowed to apply WAL data once it has
5097        been received from the primary server.  Thus, if one query has
5098        resulted in significant delay, subsequent conflicting queries will
5099        have much less grace time until the standby server has caught up
5100        again.
5101       </para>
5102      </listitem>
5103     </varlistentry>
5104
5105     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-receiver-create-temp-slot" xreflabel="wal_receiver_create_temp_slot">
5106      <term><varname>wal_receiver_create_temp_slot</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5107      <indexterm>
5108       <primary><varname>wal_receiver_create_temp_slot</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5109      </indexterm>
5110      </term>
5111      <listitem>
5112       <para>
5113        Specifies whether the WAL receiver process should create a temporary replication
5114        slot on the remote instance when no permanent replication slot to use
5115        has been configured (using <xref linkend="guc-primary-slot-name"/>).
5116        The default is off.  This parameter can only be set in the
5117        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command line.
5118        If this parameter is changed while the WAL receiver process is running,
5119        that process is signaled to shut down and expected to restart with
5120        the new setting.
5121       </para>
5122      </listitem>
5123     </varlistentry>
5124
5125     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-receiver-status-interval" xreflabel="wal_receiver_status_interval">
5126      <term><varname>wal_receiver_status_interval</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
5127      <indexterm>
5128       <primary><varname>wal_receiver_status_interval</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5129      </indexterm>
5130      </term>
5131      <listitem>
5132      <para>
5133       Specifies the minimum frequency for the WAL receiver
5134       process on the standby to send information about replication progress
5135       to the primary or upstream standby, where it can be seen using the
5136       <link linkend="monitoring-pg-stat-replication-view">
5137       <structname>pg_stat_replication</structname></link>
5138       view.  The standby will report
5139       the last write-ahead log location it has written, the last position it
5140       has flushed to disk, and the last position it has applied.
5141       This parameter's value is the maximum amount of time between reports.
5142       Updates are sent each time the write or flush positions change, or as
5143       often as specified by this parameter if set to a non-zero value.
5144       There are additional cases where updates are sent while ignoring this
5145       parameter; for example, when processing of the existing WAL completes
5146       or when <varname>synchronous_commit</varname> is set to
5147       <literal>remote_apply</literal>.
5148       Thus, the apply position may lag slightly behind the true position.
5149       If this value is specified without units, it is taken as seconds.
5150       The default value is 10 seconds. This parameter can only be set in
5151       the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server
5152       command line.
5153      </para>
5154      </listitem>
5155     </varlistentry>
5156
5157     <varlistentry id="guc-hot-standby-feedback" xreflabel="hot_standby_feedback">
5158      <term><varname>hot_standby_feedback</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5159      <indexterm>
5160       <primary><varname>hot_standby_feedback</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5161      </indexterm>
5162      </term>
5163      <listitem>
5164       <para>
5165        Specifies whether or not a hot standby will send feedback to the primary
5166        or upstream standby
5167        about queries currently executing on the standby. This parameter can
5168        be used to eliminate query cancels caused by cleanup records, but
5169        can cause database bloat on the primary for some workloads.
5170        Feedback messages will not be sent more frequently than once per
5171        <varname>wal_receiver_status_interval</varname>. The default value is
5172        <literal>off</literal>. This parameter can only be set in the
5173        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command line.
5174       </para>
5175       <para>
5176        If cascaded replication is in use the feedback is passed upstream
5177        until it eventually reaches the primary.  Standbys make no other use
5178        of feedback they receive other than to pass upstream.
5179       </para>
5180       <para>
5181        Note that if the clock on standby is moved ahead or backward, the
5182        feedback message might not be sent at the required interval.  In
5183        extreme cases, this can lead to a prolonged risk of not removing dead
5184        rows on the primary for extended periods, as the feedback mechanism is
5185        based on timestamps.
5186       </para>
5187      </listitem>
5188     </varlistentry>
5189
5190     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-receiver-timeout" xreflabel="wal_receiver_timeout">
5191      <term><varname>wal_receiver_timeout</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
5192      <indexterm>
5193       <primary><varname>wal_receiver_timeout</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5194      </indexterm>
5195      </term>
5196      <listitem>
5197       <para>
5198        Terminate replication connections that are inactive for longer
5199        than this amount of time. This is useful for
5200        the receiving standby server to detect a primary node crash or network
5201        outage.
5202        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
5203        The default value is 60 seconds.
5204        A value of zero disables the timeout mechanism.
5205        This parameter can only be set in
5206        the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server
5207        command line.
5208       </para>
5209      </listitem>
5210     </varlistentry>
5211
5212     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-retrieve-retry-interval" xreflabel="wal_retrieve_retry_interval">
5213      <term><varname>wal_retrieve_retry_interval</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
5214      <indexterm>
5215       <primary><varname>wal_retrieve_retry_interval</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5216      </indexterm>
5217      </term>
5218      <listitem>
5219       <para>
5220        Specifies how long the standby server should wait when WAL data is not
5221        available from any sources (streaming replication,
5222        local <filename>pg_wal</filename> or WAL archive) before trying
5223        again to retrieve WAL data.
5224        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
5225        The default value is 5 seconds.
5226        This parameter can only be set in
5227        the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server
5228        command line.
5229       </para>
5230       <para>
5231        This parameter is useful in configurations where a node in recovery
5232        needs to control the amount of time to wait for new WAL data to be
5233        available. For example, in archive recovery, it is possible to
5234        make the recovery more responsive in the detection of a new WAL
5235        file by reducing the value of this parameter. On a system with
5236        low WAL activity, increasing it reduces the amount of requests necessary
5237        to access WAL archives, something useful for example in cloud
5238        environments where the number of times an infrastructure is accessed
5239        is taken into account.
5240       </para>
5241       <para>
5242        In logical replication, this parameter also limits how often a failing
5243        replication apply worker or table synchronization worker will be
5244        respawned.
5245       </para>
5246      </listitem>
5247     </varlistentry>
5248
5249     <varlistentry id="guc-recovery-min-apply-delay" xreflabel="recovery_min_apply_delay">
5250      <term><varname>recovery_min_apply_delay</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
5251      <indexterm>
5252        <primary><varname>recovery_min_apply_delay</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5253      </indexterm>
5254      </term>
5255      <listitem>
5256       <para>
5257        By default, a standby server restores WAL records from the
5258        sending server as soon as possible. It may be useful to have a time-delayed
5259        copy of the data, offering opportunities to correct data loss errors.
5260        This parameter allows you to delay recovery by a specified amount
5261        of time.  For example, if
5262        you set this parameter to <literal>5min</literal>, the standby will
5263        replay each transaction commit only when the system time on the standby
5264        is at least five minutes past the commit time reported by the primary.
5265        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
5266        The default is zero, adding no delay.
5267       </para>
5268       <para>
5269        It is possible that the replication delay between servers exceeds the
5270        value of this parameter, in which case no delay is added.
5271        Note that the delay is calculated between the WAL time stamp as written
5272        on primary and the current time on the standby. Delays in transfer
5273        because of network lag or cascading replication configurations
5274        may reduce the actual wait time significantly. If the system
5275        clocks on primary and standby are not synchronized, this may lead to
5276        recovery applying records earlier than expected; but that is not a
5277        major issue because useful settings of this parameter are much larger
5278        than typical time deviations between servers.
5279       </para>
5280       <para>
5281        The delay occurs only on WAL records for transaction commits.
5282        Other records are replayed as quickly as possible, which
5283        is not a problem because MVCC visibility rules ensure their effects
5284        are not visible until the corresponding commit record is applied.
5285       </para>
5286       <para>
5287        The delay occurs once the database in recovery has reached a consistent
5288        state, until the standby is promoted or triggered. After that the standby
5289        will end recovery without further waiting.
5290       </para>
5291       <para>
5292        WAL records must be kept on the standby until they are ready to be
5293        applied. Therefore, longer delays will result in a greater accumulation
5294        of WAL files, increasing disk space requirements for the standby's
5295        <filename>pg_wal</filename> directory.
5296       </para>
5297       <para>
5298        This parameter is intended for use with streaming replication deployments;
5299        however, if the parameter is specified it will be honored in all cases
5300        except crash recovery.
5301
5302        <varname>hot_standby_feedback</varname> will be delayed by use of this feature
5303        which could lead to bloat on the primary; use both together with care.
5304
5305        <warning>
5306         <para>
5307          Synchronous replication is affected by this setting when <varname>synchronous_commit</varname>
5308          is set to <literal>remote_apply</literal>; every <literal>COMMIT</literal>
5309          will need to wait to be applied.
5310         </para>
5311        </warning>
5312       </para>
5313       <para>
5314        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
5315        file or on the server command line.
5316       </para>
5317      </listitem>
5318     </varlistentry>
5319
5320     <varlistentry id="guc-sync-replication-slots" xreflabel="sync_replication_slots">
5321      <term><varname>sync_replication_slots</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5322      <indexterm>
5323       <primary><varname>sync_replication_slots</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5324      </indexterm>
5325      </term>
5326      <listitem>
5327       <para>
5328        It enables a physical standby to synchronize logical failover slots
5329        from the primary server so that logical subscribers can resume
5330        replication from the new primary server after failover.
5331       </para>
5332       <para>
5333        It is disabled by default. This parameter can only be set in the
5334        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command line.
5335       </para>
5336      </listitem>
5337     </varlistentry>
5338     </variablelist>
5339    </sect2>
5340
5341    <sect2 id="runtime-config-replication-subscriber">
5342     <title>Subscribers</title>
5343
5344     <para>
5345      These settings control the behavior of a logical replication subscriber.
5346      Their values on the publisher are irrelevant.
5347      See <xref linkend="logical-replication-config"/> for more details.
5348     </para>
5349
5350     <variablelist>
5351
5352     <varlistentry id="guc-max-active-replication-origins" xreflabel="max_active_replication_origins">
5353      <term><varname>max_active_replication_origins</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
5354       <indexterm>
5355        <primary><varname>max_active_replication_origins</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5356       </indexterm>
5357      </term>
5358      <listitem>
5359       <para>
5360        Specifies how many replication origins (see
5361        <xref linkend="replication-origins"/>) can be tracked simultaneously,
5362        effectively limiting how many logical replication subscriptions can
5363        be created on the server. Setting it to a lower value than the current
5364        number of tracked replication origins (reflected in
5365        <link linkend="view-pg-replication-origin-status">pg_replication_origin_status</link>)
5366        will prevent the server from starting. It defaults to 10. This parameter
5367        can only be set at server start.
5368
5369        <literal>max_active_replication_origins</literal> must be set to at least the
5370        number of subscriptions that will be added to the subscriber, plus some
5371        reserve for table synchronization.
5372       </para>
5373      </listitem>
5374     </varlistentry>
5375
5376     <varlistentry id="guc-max-logical-replication-workers" xreflabel="max_logical_replication_workers">
5377      <term><varname>max_logical_replication_workers</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
5378      <indexterm>
5379       <primary><varname>max_logical_replication_workers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5380      </indexterm>
5381      </term>
5382      <listitem>
5383       <para>
5384        Specifies maximum number of logical replication workers. This includes
5385        leader apply workers, parallel apply workers, and table synchronization
5386        workers.
5387       </para>
5388       <para>
5389        Logical replication workers are taken from the pool defined by
5390        <varname>max_worker_processes</varname>.
5391       </para>
5392       <para>
5393        The default value is 4. This parameter can only be set at server
5394        start.
5395       </para>
5396      </listitem>
5397     </varlistentry>
5398
5399     <varlistentry id="guc-max-sync-workers-per-subscription" xreflabel="max_sync_workers_per_subscription">
5400      <term><varname>max_sync_workers_per_subscription</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
5401      <indexterm>
5402       <primary><varname>max_sync_workers_per_subscription</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5403      </indexterm>
5404      </term>
5405      <listitem>
5406       <para>
5407        Maximum number of synchronization workers per subscription. This
5408        parameter controls the amount of parallelism of the initial data copy
5409        during the subscription initialization or when new tables are added.
5410       </para>
5411       <para>
5412        Currently, there can be only one synchronization worker per table.
5413       </para>
5414       <para>
5415        The synchronization workers are taken from the pool defined by
5416        <varname>max_logical_replication_workers</varname>.
5417       </para>
5418       <para>
5419        The default value is 2. This parameter can only be set in the
5420        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command
5421        line.
5422       </para>
5423      </listitem>
5424     </varlistentry>
5425
5426     <varlistentry id="guc-max-parallel-apply-workers-per-subscription" xreflabel="max_parallel_apply_workers_per_subscription">
5427      <term><varname>max_parallel_apply_workers_per_subscription</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
5428      <indexterm>
5429       <primary><varname>max_parallel_apply_workers_per_subscription</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5430      </indexterm>
5431      </term>
5432      <listitem>
5433       <para>
5434        Maximum number of parallel apply workers per subscription. This
5435        parameter controls the amount of parallelism for streaming of
5436        in-progress transactions with subscription parameter
5437        <literal>streaming = parallel</literal>.
5438       </para>
5439       <para>
5440        The parallel apply workers are taken from the pool defined by
5441        <varname>max_logical_replication_workers</varname>.
5442       </para>
5443       <para>
5444        The default value is 2. This parameter can only be set in the
5445        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command
5446        line.
5447       </para>
5448      </listitem>
5449     </varlistentry>
5450
5451     </variablelist>
5452    </sect2>
5453
5454   </sect1>
5455
5456   <sect1 id="runtime-config-query">
5457    <title>Query Planning</title>
5458
5459    <sect2 id="runtime-config-query-enable">
5460     <title>Planner Method Configuration</title>
5461
5462      <para>
5463       These configuration parameters provide a crude method of
5464       influencing the query plans chosen by the query optimizer. If
5465       the default plan chosen by the optimizer for a particular query
5466       is not optimal, a <emphasis>temporary</emphasis> solution is to use one
5467       of these configuration parameters to force the optimizer to
5468       choose a different plan.
5469       Better ways to improve the quality of the
5470       plans chosen by the optimizer include adjusting the planner cost
5471       constants (see <xref linkend="runtime-config-query-constants"/>),
5472       running <link linkend="sql-analyze"><command>ANALYZE</command></link> manually, increasing
5473       the value of the <xref
5474       linkend="guc-default-statistics-target"/> configuration parameter,
5475       and increasing the amount of statistics collected for
5476       specific columns using <command>ALTER TABLE SET
5477       STATISTICS</command>.
5478      </para>
5479
5480     <variablelist>
5481     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-async-append" xreflabel="enable_async_append">
5482      <term><varname>enable_async_append</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5483      <indexterm>
5484       <primary><varname>enable_async_append</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5485      </indexterm>
5486      </term>
5487      <listitem>
5488       <para>
5489        Enables or disables the query planner's use of async-aware
5490        append plan types. The default is <literal>on</literal>.
5491       </para>
5492      </listitem>
5493     </varlistentry>
5494
5495     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-bitmapscan" xreflabel="enable_bitmapscan">
5496      <term><varname>enable_bitmapscan</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5497      <indexterm>
5498       <primary>bitmap scan</primary>
5499      </indexterm>
5500      <indexterm>
5501       <primary><varname>enable_bitmapscan</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5502      </indexterm>
5503      </term>
5504      <listitem>
5505       <para>
5506        Enables or disables the query planner's use of bitmap-scan plan
5507        types. The default is <literal>on</literal>.
5508       </para>
5509      </listitem>
5510     </varlistentry>
5511
5512     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-distinct-reordering" xreflabel="enable_distinct_reordering">
5513      <term><varname>enable_distinct_reordering</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5514      <indexterm>
5515       <primary><varname>enable_distinct_reordering</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5516      </indexterm>
5517      </term>
5518      <listitem>
5519       <para>
5520        Enables or disables the query planner's ability to reorder DISTINCT
5521        keys to match the input path's pathkeys. The default is <literal>on</literal>.
5522       </para>
5523      </listitem>
5524     </varlistentry>
5525
5526     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-gathermerge" xreflabel="enable_gathermerge">
5527      <term><varname>enable_gathermerge</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5528      <indexterm>
5529       <primary><varname>enable_gathermerge</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5530      </indexterm>
5531      </term>
5532      <listitem>
5533       <para>
5534        Enables or disables the query planner's use of gather
5535        merge plan types. The default is <literal>on</literal>.
5536       </para>
5537      </listitem>
5538     </varlistentry>
5539
5540     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-groupby-reordering" xreflabel="enable_group_by_reordering">
5541      <term><varname>enable_group_by_reordering</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5542      <indexterm>
5543       <primary><varname>enable_group_by_reordering</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5544      </indexterm>
5545      </term>
5546      <listitem>
5547       <para>
5548        Controls if the query planner will produce a plan which will provide
5549        <literal>GROUP BY</literal> keys sorted in the order of keys of
5550        a child node of the plan, such as an index scan.  When disabled, the
5551        query planner will produce a plan with <literal>GROUP BY</literal>
5552        keys only sorted to match the <literal>ORDER BY</literal> clause,
5553        if any. When enabled, the planner will try to produce a more
5554        efficient plan. The default value is <literal>on</literal>.
5555       </para>
5556      </listitem>
5557     </varlistentry>
5558
5559     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-hashagg" xreflabel="enable_hashagg">
5560      <term><varname>enable_hashagg</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5561      <indexterm>
5562       <primary><varname>enable_hashagg</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5563      </indexterm>
5564      </term>
5565      <listitem>
5566       <para>
5567        Enables or disables the query planner's use of hashed
5568        aggregation plan types. The default is <literal>on</literal>.
5569       </para>
5570      </listitem>
5571     </varlistentry>
5572
5573     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-hashjoin" xreflabel="enable_hashjoin">
5574      <term><varname>enable_hashjoin</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5575      <indexterm>
5576       <primary><varname>enable_hashjoin</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5577      </indexterm>
5578      </term>
5579      <listitem>
5580       <para>
5581        Enables or disables the query planner's use of hash-join plan
5582        types. The default is <literal>on</literal>.
5583       </para>
5584      </listitem>
5585     </varlistentry>
5586
5587     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-incremental-sort" xreflabel="enable_incremental_sort">
5588      <term><varname>enable_incremental_sort</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5589      <indexterm>
5590       <primary><varname>enable_incremental_sort</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5591      </indexterm>
5592      </term>
5593      <listitem>
5594       <para>
5595        Enables or disables the query planner's use of incremental sort steps.
5596        The default is <literal>on</literal>.
5597       </para>
5598      </listitem>
5599     </varlistentry>
5600
5601     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-indexscan" xreflabel="enable_indexscan">
5602      <term><varname>enable_indexscan</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5603      <indexterm>
5604       <primary>index scan</primary>
5605      </indexterm>
5606      <indexterm>
5607       <primary><varname>enable_indexscan</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5608      </indexterm>
5609      </term>
5610      <listitem>
5611       <para>
5612        Enables or disables the query planner's use of index-scan and
5613        index-only-scan plan types.  The default is <literal>on</literal>.
5614        Also see <xref linkend="guc-enable-indexonlyscan"/>.
5615       </para>
5616      </listitem>
5617     </varlistentry>
5618
5619     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-indexonlyscan" xreflabel="enable_indexonlyscan">
5620      <term><varname>enable_indexonlyscan</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5621      <indexterm>
5622       <primary><varname>enable_indexonlyscan</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5623      </indexterm>
5624      </term>
5625      <listitem>
5626       <para>
5627        Enables or disables the query planner's use of index-only-scan plan
5628        types (see <xref linkend="indexes-index-only-scans"/>).
5629        The default is <literal>on</literal>.  The
5630        <xref linkend="guc-enable-indexscan"/> setting must also be
5631        enabled to have the query planner consider index-only-scans.
5632       </para>
5633      </listitem>
5634     </varlistentry>
5635
5636     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-material" xreflabel="enable_material">
5637      <term><varname>enable_material</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5638      <indexterm>
5639       <primary><varname>enable_material</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5640      </indexterm>
5641      </term>
5642      <listitem>
5643       <para>
5644        Enables or disables the query planner's use of materialization.
5645        It is impossible to suppress materialization entirely,
5646        but turning this variable off prevents the planner from inserting
5647        materialize nodes except in cases where it is required for correctness.
5648        The default is <literal>on</literal>.
5649       </para>
5650      </listitem>
5651     </varlistentry>
5652
5653     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-memoize" xreflabel="enable_memoize">
5654      <term><varname>enable_memoize</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5655      <indexterm>
5656       <primary><varname>enable_memoize</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5657      </indexterm>
5658      </term>
5659      <listitem>
5660       <para>
5661        Enables or disables the query planner's use of memoize plans for
5662        caching results from parameterized scans inside nested-loop joins.
5663        This plan type allows scans to the underlying plans to be skipped when
5664        the results for the current parameters are already in the cache.  Less
5665        commonly looked up results may be evicted from the cache when more
5666        space is required for new entries. The default is
5667        <literal>on</literal>.
5668       </para>
5669      </listitem>
5670     </varlistentry>
5671
5672     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-mergejoin" xreflabel="enable_mergejoin">
5673      <term><varname>enable_mergejoin</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5674      <indexterm>
5675       <primary><varname>enable_mergejoin</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5676      </indexterm>
5677      </term>
5678      <listitem>
5679       <para>
5680        Enables or disables the query planner's use of merge-join plan
5681        types. The default is <literal>on</literal>.
5682       </para>
5683      </listitem>
5684     </varlistentry>
5685
5686     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-nestloop" xreflabel="enable_nestloop">
5687      <term><varname>enable_nestloop</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5688      <indexterm>
5689       <primary><varname>enable_nestloop</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5690      </indexterm>
5691      </term>
5692      <listitem>
5693       <para>
5694        Enables or disables the query planner's use of nested-loop join
5695        plans. It is impossible to suppress nested-loop joins entirely,
5696        but turning this variable off discourages the planner from using
5697        one if there are other methods available. The default is
5698        <literal>on</literal>.
5699       </para>
5700      </listitem>
5701     </varlistentry>
5702
5703     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-parallel-append" xreflabel="enable_parallel_append">
5704      <term><varname>enable_parallel_append</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5705      <indexterm>
5706       <primary><varname>enable_parallel_append</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5707      </indexterm>
5708      </term>
5709      <listitem>
5710       <para>
5711        Enables or disables the query planner's use of parallel-aware
5712        append plan types. The default is <literal>on</literal>.
5713       </para>
5714      </listitem>
5715     </varlistentry>
5716
5717     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-parallel-hash" xreflabel="enable_parallel_hash">
5718      <term><varname>enable_parallel_hash</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5719       <indexterm>
5720        <primary><varname>enable_parallel_hash</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5721       </indexterm>
5722      </term>
5723      <listitem>
5724       <para>
5725        Enables or disables the query planner's use of hash-join plan
5726        types with parallel hash. Has no effect if hash-join plans are not
5727        also enabled. The default is <literal>on</literal>.
5728       </para>
5729      </listitem>
5730     </varlistentry>
5731
5732     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-partition-pruning" xreflabel="enable_partition_pruning">
5733      <term><varname>enable_partition_pruning</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5734       <indexterm>
5735        <primary><varname>enable_partition_pruning</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5736       </indexterm>
5737      </term>
5738      <listitem>
5739       <para>
5740        Enables or disables the query planner's ability to eliminate a
5741        partitioned table's partitions from query plans.  This also controls
5742        the planner's ability to generate query plans which allow the query
5743        executor to remove (ignore) partitions during query execution.  The
5744        default is <literal>on</literal>.
5745        See <xref linkend="ddl-partition-pruning"/> for details.
5746       </para>
5747      </listitem>
5748     </varlistentry>
5749
5750     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-partitionwise-join" xreflabel="enable_partitionwise_join">
5751      <term><varname>enable_partitionwise_join</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5752      <indexterm>
5753       <primary><varname>enable_partitionwise_join</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5754      </indexterm>
5755      </term>
5756      <listitem>
5757       <para>
5758        Enables or disables the query planner's use of partitionwise join,
5759        which allows a join between partitioned tables to be performed by
5760        joining the matching partitions.  Partitionwise join currently applies
5761        only when the join conditions include all the partition keys, which
5762        must be of the same data type and have one-to-one matching sets of
5763        child partitions.  With this setting enabled, the number of nodes
5764        whose memory usage is restricted by <varname>work_mem</varname>
5765        appearing in the final plan can increase linearly according to the
5766        number of partitions being scanned.  This can result in a large
5767        increase in overall memory consumption during the execution of the
5768        query.  Query planning also becomes significantly more expensive in
5769        terms of memory and CPU.  The default value is <literal>off</literal>.
5770       </para>
5771      </listitem>
5772     </varlistentry>
5773
5774     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-partitionwise-aggregate" xreflabel="enable_partitionwise_aggregate">
5775      <term><varname>enable_partitionwise_aggregate</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5776      <indexterm>
5777       <primary><varname>enable_partitionwise_aggregate</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5778      </indexterm>
5779      </term>
5780      <listitem>
5781       <para>
5782        Enables or disables the query planner's use of partitionwise grouping
5783        or aggregation, which allows grouping or aggregation on partitioned
5784        tables to be performed separately for each partition.  If the
5785        <literal>GROUP BY</literal> clause does not include the partition
5786        keys, only partial aggregation can be performed on a per-partition
5787        basis, and finalization must be performed later.  With this setting
5788        enabled, the number of nodes whose memory usage is restricted by
5789        <varname>work_mem</varname> appearing in the final plan can increase
5790        linearly according to the number of partitions being scanned.  This
5791        can result in a large increase in overall memory consumption during
5792        the execution of the query.  Query planning also becomes significantly
5793        more expensive in terms of memory and CPU.  The default value is
5794        <literal>off</literal>.
5795       </para>
5796      </listitem>
5797     </varlistentry>
5798
5799     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-presorted-aggregate" xreflabel="enable_presorted_aggregate">
5800      <term><varname>enable_presorted_aggregate</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5801      <indexterm>
5802       <primary><varname>enable_presorted_aggregate</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5803      </indexterm>
5804      </term>
5805      <listitem>
5806       <para>
5807        Controls if the query planner will produce a plan which will provide
5808        rows which are presorted in the order required for the query's
5809        <literal>ORDER BY</literal> / <literal>DISTINCT</literal> aggregate
5810        functions.  When disabled, the query planner will produce a plan which
5811        will always require the executor to perform a sort before performing
5812        aggregation of each aggregate function containing an
5813        <literal>ORDER BY</literal> or <literal>DISTINCT</literal> clause.
5814        When enabled, the planner will try to produce a more efficient plan
5815        which provides input to the aggregate functions which is presorted in
5816        the order they require for aggregation.  The default value is
5817        <literal>on</literal>.
5818       </para>
5819      </listitem>
5820     </varlistentry>
5821
5822     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-self-join-elimination" xreflabel="enable_self_join_elimination">
5823      <term><varname>enable_self_join_elimination</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5824      <indexterm>
5825       <primary><varname>enable_self_join_elimination</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5826      </indexterm>
5827      </term>
5828      <listitem>
5829       <para>
5830        Enables or disables the query planner's optimization which analyses
5831        the query tree and replaces self joins with semantically equivalent
5832        single scans.  Takes into consideration only plain tables.
5833        The default is <literal>on</literal>.
5834       </para>
5835      </listitem>
5836     </varlistentry>
5837
5838     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-seqscan" xreflabel="enable_seqscan">
5839      <term><varname>enable_seqscan</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5840      <indexterm>
5841       <primary>sequential scan</primary>
5842      </indexterm>
5843      <indexterm>
5844       <primary><varname>enable_seqscan</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5845      </indexterm>
5846      </term>
5847      <listitem>
5848       <para>
5849        Enables or disables the query planner's use of sequential scan
5850        plan types. It is impossible to suppress sequential scans
5851        entirely, but turning this variable off discourages the planner
5852        from using one if there are other methods available. The
5853        default is <literal>on</literal>.
5854       </para>
5855      </listitem>
5856     </varlistentry>
5857
5858     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-sort" xreflabel="enable_sort">
5859      <term><varname>enable_sort</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5860      <indexterm>
5861       <primary><varname>enable_sort</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5862      </indexterm>
5863      </term>
5864      <listitem>
5865       <para>
5866        Enables or disables the query planner's use of explicit sort
5867        steps. It is impossible to suppress explicit sorts entirely,
5868        but turning this variable off discourages the planner from
5869        using one if there are other methods available. The default
5870        is <literal>on</literal>.
5871       </para>
5872      </listitem>
5873     </varlistentry>
5874
5875     <varlistentry id="guc-enable-tidscan" xreflabel="enable_tidscan">
5876      <term><varname>enable_tidscan</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
5877      <indexterm>
5878       <primary><varname>enable_tidscan</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5879      </indexterm>
5880      </term>
5881      <listitem>
5882       <para>
5883        Enables or disables the query planner's use of <acronym>TID</acronym>
5884        scan plan types. The default is <literal>on</literal>.
5885       </para>
5886      </listitem>
5887     </varlistentry>
5888
5889     </variablelist>
5890     </sect2>
5891     <sect2 id="runtime-config-query-constants">
5892     <title>Planner Cost Constants</title>
5893
5894    <para>
5895     The <firstterm>cost</firstterm> variables described in this section are measured
5896     on an arbitrary scale.  Only their relative values matter, hence
5897     scaling them all up or down by the same factor will result in no change
5898     in the planner's choices.  By default, these cost variables are based on
5899     the cost of sequential page fetches; that is,
5900     <varname>seq_page_cost</varname> is conventionally set to <literal>1.0</literal>
5901     and the other cost variables are set with reference to that.  But
5902     you can use a different scale if you prefer, such as actual execution
5903     times in milliseconds on a particular machine.
5904    </para>
5905
5906   <note>
5907    <para>
5908     Unfortunately, there is no well-defined method for determining ideal
5909     values for the cost variables.  They are best treated as averages over
5910     the entire mix of queries that a particular installation will receive.  This
5911     means that changing them on the basis of just a few experiments is very
5912     risky.
5913    </para>
5914   </note>
5915
5916     <variablelist>
5917
5918     <varlistentry id="guc-seq-page-cost" xreflabel="seq_page_cost">
5919      <term><varname>seq_page_cost</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
5920      <indexterm>
5921       <primary><varname>seq_page_cost</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5922      </indexterm>
5923      </term>
5924      <listitem>
5925       <para>
5926        Sets the planner's estimate of the cost of a disk page fetch
5927        that is part of a series of sequential fetches.  The default is 1.0.
5928        This value can be overridden for tables and indexes in a particular
5929        tablespace by setting the tablespace parameter of the same name
5930        (see <xref linkend="sql-altertablespace"/>).
5931       </para>
5932      </listitem>
5933     </varlistentry>
5934
5935     <varlistentry id="guc-random-page-cost" xreflabel="random_page_cost">
5936      <term><varname>random_page_cost</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
5937      <indexterm>
5938       <primary><varname>random_page_cost</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5939      </indexterm>
5940      </term>
5941      <listitem>
5942       <para>
5943        Sets the planner's estimate of the cost of a
5944        non-sequentially-fetched disk page.  The default is 4.0.
5945        This value can be overridden for tables and indexes in a particular
5946        tablespace by setting the tablespace parameter of the same name
5947        (see <xref linkend="sql-altertablespace"/>).
5948       </para>
5949
5950       <para>
5951        Reducing this value relative to <varname>seq_page_cost</varname>
5952        will cause the system to prefer index scans; raising it will
5953        make index scans look relatively more expensive.  You can raise
5954        or lower both values together to change the importance of disk I/O
5955        costs relative to CPU costs, which are described by the following
5956        parameters.
5957       </para>
5958
5959       <para>
5960        Random access to durable storage is normally much more expensive
5961        than four times sequential access.  However, a lower default is
5962        used (4.0) because the majority of random accesses to storage,
5963        such as indexed reads, are assumed to be in cache.  Also, the
5964        latency of network-attached storage tends to reduce the relative
5965        overhead of random access.
5966       </para>
5967
5968       <para>
5969        If you believe caching is less frequent than the default
5970        value reflects, and network latency is minimal, you can increase
5971        random_page_cost to better reflect the true cost of random storage
5972        reads.  Storage that has a higher random read cost relative to
5973        sequential, like magnetic disks, might also be better modeled with
5974        a higher value for random_page_cost.  Correspondingly, if your data
5975        is likely to be completely in cache, such as when the database
5976        is smaller than the total server memory, or network latency is
5977        high, decreasing random_page_cost might be appropriate.
5978       </para>
5979
5980       <tip>
5981        <para>
5982         Although the system will let you set <varname>random_page_cost</varname> to
5983         less than <varname>seq_page_cost</varname>, it is not physically sensible
5984         to do so.  However, setting them equal makes sense if the database
5985         is entirely cached in RAM, since in that case there is no penalty
5986         for touching pages out of sequence.  Also, in a heavily-cached
5987         database you should lower both values relative to the CPU parameters,
5988         since the cost of fetching a page already in RAM is much smaller
5989         than it would normally be.
5990        </para>
5991       </tip>
5992      </listitem>
5993     </varlistentry>
5994
5995     <varlistentry id="guc-cpu-tuple-cost" xreflabel="cpu_tuple_cost">
5996      <term><varname>cpu_tuple_cost</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
5997      <indexterm>
5998       <primary><varname>cpu_tuple_cost</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
5999      </indexterm>
6000      </term>
6001      <listitem>
6002       <para>
6003        Sets the planner's estimate of the cost of processing
6004        each row during a query.
6005        The default is 0.01.
6006       </para>
6007      </listitem>
6008     </varlistentry>
6009
6010     <varlistentry id="guc-cpu-index-tuple-cost" xreflabel="cpu_index_tuple_cost">
6011      <term><varname>cpu_index_tuple_cost</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
6012      <indexterm>
6013       <primary><varname>cpu_index_tuple_cost</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6014      </indexterm>
6015      </term>
6016      <listitem>
6017       <para>
6018        Sets the planner's estimate of the cost of processing
6019        each index entry during an index scan.
6020        The default is 0.005.
6021       </para>
6022      </listitem>
6023     </varlistentry>
6024
6025     <varlistentry id="guc-cpu-operator-cost" xreflabel="cpu_operator_cost">
6026      <term><varname>cpu_operator_cost</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
6027      <indexterm>
6028       <primary><varname>cpu_operator_cost</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6029      </indexterm>
6030      </term>
6031      <listitem>
6032       <para>
6033        Sets the planner's estimate of the cost of processing each
6034        operator or function executed during a query.
6035        The default is 0.0025.
6036       </para>
6037      </listitem>
6038     </varlistentry>
6039
6040     <varlistentry id="guc-parallel-setup-cost" xreflabel="parallel_setup_cost">
6041      <term><varname>parallel_setup_cost</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
6042      <indexterm>
6043       <primary><varname>parallel_setup_cost</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6044      </indexterm>
6045      </term>
6046      <listitem>
6047       <para>
6048        Sets the planner's estimate of the cost of launching parallel worker
6049        processes.
6050        The default is 1000.
6051       </para>
6052      </listitem>
6053     </varlistentry>
6054
6055     <varlistentry id="guc-parallel-tuple-cost" xreflabel="parallel_tuple_cost">
6056      <term><varname>parallel_tuple_cost</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
6057      <indexterm>
6058       <primary><varname>parallel_tuple_cost</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6059      </indexterm>
6060      </term>
6061      <listitem>
6062       <para>
6063        Sets the planner's estimate of the cost of transferring one tuple
6064        from a parallel worker process to another process.
6065        The default is 0.1.
6066       </para>
6067      </listitem>
6068     </varlistentry>
6069
6070     <varlistentry id="guc-min-parallel-table-scan-size" xreflabel="min_parallel_table_scan_size">
6071      <term><varname>min_parallel_table_scan_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
6072      <indexterm>
6073       <primary><varname>min_parallel_table_scan_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6074      </indexterm>
6075      </term>
6076      <listitem>
6077       <para>
6078        Sets the minimum amount of table data that must be scanned in order
6079        for a parallel scan to be considered.  For a parallel sequential scan,
6080        the amount of table data scanned is always equal to the size of the
6081        table, but when indexes are used the amount of table data
6082        scanned will normally be less.
6083        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
6084        that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
6085        The default is 8 megabytes (<literal>8MB</literal>).
6086       </para>
6087      </listitem>
6088     </varlistentry>
6089
6090     <varlistentry id="guc-min-parallel-index-scan-size" xreflabel="min_parallel_index_scan_size">
6091      <term><varname>min_parallel_index_scan_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
6092      <indexterm>
6093       <primary><varname>min_parallel_index_scan_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6094      </indexterm>
6095      </term>
6096      <listitem>
6097       <para>
6098        Sets the minimum amount of index data that must be scanned in order
6099        for a parallel scan to be considered.  Note that a parallel index scan
6100        typically won't touch the entire index; it is the number of pages
6101        which the planner believes will actually be touched by the scan which
6102        is relevant.  This parameter is also used to decide whether a
6103        particular index can participate in a parallel vacuum.  See
6104        <xref linkend="sql-vacuum"/>.
6105        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
6106        that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
6107        The default is 512 kilobytes (<literal>512kB</literal>).
6108       </para>
6109      </listitem>
6110     </varlistentry>
6111
6112     <varlistentry id="guc-effective-cache-size" xreflabel="effective_cache_size">
6113      <term><varname>effective_cache_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
6114      <indexterm>
6115       <primary><varname>effective_cache_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6116      </indexterm>
6117      </term>
6118      <listitem>
6119       <para>
6120        Sets the planner's assumption about the effective size of the
6121        disk cache that is available to a single query.  This is
6122        factored into estimates of the cost of using an index; a
6123        higher value makes it more likely index scans will be used, a
6124        lower value makes it more likely sequential scans will be
6125        used. When setting this parameter you should consider both
6126        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>'s shared buffers and the
6127        portion of the kernel's disk cache that will be used for
6128        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> data files, though some
6129        data might exist in both places. Also, take
6130        into account the expected number of concurrent queries on different
6131        tables, since they will have to share the available
6132        space.  This parameter has no effect on the size of shared
6133        memory allocated by <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>, nor
6134        does it reserve kernel disk cache; it is used only for estimation
6135        purposes.  The system also does not assume data remains in
6136        the disk cache between queries.
6137        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as blocks,
6138        that is <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> bytes, typically 8kB.
6139        The default is 4 gigabytes (<literal>4GB</literal>).
6140        (If <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol> is not 8kB, the default value scales
6141        proportionally to it.)
6142       </para>
6143      </listitem>
6144     </varlistentry>
6145
6146     <varlistentry id="guc-jit-above-cost" xreflabel="jit_above_cost">
6147      <term><varname>jit_above_cost</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
6148      <indexterm>
6149       <primary><varname>jit_above_cost</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6150      </indexterm>
6151      </term>
6152      <listitem>
6153       <para>
6154        Sets the query cost above which JIT compilation is activated, if
6155        enabled (see <xref linkend="jit"/>).
6156        Performing <acronym>JIT</acronym> costs planning time but can
6157        accelerate query execution.
6158        Setting this to <literal>-1</literal> disables JIT compilation.
6159        The default is <literal>100000</literal>.
6160       </para>
6161      </listitem>
6162     </varlistentry>
6163
6164     <varlistentry id="guc-jit-inline-above-cost" xreflabel="jit_inline_above_cost">
6165      <term><varname>jit_inline_above_cost</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
6166      <indexterm>
6167       <primary><varname>jit_inline_above_cost</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6168      </indexterm>
6169      </term>
6170      <listitem>
6171       <para>
6172        Sets the query cost above which JIT compilation attempts to inline
6173        functions and operators.  Inlining adds planning time, but can
6174        improve execution speed.  It is not meaningful to set this to less
6175        than <varname>jit_above_cost</varname>.
6176        Setting this to <literal>-1</literal> disables inlining.
6177        The default is <literal>500000</literal>.
6178       </para>
6179      </listitem>
6180     </varlistentry>
6181
6182     <varlistentry id="guc-jit-optimize-above-cost" xreflabel="jit_optimize_above_cost">
6183      <term><varname>jit_optimize_above_cost</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
6184      <indexterm>
6185       <primary><varname>jit_optimize_above_cost</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6186      </indexterm>
6187      </term>
6188      <listitem>
6189       <para>
6190        Sets the query cost above which JIT compilation applies expensive
6191        optimizations.  Such optimization adds planning time, but can improve
6192        execution speed.  It is not meaningful to set this to less
6193        than <varname>jit_above_cost</varname>, and it is unlikely to be
6194        beneficial to set it to more
6195        than <varname>jit_inline_above_cost</varname>.
6196        Setting this to <literal>-1</literal> disables expensive optimizations.
6197        The default is <literal>500000</literal>.
6198       </para>
6199      </listitem>
6200     </varlistentry>
6201
6202     </variablelist>
6203
6204    </sect2>
6205     <sect2 id="runtime-config-query-geqo">
6206     <title>Genetic Query Optimizer</title>
6207
6208     <para>
6209      The genetic query optimizer (GEQO) is an algorithm that does query
6210      planning using heuristic searching.  This reduces planning time for
6211      complex queries (those joining many relations), at the cost of producing
6212      plans that are sometimes inferior to those found by the normal
6213      exhaustive-search algorithm.
6214      For more information see <xref linkend="geqo"/>.
6215     </para>
6216
6217     <variablelist>
6218
6219     <varlistentry id="guc-geqo" xreflabel="geqo">
6220      <term><varname>geqo</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
6221      <indexterm>
6222       <primary>genetic query optimization</primary>
6223      </indexterm>
6224      <indexterm>
6225       <primary>GEQO</primary>
6226       <see>genetic query optimization</see>
6227      </indexterm>
6228      <indexterm>
6229       <primary><varname>geqo</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6230      </indexterm>
6231      </term>
6232      <listitem>
6233       <para>
6234        Enables or disables genetic query optimization.
6235        This is on by default.  It is usually best not to turn it off in
6236        production; the <varname>geqo_threshold</varname> variable provides
6237        more granular control of GEQO.
6238       </para>
6239      </listitem>
6240     </varlistentry>
6241
6242     <varlistentry id="guc-geqo-threshold" xreflabel="geqo_threshold">
6243      <term><varname>geqo_threshold</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
6244      <indexterm>
6245       <primary><varname>geqo_threshold</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6246      </indexterm>
6247      </term>
6248      <listitem>
6249       <para>
6250        Use genetic query optimization to plan queries with at least
6251        this many <literal>FROM</literal> items involved. (Note that a
6252        <literal>FULL OUTER JOIN</literal> construct counts as only one <literal>FROM</literal>
6253        item.) The default is 12. For simpler queries it is usually best
6254        to use the regular, exhaustive-search planner, but for queries with
6255        many tables the exhaustive search takes too long, often
6256        longer than the penalty of executing a suboptimal plan.  Thus,
6257        a threshold on the size of the query is a convenient way to manage
6258        use of GEQO.
6259       </para>
6260      </listitem>
6261     </varlistentry>
6262
6263     <varlistentry id="guc-geqo-effort" xreflabel="geqo_effort">
6264      <term><varname>geqo_effort</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
6265      <indexterm>
6266       <primary><varname>geqo_effort</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6267      </indexterm>
6268      </term>
6269      <listitem>
6270       <para>
6271        Controls the trade-off between planning time and query plan
6272        quality in GEQO. This variable must be an integer in the
6273        range from 1 to 10. The default value is five. Larger values
6274        increase the time spent doing query planning, but also
6275        increase the likelihood that an efficient query plan will be
6276        chosen.
6277       </para>
6278
6279       <para>
6280        <varname>geqo_effort</varname> doesn't actually do anything
6281        directly; it is only used to compute the default values for
6282        the other variables that influence GEQO behavior (described
6283        below). If you prefer, you can set the other parameters by
6284        hand instead.
6285       </para>
6286      </listitem>
6287     </varlistentry>
6288
6289     <varlistentry id="guc-geqo-pool-size" xreflabel="geqo_pool_size">
6290      <term><varname>geqo_pool_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
6291      <indexterm>
6292       <primary><varname>geqo_pool_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6293      </indexterm>
6294      </term>
6295      <listitem>
6296       <para>
6297        Controls the pool size used by GEQO, that is the
6298        number of individuals in the genetic population.  It must be
6299        at least two, and useful values are typically 100 to 1000.  If
6300        it is set to zero (the default setting) then a suitable
6301        value is chosen based on <varname>geqo_effort</varname> and
6302        the number of tables in the query.
6303       </para>
6304      </listitem>
6305     </varlistentry>
6306
6307     <varlistentry id="guc-geqo-generations" xreflabel="geqo_generations">
6308      <term><varname>geqo_generations</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
6309      <indexterm>
6310       <primary><varname>geqo_generations</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6311      </indexterm>
6312      </term>
6313      <listitem>
6314       <para>
6315        Controls the number of generations used by GEQO, that is
6316        the number of iterations of the algorithm.  It must
6317        be at least one, and useful values are in the same range as
6318        the pool size.  If it is set to zero (the default setting)
6319        then a suitable value is chosen based on
6320        <varname>geqo_pool_size</varname>.
6321       </para>
6322      </listitem>
6323     </varlistentry>
6324
6325     <varlistentry id="guc-geqo-selection-bias" xreflabel="geqo_selection_bias">
6326      <term><varname>geqo_selection_bias</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
6327      <indexterm>
6328       <primary><varname>geqo_selection_bias</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6329      </indexterm>
6330      </term>
6331      <listitem>
6332       <para>
6333        Controls the selection bias used by GEQO. The selection bias
6334        is the selective pressure within the population. Values can be
6335        from 1.50 to 2.00; the latter is the default.
6336       </para>
6337      </listitem>
6338     </varlistentry>
6339
6340     <varlistentry id="guc-geqo-seed" xreflabel="geqo_seed">
6341      <term><varname>geqo_seed</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
6342      <indexterm>
6343       <primary><varname>geqo_seed</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6344      </indexterm>
6345      </term>
6346      <listitem>
6347       <para>
6348        Controls the initial value of the random number generator used
6349        by GEQO to select random paths through the join order search space.
6350        The value can range from zero (the default) to one.  Varying the
6351        value changes the set of join paths explored, and may result in a
6352        better or worse best path being found.
6353       </para>
6354      </listitem>
6355     </varlistentry>
6356
6357     </variablelist>
6358    </sect2>
6359     <sect2 id="runtime-config-query-other">
6360     <title>Other Planner Options</title>
6361
6362     <variablelist>
6363
6364     <varlistentry id="guc-default-statistics-target" xreflabel="default_statistics_target">
6365      <term><varname>default_statistics_target</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
6366      <indexterm>
6367       <primary><varname>default_statistics_target</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6368      </indexterm>
6369      </term>
6370      <listitem>
6371       <para>
6372        Sets the default statistics target for table columns without
6373        a column-specific target set via <command>ALTER TABLE
6374        SET STATISTICS</command>.  Larger values increase the time needed to
6375        do <command>ANALYZE</command>, but might improve the quality of the
6376        planner's estimates. The default is 100. For more information
6377        on the use of statistics by the <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
6378        query planner, refer to <xref linkend="planner-stats"/>.
6379       </para>
6380      </listitem>
6381     </varlistentry>
6382
6383     <varlistentry id="guc-constraint-exclusion" xreflabel="constraint_exclusion">
6384      <term><varname>constraint_exclusion</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
6385      <indexterm>
6386       <primary>constraint exclusion</primary>
6387      </indexterm>
6388      <indexterm>
6389       <primary><varname>constraint_exclusion</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6390      </indexterm>
6391      </term>
6392      <listitem>
6393       <para>
6394        Controls the query planner's use of table constraints to
6395        optimize queries.
6396        The allowed values of <varname>constraint_exclusion</varname> are
6397        <literal>on</literal> (examine constraints for all tables),
6398        <literal>off</literal> (never examine constraints), and
6399        <literal>partition</literal> (examine constraints only for inheritance
6400        child tables and <literal>UNION ALL</literal> subqueries).
6401        <literal>partition</literal> is the default setting.
6402        It is often used with traditional inheritance trees to improve
6403        performance.
6404      </para>
6405
6406       <para>
6407        When this parameter allows it for a particular table, the planner
6408        compares query conditions with the table's <literal>CHECK</literal>
6409        constraints, and omits scanning tables for which the conditions
6410        contradict the constraints.  For example:
6411
6412<programlisting>
6413CREATE TABLE parent(key integer, ...);
6414CREATE TABLE child1000(check (key between 1000 and 1999)) INHERITS(parent);
6415CREATE TABLE child2000(check (key between 2000 and 2999)) INHERITS(parent);
6416...
6417SELECT * FROM parent WHERE key = 2400;
6418</programlisting>
6419
6420        With constraint exclusion enabled, this <command>SELECT</command>
6421        will not scan <structname>child1000</structname> at all, improving performance.
6422       </para>
6423
6424       <para>
6425        Currently, constraint exclusion is enabled by default
6426        only for cases that are often used to implement table partitioning via
6427        inheritance trees.  Turning it on for all tables imposes extra
6428        planning overhead that is quite noticeable on simple queries, and most
6429        often will yield no benefit for simple queries.  If you have no
6430        tables that are partitioned using traditional inheritance, you might
6431        prefer to turn it off entirely.  (Note that the equivalent feature for
6432        partitioned tables is controlled by a separate parameter,
6433        <xref linkend="guc-enable-partition-pruning"/>.)
6434       </para>
6435
6436       <para>
6437        Refer to <xref linkend="ddl-partitioning-constraint-exclusion"/> for
6438        more information on using constraint exclusion to implement
6439        partitioning.
6440       </para>
6441      </listitem>
6442     </varlistentry>
6443
6444     <varlistentry id="guc-cursor-tuple-fraction" xreflabel="cursor_tuple_fraction">
6445      <term><varname>cursor_tuple_fraction</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
6446      <indexterm>
6447       <primary><varname>cursor_tuple_fraction</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6448      </indexterm>
6449      </term>
6450      <listitem>
6451       <para>
6452        Sets the planner's estimate of the fraction of a cursor's rows that
6453        will be retrieved.  The default is 0.1.  Smaller values of this
6454        setting bias the planner towards using <quote>fast start</quote> plans
6455        for cursors, which will retrieve the first few rows quickly while
6456        perhaps taking a long time to fetch all rows.  Larger values
6457        put more emphasis on the total estimated time.  At the maximum
6458        setting of 1.0, cursors are planned exactly like regular queries,
6459        considering only the total estimated time and not how soon the
6460        first rows might be delivered.
6461       </para>
6462      </listitem>
6463     </varlistentry>
6464
6465     <varlistentry id="guc-from-collapse-limit" xreflabel="from_collapse_limit">
6466      <term><varname>from_collapse_limit</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
6467      <indexterm>
6468       <primary><varname>from_collapse_limit</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6469      </indexterm>
6470      </term>
6471      <listitem>
6472       <para>
6473        The planner will merge sub-queries into upper queries if the
6474        resulting <literal>FROM</literal> list would have no more than
6475        this many items.  Smaller values reduce planning time but might
6476        yield inferior query plans.  The default is eight.
6477        For more information see <xref linkend="explicit-joins"/>.
6478       </para>
6479
6480       <para>
6481        Setting this value to <xref linkend="guc-geqo-threshold"/> or more
6482        may trigger use of the GEQO planner, resulting in non-optimal
6483        plans.  See <xref linkend="runtime-config-query-geqo"/>.
6484       </para>
6485      </listitem>
6486     </varlistentry>
6487
6488     <varlistentry id="guc-jit" xreflabel="jit">
6489      <term><varname>jit</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
6490      <indexterm>
6491       <primary><varname>jit</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6492      </indexterm>
6493      </term>
6494      <listitem>
6495       <para>
6496        Determines whether <acronym>JIT</acronym> compilation may be used by
6497        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>, if available (see <xref
6498        linkend="jit"/>).
6499        The default is <literal>on</literal>.
6500       </para>
6501      </listitem>
6502     </varlistentry>
6503
6504     <varlistentry id="guc-join-collapse-limit" xreflabel="join_collapse_limit">
6505      <term><varname>join_collapse_limit</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
6506      <indexterm>
6507       <primary><varname>join_collapse_limit</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6508      </indexterm>
6509      </term>
6510      <listitem>
6511       <para>
6512        The planner will rewrite explicit <literal>JOIN</literal>
6513        constructs (except <literal>FULL JOIN</literal>s) into lists of
6514        <literal>FROM</literal> items whenever a list of no more than this many items
6515        would result.  Smaller values reduce planning time but might
6516        yield inferior query plans.
6517       </para>
6518
6519       <para>
6520        By default, this variable is set the same as
6521        <varname>from_collapse_limit</varname>, which is appropriate
6522        for most uses. Setting it to 1 prevents any reordering of
6523        explicit <literal>JOIN</literal>s. Thus, the explicit join order
6524        specified in the query will be the actual order in which the
6525        relations are joined. Because the query planner does not always choose
6526        the optimal join order, advanced users can elect to
6527        temporarily set this variable to 1, and then specify the join
6528        order they desire explicitly.
6529        For more information see <xref linkend="explicit-joins"/>.
6530       </para>
6531
6532       <para>
6533        Setting this value to <xref linkend="guc-geqo-threshold"/> or more
6534        may trigger use of the GEQO planner, resulting in non-optimal
6535        plans.  See <xref linkend="runtime-config-query-geqo"/>.
6536       </para>
6537      </listitem>
6538     </varlistentry>
6539
6540     <varlistentry id="guc-plan-cache-mode" xreflabel="plan_cache_mode">
6541      <term><varname>plan_cache_mode</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
6542      <indexterm>
6543       <primary><varname>plan_cache_mode</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6544      </indexterm>
6545      </term>
6546      <listitem>
6547       <para>
6548        Prepared statements (either explicitly prepared or implicitly
6549        generated, for example by PL/pgSQL) can be executed using custom or
6550        generic plans.  Custom plans are made afresh for each execution
6551        using its specific set of parameter values, while generic plans do
6552        not rely on the parameter values and can be re-used across
6553        executions.  Thus, use of a generic plan saves planning time, but if
6554        the ideal plan depends strongly on the parameter values then a
6555        generic plan may be inefficient.  The choice between these options
6556        is normally made automatically, but it can be overridden
6557        with <varname>plan_cache_mode</varname>.
6558        The allowed values are <literal>auto</literal> (the default),
6559        <literal>force_custom_plan</literal> and
6560        <literal>force_generic_plan</literal>.
6561        This setting is considered when a cached plan is to be executed,
6562        not when it is prepared.
6563        For more information see <xref linkend="sql-prepare"/>.
6564       </para>
6565      </listitem>
6566     </varlistentry>
6567
6568     <varlistentry id="guc-recursive-worktable-factor" xreflabel="recursive_worktable_factor">
6569      <term><varname>recursive_worktable_factor</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
6570      <indexterm>
6571       <primary><varname>recursive_worktable_factor</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6572      </indexterm>
6573      </term>
6574      <listitem>
6575       <para>
6576        Sets the planner's estimate of the average size of the working
6577        table of a <link linkend="queries-with-recursive">recursive
6578        query</link>, as a multiple of the estimated size of the initial
