6579        non-recursive term of the query.  This helps the planner choose
6580        the most appropriate method for joining the working table to the
6581        query's other tables.
6582        The default value is <literal>10.0</literal>.  A smaller value
6583        such as <literal>1.0</literal> can be helpful when the recursion
6584        has low <quote>fan-out</quote> from one step to the next, as for
6585        example in shortest-path queries.  Graph analytics queries may
6586        benefit from larger-than-default values.
6587       </para>
6588      </listitem>
6589     </varlistentry>
6590
6591     </variablelist>
6592    </sect2>
6593   </sect1>
6594
6595   <sect1 id="runtime-config-logging">
6596    <title>Error Reporting and Logging</title>
6597
6598    <indexterm zone="runtime-config-logging">
6599     <primary>server log</primary>
6600    </indexterm>
6601
6602    <sect2 id="runtime-config-logging-where">
6603     <title>Where to Log</title>
6604
6605     <indexterm zone="runtime-config-logging-where">
6606      <primary>where to log</primary>
6607     </indexterm>
6608
6609     <indexterm>
6610       <primary>current_logfiles</primary>
6611       <secondary>and the log_destination configuration parameter</secondary>
6612     </indexterm>
6613
6614     <variablelist>
6615
6616     <varlistentry id="guc-log-destination" xreflabel="log_destination">
6617      <term><varname>log_destination</varname> (<type>string</type>)
6618      <indexterm>
6619       <primary><varname>log_destination</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6620      </indexterm>
6621      </term>
6622      <listitem>
6623       <para>
6624        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> supports several methods
6625         for logging server messages, including
6626         <systemitem>stderr</systemitem>, <systemitem>csvlog</systemitem>,
6627         <systemitem>jsonlog</systemitem>, and
6628         <systemitem>syslog</systemitem>. On Windows,
6629         <systemitem>eventlog</systemitem> is also supported. Set this
6630         parameter to a list of desired log destinations separated by
6631         commas. The default is to log to <systemitem>stderr</systemitem>
6632         only.
6633         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
6634         file or on the server command line.
6635       </para>
6636       <para>
6637        If <systemitem>csvlog</systemitem> is included in <varname>log_destination</varname>,
6638        log entries are output in <quote>comma-separated
6639        value</quote> (<acronym>CSV</acronym>) format, which is convenient for
6640        loading logs into programs.
6641        See <xref linkend="runtime-config-logging-csvlog"/> for details.
6642        <xref linkend="guc-logging-collector"/> must be enabled to generate
6643        CSV-format log output.
6644       </para>
6645       <para>
6646        If <systemitem>jsonlog</systemitem> is included in
6647        <varname>log_destination</varname>, log entries are output in
6648        <acronym>JSON</acronym> format, which is convenient for loading logs
6649        into programs.
6650        See <xref linkend="runtime-config-logging-jsonlog"/> for details.
6651        <xref linkend="guc-logging-collector"/> must be enabled to generate
6652        JSON-format log output.
6653       </para>
6654       <para>
6655        When either <systemitem>stderr</systemitem>,
6656        <systemitem>csvlog</systemitem> or <systemitem>jsonlog</systemitem> are
6657        included, the file <filename>current_logfiles</filename> is created to
6658        record the location of the log file(s) currently in use by the logging
6659        collector and the associated logging destination. This provides a
6660        convenient way to find the logs currently in use by the instance. Here
6661        is an example of this file's content:
6662<programlisting>
6663stderr log/postgresql.log
6664csvlog log/postgresql.csv
6665jsonlog log/postgresql.json
6666</programlisting>
6667
6668        <filename>current_logfiles</filename> is recreated when a new log file
6669        is created as an effect of rotation, and
6670        when <varname>log_destination</varname> is reloaded.  It is removed when
6671        none of <systemitem>stderr</systemitem>,
6672        <systemitem>csvlog</systemitem> or <systemitem>jsonlog</systemitem> are
6673        included in <varname>log_destination</varname>, and when the logging
6674        collector is disabled.
6675       </para>
6676
6677       <note>
6678        <para>
6679         On most Unix systems, you will need to alter the configuration of
6680         your system's <application>syslog</application> daemon in order
6681         to make use of the <systemitem>syslog</systemitem> option for
6682         <varname>log_destination</varname>.  <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
6683         can log to <application>syslog</application> facilities
6684         <literal>LOCAL0</literal> through <literal>LOCAL7</literal> (see <xref
6685         linkend="guc-syslog-facility"/>), but the default
6686         <application>syslog</application> configuration on most platforms
6687         will discard all such messages.  You will need to add something like:
6688<programlisting>
6689local0.*    /var/log/postgresql
6690</programlisting>
6691         to the  <application>syslog</application> daemon's configuration file
6692         to make it work.
6693        </para>
6694        <para>
6695         On Windows, when you use the <literal>eventlog</literal>
6696         option for <varname>log_destination</varname>, you should
6697         register an event source and its library with the operating
6698         system so that the Windows Event Viewer can display event
6699         log messages cleanly.
6700         See <xref linkend="event-log-registration"/> for details.
6701        </para>
6702       </note>
6703      </listitem>
6704     </varlistentry>
6705
6706     <varlistentry id="guc-logging-collector" xreflabel="logging_collector">
6707      <term><varname>logging_collector</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
6708      <indexterm>
6709       <primary><varname>logging_collector</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6710      </indexterm>
6711      </term>
6712      <listitem>
6713       <para>
6714         This parameter enables the <firstterm>logging collector</firstterm>, which
6715         is a background process that captures log messages
6716         sent to <systemitem>stderr</systemitem> and redirects them into log files.
6717         This approach is often more useful than
6718         logging to <application>syslog</application>, since some types of messages
6719         might not appear in <application>syslog</application> output.  (One common
6720         example is dynamic-linker failure messages; another is error messages
6721         produced by scripts such as <varname>archive_command</varname>.)
6722         This parameter can only be set at server start.
6723       </para>
6724
6725       <note>
6726        <para>
6727         It is possible to log to <systemitem>stderr</systemitem> without using the
6728         logging collector; the log messages will just go to wherever the
6729         server's <systemitem>stderr</systemitem> is directed.  However, that method is
6730         only suitable for low log volumes, since it provides no convenient
6731         way to rotate log files.  Also, on some platforms not using the
6732         logging collector can result in lost or garbled log output, because
6733         multiple processes writing concurrently to the same log file can
6734         overwrite each other's output.
6735        </para>
6736       </note>
6737
6738       <note>
6739        <para>
6740          The logging collector is designed to never lose messages.  This means
6741          that in case of extremely high load, server processes could be
6742          blocked while trying to send additional log messages when the
6743          collector has fallen behind.  In contrast, <application>syslog</application>
6744          prefers to drop messages if it cannot write them, which means it
6745          may fail to log some messages in such cases but it will not block
6746          the rest of the system.
6747        </para>
6748       </note>
6749
6750      </listitem>
6751     </varlistentry>
6752
6753     <varlistentry id="guc-log-directory" xreflabel="log_directory">
6754      <term><varname>log_directory</varname> (<type>string</type>)
6755      <indexterm>
6756       <primary><varname>log_directory</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6757      </indexterm>
6758      </term>
6759      <listitem>
6760       <para>
6761        When <varname>logging_collector</varname> is enabled,
6762        this parameter determines the directory in which log files will be created.
6763        It can be specified as an absolute path, or relative to the
6764        cluster data directory.
6765        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
6766        file or on the server command line.
6767        The default is <literal>log</literal>.
6768       </para>
6769      </listitem>
6770     </varlistentry>
6771
6772     <varlistentry id="guc-log-filename" xreflabel="log_filename">
6773      <term><varname>log_filename</varname> (<type>string</type>)
6774      <indexterm>
6775       <primary><varname>log_filename</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6776      </indexterm>
6777      </term>
6778      <listitem>
6779       <para>
6780        When <varname>logging_collector</varname> is enabled,
6781        this parameter sets the file names of the created log files.  The value
6782        is treated as a <function>strftime</function> pattern,
6783        so <literal>%</literal>-escapes can be used to specify time-varying
6784        file names.  (Note that if there are
6785        any time-zone-dependent <literal>%</literal>-escapes, the computation
6786        is done in the zone specified
6787        by <xref linkend="guc-log-timezone"/>.)
6788        The supported <literal>%</literal>-escapes are similar to those
6789        listed in the Open Group's <ulink
6790        url="https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/009695399/functions/strftime.html">strftime
6791        </ulink> specification.
6792        Note that the system's <function>strftime</function> is not used
6793        directly, so platform-specific (nonstandard) extensions do not work.
6794        The default is <literal>postgresql-%Y-%m-%d_%H%M%S.log</literal>.
6795       </para>
6796       <para>
6797        If you specify a file name without escapes, you should plan to
6798        use a log rotation utility to avoid eventually filling the
6799        entire disk.  In releases prior to 8.4, if
6800        no <literal>%</literal> escapes were
6801        present, <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> would append
6802        the epoch of the new log file's creation time, but this is no
6803        longer the case.
6804       </para>
6805       <para>
6806        If CSV-format output is enabled in <varname>log_destination</varname>,
6807        <literal>.csv</literal> will be appended to the timestamped
6808        log file name to create the file name for CSV-format output.
6809        (If <varname>log_filename</varname> ends in <literal>.log</literal>, the suffix is
6810        replaced instead.)
6811       </para>
6812       <para>
6813        If JSON-format output is enabled in <varname>log_destination</varname>,
6814        <literal>.json</literal> will be appended to the timestamped
6815        log file name to create the file name for JSON-format output.
6816        (If <varname>log_filename</varname> ends in <literal>.log</literal>, the suffix is
6817        replaced instead.)
6818       </para>
6819       <para>
6820        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
6821        file or on the server command line.
6822       </para>
6823      </listitem>
6824     </varlistentry>
6825
6826     <varlistentry id="guc-log-file-mode" xreflabel="log_file_mode">
6827      <term><varname>log_file_mode</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
6828      <indexterm>
6829       <primary><varname>log_file_mode</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6830      </indexterm>
6831      </term>
6832      <listitem>
6833       <para>
6834        On Unix systems this parameter sets the permissions for log files
6835        when <varname>logging_collector</varname> is enabled. (On Microsoft
6836        Windows this parameter is ignored.)
6837        The parameter value is expected to be a numeric mode
6838        specified in the format accepted by the
6839        <function>chmod</function> and <function>umask</function>
6840        system calls.  (To use the customary octal format the number
6841        must start with a <literal>0</literal> (zero).)
6842       </para>
6843       <para>
6844        The default permissions are <literal>0600</literal>, meaning only the
6845        server owner can read or write the log files.  The other commonly
6846        useful setting is <literal>0640</literal>, allowing members of the owner's
6847        group to read the files.  Note however that to make use of such a
6848        setting, you'll need to alter <xref linkend="guc-log-directory"/> to
6849        store the files somewhere outside the cluster data directory.  In
6850        any case, it's unwise to make the log files world-readable, since
6851        they might contain sensitive data.
6852       </para>
6853       <para>
6854        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
6855        file or on the server command line.
6856       </para>
6857      </listitem>
6858     </varlistentry>
6859
6860     <varlistentry id="guc-log-rotation-age" xreflabel="log_rotation_age">
6861      <term><varname>log_rotation_age</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
6862      <indexterm>
6863       <primary><varname>log_rotation_age</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6864      </indexterm>
6865      </term>
6866      <listitem>
6867       <para>
6868        When <varname>logging_collector</varname> is enabled,
6869        this parameter determines the maximum amount of time to use an
6870        individual log file, after which a new log file will be created.
6871        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as minutes.
6872        The default is 24 hours.
6873        Set to zero to disable time-based creation of new log files.
6874        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
6875        file or on the server command line.
6876       </para>
6877      </listitem>
6878     </varlistentry>
6879
6880     <varlistentry id="guc-log-rotation-size" xreflabel="log_rotation_size">
6881      <term><varname>log_rotation_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
6882      <indexterm>
6883       <primary><varname>log_rotation_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6884      </indexterm>
6885      </term>
6886      <listitem>
6887       <para>
6888        When <varname>logging_collector</varname> is enabled,
6889        this parameter determines the maximum size of an individual log file.
6890        After this amount of data has been emitted into a log file,
6891        a new log file will be created.
6892        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as kilobytes.
6893        The default is 10 megabytes.
6894        Set to zero to disable size-based creation of new log files.
6895        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
6896        file or on the server command line.
6897       </para>
6898      </listitem>
6899     </varlistentry>
6900
6901     <varlistentry id="guc-log-truncate-on-rotation" xreflabel="log_truncate_on_rotation">
6902      <term><varname>log_truncate_on_rotation</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
6903      <indexterm>
6904       <primary><varname>log_truncate_on_rotation</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6905      </indexterm>
6906      </term>
6907      <listitem>
6908       <para>
6909        When <varname>logging_collector</varname> is enabled,
6910        this parameter will cause <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> to truncate (overwrite),
6911        rather than append to, any existing log file of the same name.
6912        However, truncation will occur only when a new file is being opened
6913        due to time-based rotation, not during server startup or size-based
6914        rotation.  When off, pre-existing files will be appended to in
6915        all cases.  For example, using this setting in combination with
6916        a <varname>log_filename</varname> like <literal>postgresql-%H.log</literal>
6917        would result in generating twenty-four hourly log files and then
6918        cyclically overwriting them.
6919        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
6920        file or on the server command line.
6921       </para>
6922       <para>
6923        Example:  To keep 7 days of logs, one log file per day named
6924        <literal>server_log.Mon</literal>, <literal>server_log.Tue</literal>,
6925        etc., and automatically overwrite last week's log with this week's log,
6926        set <varname>log_filename</varname> to <literal>server_log.%a</literal>,
6927        <varname>log_truncate_on_rotation</varname> to <literal>on</literal>, and
6928        <varname>log_rotation_age</varname> to <literal>1440</literal>.
6929       </para>
6930       <para>
6931        Example: To keep 24 hours of logs, one log file per hour, but
6932        also rotate sooner if the log file size exceeds 1GB, set
6933        <varname>log_filename</varname> to <literal>server_log.%H%M</literal>,
6934        <varname>log_truncate_on_rotation</varname> to <literal>on</literal>,
6935        <varname>log_rotation_age</varname> to <literal>60</literal>, and
6936        <varname>log_rotation_size</varname> to <literal>1000000</literal>.
6937        Including <literal>%M</literal> in <varname>log_filename</varname> allows
6938        any size-driven rotations that might occur to select a file name
6939        different from the hour's initial file name.
6940       </para>
6941      </listitem>
6942     </varlistentry>
6943
6944     <varlistentry id="guc-syslog-facility" xreflabel="syslog_facility">
6945      <term><varname>syslog_facility</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
6946      <indexterm>
6947       <primary><varname>syslog_facility</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6948      </indexterm>
6949      </term>
6950      <listitem>
6951       <para>
6952        When logging to <application>syslog</application> is enabled, this parameter
6953        determines the <application>syslog</application>
6954        <quote>facility</quote> to be used.  You can choose
6955        from <literal>LOCAL0</literal>, <literal>LOCAL1</literal>,
6956        <literal>LOCAL2</literal>, <literal>LOCAL3</literal>, <literal>LOCAL4</literal>,
6957        <literal>LOCAL5</literal>, <literal>LOCAL6</literal>, <literal>LOCAL7</literal>;
6958        the default is <literal>LOCAL0</literal>. See also the
6959        documentation of your system's
6960        <application>syslog</application> daemon.
6961        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
6962        file or on the server command line.
6963       </para>
6964      </listitem>
6965     </varlistentry>
6966
6967     <varlistentry id="guc-syslog-ident" xreflabel="syslog_ident">
6968      <term><varname>syslog_ident</varname> (<type>string</type>)
6969      <indexterm>
6970       <primary><varname>syslog_ident</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6971      </indexterm>
6972      </term>
6973       <listitem>
6974        <para>
6975         When logging to <application>syslog</application> is enabled, this parameter
6976         determines the program name used to identify
6977         <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> messages in
6978         <application>syslog</application> logs. The default is
6979         <literal>postgres</literal>.
6980         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
6981         file or on the server command line.
6982        </para>
6983       </listitem>
6984      </varlistentry>
6985
6986      <varlistentry id="guc-syslog-sequence-numbers" xreflabel="syslog_sequence_numbers">
6987       <term><varname>syslog_sequence_numbers</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
6988        <indexterm>
6989         <primary><varname>syslog_sequence_numbers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
6990        </indexterm>
6991       </term>
6992
6993       <listitem>
6994        <para>
6995         When logging to <application>syslog</application> and this is on (the
6996         default), then each message will be prefixed by an increasing
6997         sequence number (such as <literal>[2]</literal>).  This circumvents
6998         the <quote>--- last message repeated N times ---</quote> suppression
6999         that many syslog implementations perform by default.  In more modern
7000         syslog implementations, repeated message suppression can be configured
7001         (for example, <literal>$RepeatedMsgReduction</literal>
7002         in <productname>rsyslog</productname>), so this might not be
7003         necessary.  Also, you could turn this off if you actually want to
7004         suppress repeated messages.
7005        </para>
7006
7007        <para>
7008         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
7009         file or on the server command line.
7010        </para>
7011      </listitem>
7012     </varlistentry>
7013
7014     <varlistentry id="guc-syslog-split-messages" xreflabel="syslog_split_messages">
7015      <term><varname>syslog_split_messages</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
7016      <indexterm>
7017       <primary><varname>syslog_split_messages</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7018      </indexterm>
7019      </term>
7020      <listitem>
7021       <para>
7022        When logging to <application>syslog</application> is enabled, this parameter
7023        determines how messages are delivered to syslog.  When on (the
7024        default), messages are split by lines, and long lines are split so
7025        that they will fit into 1024 bytes, which is a typical size limit for
7026        traditional syslog implementations.  When off, PostgreSQL server log
7027        messages are delivered to the syslog service as is, and it is up to
7028        the syslog service to cope with the potentially bulky messages.
7029       </para>
7030
7031       <para>
7032        If syslog is ultimately logging to a text file, then the effect will
7033        be the same either way, and it is best to leave the setting on, since
7034        most syslog implementations either cannot handle large messages or
7035        would need to be specially configured to handle them.  But if syslog
7036        is ultimately writing into some other medium, it might be necessary or
7037        more useful to keep messages logically together.
7038       </para>
7039
7040       <para>
7041        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
7042        file or on the server command line.
7043       </para>
7044      </listitem>
7045     </varlistentry>
7046
7047     <varlistentry id="guc-event-source" xreflabel="event_source">
7048      <term><varname>event_source</varname> (<type>string</type>)
7049      <indexterm>
7050       <primary><varname>event_source</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7051      </indexterm>
7052      </term>
7053      <listitem>
7054       <para>
7055        When logging to <application>event log</application> is enabled, this parameter
7056        determines the program name used to identify
7057        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> messages in
7058        the log. The default is <literal>PostgreSQL</literal>.
7059        This parameter can only be set at server start.
7060       </para>
7061      </listitem>
7062     </varlistentry>
7063
7064      </variablelist>
7065    </sect2>
7066     <sect2 id="runtime-config-logging-when">
7067     <title>When to Log</title>
7068
7069     <variablelist>
7070
7071     <varlistentry id="guc-log-min-messages" xreflabel="log_min_messages">
7072      <term><varname>log_min_messages</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
7073      <indexterm>
7074       <primary><varname>log_min_messages</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7075      </indexterm>
7076      </term>
7077      <listitem>
7078       <para>
7079        Controls which <link linkend="runtime-config-severity-levels">message
7080        levels</link> are written to the server log.
7081        Valid values are <literal>DEBUG5</literal>, <literal>DEBUG4</literal>,
7082        <literal>DEBUG3</literal>, <literal>DEBUG2</literal>, <literal>DEBUG1</literal>,
7083        <literal>INFO</literal>, <literal>NOTICE</literal>, <literal>WARNING</literal>,
7084        <literal>ERROR</literal>, <literal>LOG</literal>, <literal>FATAL</literal>, and
7085        <literal>PANIC</literal>.  Each level includes all the levels that
7086        follow it.  The later the level, the fewer messages are sent
7087        to the log.  The default is <literal>WARNING</literal>.  Note that
7088        <literal>LOG</literal> has a different rank here than in
7089        <xref linkend="guc-client-min-messages"/>.
7090        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
7091        privilege can change this setting.
7092       </para>
7093      </listitem>
7094     </varlistentry>
7095
7096     <varlistentry id="guc-log-min-error-statement" xreflabel="log_min_error_statement">
7097      <term><varname>log_min_error_statement</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
7098      <indexterm>
7099       <primary><varname>log_min_error_statement</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7100      </indexterm>
7101      </term>
7102      <listitem>
7103       <para>
7104        Controls which SQL statements that cause an error
7105        condition are recorded in the server log.  The current
7106        SQL statement is included in the log entry for any message of
7107        the specified
7108        <link linkend="runtime-config-severity-levels">severity</link>
7109        or higher.
7110        Valid values are <literal>DEBUG5</literal>,
7111        <literal>DEBUG4</literal>, <literal>DEBUG3</literal>,
7112        <literal>DEBUG2</literal>, <literal>DEBUG1</literal>,
7113        <literal>INFO</literal>, <literal>NOTICE</literal>,
7114        <literal>WARNING</literal>, <literal>ERROR</literal>,
7115        <literal>LOG</literal>,
7116        <literal>FATAL</literal>, and <literal>PANIC</literal>.
7117        The default is <literal>ERROR</literal>, which means statements
7118        causing errors, log messages, fatal errors, or panics will be logged.
7119        To effectively turn off logging of failing statements,
7120        set this parameter to <literal>PANIC</literal>.
7121        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
7122        privilege can change this setting.
7123       </para>
7124      </listitem>
7125     </varlistentry>
7126
7127     <varlistentry id="guc-log-min-duration-statement" xreflabel="log_min_duration_statement">
7128      <term><varname>log_min_duration_statement</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
7129      <indexterm>
7130       <primary><varname>log_min_duration_statement</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7131      </indexterm>
7132      </term>
7133       <listitem>
7134        <para>
7135         Causes the duration of each completed statement to be logged
7136         if the statement ran for at least the specified amount of time.
7137         For example, if you set it to <literal>250ms</literal>
7138         then all SQL statements that run 250ms or longer will be
7139         logged.  Enabling this parameter can be helpful in tracking down
7140         unoptimized queries in your applications.
7141         If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
7142         Setting this to zero prints all statement durations.
7143         <literal>-1</literal> (the default) disables logging statement
7144         durations.
7145         Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
7146         privilege can change this setting.
7147        </para>
7148
7149        <para>
7150         This overrides <xref linkend="guc-log-min-duration-sample"/>,
7151         meaning that queries with duration exceeding this setting are not
7152         subject to sampling and are always logged.
7153        </para>
7154
7155        <para>
7156         For clients using extended query protocol, durations of the Parse,
7157         Bind, and Execute steps are logged independently.
7158        </para>
7159
7160       <note>
7161        <para>
7162         When using this option together with
7163         <xref linkend="guc-log-statement"/>,
7164         the text of statements that are logged because of
7165         <varname>log_statement</varname> will not be repeated in the
7166         duration log message.
7167         If you are not using <application>syslog</application>, it is recommended
7168         that you log the PID or session ID using
7169         <xref linkend="guc-log-line-prefix"/>
7170         so that you can link the statement message to the later
7171         duration message using the process ID or session ID.
7172        </para>
7173       </note>
7174       </listitem>
7175      </varlistentry>
7176
7177     <varlistentry id="guc-log-min-duration-sample" xreflabel="log_min_duration_sample">
7178      <term><varname>log_min_duration_sample</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
7179      <indexterm>
7180       <primary><varname>log_min_duration_sample</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7181      </indexterm>
7182      </term>
7183       <listitem>
7184        <para>
7185         Allows sampling the duration of completed statements that ran for
7186         at least the specified amount of time.  This produces the same
7187         kind of log entries as
7188         <xref linkend="guc-log-min-duration-statement"/>, but only for a
7189         subset of the executed statements, with sample rate controlled by
7190         <xref linkend="guc-log-statement-sample-rate"/>.
7191         For example, if you set it to <literal>100ms</literal> then all
7192         SQL statements that run 100ms or longer will be considered for
7193         sampling.  Enabling this parameter can be helpful when the
7194         traffic is too high to log all queries.
7195         If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
7196         Setting this to zero samples all statement durations.
7197         <literal>-1</literal> (the default) disables sampling statement
7198         durations.
7199         Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
7200         privilege can change this setting.
7201        </para>
7202
7203        <para>
7204         This setting has lower priority
7205         than <varname>log_min_duration_statement</varname>, meaning that
7206         statements with durations
7207         exceeding <varname>log_min_duration_statement</varname> are not
7208         subject to sampling and are always logged.
7209        </para>
7210
7211        <para>
7212         Other notes for <varname>log_min_duration_statement</varname>
7213         apply also to this setting.
7214        </para>
7215       </listitem>
7216     </varlistentry>
7217
7218     <varlistentry id="guc-log-statement-sample-rate" xreflabel="log_statement_sample_rate">
7219      <term><varname>log_statement_sample_rate</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
7220      <indexterm>
7221       <primary><varname>log_statement_sample_rate</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7222      </indexterm>
7223      </term>
7224       <listitem>
7225        <para>
7226         Determines the fraction of statements with duration exceeding
7227         <xref linkend="guc-log-min-duration-sample"/> that will be logged.
7228         Sampling is stochastic, for example <literal>0.5</literal> means
7229         there is statistically one chance in two that any given statement
7230         will be logged.
7231         The default is <literal>1.0</literal>, meaning to log all sampled
7232         statements.
7233         Setting this to zero disables sampled statement-duration logging,
7234         the same as setting
7235         <varname>log_min_duration_sample</varname> to
7236         <literal>-1</literal>.
7237         Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
7238         privilege can change this setting.
7239        </para>
7240       </listitem>
7241     </varlistentry>
7242
7243     <varlistentry id="guc-log-transaction-sample-rate" xreflabel="log_transaction_sample_rate">
7244      <term><varname>log_transaction_sample_rate</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
7245      <indexterm>
7246       <primary><varname>log_transaction_sample_rate</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7247      </indexterm>
7248      </term>
7249       <listitem>
7250        <para>
7251         Sets the fraction of transactions whose statements are all logged,
7252         in addition to statements logged for other reasons.  It applies to
7253         each new transaction regardless of its statements' durations.
7254         Sampling is stochastic, for example <literal>0.1</literal> means
7255         there is statistically one chance in ten that any given transaction
7256         will be logged.
7257         <varname>log_transaction_sample_rate</varname> can be helpful to
7258         construct a sample of transactions.
7259         The default is <literal>0</literal>, meaning not to log
7260         statements from any additional transactions.  Setting this
7261         to <literal>1</literal> logs all statements of all transactions.
7262         Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
7263         privilege can change this setting.
7264        </para>
7265       <note>
7266        <para>
7267         Like all statement-logging options, this option can add significant
7268         overhead.
7269        </para>
7270       </note>
7271       </listitem>
7272     </varlistentry>
7273
7274     <varlistentry id="guc-log-startup-progress-interval" xreflabel="log_startup_progress_interval">
7275      <term><varname>log_startup_progress_interval</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
7276      <indexterm>
7277       <primary><varname>log_startup_progress_interval</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7278      </indexterm>
7279      </term>
7280       <listitem>
7281        <para>
7282         Sets the amount of time after which the startup process will log
7283         a message about a long-running operation that is still in progress,
7284         as well as the interval between further progress messages for that
7285         operation. The default is 10 seconds. A setting of <literal>0</literal>
7286         disables the feature.  If this value is specified without units,
7287         it is taken as milliseconds.  This setting is applied separately to
7288         each operation.
7289         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
7290         file or on the server command line.
7291        </para>
7292
7293        <para>
7294         For example, if syncing the data directory takes 25 seconds and
7295         thereafter resetting unlogged relations takes 8 seconds, and if this
7296         setting has the default value of 10 seconds, then a messages will be
7297         logged for syncing the data directory after it has been in progress
7298         for 10 seconds and again after it has been in progress for 20 seconds,
7299         but nothing will be logged for resetting unlogged relations.
7300        </para>
7301       </listitem>
7302     </varlistentry>
7303
7304     </variablelist>
7305
7306    <para>
7307     <xref linkend="runtime-config-severity-levels"/> explains the message
7308     severity levels used by <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>.  If logging output
7309     is sent to <systemitem>syslog</systemitem> or Windows'
7310     <systemitem>eventlog</systemitem>, the severity levels are translated
7311     as shown in the table.
7312    </para>
7313
7314    <table id="runtime-config-severity-levels">
7315     <title>Message Severity Levels</title>
7316     <tgroup cols="4">
7317      <colspec colname="col1" colwidth="1*"/>
7318      <colspec colname="col2" colwidth="2*"/>
7319      <colspec colname="col3" colwidth="1*"/>
7320      <colspec colname="col4" colwidth="1*"/>
7321      <thead>
7322       <row>
7323        <entry>Severity</entry>
7324        <entry>Usage</entry>
7325        <entry><systemitem>syslog</systemitem></entry>
7326        <entry><systemitem>eventlog</systemitem></entry>
7327       </row>
7328      </thead>
7329
7330      <tbody>
7331       <row>
7332        <entry><literal>DEBUG1 .. DEBUG5</literal></entry>
7333        <entry>Provides successively-more-detailed information for use by
7334         developers.</entry>
7335        <entry><literal>DEBUG</literal></entry>
7336        <entry><literal>INFORMATION</literal></entry>
7337       </row>
7338
7339       <row>
7340        <entry><literal>INFO</literal></entry>
7341        <entry>Provides information implicitly requested by the user,
7342         e.g., output from <command>VACUUM VERBOSE</command>.</entry>
7343        <entry><literal>INFO</literal></entry>
7344        <entry><literal>INFORMATION</literal></entry>
7345       </row>
7346
7347       <row>
7348        <entry><literal>NOTICE</literal></entry>
7349        <entry>Provides information that might be helpful to users, e.g.,
7350         notice of truncation of long identifiers.</entry>
7351        <entry><literal>NOTICE</literal></entry>
7352        <entry><literal>INFORMATION</literal></entry>
7353       </row>
7354
7355       <row>
7356        <entry><literal>WARNING</literal></entry>
7357        <entry>Provides warnings of likely problems, e.g., <command>COMMIT</command>
7358         outside a transaction block.</entry>
7359        <entry><literal>NOTICE</literal></entry>
7360        <entry><literal>WARNING</literal></entry>
7361       </row>
7362
7363       <row>
7364        <entry><literal>ERROR</literal></entry>
7365        <entry>Reports an error that caused the current command to
7366         abort.</entry>
7367        <entry><literal>WARNING</literal></entry>
7368        <entry><literal>ERROR</literal></entry>
7369       </row>
7370
7371       <row>
7372        <entry><literal>LOG</literal></entry>
7373        <entry>Reports information of interest to administrators, e.g.,
7374         checkpoint activity.</entry>
7375        <entry><literal>INFO</literal></entry>
7376        <entry><literal>INFORMATION</literal></entry>
7377       </row>
7378
7379       <row>
7380        <entry><literal>FATAL</literal></entry>
7381        <entry>Reports an error that caused the current session to
7382         abort.</entry>
7383        <entry><literal>ERR</literal></entry>
7384        <entry><literal>ERROR</literal></entry>
7385       </row>
7386
7387       <row>
7388        <entry><literal>PANIC</literal></entry>
7389        <entry>Reports an error that caused all database sessions to abort.</entry>
7390        <entry><literal>CRIT</literal></entry>
7391        <entry><literal>ERROR</literal></entry>
7392       </row>
7393      </tbody>
7394     </tgroup>
7395    </table>
7396
7397    </sect2>
7398     <sect2 id="runtime-config-logging-what">
7399     <title>What to Log</title>
7400
7401     <note>
7402      <para>
7403       What you choose to log can have security implications;  see
7404       <xref linkend="logfile-maintenance"/>.
7405      </para>
7406     </note>
7407
7408     <variablelist>
7409
7410     <varlistentry id="guc-application-name" xreflabel="application_name">
7411      <term><varname>application_name</varname> (<type>string</type>)
7412      <indexterm>
7413       <primary><varname>application_name</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7414      </indexterm>
7415      </term>
7416      <listitem>
7417       <para>
7418        The <varname>application_name</varname> can be any string of less than
7419        <symbol>NAMEDATALEN</symbol> characters (64 characters in a standard build).
7420        It is typically set by an application upon connection to the server.
7421        The name will be displayed in the <structname>pg_stat_activity</structname> view
7422        and included in CSV log entries.  It can also be included in regular
7423        log entries via the <xref linkend="guc-log-line-prefix"/> parameter.
7424        Only printable ASCII characters may be used in the
7425        <varname>application_name</varname> value.
7426        Other characters are replaced with <link
7427        linkend="sql-syntax-strings-escape">C-style hexadecimal escapes</link>.
7428       </para>
7429      </listitem>
7430     </varlistentry>
7431
7432     <varlistentry id="guc-debug-print-parse">
7433      <term><varname>debug_print_parse</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
7434      <indexterm>
7435       <primary><varname>debug_print_parse</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7436      </indexterm>
7437      </term>
7438      <term><varname>debug_print_rewritten</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
7439      <indexterm>
7440       <primary><varname>debug_print_rewritten</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7441      </indexterm>
7442      </term>
7443      <term><varname>debug_print_plan</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
7444      <indexterm>
7445       <primary><varname>debug_print_plan</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7446      </indexterm>
7447      </term>
7448      <listitem>
7449       <para>
7450        These parameters enable various debugging output to be emitted.
7451        When set, they print the resulting parse tree, the query rewriter
7452        output, or the execution plan for each executed query.
7453        These messages are emitted at <literal>LOG</literal> message level, so by
7454        default they will appear in the server log but will not be sent to the
7455        client.  You can change that by adjusting
7456        <xref linkend="guc-client-min-messages"/> and/or
7457        <xref linkend="guc-log-min-messages"/>.
7458        These parameters are off by default.
7459       </para>
7460      </listitem>
7461     </varlistentry>
7462
7463     <varlistentry id="guc-debug-pretty-print">
7464      <term><varname>debug_pretty_print</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
7465      <indexterm>
7466       <primary><varname>debug_pretty_print</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7467      </indexterm>
7468      </term>
7469      <listitem>
7470       <para>
7471        When set, <varname>debug_pretty_print</varname> indents the messages
7472        produced by <varname>debug_print_parse</varname>,
7473        <varname>debug_print_rewritten</varname>, or
7474        <varname>debug_print_plan</varname>.  This results in more readable
7475        but much longer output than the <quote>compact</quote> format used when
7476        it is off.  It is on by default.
7477       </para>
7478      </listitem>
7479     </varlistentry>
7480
7481     <varlistentry id="guc-log-autovacuum-min-duration" xreflabel="log_autovacuum_min_duration">
7482      <term><varname>log_autovacuum_min_duration</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
7483      <indexterm>
7484       <primary><varname>log_autovacuum_min_duration</varname></primary>
7485       <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
7486      </indexterm>
7487      </term>
7488      <listitem>
7489       <para>
7490        Causes each action executed by autovacuum to be logged if it ran for at
7491        least the specified amount of time.  Setting this to zero logs
7492        all autovacuum actions. <literal>-1</literal> disables logging autovacuum
7493        actions. If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
7494        For example, if you set this to
7495        <literal>250ms</literal> then all automatic vacuums and analyzes that run
7496        250ms or longer will be logged.  In addition, when this parameter is
7497        set to any value other than <literal>-1</literal>, a message will be
7498        logged if an autovacuum action is skipped due to a conflicting lock or a
7499        concurrently dropped relation. The default is <literal>10min</literal>.
7500        Enabling this parameter can be helpful in tracking autovacuum activity.
7501        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
7502        file or on the server command line; but the setting can be overridden for
7503        individual tables by changing table storage parameters.
7504       </para>
7505      </listitem>
7506     </varlistentry>
7507
7508     <varlistentry id="guc-log-checkpoints" xreflabel="log_checkpoints">
7509      <term><varname>log_checkpoints</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
7510      <indexterm>
7511       <primary><varname>log_checkpoints</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7512      </indexterm>
7513      </term>
7514      <listitem>
7515       <para>
7516        Causes checkpoints and restartpoints to be logged in the server log.
7517        Some statistics are included in the log messages, including the number
7518        of buffers written and the time spent writing them.
7519        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
7520        file or on the server command line. The default is on.
7521       </para>
7522      </listitem>
7523     </varlistentry>
7524
7525     <varlistentry id="guc-log-connections" xreflabel="log_connections">
7526      <term><varname>log_connections</varname> (<type>string</type>)
7527      <indexterm>
7528       <primary><varname>log_connections</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7529      </indexterm>
7530      </term>
7531      <listitem>
7532       <para>
7533        Causes aspects of each connection to the server to be logged.
7534        The default is the empty string, <literal>''</literal>, which
7535        disables all connection logging. The following options may be
7536        specified alone or in a comma-separated list:
7537       </para>
7538
7539       <table id="log-connections-options">
7540        <title>Log Connection Options</title>
7541        <tgroup cols="2">
7542         <colspec colname="col1" colwidth="1*"/>
7543         <colspec colname="col2" colwidth="2*"/>
7544         <thead>
7545          <row>
7546           <entry>Name</entry>
7547           <entry>Description</entry>
7548          </row>
7549         </thead>
7550         <tbody>
7551          <row>
7552           <entry><literal>receipt</literal></entry>
7553           <entry>Logs receipt of a connection.</entry>
7554          </row>
7555
7556          <row>
7557           <entry><literal>authentication</literal></entry>
7558           <entry>
7559            Logs the original identity used by an authentication method
7560            to identify a user. In most cases, the identity string
7561            matches the <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> username,
7562            but some third-party authentication methods may alter the
7563            original user identifier before the server stores it. Failed
7564            authentication is always logged regardless of the value of
7565            this setting.
7566           </entry>
7567          </row>
7568
7569          <row>
7570           <entry><literal>authorization</literal></entry>
7571           <entry>
7572            Logs successful completion of authorization. At this point
7573            the connection has been established but the backend is not
7574            yet fully set up. The log message includes the authorized
7575            username as well as the database name and application name,
7576            if applicable.
7577           </entry>
7578          </row>
7579
7580          <row>
7581           <entry><literal>setup_durations</literal></entry>
7582           <entry>
7583            Logs the time spent establishing the connection and setting up the
7584            backend until the connection is ready to execute its first
7585            query. The log message includes three durations:  the total
7586            setup duration (starting from the postmaster accepting the
7587            incoming connection and ending when the connection is ready
7588            for query), the time it took to fork the new backend, and
7589            the time it took to authenticate the user.
7590           </entry>
7591          </row>
7592
7593          <row>
7594           <entry><literal>all</literal></entry>
7595           <entry>
7596            A convenience alias equivalent to specifying all options. If
7597            <literal>all</literal> is specified in a list of other
7598            options, all connection aspects will be logged.
7599           </entry>
7600          </row>
7601
7602         </tbody>
7603        </tgroup>
7604       </table>
7605
7606       <para>
7607        Disconnection logging is separately controlled by <xref
7608        linkend="guc-log-disconnections"/>.
7609       </para>
7610
7611       <para>
7612        For the purposes of backwards compatibility, <literal>on</literal>,
7613        <literal>off</literal>, <literal>true</literal>,
7614        <literal>false</literal>, <literal>yes</literal>,
7615        <literal>no</literal>, <literal>1</literal>, and <literal>0</literal>
7616        are still supported. The positive values are equivalent to specifying
7617        the <literal>receipt</literal>, <literal>authentication</literal>, and
7618        <literal>authorization</literal> options.
7619       </para>
7620
7621       <para>
7622        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
7623        privilege can change this parameter at session start,
7624        and it cannot be changed at all within a session.
7625       </para>
7626
7627       <note>
7628        <para>
7629         Some client programs, like <application>psql</application>, attempt
7630         to connect twice while determining if a password is required, so
7631         duplicate <quote>connection received</quote> messages do not
7632         necessarily indicate a problem.
7633        </para>
7634       </note>
7635      </listitem>
7636     </varlistentry>
7637
7638     <varlistentry id="guc-log-disconnections" xreflabel="log_disconnections">
7639      <term><varname>log_disconnections</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
7640      <indexterm>
7641       <primary><varname>log_disconnections</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7642      </indexterm>
7643      </term>
7644      <listitem>
7645       <para>
7646        Causes session terminations to be logged.  The log output
7647        provides information similar to <varname>log_connections</varname>,
7648        plus the duration of the session.
7649        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
7650        privilege can change this parameter at session start,
7651        and it cannot be changed at all within a session.
7652        The default is <literal>off</literal>.
7653       </para>
7654      </listitem>
7655     </varlistentry>
7656
7657
7658     <varlistentry id="guc-log-duration" xreflabel="log_duration">
7659      <term><varname>log_duration</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
7660      <indexterm>
7661       <primary><varname>log_duration</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7662      </indexterm>
7663      </term>
7664      <listitem>
7665       <para>
7666        Causes the duration of every completed statement to be logged.
7667        The default is <literal>off</literal>.
7668        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
7669        privilege can change this setting.
7670       </para>
7671
7672       <para>
7673        For clients using extended query protocol, durations of the Parse,
7674        Bind, and Execute steps are logged independently.
7675       </para>
7676
7677       <note>
7678        <para>
7679         The difference between enabling <varname>log_duration</varname> and setting
7680         <xref linkend="guc-log-min-duration-statement"/> to zero is that
7681         exceeding <varname>log_min_duration_statement</varname> forces the text of
7682         the query to be logged, but this option doesn't.  Thus, if
7683         <varname>log_duration</varname> is <literal>on</literal> and
7684         <varname>log_min_duration_statement</varname> has a positive value, all
7685         durations are logged but the query text is included only for
7686         statements exceeding the threshold.  This behavior can be useful for
7687         gathering statistics in high-load installations.
7688        </para>
7689       </note>
7690      </listitem>
7691     </varlistentry>
7692
7693     <varlistentry id="guc-log-error-verbosity" xreflabel="log_error_verbosity">
7694      <term><varname>log_error_verbosity</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
7695      <indexterm>
7696       <primary><varname>log_error_verbosity</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7697      </indexterm>
7698      </term>
7699      <listitem>
7700       <para>
7701        Controls the amount of detail written in the server log for each
7702        message that is logged.  Valid values are <literal>TERSE</literal>,
7703        <literal>DEFAULT</literal>, and <literal>VERBOSE</literal>, each adding more
7704        fields to displayed messages.  <literal>TERSE</literal> excludes
7705        the logging of <literal>DETAIL</literal>, <literal>HINT</literal>,
7706        <literal>QUERY</literal>, and <literal>CONTEXT</literal> error information.
7707        <literal>VERBOSE</literal> output includes the <symbol>SQLSTATE</symbol> error
7708        code (see also <xref linkend="errcodes-appendix"/>) and the source code file name, function name,
7709        and line number that generated the error.
7710        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
7711        privilege can change this setting.
7712       </para>
7713      </listitem>
7714     </varlistentry>
7715
7716     <varlistentry id="guc-log-hostname" xreflabel="log_hostname">
7717      <term><varname>log_hostname</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
7718      <indexterm>
7719       <primary><varname>log_hostname</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7720      </indexterm>
7721      </term>
7722      <listitem>
7723       <para>
7724        By default, connection log messages only show the IP address of the
7725        connecting host. Turning this parameter on causes logging of the
7726        host name as well.  Note that depending on your host name resolution
7727        setup this might impose a non-negligible performance penalty.
7728        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
7729        file or on the server command line.
7730       </para>
7731      </listitem>
7732     </varlistentry>
7733
7734     <varlistentry id="guc-log-line-prefix" xreflabel="log_line_prefix">
7735      <term><varname>log_line_prefix</varname> (<type>string</type>)
7736      <indexterm>
7737       <primary><varname>log_line_prefix</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7738      </indexterm>
7739      </term>
7740      <listitem>
7741       <para>
7742         This is a <function>printf</function>-style string that is output at the
7743         beginning of each log line.
7744         <literal>%</literal> characters begin <quote>escape sequences</quote>
7745         that are replaced with status information as outlined below.
7746         Unrecognized escapes are ignored. Other
7747         characters are copied straight to the log line. Some escapes are
7748         only recognized by session processes, and will be treated as empty by
7749         background processes such as the main server process. Status
7750         information may be aligned either left or right by specifying a
7751         numeric literal after the % and before the option. A negative
7752         value will cause the status information to be padded on the
7753         right with spaces to give it a minimum width, whereas a positive
7754         value will pad on the left. Padding can be useful to aid human
7755         readability in log files.
7756       </para>
7757
7758       <para>
7759         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
7760         file or on the server command line. The default is
7761         <literal>'%m [%p] '</literal> which logs a time stamp and the process ID.
7762       </para>
7763
7764         <informaltable>
7765          <tgroup cols="3">
7766           <thead>
7767            <row>
7768             <entry>Escape</entry>
7769             <entry>Effect</entry>
7770             <entry>Session only</entry>
7771             </row>
7772            </thead>
7773           <tbody>
7774            <row>
7775             <entry><literal>%a</literal></entry>
7776             <entry>Application name</entry>
7777             <entry>yes</entry>
7778            </row>
7779            <row>
7780             <entry><literal>%u</literal></entry>
7781             <entry>User name</entry>
7782             <entry>yes</entry>
7783            </row>
7784            <row>
7785             <entry><literal>%d</literal></entry>
7786             <entry>Database name</entry>
7787             <entry>yes</entry>
7788            </row>
7789            <row>
7790             <entry><literal>%r</literal></entry>
7791             <entry>Remote host name or IP address, and remote port</entry>
7792             <entry>yes</entry>
7793            </row>
7794            <row>
7795             <entry><literal>%h</literal></entry>
7796             <entry>Remote host name or IP address</entry>
7797             <entry>yes</entry>
7798            </row>
7799            <row>
7800             <entry><literal>%L</literal></entry>
7801             <entry>Local address (the IP address on the server that the
7802             client connected to)</entry>
7803             <entry>yes</entry>
7804            </row>
7805            <row>
7806             <entry><literal>%b</literal></entry>
7807             <entry>Backend type</entry>
7808             <entry>no</entry>
7809            </row>
7810            <row>
7811             <entry><literal>%p</literal></entry>
7812             <entry>Process ID</entry>
7813             <entry>no</entry>
7814            </row>
7815            <row>
7816             <entry><literal>%P</literal></entry>
7817             <entry>Process ID of the parallel group leader, if this process
7818              is a parallel query worker</entry>
7819             <entry>no</entry>
7820            </row>
7821            <row>
7822             <entry><literal>%t</literal></entry>
7823             <entry>Time stamp without milliseconds</entry>
7824             <entry>no</entry>
7825            </row>
7826            <row>
7827             <entry><literal>%m</literal></entry>
7828             <entry>Time stamp with milliseconds</entry>
7829             <entry>no</entry>
7830            </row>
7831            <row>
7832             <entry><literal>%n</literal></entry>
7833             <entry>Time stamp with milliseconds (as a Unix epoch)</entry>
7834             <entry>no</entry>
7835            </row>
7836            <row>
7837             <entry><literal>%i</literal></entry>
7838             <entry>Command tag: type of session's current command</entry>
7839             <entry>yes</entry>
7840            </row>
7841            <row>
7842             <entry><literal>%e</literal></entry>
7843             <entry>SQLSTATE error code</entry>
7844             <entry>no</entry>
7845            </row>
7846            <row>
7847             <entry><literal>%c</literal></entry>
7848             <entry>Session ID: see below</entry>
7849             <entry>no</entry>
7850            </row>
7851            <row>
7852             <entry><literal>%l</literal></entry>
7853             <entry>Number of the log line for each session or process, starting at 1</entry>
7854             <entry>no</entry>
7855            </row>
7856            <row>
7857             <entry><literal>%s</literal></entry>
7858             <entry>Process start time stamp</entry>
7859             <entry>no</entry>
7860            </row>
7861            <row>
7862             <entry><literal>%v</literal></entry>
7863             <entry>Virtual transaction ID (procNumber/localXID);  see
7864             <xref linkend="transaction-id"/></entry>
7865             <entry>no</entry>
7866            </row>
7867            <row>
7868             <entry><literal>%x</literal></entry>
7869             <entry>Transaction ID (0 if none is assigned);  see
7870             <xref linkend="transaction-id"/></entry>
7871             <entry>no</entry>
7872            </row>
7873            <row>
7874             <entry><literal>%q</literal></entry>
7875             <entry>Produces no output, but tells non-session
7876             processes to stop at this point in the string; ignored by
7877             session processes</entry>
7878             <entry>no</entry>
7879            </row>
7880            <row>
7881             <entry><literal>%Q</literal></entry>
7882             <entry>Query identifier of the current query.  Query
7883             identifiers are not computed by default, so this field
7884             will be zero unless <xref linkend="guc-compute-query-id"/>
7885             parameter is enabled or a third-party module that computes
7886             query identifiers is configured.</entry>
7887             <entry>yes</entry>
7888            </row>
7889            <row>
7890             <entry><literal>%%</literal></entry>
7891             <entry>Literal <literal>%</literal></entry>
7892             <entry>no</entry>
7893            </row>
7894           </tbody>
7895          </tgroup>
7896         </informaltable>
7897
7898         <para>
7899          The backend type corresponds to the column
7900          <structfield>backend_type</structfield> in the view
7901          <link linkend="monitoring-pg-stat-activity-view">
7902          <structname>pg_stat_activity</structname></link>,
7903          but additional types can appear
7904          in the log that don't show in that view.
7905         </para>
7906
7907         <para>
7908         The <literal>%c</literal> escape prints a quasi-unique session identifier,
7909         consisting of two 4-byte hexadecimal numbers (without leading zeros)
7910         separated by a dot.  The numbers are the process start time and the
7911         process ID, so <literal>%c</literal> can also be used as a space saving way
7912         of printing those items.  For example, to generate the session
7913         identifier from <literal>pg_stat_activity</literal>, use this query:
7914<programlisting>
7915SELECT to_hex(trunc(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM backend_start))::integer) || '.' ||
7916       to_hex(pid)
7917FROM pg_stat_activity;
7918</programlisting>
7919
7920       </para>
7921
7922       <tip>
7923        <para>
7924         If you set a nonempty value for <varname>log_line_prefix</varname>,
7925         you should usually make its last character be a space, to provide
7926         visual separation from the rest of the log line.  A punctuation
7927         character can be used too.
7928        </para>
7929       </tip>
7930
7931       <tip>
7932        <para>
7933         <application>Syslog</application> produces its own
7934         time stamp and process ID information, so you probably do not want to
7935         include those escapes if you are logging to <application>syslog</application>.
7936        </para>
7937       </tip>
7938
7939       <tip>
7940        <para>
7941         The <literal>%q</literal> escape is useful when including information that is
7942         only available in session (backend) context like user or database
7943         name.  For example:
7944<programlisting>
7945log_line_prefix = '%m [%p] %q%u@%d/%a '
7946</programlisting>
7947        </para>
7948       </tip>
7949
7950       <note>
7951        <para>
7952         The <literal>%Q</literal> escape always reports a zero identifier
7953         for lines output by <xref linkend="guc-log-statement"/> because
7954         <varname>log_statement</varname> generates output before an
7955         identifier can be calculated, including invalid statements for
7956         which an identifier cannot be calculated.
7957        </para>
7958       </note>
7959      </listitem>
7960     </varlistentry>
7961
7962     <varlistentry id="guc-log-lock-waits" xreflabel="log_lock_waits">
7963      <term><varname>log_lock_waits</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
7964      <indexterm>
7965       <primary><varname>log_lock_waits</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7966      </indexterm>
7967      </term>
7968      <listitem>
7969       <para>
7970        Controls whether a log message is produced when a session waits
7971        longer than <xref linkend="guc-deadlock-timeout"/> to acquire a
7972        lock.  This is useful in determining if lock waits are causing
7973        poor performance.  The default is <literal>off</literal>.
7974        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
7975        privilege can change this setting.
7976       </para>
7977      </listitem>
7978     </varlistentry>
7979
7980     <varlistentry id="guc-log-lock-failures" xreflabel="log_lock_failures">
7981      <term><varname>log_lock_failures</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
7982      <indexterm>
7983       <primary><varname>log_lock_failures</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
7984      </indexterm>
7985      </term>
7986      <listitem>
7987       <para>
7988        Controls whether a detailed log message is produced
7989        when a lock acquisition fails.  This is useful for analyzing
7990        the causes of lock failures.  Currently, only lock failures
7991        due to <literal>SELECT NOWAIT</literal> is supported.
7992        The default is <literal>off</literal>.  Only superusers and
7993        users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal> privilege
7994        can change this setting.
7995       </para>
7996      </listitem>
7997     </varlistentry>
7998
7999
8000     <varlistentry id="guc-log-recovery-conflict-waits" xreflabel="log_recovery_conflict_waits">
8001      <term><varname>log_recovery_conflict_waits</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
8002      <indexterm>
8003       <primary><varname>log_recovery_conflict_waits</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8004      </indexterm>
8005      </term>
8006      <listitem>
8007       <para>
8008        Controls whether a log message is produced when the startup process
8009        waits longer than <varname>deadlock_timeout</varname>
8010        for recovery conflicts.  This is useful in determining if recovery
8011        conflicts prevent the recovery from applying WAL.
8012       </para>
8013
8014       <para>
8015        The default is <literal>off</literal>.  This parameter can only be set
8016        in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server
8017        command line.
8018       </para>
8019      </listitem>
8020     </varlistentry>
8021
8022     <varlistentry id="guc-log-parameter-max-length" xreflabel="log_parameter_max_length">
8023      <term><varname>log_parameter_max_length</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
8024      <indexterm>
8025       <primary><varname>log_parameter_max_length</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8026      </indexterm>
8027      </term>
8028      <listitem>
8029       <para>
8030        If greater than zero, each bind parameter value logged with a
8031        non-error statement-logging message is trimmed to this many bytes.
8032        Zero disables logging of bind parameters for non-error statement logs.
8033        <literal>-1</literal> (the default) allows bind parameters to be
8034        logged in full.
8035        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as bytes.
8036        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
8037        privilege can change this setting.
8038       </para>
8039
8040       <para>
8041        This setting only affects log messages printed as a result of
8042        <xref linkend="guc-log-statement"/>,
8043        <xref linkend="guc-log-duration"/>, and related settings.  Non-zero
8044        values of this setting add some overhead, particularly if parameters
8045        are sent in binary form, since then conversion to text is required.
8046       </para>
8047      </listitem>
8048     </varlistentry>
8049
8050     <varlistentry id="guc-log-parameter-max-length-on-error" xreflabel="log_parameter_max_length_on_error">
8051      <term><varname>log_parameter_max_length_on_error</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
8052      <indexterm>
8053       <primary><varname>log_parameter_max_length_on_error</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8054      </indexterm>
8055      </term>
8056      <listitem>
8057       <para>
8058        If greater than zero, each bind parameter value reported in error
8059        messages is trimmed to this many bytes.
8060        Zero (the default) disables including bind parameters in error
8061        messages.
8062        <literal>-1</literal> allows bind parameters to be printed in full.
8063        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as bytes.
8064       </para>
8065
8066       <para>
8067        Non-zero values of this setting add overhead, as
8068        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> will need to store textual
8069        representations of parameter values in memory at the start of each
8070        statement, whether or not an error eventually occurs.  The overhead
8071        is greater when bind parameters are sent in binary form than when
8072        they are sent as text, since the former case requires data
8073        conversion while the latter only requires copying the string.
8074       </para>
8075      </listitem>
8076     </varlistentry>
8077
8078     <varlistentry id="guc-log-statement" xreflabel="log_statement">
8079      <term><varname>log_statement</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
8080      <indexterm>
8081       <primary><varname>log_statement</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8082      </indexterm>
8083      </term>
8084      <listitem>
8085       <para>
8086        Controls which SQL statements are logged. Valid values are
8087        <literal>none</literal> (off), <literal>ddl</literal>, <literal>mod</literal>, and
8088        <literal>all</literal> (all statements). <literal>ddl</literal> logs all data definition
8089        statements, such as <command>CREATE</command>, <command>ALTER</command>, and
8090        <command>DROP</command> statements. <literal>mod</literal> logs all
8091        <literal>ddl</literal> statements, plus data-modifying statements
8092        such as <command>INSERT</command>,
8093        <command>UPDATE</command>, <command>DELETE</command>, <command>TRUNCATE</command>,
8094        and <command>COPY FROM</command>.
8095        <command>PREPARE</command>, <command>EXECUTE</command>, and
8096        <command>EXPLAIN ANALYZE</command> statements are also logged if their
8097        contained command is of an appropriate type.  For clients using
8098        extended query protocol, logging occurs when an Execute message
8099        is received, and values of the Bind parameters are included
8100        (with any embedded single-quote marks doubled).
8101       </para>
8102
8103       <para>
8104        The default is <literal>none</literal>.
8105        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
8106        privilege can change this setting.
8107       </para>
8108
8109       <note>
8110        <para>
8111         Statements that contain simple syntax errors are not logged
8112         even by the <varname>log_statement</varname> = <literal>all</literal> setting,
8113         because the log message is emitted only after basic parsing has
8114         been done to determine the statement type.  In the case of extended
8115         query protocol, this setting likewise does not log statements that
8116         fail before the Execute phase (i.e., during parse analysis or
8117         planning).  Set <varname>log_min_error_statement</varname> to
8118         <literal>ERROR</literal> (or lower) to log such statements.
8119        </para>
8120        <para>
8121         Logged statements might reveal sensitive data and even contain
8122         plaintext passwords.
8123        </para>
8124       </note>
8125      </listitem>
8126     </varlistentry>
8127
8128     <varlistentry id="guc-log-replication-commands" xreflabel="log_replication_commands">
8129      <term><varname>log_replication_commands</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
8130      <indexterm>
8131       <primary><varname>log_replication_commands</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8132      </indexterm>
8133      </term>
8134      <listitem>
8135       <para>
8136        Causes each replication command and <literal>walsender</literal>
8137        process's replication slot acquisition/release to be logged in the
8138        server log. See <xref linkend="protocol-replication"/> for more
8139        information about replication command. The default value is
8140        <literal>off</literal>. Only superusers and users with the appropriate
8141        <literal>SET</literal> privilege can change this setting.
8142       </para>
8143      </listitem>
8144     </varlistentry>
8145
8146     <varlistentry id="guc-log-temp-files" xreflabel="log_temp_files">
8147      <term><varname>log_temp_files</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
8148      <indexterm>
8149       <primary><varname>log_temp_files</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8150      </indexterm>
8151      </term>
8152      <listitem>
8153       <para>
8154        Controls logging of temporary file names and sizes.
8155        Temporary files can be
8156        created for sorts, hashes, and temporary query results.
8157        If enabled by this setting, a log entry is emitted for each
8158        temporary file, with the file size specified in bytes, when it is deleted.
8159        A value of zero logs all temporary file information, while positive
8160        values log only files whose size is greater than or equal to
8161        the specified amount of data.
8162        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as kilobytes.
8163        The default setting is -1, which disables such logging.
8164        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
8165        privilege can change this setting.
8166       </para>
8167      </listitem>
8168     </varlistentry>
8169
8170     <varlistentry id="guc-log-timezone" xreflabel="log_timezone">
8171      <term><varname>log_timezone</varname> (<type>string</type>)
8172      <indexterm>
8173       <primary><varname>log_timezone</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8174      </indexterm>
8175      </term>
8176      <listitem>
8177       <para>
8178        Sets the time zone used for timestamps written in the server log.
8179        Unlike <xref linkend="guc-timezone"/>, this value is cluster-wide,
8180        so that all sessions will report timestamps consistently.
8181        The built-in default is <literal>GMT</literal>, but that is typically
8182        overridden in <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>; <application>initdb</application>
8183        will install a setting there corresponding to its system environment.
8184        See <xref linkend="datatype-timezones"/> for more information.
8185        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
8186        file or on the server command line.
8187       </para>
8188      </listitem>
8189     </varlistentry>
8190
8191     </variablelist>
8192    </sect2>
8193     <sect2 id="runtime-config-logging-csvlog">
8194     <title>Using CSV-Format Log Output</title>
8195
8196       <para>
8197        Including <literal>csvlog</literal> in the <varname>log_destination</varname> list
8198        provides a convenient way to import log files into a database table.
8199        This option emits log lines in comma-separated-values
8200        (<acronym>CSV</acronym>) format,
8201        with these columns:
8202        time stamp with milliseconds,
8203        user name,
8204        database name,
8205        process ID,
8206        client host:port number,
8207        session ID,
8208        per-session line number,
8209        command tag,
8210        session start time,
8211        virtual transaction ID,
8212        regular transaction ID,
8213        error severity,
8214        SQLSTATE code,
8215        error message,
8216        error message detail,
8217        hint,
8218        internal query that led to the error (if any),
8219        character count of the error position therein,
8220        error context,
8221        user query that led to the error (if any and enabled by
8222        <varname>log_min_error_statement</varname>),
8223        character count of the error position therein,
8224        location of the error in the PostgreSQL source code
8225        (if <varname>log_error_verbosity</varname> is set to <literal>verbose</literal>),
8226        application name, backend type, process ID of parallel group leader,
8227        and query id.
8228        Here is a sample table definition for storing CSV-format log output:
8229
8230<programlisting>
8231CREATE TABLE postgres_log
8232(
8233  log_time timestamp(3) with time zone,
8234  user_name text,
8235  database_name text,
8236  process_id integer,
8237  connection_from text,
8238  session_id text,
8239  session_line_num bigint,
8240  command_tag text,
8241  session_start_time timestamp with time zone,
8242  virtual_transaction_id text,
8243  transaction_id bigint,
8244  error_severity text,
8245  sql_state_code text,
8246  message text,
8247  detail text,
8248  hint text,
8249  internal_query text,
8250  internal_query_pos integer,
8251  context text,
8252  query text,
8253  query_pos integer,
8254  location text,
8255  application_name text,
8256  backend_type text,
8257  leader_pid integer,
8258  query_id bigint,
8259  PRIMARY KEY (session_id, session_line_num)
8260);
8261</programlisting>
8262       </para>
8263
8264       <para>
8265        To import a log file into this table, use the <command>COPY FROM</command>
8266        command:
8267
8268<programlisting>
8269COPY postgres_log FROM '/full/path/to/logfile.csv' WITH csv;
8270</programlisting>
8271        It is also possible to access the file as a foreign table, using
8272        the supplied <xref linkend="file-fdw"/> module.
8273       </para>
8274
8275       <para>
8276       There are a few things you need to do to simplify importing CSV log
8277       files:
8278
8279       <orderedlist>
8280         <listitem>
8281           <para>
8282            Set <varname>log_filename</varname> and
8283            <varname>log_rotation_age</varname> to provide a consistent,
8284            predictable naming scheme for your log files.  This lets you
8285            predict what the file name will be and know when an individual log
