8286            file is complete and therefore ready to be imported.
8287         </para>
8288        </listitem>
8289
8290        <listitem>
8291           <para>
8292            Set <varname>log_rotation_size</varname> to 0 to disable
8293            size-based log rotation, as it makes the log file name difficult
8294            to predict.
8295           </para>
8296        </listitem>
8297
8298        <listitem>
8299          <para>
8300           Set <varname>log_truncate_on_rotation</varname> to <literal>on</literal> so
8301           that old log data isn't mixed with the new in the same file.
8302          </para>
8303        </listitem>
8304
8305        <listitem>
8306          <para>
8307           The table definition above includes a primary key specification.
8308           This is useful to protect against accidentally importing the same
8309           information twice.  The <command>COPY</command> command commits all of the
8310           data it imports at one time, so any error will cause the entire
8311           import to fail.  If you import a partial log file and later import
8312           the file again when it is complete, the primary key violation will
8313           cause the import to fail.  Wait until the log is complete and
8314           closed before importing.  This procedure will also protect against
8315           accidentally importing a partial line that hasn't been completely
8316           written, which would also cause <command>COPY</command> to fail.
8317          </para>
8318        </listitem>
8319        </orderedlist>
8320      </para>
8321    </sect2>
8322     <sect2 id="runtime-config-logging-jsonlog">
8323     <title>Using JSON-Format Log Output</title>
8324
8325     <para>
8326      Including <literal>jsonlog</literal> in the
8327      <varname>log_destination</varname> list provides a convenient way to
8328      import log files into many different programs. This option emits log
8329      lines in <acronym>JSON</acronym> format.
8330     </para>
8331
8332     <para>
8333      String fields with null values are excluded from output.
8334      Additional fields may be added in the future. User applications that
8335      process <literal>jsonlog</literal> output should ignore unknown fields.
8336     </para>
8337
8338     <para>
8339      Each log line is serialized as a JSON object with the set of keys and
8340      their associated values shown in <xref
8341      linkend="runtime-config-logging-jsonlog-keys-values"/>.
8342     </para>
8343
8344     <table id="runtime-config-logging-jsonlog-keys-values">
8345      <title>Keys and Values of JSON Log Entries</title>
8346      <tgroup cols="3">
8347       <thead>
8348        <row>
8349         <entry>Key name</entry>
8350         <entry>Type</entry>
8351         <entry>Description</entry>
8352        </row>
8353       </thead>
8354       <tbody>
8355        <row>
8356         <entry><literal>timestamp</literal></entry>
8357         <entry>string</entry>
8358         <entry>Time stamp with milliseconds</entry>
8359        </row>
8360        <row>
8361         <entry><literal>user</literal></entry>
8362         <entry>string</entry>
8363         <entry>User name</entry>
8364        </row>
8365        <row>
8366         <entry><literal>dbname</literal></entry>
8367         <entry>string</entry>
8368         <entry>Database name</entry>
8369        </row>
8370        <row>
8371         <entry><literal>pid</literal></entry>
8372         <entry>number</entry>
8373         <entry>Process ID</entry>
8374        </row>
8375        <row>
8376         <entry><literal>remote_host</literal></entry>
8377         <entry>string</entry>
8378         <entry>Client host</entry>
8379        </row>
8380        <row>
8381         <entry><literal>remote_port</literal></entry>
8382         <entry>number</entry>
8383         <entry>Client port</entry>
8384        </row>
8385        <row>
8386         <entry><literal>session_id</literal></entry>
8387         <entry>string</entry>
8388         <entry>Session ID</entry>
8389        </row>
8390        <row>
8391         <entry><literal>line_num</literal></entry>
8392         <entry>number</entry>
8393         <entry>Per-session line number</entry>
8394        </row>
8395        <row>
8396         <entry><literal>ps</literal></entry>
8397         <entry>string</entry>
8398         <entry>Current ps display</entry>
8399        </row>
8400        <row>
8401         <entry><literal>session_start</literal></entry>
8402         <entry>string</entry>
8403         <entry>Session start time</entry>
8404        </row>
8405        <row>
8406         <entry><literal>vxid</literal></entry>
8407         <entry>string</entry>
8408         <entry>Virtual transaction ID</entry>
8409        </row>
8410        <row>
8411         <entry><literal>txid</literal></entry>
8412         <entry>string</entry>
8413         <entry>Regular transaction ID</entry>
8414        </row>
8415        <row>
8416         <entry><literal>error_severity</literal></entry>
8417         <entry>string</entry>
8418         <entry>Error severity</entry>
8419        </row>
8420        <row>
8421         <entry><literal>state_code</literal></entry>
8422         <entry>string</entry>
8423         <entry>SQLSTATE code</entry>
8424        </row>
8425        <row>
8426         <entry><literal>message</literal></entry>
8427         <entry>string</entry>
8428         <entry>Error message</entry>
8429        </row>
8430        <row>
8431         <entry><literal>detail</literal></entry>
8432         <entry>string</entry>
8433         <entry>Error message detail</entry>
8434        </row>
8435        <row>
8436         <entry><literal>hint</literal></entry>
8437         <entry>string</entry>
8438         <entry>Error message hint</entry>
8439        </row>
8440        <row>
8441         <entry><literal>internal_query</literal></entry>
8442         <entry>string</entry>
8443         <entry>Internal query that led to the error</entry>
8444        </row>
8445        <row>
8446         <entry><literal>internal_position</literal></entry>
8447         <entry>number</entry>
8448         <entry>Cursor index into internal query</entry>
8449        </row>
8450        <row>
8451         <entry><literal>context</literal></entry>
8452         <entry>string</entry>
8453         <entry>Error context</entry>
8454        </row>
8455        <row>
8456         <entry><literal>statement</literal></entry>
8457         <entry>string</entry>
8458         <entry>Client-supplied query string</entry>
8459        </row>
8460        <row>
8461         <entry><literal>cursor_position</literal></entry>
8462         <entry>number</entry>
8463         <entry>Cursor index into query string</entry>
8464        </row>
8465        <row>
8466         <entry><literal>func_name</literal></entry>
8467         <entry>string</entry>
8468         <entry>Error location function name</entry>
8469        </row>
8470        <row>
8471         <entry><literal>file_name</literal></entry>
8472         <entry>string</entry>
8473         <entry>File name of error location</entry>
8474        </row>
8475        <row>
8476         <entry><literal>file_line_num</literal></entry>
8477         <entry>number</entry>
8478         <entry>File line number of the error location</entry>
8479        </row>
8480        <row>
8481         <entry><literal>application_name</literal></entry>
8482         <entry>string</entry>
8483         <entry>Client application name</entry>
8484        </row>
8485        <row>
8486         <entry><literal>backend_type</literal></entry>
8487         <entry>string</entry>
8488         <entry>Type of backend</entry>
8489        </row>
8490        <row>
8491         <entry><literal>leader_pid</literal></entry>
8492         <entry>number</entry>
8493         <entry>Process ID of leader for active parallel workers</entry>
8494        </row>
8495        <row>
8496         <entry><literal>query_id</literal></entry>
8497         <entry>number</entry>
8498         <entry>Query ID</entry>
8499        </row>
8500       </tbody>
8501      </tgroup>
8502     </table>
8503    </sect2>
8504
8505   <sect2 id="runtime-config-logging-proc-title">
8506    <title>Process Title</title>
8507
8508    <para>
8509     These settings control how process titles of server processes are
8510     modified.  Process titles are typically viewed using programs like
8511     <application>ps</application> or, on Windows, <application>Process Explorer</application>.
8512     See <xref linkend="monitoring-ps"/> for details.
8513    </para>
8514
8515    <variablelist>
8516     <varlistentry id="guc-cluster-name" xreflabel="cluster_name">
8517      <term><varname>cluster_name</varname> (<type>string</type>)
8518      <indexterm>
8519       <primary><varname>cluster_name</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8520      </indexterm>
8521      </term>
8522      <listitem>
8523       <para>
8524        Sets a name that identifies this database cluster (instance) for
8525        various purposes.  The cluster name appears in the process title for
8526        all server processes in this cluster.  Moreover, it is the default
8527        application name for a standby connection (see <xref
8528        linkend="guc-synchronous-standby-names"/>).
8529       </para>
8530
8531       <para>
8532        The name can be any string of less
8533        than <symbol>NAMEDATALEN</symbol> characters (64 characters in a standard
8534        build). Only printable ASCII characters may be used in the
8535        <varname>cluster_name</varname> value.
8536        Other characters are replaced with <link
8537        linkend="sql-syntax-strings-escape">C-style hexadecimal escapes</link>.
8538        No name is shown if this parameter is set to the empty string
8539        <literal>''</literal> (which is the default).
8540        This parameter can only be set at server start.
8541       </para>
8542      </listitem>
8543     </varlistentry>
8544
8545     <varlistentry id="guc-update-process-title" xreflabel="update_process_title">
8546      <term><varname>update_process_title</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
8547      <indexterm>
8548       <primary><varname>update_process_title</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8549      </indexterm>
8550      </term>
8551      <listitem>
8552       <para>
8553        Enables updating of the process title every time a new SQL command
8554        is received by the server.
8555        This setting defaults to <literal>on</literal> on most platforms, but it
8556        defaults to <literal>off</literal> on Windows due to that platform's larger
8557        overhead for updating the process title.
8558        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
8559        privilege can change this setting.
8560       </para>
8561      </listitem>
8562     </varlistentry>
8563    </variablelist>
8564   </sect2>
8565  </sect1>
8566
8567   <sect1 id="runtime-config-statistics">
8568    <title>Run-time Statistics</title>
8569
8570    <sect2 id="runtime-config-cumulative-statistics">
8571     <title>Cumulative Query and Index Statistics</title>
8572
8573     <para>
8574      These parameters control the server-wide cumulative statistics system.
8575      When enabled, the data that is collected can be accessed via the
8576      <structname>pg_stat</structname> and <structname>pg_statio</structname>
8577      family of system views.  Refer to <xref linkend="monitoring"/> for more
8578      information.
8579     </para>
8580
8581     <variablelist>
8582
8583     <varlistentry id="guc-track-activities" xreflabel="track_activities">
8584      <term><varname>track_activities</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
8585      <indexterm>
8586       <primary><varname>track_activities</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8587      </indexterm>
8588      </term>
8589      <listitem>
8590       <para>
8591        Enables the collection of information on the currently
8592        executing command of each session, along with its identifier and the
8593        time when that command began execution. This parameter is on by
8594        default. Note that even when enabled, this information is only
8595        visible to superusers, roles with privileges of the
8596        <literal>pg_read_all_stats</literal> role and the user owning the
8597        sessions being reported on (including sessions belonging to a role they
8598        have the privileges of), so it should not represent a security risk.
8599        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
8600        privilege can change this setting.
8601       </para>
8602      </listitem>
8603     </varlistentry>
8604
8605     <varlistentry id="guc-track-activity-query-size" xreflabel="track_activity_query_size">
8606      <term><varname>track_activity_query_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
8607      <indexterm>
8608       <primary><varname>track_activity_query_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8609      </indexterm>
8610      </term>
8611      <listitem>
8612       <para>
8613       Specifies the amount of memory reserved to store the text of the
8614       currently executing command for each active session, for the
8615       <structname>pg_stat_activity</structname>.<structfield>query</structfield> field.
8616       If this value is specified without units, it is taken as bytes.
8617       The default value is 1024 bytes.
8618       This parameter can only be set at server start.
8619       </para>
8620      </listitem>
8621     </varlistentry>
8622
8623     <varlistentry id="guc-track-counts" xreflabel="track_counts">
8624      <term><varname>track_counts</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
8625      <indexterm>
8626       <primary><varname>track_counts</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8627      </indexterm>
8628      </term>
8629      <listitem>
8630       <para>
8631        Enables collection of statistics on database activity.
8632        This parameter is on by default, because the autovacuum
8633        daemon needs the collected information.
8634        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
8635        privilege can change this setting.
8636       </para>
8637      </listitem>
8638     </varlistentry>
8639
8640     <varlistentry id="guc-track-cost-delay-timing" xreflabel="track_cost_delay_timing">
8641      <term><varname>track_cost_delay_timing</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
8642      <indexterm>
8643       <primary><varname>track_cost_delay_timing</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8644      </indexterm>
8645      </term>
8646      <listitem>
8647       <para>
8648        Enables timing of cost-based vacuum delay (see
8649        <xref linkend="runtime-config-resource-vacuum-cost"/>).  This parameter
8650        is off by default, as it will repeatedly query the operating system for
8651        the current time, which may cause significant overhead on some
8652        platforms.  You can use the <xref linkend="pgtesttiming"/> tool to
8653        measure the overhead of timing on your system.  Cost-based vacuum delay
8654        timing information is displayed in
8655        <link linkend="vacuum-progress-reporting"><structname>pg_stat_progress_vacuum</structname></link>,
8656        <link linkend="analyze-progress-reporting"><structname>pg_stat_progress_analyze</structname></link>,
8657        in the output of <xref linkend="sql-vacuum"/> and
8658        <xref linkend="sql-analyze"/> when the
8659        <literal>VERBOSE</literal> option is used, and by autovacuum for
8660        auto-vacuums and auto-analyzes when
8661        <xref linkend="guc-log-autovacuum-min-duration"/> is set.
8662        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
8663        privilege can change this setting.
8664       </para>
8665      </listitem>
8666     </varlistentry>
8667
8668     <varlistentry id="guc-track-io-timing" xreflabel="track_io_timing">
8669      <term><varname>track_io_timing</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
8670      <indexterm>
8671       <primary><varname>track_io_timing</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8672      </indexterm>
8673      </term>
8674      <listitem>
8675       <para>
8676        Enables timing of database I/O waits.  This parameter is off by
8677        default, as it will repeatedly query the operating system for
8678        the current time, which may cause significant overhead on some
8679        platforms.  You can use the <xref linkend="pgtesttiming"/> tool to
8680        measure the overhead of timing on your system.
8681        I/O timing information is
8682        displayed in <link linkend="monitoring-pg-stat-database-view">
8683        <structname>pg_stat_database</structname></link>,
8684        <link linkend="monitoring-pg-stat-io-view">
8685        <structname>pg_stat_io</structname></link> (if <varname>object</varname>
8686        is not <literal>wal</literal>), in the output of the
8687        <link linkend="pg-stat-get-backend-io">
8688        <function>pg_stat_get_backend_io()</function></link> function (if
8689        <varname>object</varname> is not <literal>wal</literal>), in the
8690        output of <xref linkend="sql-explain"/> when the <literal>BUFFERS</literal>
8691        option is used, in the output of <xref linkend="sql-vacuum"/> when
8692        the <literal>VERBOSE</literal> option is used, by autovacuum
8693        for auto-vacuums and auto-analyzes, when <xref
8694        linkend="guc-log-autovacuum-min-duration"/> is set and by
8695        <xref linkend="pgstatstatements"/>.
8696        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
8697        privilege can change this setting.
8698       </para>
8699      </listitem>
8700     </varlistentry>
8701
8702     <varlistentry id="guc-track-wal-io-timing" xreflabel="track_wal_io_timing">
8703      <term><varname>track_wal_io_timing</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
8704      <indexterm>
8705       <primary><varname>track_wal_io_timing</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8706      </indexterm>
8707      </term>
8708      <listitem>
8709       <para>
8710        Enables timing of WAL I/O waits. This parameter is off by default,
8711        as it will repeatedly query the operating system for the current time,
8712        which may cause significant overhead on some platforms.
8713        You can use the <application>pg_test_timing</application> tool to
8714        measure the overhead of timing on your system.
8715        I/O timing information is displayed in
8716        <link linkend="monitoring-pg-stat-io-view">
8717        <structname>pg_stat_io</structname></link> for the
8718        <varname>object</varname> <literal>wal</literal> and in the output of
8719        the <link linkend="pg-stat-get-backend-io">
8720        <function>pg_stat_get_backend_io()</function></link> function for the
8721        <varname>object</varname> <literal>wal</literal>.
8722        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
8723        privilege can change this setting.
8724       </para>
8725      </listitem>
8726     </varlistentry>
8727
8728     <varlistentry id="guc-track-functions" xreflabel="track_functions">
8729      <term><varname>track_functions</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
8730      <indexterm>
8731       <primary><varname>track_functions</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8732      </indexterm>
8733      </term>
8734      <listitem>
8735       <para>
8736        Enables tracking of function call counts and time used. Specify
8737        <literal>pl</literal> to track only procedural-language functions,
8738        <literal>all</literal> to also track SQL and C language functions.
8739        The default is <literal>none</literal>, which disables function
8740        statistics tracking.
8741        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
8742        privilege can change this setting.
8743       </para>
8744
8745       <note>
8746        <para>
8747         SQL-language functions that are simple enough to be <quote>inlined</quote>
8748         into the calling query will not be tracked, regardless of this
8749         setting.
8750        </para>
8751       </note>
8752      </listitem>
8753     </varlistentry>
8754
8755     <varlistentry id="guc-stats-fetch-consistency" xreflabel="stats_fetch_consistency">
8756      <term><varname>stats_fetch_consistency</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
8757      <indexterm>
8758       <primary><varname>stats_fetch_consistency</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8759      </indexterm>
8760      </term>
8761      <listitem>
8762       <para>
8763        Determines the behavior when cumulative statistics are accessed
8764        multiple times within a transaction. When set to
8765        <literal>none</literal>, each access re-fetches counters from shared
8766        memory. When set to <literal>cache</literal>, the first access to
8767        statistics for an object caches those statistics until the end of the
8768        transaction unless <function>pg_stat_clear_snapshot()</function> is
8769        called. When set to <literal>snapshot</literal>, the first statistics
8770        access caches all statistics accessible in the current database, until
8771        the end of the transaction unless
8772        <function>pg_stat_clear_snapshot()</function> is called. Changing this
8773        parameter in a transaction discards the statistics snapshot.
8774        The default is <literal>cache</literal>.
8775       </para>
8776       <note>
8777        <para>
8778         <literal>none</literal> is most suitable for monitoring systems. If
8779         values are only accessed once, it is the most
8780         efficient. <literal>cache</literal> ensures repeat accesses yield the
8781         same values, which is important for queries involving
8782         e.g. self-joins. <literal>snapshot</literal> can be useful when
8783         interactively inspecting statistics, but has higher overhead,
8784         particularly if many database objects exist.
8785        </para>
8786       </note>
8787      </listitem>
8788     </varlistentry>
8789
8790     </variablelist>
8791    </sect2>
8792
8793    <sect2 id="runtime-config-statistics-monitor">
8794     <title>Statistics Monitoring</title>
8795     <variablelist>
8796
8797     <varlistentry id="guc-compute-query-id" xreflabel="compute_query_id">
8798      <term><varname>compute_query_id</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
8799      <indexterm>
8800       <primary><varname>compute_query_id</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8801      </indexterm>
8802      </term>
8803      <listitem>
8804       <para>
8805        Enables in-core computation of a query identifier.
8806        Query identifiers can be displayed in the <link
8807        linkend="monitoring-pg-stat-activity-view"><structname>pg_stat_activity</structname></link>
8808        view, using <command>EXPLAIN</command>, or emitted in the log if
8809        configured via the <xref linkend="guc-log-line-prefix"/> parameter.
8810        The <xref linkend="pgstatstatements"/> extension also requires a query
8811        identifier to be computed.  Note that an external module can
8812        alternatively be used if the in-core query identifier computation
8813        method is not acceptable.  In this case, in-core computation
8814        must be always disabled.
8815        Valid values are <literal>off</literal> (always disabled),
8816        <literal>on</literal> (always enabled), <literal>auto</literal>,
8817        which lets modules such as <xref linkend="pgstatstatements"/>
8818        automatically enable it, and <literal>regress</literal> which
8819        has the same effect as <literal>auto</literal>, except that the
8820        query identifier is not shown in the <literal>EXPLAIN</literal> output
8821        in order to facilitate automated regression testing.
8822        The default is <literal>auto</literal>.
8823       </para>
8824       <note>
8825        <para>
8826         To ensure that only one query identifier is calculated and
8827         displayed, extensions that calculate query identifiers should
8828         throw an error if a query identifier has already been computed.
8829        </para>
8830       </note>
8831      </listitem>
8832     </varlistentry>
8833
8834     <varlistentry id="guc-log-statement-stats">
8835      <term><varname>log_statement_stats</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
8836      <indexterm>
8837       <primary><varname>log_statement_stats</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8838      </indexterm>
8839      </term>
8840      <term><varname>log_parser_stats</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
8841      <indexterm>
8842       <primary><varname>log_parser_stats</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8843      </indexterm>
8844      </term>
8845      <term><varname>log_planner_stats</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
8846      <indexterm>
8847       <primary><varname>log_planner_stats</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8848      </indexterm>
8849      </term>
8850      <term><varname>log_executor_stats</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
8851      <indexterm>
8852       <primary><varname>log_executor_stats</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8853      </indexterm>
8854      </term>
8855      <listitem>
8856       <para>
8857        For each query, output performance statistics of the respective
8858        module to the server log. This is a crude profiling
8859        instrument, similar to the Unix <function>getrusage()</function> operating
8860        system facility.  <varname>log_statement_stats</varname> reports total
8861        statement statistics, while the others report per-module statistics.
8862        <varname>log_statement_stats</varname> cannot be enabled together with
8863        any of the per-module options.  All of these options are disabled by
8864        default.
8865        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
8866        privilege can change these settings.
8867       </para>
8868      </listitem>
8869     </varlistentry>
8870
8871     </variablelist>
8872
8873    </sect2>
8874   </sect1>
8875
8876   <sect1 id="runtime-config-vacuum">
8877    <title>Vacuuming</title>
8878
8879    <indexterm>
8880     <primary>vacuum</primary>
8881     <secondary>configuration parameters</secondary>
8882    </indexterm>
8883
8884    <para>
8885     These parameters control vacuuming behavior. For more information on the
8886     purpose and responsibilities of vacuum, see <xref linkend="routine-vacuuming"/>.
8887    </para>
8888
8889    <sect2 id="runtime-config-autovacuum">
8890     <title>Automatic Vacuuming</title>
8891
8892     <para>
8893      These settings control the behavior of the <firstterm>autovacuum</firstterm>
8894      feature.  Refer to <xref linkend="autovacuum"/> for more information.
8895      Note that many of these settings can be overridden on a per-table
8896      basis; see <xref linkend="sql-createtable-storage-parameters"/>.
8897     </para>
8898
8899     <variablelist>
8900
8901      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum" xreflabel="autovacuum">
8902       <term><varname>autovacuum</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
8903       <indexterm>
8904        <primary><varname>autovacuum</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8905       </indexterm>
8906       </term>
8907       <listitem>
8908        <para>
8909         Controls whether the server should run the
8910         autovacuum launcher daemon.  This is on by default; however,
8911         <xref linkend="guc-track-counts"/> must also be enabled for
8912         autovacuum to work.
8913         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
8914         file or on the server command line; however, autovacuuming can be
8915         disabled for individual tables by changing table storage parameters.
8916        </para>
8917        <para>
8918         Note that even when this parameter is disabled, the system
8919         will launch autovacuum processes if necessary to
8920         prevent transaction ID wraparound.  See <xref
8921         linkend="vacuum-for-wraparound"/> for more information.
8922        </para>
8923       </listitem>
8924      </varlistentry>
8925
8926      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-worker-slots" xreflabel="autovacuum_worker_slots">
8927       <term><varname>autovacuum_worker_slots</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
8928       <indexterm>
8929        <primary><varname>autovacuum_worker_slots</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8930       </indexterm>
8931       </term>
8932       <listitem>
8933        <para>
8934         Specifies the number of backend slots to reserve for autovacuum worker
8935         processes.  The default is typically 16 slots, but might be less if
8936         your kernel settings will not support it (as determined during initdb).
8937         This parameter can only be set at server start.
8938        </para>
8939        <para>
8940         When changing this value, consider also adjusting
8941         <xref linkend="guc-autovacuum-max-workers"/>.
8942        </para>
8943       </listitem>
8944      </varlistentry>
8945
8946      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-max-workers" xreflabel="autovacuum_max_workers">
8947       <term><varname>autovacuum_max_workers</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
8948       <indexterm>
8949        <primary><varname>autovacuum_max_workers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8950       </indexterm>
8951       </term>
8952       <listitem>
8953        <para>
8954         Specifies the maximum number of autovacuum processes (other than the
8955         autovacuum launcher) that may be running at any one time.  The default
8956         is <literal>3</literal>.  This parameter can only be set in the
8957         <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command line.
8958        </para>
8959        <para>
8960         Note that a setting for this value which is higher than
8961         <xref linkend="guc-autovacuum-worker-slots"/> will have no effect,
8962         since autovacuum workers are taken from the pool of slots established
8963         by that setting.
8964        </para>
8965       </listitem>
8966      </varlistentry>
8967
8968      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-naptime" xreflabel="autovacuum_naptime">
8969       <term><varname>autovacuum_naptime</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
8970       <indexterm>
8971        <primary><varname>autovacuum_naptime</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
8972       </indexterm>
8973       </term>
8974       <listitem>
8975        <para>
8976         Specifies the minimum delay between autovacuum runs on any given
8977         database.  In each round the daemon examines the
8978         database and issues <command>VACUUM</command> and <command>ANALYZE</command> commands
8979         as needed for tables in that database.
8980         If this value is specified without units, it is taken as seconds.
8981         The default is one minute (<literal>1min</literal>).
8982         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
8983         file or on the server command line.
8984        </para>
8985       </listitem>
8986      </varlistentry>
8987
8988      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-vacuum-threshold" xreflabel="autovacuum_vacuum_threshold">
8989       <term><varname>autovacuum_vacuum_threshold</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
8990       <indexterm>
8991        <primary><varname>autovacuum_vacuum_threshold</varname></primary>
8992        <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
8993       </indexterm>
8994       </term>
8995       <listitem>
8996        <para>
8997         Specifies the minimum number of updated or deleted tuples needed
8998         to trigger a <command>VACUUM</command> in any one table.
8999         The default is 50 tuples.
9000         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
9001         file or on the server command line;
9002         but the setting can be overridden for individual tables by
9003         changing table storage parameters.
9004        </para>
9005       </listitem>
9006      </varlistentry>
9007
9008      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-vacuum-insert-threshold" xreflabel="autovacuum_vacuum_insert_threshold">
9009       <term><varname>autovacuum_vacuum_insert_threshold</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9010       <indexterm>
9011        <primary><varname>autovacuum_vacuum_insert_threshold</varname></primary>
9012        <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
9013       </indexterm>
9014       </term>
9015       <listitem>
9016        <para>
9017         Specifies the number of inserted tuples needed to trigger a
9018         <command>VACUUM</command> in any one table.
9019         The default is 1000 tuples.  If -1 is specified, autovacuum will not
9020         trigger a <command>VACUUM</command> operation on any tables based on
9021         the number of inserts.
9022         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
9023         file or on the server command line;
9024         but the setting can be overridden for individual tables by
9025         changing table storage parameters.
9026        </para>
9027       </listitem>
9028      </varlistentry>
9029
9030      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-analyze-threshold" xreflabel="autovacuum_analyze_threshold">
9031       <term><varname>autovacuum_analyze_threshold</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9032       <indexterm>
9033        <primary><varname>autovacuum_analyze_threshold</varname></primary>
9034        <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
9035       </indexterm>
9036       </term>
9037       <listitem>
9038        <para>
9039         Specifies the minimum number of inserted, updated or deleted tuples
9040         needed to trigger an <command>ANALYZE</command> in any one table.
9041         The default is 50 tuples.
9042         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
9043         file or on the server command line;
9044         but the setting can be overridden for individual tables by
9045         changing table storage parameters.
9046        </para>
9047       </listitem>
9048      </varlistentry>
9049
9050      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-vacuum-scale-factor" xreflabel="autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor">
9051       <term><varname>autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
9052       <indexterm>
9053        <primary><varname>autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor</varname></primary>
9054        <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
9055       </indexterm>
9056       </term>
9057       <listitem>
9058        <para>
9059         Specifies a fraction of the table size to add to
9060         <varname>autovacuum_vacuum_threshold</varname>
9061         when deciding whether to trigger a <command>VACUUM</command>.
9062         The default is <literal>0.2</literal> (20% of table size).
9063         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
9064         file or on the server command line;
9065         but the setting can be overridden for individual tables by
9066         changing table storage parameters.
9067        </para>
9068       </listitem>
9069      </varlistentry>
9070
9071      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-vacuum-insert-scale-factor" xreflabel="autovacuum_vacuum_insert_scale_factor">
9072       <term><varname>autovacuum_vacuum_insert_scale_factor</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
9073       <indexterm>
9074        <primary><varname>autovacuum_vacuum_insert_scale_factor</varname></primary>
9075        <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
9076       </indexterm>
9077       </term>
9078       <listitem>
9079        <para>
9080         Specifies a fraction of the unfrozen pages in the table to add to
9081         <varname>autovacuum_vacuum_insert_threshold</varname> when deciding
9082         whether to trigger a <command>VACUUM</command>. The default is
9083         <literal>0.2</literal> (20% of unfrozen pages in table). This
9084         parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
9085         file or on the server command line; but the setting can be overridden
9086         for individual tables by changing table storage parameters.
9087        </para>
9088       </listitem>
9089      </varlistentry>
9090
9091      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-analyze-scale-factor" xreflabel="autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor">
9092       <term><varname>autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
9093       <indexterm>
9094        <primary><varname>autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor</varname></primary>
9095        <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
9096       </indexterm>
9097       </term>
9098       <listitem>
9099        <para>
9100         Specifies a fraction of the table size to add to
9101         <varname>autovacuum_analyze_threshold</varname>
9102         when deciding whether to trigger an <command>ANALYZE</command>.
9103         The default is <literal>0.1</literal> (10% of table size).
9104         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
9105         file or on the server command line;
9106         but the setting can be overridden for individual tables by
9107         changing table storage parameters.
9108        </para>
9109       </listitem>
9110      </varlistentry>
9111
9112      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-vacuum-max-threshold" xreflabel="autovacuum_vacuum_max_threshold">
9113       <term><varname>autovacuum_vacuum_max_threshold</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9114       <indexterm>
9115        <primary><varname>autovacuum_vacuum_max_threshold</varname></primary>
9116        <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
9117       </indexterm>
9118       </term>
9119       <listitem>
9120        <para>
9121         Specifies the maximum number of updated or deleted tuples needed to
9122         trigger a <command>VACUUM</command> in any one table, i.e., a limit on
9123         the value calculated with
9124         <varname>autovacuum_vacuum_threshold</varname> and
9125         <varname>autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor</varname>.  The default is
9126         100,000,000 tuples.  If -1 is specified, autovacuum will not enforce a
9127         maximum number of updated or deleted tuples that will trigger a
9128         <command>VACUUM</command> operation.  This parameter can only be set
9129         in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server
9130         command line; but the setting can be overridden for individual tables
9131         by changing storage parameters.
9132        </para>
9133       </listitem>
9134      </varlistentry>
9135
9136      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-freeze-max-age" xreflabel="autovacuum_freeze_max_age">
9137       <term><varname>autovacuum_freeze_max_age</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9138       <indexterm>
9139        <primary><varname>autovacuum_freeze_max_age</varname></primary>
9140        <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
9141       </indexterm>
9142       </term>
9143       <listitem>
9144        <para>
9145         Specifies the maximum age (in transactions) that a table's
9146         <structname>pg_class</structname>.<structfield>relfrozenxid</structfield> field can
9147         attain before a <command>VACUUM</command> operation is forced
9148         to prevent transaction ID wraparound within the table.
9149         Note that the system will launch autovacuum processes to
9150         prevent wraparound even when autovacuum is otherwise disabled.
9151        </para>
9152
9153        <para>
9154         Vacuum also allows removal of old files from the
9155         <filename>pg_xact</filename> subdirectory, which is why the default
9156         is a relatively low 200 million transactions.
9157         This parameter can only be set at server start, but the setting
9158         can be reduced for individual tables by
9159         changing table storage parameters.
9160         For more information see <xref linkend="vacuum-for-wraparound"/>.
9161        </para>
9162       </listitem>
9163      </varlistentry>
9164
9165      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-multixact-freeze-max-age" xreflabel="autovacuum_multixact_freeze_max_age">
9166       <term><varname>autovacuum_multixact_freeze_max_age</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9167       <indexterm>
9168        <primary><varname>autovacuum_multixact_freeze_max_age</varname></primary>
9169        <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
9170       </indexterm>
9171       </term>
9172       <listitem>
9173        <para>
9174         Specifies the maximum age (in multixacts) that a table's
9175         <structname>pg_class</structname>.<structfield>relminmxid</structfield> field can
9176         attain before a <command>VACUUM</command> operation is forced to
9177         prevent multixact ID wraparound within the table.
9178         Note that the system will launch autovacuum processes to
9179         prevent wraparound even when autovacuum is otherwise disabled.
9180        </para>
9181
9182        <para>
9183         Vacuuming multixacts also allows removal of old files from the
9184         <filename>pg_multixact/members</filename> and <filename>pg_multixact/offsets</filename>
9185         subdirectories, which is why the default is a relatively low
9186         400 million multixacts.
9187         This parameter can only be set at server start, but the setting can
9188         be reduced for individual tables by changing table storage parameters.
9189         For more information see <xref linkend="vacuum-for-multixact-wraparound"/>.
9190        </para>
9191       </listitem>
9192      </varlistentry>
9193
9194      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-vacuum-cost-delay" xreflabel="autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay">
9195       <term><varname>autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
9196       <indexterm>
9197        <primary><varname>autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay</varname></primary>
9198        <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
9199       </indexterm>
9200       </term>
9201       <listitem>
9202        <para>
9203         Specifies the cost delay value that will be used in automatic
9204         <command>VACUUM</command> operations.  If -1 is specified, the regular
9205         <xref linkend="guc-vacuum-cost-delay"/> value will be used.
9206         If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
9207         The default value is 2 milliseconds.
9208         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
9209         file or on the server command line;
9210         but the setting can be overridden for individual tables by
9211         changing table storage parameters.
9212        </para>
9213       </listitem>
9214      </varlistentry>
9215
9216      <varlistentry id="guc-autovacuum-vacuum-cost-limit" xreflabel="autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit">
9217       <term><varname>autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9218       <indexterm>
9219        <primary><varname>autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit</varname></primary>
9220        <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
9221       </indexterm>
9222       </term>
9223       <listitem>
9224        <para>
9225         Specifies the cost limit value that will be used in automatic
9226         <command>VACUUM</command> operations.  If <literal>-1</literal>
9227         is specified (which is the default), the regular
9228         <xref linkend="guc-vacuum-cost-limit"/> value will be used.  Note that
9229         the value is distributed proportionally among the running autovacuum
9230         workers, if there is more than one, so that the sum of the limits for
9231         each worker does not exceed the value of this variable.
9232         This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
9233         file or on the server command line;
9234         but the setting can be overridden for individual tables by
9235         changing table storage parameters.
9236        </para>
9237       </listitem>
9238      </varlistentry>
9239
9240     </variablelist>
9241    </sect2>
9242
9243    <sect2 id="runtime-config-resource-vacuum-cost">
9244     <title>Cost-based Vacuum Delay</title>
9245
9246     <para>
9247      During the execution of <xref linkend="sql-vacuum"/>
9248      and <xref linkend="sql-analyze"/>
9249      commands, the system maintains an
9250      internal counter that keeps track of the estimated cost of the
9251      various I/O operations that are performed.  When the accumulated
9252      cost reaches a limit (specified by
9253      <varname>vacuum_cost_limit</varname>), the process performing
9254      the operation will sleep for a short period of time, as specified by
9255      <varname>vacuum_cost_delay</varname>. Then it will reset the
9256      counter and continue execution.
9257     </para>
9258
9259     <para>
9260      The intent of this feature is to allow administrators to reduce
9261      the I/O impact of these commands on concurrent database
9262      activity. There are many situations where it is not
9263      important that maintenance commands like
9264      <command>VACUUM</command> and <command>ANALYZE</command> finish
9265      quickly; however, it is usually very important that these
9266      commands do not significantly interfere with the ability of the
9267      system to perform other database operations. Cost-based vacuum
9268      delay provides a way for administrators to achieve this.
9269     </para>
9270
9271     <para>
9272      This feature is disabled by default for manually issued
9273      <command>VACUUM</command> commands. To enable it, set the
9274      <varname>vacuum_cost_delay</varname> variable to a nonzero
9275      value.
9276     </para>
9277
9278     <variablelist>
9279      <varlistentry id="guc-vacuum-cost-delay" xreflabel="vacuum_cost_delay">
9280       <term><varname>vacuum_cost_delay</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
9281       <indexterm>
9282        <primary><varname>vacuum_cost_delay</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9283       </indexterm>
9284       </term>
9285       <listitem>
9286        <para>
9287         The amount of time that the process will sleep when the cost
9288         limit has been exceeded. If this value is specified without
9289         units, it is taken as milliseconds. The default value is
9290         <literal>0</literal>, which disables the cost-based vacuum delay
9291         feature.  Positive values enable cost-based vacuuming.
9292        </para>
9293
9294        <para>
9295         When using cost-based vacuuming, appropriate values for
9296         <varname>vacuum_cost_delay</varname> are usually quite small, perhaps
9297         less than 1 millisecond.  While <varname>vacuum_cost_delay</varname>
9298         can be set to fractional-millisecond values, such delays may not be
9299         measured accurately on older platforms.  On such platforms,
9300         increasing <command>VACUUM</command>'s throttled resource consumption
9301         above what you get at 1ms will require changing the other vacuum cost
9302         parameters.  You should, nonetheless,
9303         keep <varname>vacuum_cost_delay</varname> as small as your platform
9304         will consistently measure; large delays are not helpful.
9305        </para>
9306       </listitem>
9307      </varlistentry>
9308
9309      <varlistentry id="guc-vacuum-cost-page-hit" xreflabel="vacuum_cost_page_hit">
9310       <term><varname>vacuum_cost_page_hit</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9311       <indexterm>
9312        <primary><varname>vacuum_cost_page_hit</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9313       </indexterm>
9314       </term>
9315       <listitem>
9316        <para>
9317         The estimated cost for vacuuming a buffer found in the shared
9318         buffer cache. It represents the cost to lock the buffer pool,
9319         lookup the shared hash table and scan the content of the page.
9320         The default value is <literal>1</literal>.
9321        </para>
9322       </listitem>
9323      </varlistentry>
9324
9325      <varlistentry id="guc-vacuum-cost-page-miss" xreflabel="vacuum_cost_page_miss">
9326       <term><varname>vacuum_cost_page_miss</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9327       <indexterm>
9328        <primary><varname>vacuum_cost_page_miss</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9329       </indexterm>
9330       </term>
9331       <listitem>
9332        <para>
9333         The estimated cost for vacuuming a buffer that has to be read from
9334         disk.  This represents the effort to lock the buffer pool,
9335         lookup the shared hash table, read the desired block in from
9336         the disk and scan its content. The default value is
9337         <literal>2</literal>.
9338        </para>
9339       </listitem>
9340      </varlistentry>
9341
9342      <varlistentry id="guc-vacuum-cost-page-dirty" xreflabel="vacuum_cost_page_dirty">
9343       <term><varname>vacuum_cost_page_dirty</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9344       <indexterm>
9345        <primary><varname>vacuum_cost_page_dirty</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9346       </indexterm>
9347       </term>
9348       <listitem>
9349        <para>
9350         The estimated cost charged when vacuum modifies a block that was
9351         previously clean. It represents the extra I/O required to
9352         flush the dirty block out to disk again. The default value is
9353         <literal>20</literal>.
9354        </para>
9355       </listitem>
9356      </varlistentry>
9357
9358      <varlistentry id="guc-vacuum-cost-limit" xreflabel="vacuum_cost_limit">
9359       <term><varname>vacuum_cost_limit</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9360       <indexterm>
9361        <primary><varname>vacuum_cost_limit</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9362       </indexterm>
9363       </term>
9364       <listitem>
9365        <para>
9366         This is the accumulated cost that will cause the vacuuming
9367         process to sleep for <varname>vacuum_cost_delay</varname>.  The
9368         default is <literal>200</literal>.
9369        </para>
9370       </listitem>
9371      </varlistentry>
9372     </variablelist>
9373
9374     <note>
9375      <para>
9376       There are certain operations that hold critical locks and should
9377       therefore complete as quickly as possible.  Cost-based vacuum
9378       delays do not occur during such operations.  Therefore it is
9379       possible that the cost accumulates far higher than the specified
9380       limit.  To avoid uselessly long delays in such cases, the actual
9381       delay is calculated as <varname>vacuum_cost_delay</varname> *
9382       <varname>accumulated_balance</varname> /
9383       <varname>vacuum_cost_limit</varname> with a maximum of
9384       <varname>vacuum_cost_delay</varname> * 4.
9385      </para>
9386     </note>
9387    </sect2>
9388
9389    <sect2 id="runtime-config-vacuum-default">
9390     <title>Default Behavior</title>
9391
9392     <variablelist>
9393      <varlistentry id="guc-vacuum-truncate" xreflabel="vacuum_truncate">
9394       <term><varname>vacuum_truncate</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
9395       <indexterm>
9396        <primary><varname>vacuum_truncate</varname></primary>
9397        <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
9398       </indexterm>
9399       </term>
9400       <listitem>
9401        <para>
9402         Enables or disables vacuum to try to truncate off any empty pages at
9403         the end of the table.  The default value is <literal>true</literal>.
9404         If <literal>true</literal>, <command>VACUUM</command> and autovacuum
9405         do the truncation and the disk space for the truncated pages is
9406         returned to the operating system.  Note that the truncation requires
9407         an <literal>ACCESS EXCLUSIVE</literal> lock on the table.  The
9408         <literal>TRUNCATE</literal> parameter of
9409         <link linkend="sql-vacuum"><command>VACUUM</command></link>, if
9410         specified, overrides the value of this parameter.  The setting can
9411         also be overridden for individual tables by changing table storage
9412         parameters.
9413        </para>
9414       </listitem>
9415      </varlistentry>
9416     </variablelist>
9417    </sect2>
9418
9419    <sect2 id="runtime-config-vacuum-freezing">
9420     <title>Freezing</title>
9421
9422     <para>
9423      To maintain correctness even after transaction IDs wrap around,
9424      <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> marks rows that are sufficiently
9425      old as <emphasis>frozen</emphasis>. These rows are visible to everyone;
9426      other transactions do not need to examine their inserting XID to
9427      determine visibility. <command>VACUUM</command> is responsible for
9428      marking rows as frozen. The following settings control
9429      <command>VACUUM</command>'s freezing behavior and should be tuned based
9430      on the XID consumption rate of the system and data access patterns of the
9431      dominant workloads. See <xref linkend="vacuum-for-wraparound"/> for more
9432      information on transaction ID wraparound and tuning these parameters.
9433     </para>
9434
9435     <variablelist>
9436      <varlistentry id="guc-vacuum-freeze-table-age" xreflabel="vacuum_freeze_table_age">
9437       <term><varname>vacuum_freeze_table_age</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9438       <indexterm>
9439        <primary><varname>vacuum_freeze_table_age</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9440       </indexterm>
9441       </term>
9442       <listitem>
9443        <para>
9444         <command>VACUUM</command> performs an aggressive scan if the table's
9445         <structname>pg_class</structname>.<structfield>relfrozenxid</structfield> field has reached
9446         the age specified by this setting.  An aggressive scan differs from
9447         a regular <command>VACUUM</command> in that it visits every page that might
9448         contain unfrozen XIDs or MXIDs, not just those that might contain dead
9449         tuples.  The default is 150 million transactions.  Although users can
9450         set this value anywhere from zero to two billion, <command>VACUUM</command>
9451         will silently limit the effective value to 95% of
9452         <xref linkend="guc-autovacuum-freeze-max-age"/>, so that a
9453         periodic manual <command>VACUUM</command> has a chance to run before an
9454         anti-wraparound autovacuum is launched for the table. For more
9455         information see
9456         <xref linkend="vacuum-for-wraparound"/>.
9457        </para>
9458       </listitem>
9459      </varlistentry>
9460
9461      <varlistentry id="guc-vacuum-freeze-min-age" xreflabel="vacuum_freeze_min_age">
9462       <term><varname>vacuum_freeze_min_age</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9463       <indexterm>
9464        <primary><varname>vacuum_freeze_min_age</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9465       </indexterm>
9466       </term>
9467       <listitem>
9468        <para>
9469         Specifies the cutoff age (in transactions) that
9470         <command>VACUUM</command> should use to decide whether to
9471         trigger freezing of pages that have an older XID.
9472         The default is 50 million transactions.  Although
9473         users can set this value anywhere from zero to one billion,
9474         <command>VACUUM</command> will silently limit the effective value to half
9475         the value of <xref linkend="guc-autovacuum-freeze-max-age"/>, so
9476         that there is not an unreasonably short time between forced
9477         autovacuums.  For more information see <xref
9478         linkend="vacuum-for-wraparound"/>.
9479        </para>
9480       </listitem>
9481      </varlistentry>
9482
9483      <varlistentry id="guc-vacuum-failsafe-age" xreflabel="vacuum_failsafe_age">
9484       <term><varname>vacuum_failsafe_age</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9485       <indexterm>
9486        <primary><varname>vacuum_failsafe_age</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9487       </indexterm>
9488       </term>
9489       <listitem>
9490        <para>
9491         Specifies the maximum age (in transactions) that a table's
9492         <structname>pg_class</structname>.<structfield>relfrozenxid</structfield>
9493         field can attain before <command>VACUUM</command> takes
9494         extraordinary measures to avoid system-wide transaction ID
9495         wraparound failure.  This is <command>VACUUM</command>'s
9496         strategy of last resort.  The failsafe typically triggers
9497         when an autovacuum to prevent transaction ID wraparound has
9498         already been running for some time, though it's possible for
9499         the failsafe to trigger during any <command>VACUUM</command>.
9500        </para>
9501        <para>
9502         When the failsafe is triggered, any cost-based delay that is
9503         in effect will no longer be applied, further non-essential
9504         maintenance tasks (such as index vacuuming) are bypassed, and any
9505         <glossterm linkend="glossary-buffer-access-strategy">Buffer Access Strategy</glossterm>
9506         in use will be disabled resulting in <command>VACUUM</command> being
9507         free to make use of all of
9508         <glossterm linkend="glossary-shared-memory">shared buffers</glossterm>.
9509        </para>
9510        <para>
9511         The default is 1.6 billion transactions.  Although users can
9512         set this value anywhere from zero to 2.1 billion,
9513         <command>VACUUM</command> will silently adjust the effective
9514         value to no less than 105% of <xref
9515          linkend="guc-autovacuum-freeze-max-age"/>.
9516        </para>
9517       </listitem>
9518      </varlistentry>
9519
9520      <varlistentry id="guc-vacuum-multixact-freeze-table-age" xreflabel="vacuum_multixact_freeze_table_age">
9521       <term><varname>vacuum_multixact_freeze_table_age</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9522       <indexterm>
9523        <primary><varname>vacuum_multixact_freeze_table_age</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9524       </indexterm>
9525       </term>
9526       <listitem>
9527        <para>
9528         <command>VACUUM</command> performs an aggressive scan if the table's
9529         <structname>pg_class</structname>.<structfield>relminmxid</structfield> field has reached
9530         the age specified by this setting.  An aggressive scan differs from
9531         a regular <command>VACUUM</command> in that it visits every page that might
9532         contain unfrozen XIDs or MXIDs, not just those that might contain dead
9533         tuples.  The default is 150 million multixacts.
9534         Although users can set this value anywhere from zero to two billion,
9535         <command>VACUUM</command> will silently limit the effective value to 95% of
9536         <xref linkend="guc-autovacuum-multixact-freeze-max-age"/>, so that a
9537         periodic manual <command>VACUUM</command> has a chance to run before an
9538         anti-wraparound is launched for the table.
9539         For more information see <xref linkend="vacuum-for-multixact-wraparound"/>.
9540        </para>
9541       </listitem>
9542      </varlistentry>
9543
9544      <varlistentry id="guc-vacuum-multixact-freeze-min-age" xreflabel="vacuum_multixact_freeze_min_age">
9545       <term><varname>vacuum_multixact_freeze_min_age</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9546       <indexterm>
9547        <primary><varname>vacuum_multixact_freeze_min_age</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9548       </indexterm>
9549       </term>
9550       <listitem>
9551        <para>
9552         Specifies the cutoff age (in multixacts) that <command>VACUUM</command>
9553         should use to decide whether to trigger freezing of pages with
9554         an older multixact ID.  The default is 5 million multixacts.
9555         Although users can set this value anywhere from zero to one billion,
9556         <command>VACUUM</command> will silently limit the effective value to half
9557         the value of <xref linkend="guc-autovacuum-multixact-freeze-max-age"/>,
9558         so that there is not an unreasonably short time between forced
9559         autovacuums.
9560         For more information see <xref linkend="vacuum-for-multixact-wraparound"/>.
9561        </para>
9562       </listitem>
9563      </varlistentry>
9564
9565      <varlistentry id="guc-vacuum-multixact-failsafe-age" xreflabel="vacuum_multixact_failsafe_age">
9566       <term><varname>vacuum_multixact_failsafe_age</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
9567       <indexterm>
9568        <primary><varname>vacuum_multixact_failsafe_age</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9569       </indexterm>
9570       </term>
9571       <listitem>
9572        <para>
9573         Specifies the maximum age (in multixacts) that a table's
9574         <structname>pg_class</structname>.<structfield>relminmxid</structfield>
9575         field can attain before <command>VACUUM</command> takes
9576         extraordinary measures to avoid system-wide multixact ID
9577         wraparound failure.  This is <command>VACUUM</command>'s
9578         strategy of last resort.  The failsafe typically triggers when
9579         an autovacuum to prevent transaction ID wraparound has already
9580         been running for some time, though it's possible for the
9581         failsafe to trigger during any <command>VACUUM</command>.
9582        </para>
9583        <para>
9584         When the failsafe is triggered, any cost-based delay that is
9585         in effect will no longer be applied, and further non-essential
9586         maintenance tasks (such as index vacuuming) are bypassed.
9587        </para>
9588        <para>
9589         The default is 1.6 billion multixacts.  Although users can set
9590         this value anywhere from zero to 2.1 billion,
9591         <command>VACUUM</command> will silently adjust the effective
9592         value to no less than 105% of <xref
9593          linkend="guc-autovacuum-multixact-freeze-max-age"/>.
9594        </para>
9595       </listitem>
9596      </varlistentry>
9597
9598     <varlistentry id="guc-vacuum-max-eager-freeze-failure-rate" xreflabel="vacuum_max_eager_freeze_failure_rate">
9599      <term><varname>vacuum_max_eager_freeze_failure_rate</varname> (<type>floating point</type>)
9600      <indexterm>
9601       <primary><varname>vacuum_max_eager_freeze_failure_rate</varname></primary>
9602       <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
9603      </indexterm>
9604      </term>
9605      <listitem>
9606       <para>
9607        Specifies the maximum number of pages (as a fraction of total pages in
9608        the relation) that <command>VACUUM</command> may scan and
9609        <emphasis>fail</emphasis> to set all-frozen in the visibility map
9610        before disabling eager scanning. A value of <literal>0</literal>
9611        disables eager scanning altogether. The default is
9612        <literal>0.03</literal> (3%).
9613       </para>
9614
9615       <para>
9616        Note that when eager scanning is enabled, only freeze failures
9617        count against the cap, not successful freezing. Successful page
9618        freezes are capped internally at 20% of the all-visible but not
9619        all-frozen pages in the relation. Capping successful page freezes helps
9620        amortize the overhead across multiple normal vacuums and limits the
9621        potential downside of wasted eager freezes of pages that are modified
9622        again before the next aggressive vacuum.
9623       </para>
9624
9625       <para>
9626        This parameter can only be set in the
9627        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command
9628        line; but the setting can be overridden for individual tables by
9629        changing the
9630        <link linkend="reloption-vacuum-max-eager-freeze-failure-rate">
9631        corresponding table storage parameter</link>.
9632        For more information on tuning vacuum's freezing behavior,
9633        see <xref linkend="vacuum-for-wraparound"/>.
9634       </para>
9635      </listitem>
9636     </varlistentry>
9637
9638     </variablelist>
9639    </sect2>
9640   </sect1>
9641
9642   <sect1 id="runtime-config-client">
9643    <title>Client Connection Defaults</title>
9644
9645    <sect2 id="runtime-config-client-statement">
9646     <title>Statement Behavior</title>
9647     <variablelist>
9648
9649     <varlistentry id="guc-client-min-messages" xreflabel="client_min_messages">
9650      <term><varname>client_min_messages</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
9651      <indexterm>
9652       <primary><varname>client_min_messages</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9653      </indexterm>
9654      </term>
9655      <listitem>
9656       <para>
9657        Controls which
9658        <link linkend="runtime-config-severity-levels">message levels</link>
9659        are sent to the client.
9660        Valid values are <literal>DEBUG5</literal>,
9661        <literal>DEBUG4</literal>, <literal>DEBUG3</literal>, <literal>DEBUG2</literal>,
9662        <literal>DEBUG1</literal>, <literal>LOG</literal>, <literal>NOTICE</literal>,
9663        <literal>WARNING</literal>, and <literal>ERROR</literal>.
9664        Each level includes all the levels that follow it.  The later the level,
9665        the fewer messages are sent.  The default is
9666        <literal>NOTICE</literal>.  Note that <literal>LOG</literal> has a different
9667        rank here than in <xref linkend="guc-log-min-messages"/>.
9668       </para>
9669       <para>
9670        <literal>INFO</literal> level messages are always sent to the client.
9671       </para>
9672      </listitem>
9673     </varlistentry>
9674
9675     <varlistentry id="guc-search-path" xreflabel="search_path">
9676      <term><varname>search_path</varname> (<type>string</type>)
9677      <indexterm>
9678       <primary><varname>search_path</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9679      </indexterm>
9680      <indexterm><primary>path</primary><secondary>for schemas</secondary></indexterm>
9681      </term>
9682      <listitem>
9683       <para>
9684        This variable specifies the order in which schemas are searched
9685        when an object (table, data type, function, etc.) is referenced by a
9686        simple name with no schema specified.  When there are objects of
9687        identical names in different schemas, the one found first
9688        in the search path is used.  An object that is not in any of the
9689        schemas in the search path can only be referenced by specifying
9690        its containing schema with a qualified (dotted) name.
9691       </para>
9692
9693       <para>
9694        The value for <varname>search_path</varname> must be a comma-separated
9695        list of schema names.  Any name that is not an existing schema, or is
9696        a schema for which the user does not have <literal>USAGE</literal>
9697        permission, is silently ignored.
9698       </para>
9699
9700       <para>
9701        If one of the list items is the special name
9702        <literal>$user</literal>, then the schema having the name returned by
9703        <function>CURRENT_USER</function> is substituted, if there is such a schema
9704        and the user has <literal>USAGE</literal> permission for it.
9705        (If not, <literal>$user</literal> is ignored.)
9706       </para>
9707
9708       <para>
9709        The system catalog schema, <literal>pg_catalog</literal>, is always
9710        searched, whether it is mentioned in the path or not.  If it is
9711        mentioned in the path then it will be searched in the specified
9712        order.  If <literal>pg_catalog</literal> is not in the path then it will
9713        be searched <emphasis>before</emphasis> searching any of the path items.
9714       </para>
9715
9716       <!-- To further split hairs, funcname('foo') does not use the temporary
9717            schema, even when it considers typname='funcname'.  This paragraph
9718            refers to function names in a loose sense, "pg_proc.proname or
9719            func_name grammar production". -->
9720       <para>
9721        Likewise, the current session's temporary-table schema,
9722        <literal>pg_temp_<replaceable>nnn</replaceable></literal>, is always searched if it
9723        exists.  It can be explicitly listed in the path by using the
9724        alias <literal>pg_temp</literal><indexterm><primary>pg_temp</primary></indexterm>.  If it is not listed in the path then
9725        it is searched first (even before <literal>pg_catalog</literal>).  However,
9726        the temporary schema is only searched for relation (table, view,
9727        sequence, etc.) and data type names.  It is never searched for
9728        function or operator names.
9729       </para>
9730
9731       <para>
9732        When objects are created without specifying a particular target
9733        schema, they will be placed in the first valid schema named in
9734        <varname>search_path</varname>.  An error is reported if the search
9735        path is empty.
9736       </para>
9737
9738       <para>
9739        The default value for this parameter is
9740        <literal>"$user", public</literal>.
9741        This setting supports shared use of a database (where no users
9742        have private schemas, and all share use of <literal>public</literal>),
9743        private per-user schemas, and combinations of these.  Other
9744        effects can be obtained by altering the default search path
9745        setting, either globally or per-user.
9746       </para>
9747
9748       <para>
9749        For more information on schema handling, see
9750        <xref linkend="ddl-schemas"/>.  In particular, the default
9751        configuration is suitable only when the database has a single user or
9752        a few mutually-trusting users.
9753       </para>
9754
9755       <para>
9756        The current effective value of the search path can be examined
9757        via the <acronym>SQL</acronym> function
9758        <function>current_schemas</function>
9759        (see <xref linkend="functions-info"/>).
9760        This is not quite the same as
9761        examining the value of <varname>search_path</varname>, since
9762        <function>current_schemas</function> shows how the items
9763        appearing in <varname>search_path</varname> were resolved.
9764       </para>
9765
9766      </listitem>
9767     </varlistentry>
9768
9769     <varlistentry id="guc-row-security" xreflabel="row_security">
9770      <term><varname>row_security</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
9771      <indexterm>
9772       <primary><varname>row_security</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9773      </indexterm>
9774      </term>
9775      <listitem>
9776       <para>
9777        This variable controls whether to raise an error in lieu of applying a
9778        row security policy.  When set to <literal>on</literal>, policies apply
9779        normally.  When set to <literal>off</literal>, queries fail which would
9780        otherwise apply at least one policy.  The default is <literal>on</literal>.
9781        Change to <literal>off</literal> where limited row visibility could cause
9782        incorrect results; for example, <application>pg_dump</application> makes that
9783        change by default.  This variable has no effect on roles which bypass
9784        every row security policy, to wit, superusers and roles with
9785        the <literal>BYPASSRLS</literal> attribute.
9786       </para>
9787
9788       <para>
9789        For more information on row security policies,
9790        see <xref linkend="sql-createpolicy"/>.
9791       </para>
9792      </listitem>
9793     </varlistentry>
9794
9795     <varlistentry id="guc-default-table-access-method" xreflabel="default_table_access_method">
9796      <term><varname>default_table_access_method</varname> (<type>string</type>)
9797      <indexterm>
9798       <primary><varname>default_table_access_method</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9799      </indexterm>
9800      </term>
9801      <listitem>
9802       <para>
9803        This parameter specifies the default table access method to use when
9804        creating tables or materialized views if the <command>CREATE</command>
9805        command does not explicitly specify an access method, or when
9806        <command>SELECT ... INTO</command> is used, which does not allow
9807        specifying a table access method. The default is <literal>heap</literal>.
9808       </para>
9809      </listitem>
9810     </varlistentry>
9811
9812     <varlistentry id="guc-default-tablespace" xreflabel="default_tablespace">
9813      <term><varname>default_tablespace</varname> (<type>string</type>)
9814      <indexterm>
9815       <primary><varname>default_tablespace</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9816      </indexterm>
9817      <indexterm><primary>tablespace</primary><secondary>default</secondary></indexterm>
9818      </term>
9819      <listitem>
9820       <para>
9821        This variable specifies the default tablespace in which to create
9822        objects (tables and indexes) when a <command>CREATE</command> command does
9823        not explicitly specify a tablespace.
9824       </para>
9825
9826       <para>
9827        The value is either the name of a tablespace, or an empty string
9828        to specify using the default tablespace of the current database.
9829        If the value does not match the name of any existing tablespace,
9830        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> will automatically use the default
9831        tablespace of the current database.  If a nondefault tablespace
9832        is specified, the user must have <literal>CREATE</literal> privilege
9833        for it, or creation attempts will fail.
9834       </para>
9835
9836       <para>
9837        This variable is not used for temporary tables; for them,
9838        <xref linkend="guc-temp-tablespaces"/> is consulted instead.
9839       </para>
9840
9841       <para>
9842        This variable is also not used when creating databases.
9843        By default, a new database inherits its tablespace setting from
9844        the template database it is copied from.
9845       </para>
9846
9847       <para>
9848        If this parameter is set to a value other than the empty string
9849        when a partitioned table is created, the partitioned table's
9850        tablespace will be set to that value, which will be used as
9851        the default tablespace for partitions created in the future,
9852        even if <varname>default_tablespace</varname> has changed since then.
9853       </para>
9854
9855       <para>
9856        For more information on tablespaces,
9857        see <xref linkend="manage-ag-tablespaces"/>.
9858       </para>
9859      </listitem>
9860     </varlistentry>
9861
9862     <varlistentry id="guc-default-toast-compression" xreflabel="default_toast_compression">
9863      <term><varname>default_toast_compression</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
9864      <indexterm>
9865       <primary><varname>default_toast_compression</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9866      </indexterm>
9867      </term>
9868      <listitem>
9869       <para>
9870        This variable sets the default
9871        <link linkend="storage-toast">TOAST</link>
9872        compression method for values of compressible columns.
9873        (This can be overridden for individual columns by setting
9874        the <literal>COMPRESSION</literal> column option in
9875        <command>CREATE TABLE</command> or
9876        <command>ALTER TABLE</command>.)
9877        The supported compression methods are <literal>pglz</literal> and
9878        (if <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> was compiled with
9879        <option>--with-lz4</option>) <literal>lz4</literal>.
9880        The default is <literal>pglz</literal>.
9881       </para>
9882      </listitem>
9883     </varlistentry>
9884
9885     <varlistentry id="guc-temp-tablespaces" xreflabel="temp_tablespaces">
9886      <term><varname>temp_tablespaces</varname> (<type>string</type>)
9887      <indexterm>
9888       <primary><varname>temp_tablespaces</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9889      </indexterm>
9890      <indexterm><primary>tablespace</primary><secondary>temporary</secondary></indexterm>
9891      </term>
9892      <listitem>
9893       <para>
9894        This variable specifies tablespaces in which to create temporary
9895        objects (temp tables and indexes on temp tables) when a
9896        <command>CREATE</command> command does not explicitly specify a tablespace.
9897        Temporary files for purposes such as sorting large data sets
9898        are also created in these tablespaces.
9899       </para>
9900
9901       <para>
9902        The value is a list of names of tablespaces.  When there is more than
9903        one name in the list, <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> chooses a random
9904        member of the list each time a temporary object is to be created;
9905        except that within a transaction, successively created temporary
9906        objects are placed in successive tablespaces from the list.
9907        If the selected element of the list is an empty string,
9908        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> will automatically use the default
9909        tablespace of the current database instead.
9910       </para>
9911
9912       <para>
9913        When <varname>temp_tablespaces</varname> is set interactively, specifying a
9914        nonexistent tablespace is an error, as is specifying a tablespace for
9915        which the user does not have <literal>CREATE</literal> privilege.  However,
9916        when using a previously set value, nonexistent tablespaces are
9917        ignored, as are tablespaces for which the user lacks
9918        <literal>CREATE</literal> privilege.  In particular, this rule applies when
9919        using a value set in <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>.
9920       </para>
9921
9922       <para>
9923        The default value is an empty string, which results in all temporary
9924        objects being created in the default tablespace of the current
9925        database.
9926       </para>
9927
9928       <para>
9929        See also <xref linkend="guc-default-tablespace"/>.
9930       </para>
9931      </listitem>
9932     </varlistentry>
9933
9934     <varlistentry id="guc-check-function-bodies" xreflabel="check_function_bodies">
9935      <term><varname>check_function_bodies</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
9936      <indexterm>
9937       <primary><varname>check_function_bodies</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9938      </indexterm>
9939      </term>
9940      <listitem>
9941       <para>
9942        This parameter is normally on. When set to <literal>off</literal>, it
9943        disables validation of the routine body string during <xref
9944        linkend="sql-createfunction"/> and <xref
9945        linkend="sql-createprocedure"/>.  Disabling validation avoids side
9946        effects of the validation process, in particular preventing false
9947        positives due to problems such as forward references.
