9948        Set this parameter
9949        to <literal>off</literal> before loading functions on behalf of other
9950        users; <application>pg_dump</application> does so automatically.
9951       </para>
9952      </listitem>
9953     </varlistentry>
9954
9955     <varlistentry id="guc-default-transaction-isolation" xreflabel="default_transaction_isolation">
9956      <term><varname>default_transaction_isolation</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
9957      <indexterm>
9958       <primary>transaction isolation level</primary>
9959       <secondary>setting default</secondary>
9960      </indexterm>
9961      <indexterm>
9962       <primary><varname>default_transaction_isolation</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9963      </indexterm>
9964      </term>
9965      <listitem>
9966       <para>
9967        Each SQL transaction has an isolation level, which can be
9968        either <quote>read uncommitted</quote>, <quote>read
9969        committed</quote>, <quote>repeatable read</quote>, or
9970        <quote>serializable</quote>.  This parameter controls the
9971        default isolation level of each new transaction. The default
9972        is <quote>read committed</quote>.
9973       </para>
9974
9975       <para>
9976        Consult <xref linkend="mvcc"/> and <xref
9977        linkend="sql-set-transaction"/> for more information.
9978       </para>
9979      </listitem>
9980     </varlistentry>
9981
9982     <varlistentry id="guc-default-transaction-read-only" xreflabel="default_transaction_read_only">
9983      <term><varname>default_transaction_read_only</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
9984      <indexterm>
9985       <primary>read-only transaction</primary>
9986       <secondary>setting default</secondary>
9987      </indexterm>
9988      <indexterm>
9989       <primary><varname>default_transaction_read_only</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
9990      </indexterm>
9991      </term>
9992      <listitem>
9993       <para>
9994        A read-only SQL transaction cannot alter non-temporary tables.
9995        This parameter controls the default read-only status of each new
9996        transaction. The default is <literal>off</literal> (read/write).
9997       </para>
9998
9999       <para>
10000        Consult <xref linkend="sql-set-transaction"/> for more information.
10001       </para>
10002      </listitem>
10003     </varlistentry>
10004
10005     <varlistentry id="guc-default-transaction-deferrable" xreflabel="default_transaction_deferrable">
10006      <term><varname>default_transaction_deferrable</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
10007      <indexterm>
10008       <primary>deferrable transaction</primary>
10009       <secondary>setting default</secondary>
10010      </indexterm>
10011      <indexterm>
10012       <primary><varname>default_transaction_deferrable</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10013      </indexterm>
10014      </term>
10015      <listitem>
10016       <para>
10017        When running at the <literal>serializable</literal> isolation level,
10018        a deferrable read-only SQL transaction may be delayed before
10019        it is allowed to proceed.  However, once it begins executing
10020        it does not incur any of the overhead required to ensure
10021        serializability; so serialization code will have no reason to
10022        force it to abort because of concurrent updates, making this
10023        option suitable for long-running read-only transactions.
10024        </para>
10025
10026        <para>
10027        This parameter controls the default deferrable status of each
10028        new transaction.  It currently has no effect on read-write
10029        transactions or those operating at isolation levels lower
10030        than <literal>serializable</literal>. The default is <literal>off</literal>.
10031       </para>
10032
10033       <para>
10034        Consult <xref linkend="sql-set-transaction"/> for more information.
10035       </para>
10036      </listitem>
10037     </varlistentry>
10038
10039     <varlistentry id="guc-transaction-isolation" xreflabel="transaction_isolation">
10040      <term><varname>transaction_isolation</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
10041      <indexterm>
10042       <primary>transaction isolation level</primary>
10043      </indexterm>
10044      <indexterm>
10045       <primary><varname>transaction_isolation</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10046      </indexterm>
10047      </term>
10048      <listitem>
10049       <para>
10050        This parameter reflects the current transaction's isolation level.
10051        At the beginning of each transaction, it is set to the current value
10052        of <xref linkend="guc-default-transaction-isolation"/>.
10053        Any subsequent attempt to change it is equivalent to a <xref
10054        linkend="sql-set-transaction"/> command.
10055       </para>
10056      </listitem>
10057     </varlistentry>
10058
10059     <varlistentry id="guc-transaction-read-only" xreflabel="transaction_read_only">
10060      <term><varname>transaction_read_only</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
10061      <indexterm>
10062       <primary>read-only transaction</primary>
10063      </indexterm>
10064      <indexterm>
10065       <primary><varname>transaction_read_only</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10066      </indexterm>
10067      </term>
10068      <listitem>
10069       <para>
10070        This parameter reflects the current transaction's read-only status.
10071        At the beginning of each transaction, it is set to the current value
10072        of <xref linkend="guc-default-transaction-read-only"/>.
10073        Any subsequent attempt to change it is equivalent to a <xref
10074        linkend="sql-set-transaction"/> command.
10075       </para>
10076      </listitem>
10077     </varlistentry>
10078
10079     <varlistentry id="guc-transaction-deferrable" xreflabel="transaction_deferrable">
10080      <term><varname>transaction_deferrable</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
10081      <indexterm>
10082       <primary>deferrable transaction</primary>
10083      </indexterm>
10084      <indexterm>
10085       <primary><varname>transaction_deferrable</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10086      </indexterm>
10087      </term>
10088      <listitem>
10089       <para>
10090        This parameter reflects the current transaction's deferrability status.
10091        At the beginning of each transaction, it is set to the current value
10092        of <xref linkend="guc-default-transaction-deferrable"/>.
10093        Any subsequent attempt to change it is equivalent to a <xref
10094        linkend="sql-set-transaction"/> command.
10095       </para>
10096      </listitem>
10097     </varlistentry>
10098
10099
10100     <varlistentry id="guc-session-replication-role" xreflabel="session_replication_role">
10101      <term><varname>session_replication_role</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
10102      <indexterm>
10103       <primary><varname>session_replication_role</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10104      </indexterm>
10105      </term>
10106      <listitem>
10107       <para>
10108        Controls firing of replication-related triggers and rules for the
10109        current session.
10110        Possible values are <literal>origin</literal> (the default),
10111        <literal>replica</literal> and <literal>local</literal>.
10112        Setting this parameter results in discarding any previously cached
10113        query plans.
10114        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
10115        privilege can change this setting.
10116       </para>
10117
10118       <para>
10119        The intended use of this setting is that logical replication systems
10120        set it to <literal>replica</literal> when they are applying replicated
10121        changes.  The effect of that will be that triggers and rules (that
10122        have not been altered from their default configuration) will not fire
10123        on the replica.  See the <link linkend="sql-altertable"><command>ALTER TABLE</command></link> clauses
10124        <literal>ENABLE TRIGGER</literal> and <literal>ENABLE RULE</literal>
10125        for more information.
10126       </para>
10127
10128       <para>
10129        PostgreSQL treats the settings <literal>origin</literal> and
10130        <literal>local</literal> the same internally.  Third-party replication
10131        systems may use these two values for their internal purposes, for
10132        example using <literal>local</literal> to designate a session whose
10133        changes should not be replicated.
10134       </para>
10135
10136       <para>
10137        Since foreign keys are implemented as triggers, setting this parameter
10138        to <literal>replica</literal> also disables all foreign key checks,
10139        which can leave data in an inconsistent state if improperly used.
10140       </para>
10141      </listitem>
10142     </varlistentry>
10143
10144     <varlistentry id="guc-statement-timeout" xreflabel="statement_timeout">
10145      <term><varname>statement_timeout</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
10146      <indexterm>
10147       <primary><varname>statement_timeout</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10148      </indexterm>
10149      </term>
10150      <listitem>
10151       <para>
10152        Abort any statement that takes more than the specified amount of time.
10153        If <varname>log_min_error_statement</varname> is set
10154        to <literal>ERROR</literal> or lower, the statement that timed out
10155        will also be logged.
10156        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
10157        A value of zero (the default) disables the timeout.
10158       </para>
10159
10160       <para>
10161        The timeout is measured from the time a command arrives at the
10162        server until it is completed by the server.  If multiple SQL
10163        statements appear in a single simple-query message, the timeout
10164        is applied to each statement separately.
10165        (<productname>PostgreSQL</productname> versions before 13 usually
10166        treated the timeout as applying to the whole query string.)
10167        In extended query protocol, the timeout starts running when any
10168        query-related message (Parse, Bind, Execute, Describe) arrives, and
10169        it is canceled by completion of an Execute or Sync message.
10170       </para>
10171
10172       <para>
10173        Setting <varname>statement_timeout</varname> in
10174        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> is not recommended because it would
10175        affect all sessions.
10176       </para>
10177      </listitem>
10178     </varlistentry>
10179
10180     <varlistentry id="guc-transaction-timeout" xreflabel="transaction_timeout">
10181      <term><varname>transaction_timeout</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
10182      <indexterm>
10183       <primary><varname>transaction_timeout</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10184      </indexterm>
10185      </term>
10186      <listitem>
10187       <para>
10188        Terminate any session that spans longer than the specified amount of
10189        time in a transaction. The limit applies both to explicit transactions
10190        (started with <command>BEGIN</command>) and to an implicitly started
10191        transaction corresponding to a single statement.
10192        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
10193        A value of zero (the default) disables the timeout.
10194       </para>
10195
10196       <para>
10197        If <varname>transaction_timeout</varname> is shorter or equal to
10198        <varname>idle_in_transaction_session_timeout</varname> or <varname>statement_timeout</varname>
10199        then the longer timeout is ignored.
10200       </para>
10201
10202       <para>
10203        Setting <varname>transaction_timeout</varname> in
10204        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> is not recommended because it would
10205        affect all sessions.
10206       </para>
10207
10208       <note>
10209        <para>
10210         Prepared transactions are not subject to this timeout.
10211        </para>
10212       </note>
10213      </listitem>
10214     </varlistentry>
10215
10216     <varlistentry id="guc-lock-timeout" xreflabel="lock_timeout">
10217      <term><varname>lock_timeout</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
10218      <indexterm>
10219       <primary><varname>lock_timeout</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10220      </indexterm>
10221      </term>
10222      <listitem>
10223       <para>
10224        Abort any statement that waits longer than the specified amount of
10225        time while attempting to acquire a lock on a table, index,
10226        row, or other database object.  The time limit applies separately to
10227        each lock acquisition attempt.  The limit applies both to explicit
10228        locking requests (such as <command>LOCK TABLE</command>, or <command>SELECT
10229        FOR UPDATE</command> without <literal>NOWAIT</literal>) and to implicitly-acquired
10230        locks.
10231        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
10232        A value of zero (the default) disables the timeout.
10233       </para>
10234
10235       <para>
10236        Unlike <varname>statement_timeout</varname>, this timeout can only occur
10237        while waiting for locks.  Note that if <varname>statement_timeout</varname>
10238        is nonzero, it is rather pointless to set <varname>lock_timeout</varname> to
10239        the same or larger value, since the statement timeout would always
10240        trigger first.  If <varname>log_min_error_statement</varname> is set to
10241        <literal>ERROR</literal> or lower, the statement that timed out will be
10242        logged.
10243       </para>
10244
10245       <para>
10246        Setting <varname>lock_timeout</varname> in
10247        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> is not recommended because it would
10248        affect all sessions.
10249       </para>
10250      </listitem>
10251     </varlistentry>
10252
10253     <varlistentry id="guc-idle-in-transaction-session-timeout" xreflabel="idle_in_transaction_session_timeout">
10254      <term><varname>idle_in_transaction_session_timeout</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
10255      <indexterm>
10256       <primary><varname>idle_in_transaction_session_timeout</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10257      </indexterm>
10258      </term>
10259      <listitem>
10260       <para>
10261        Terminate any session that has been idle (that is, waiting for a
10262        client query) within an open transaction for longer than the
10263        specified amount of time.
10264        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
10265        A value of zero (the default) disables the timeout.
10266       </para>
10267
10268       <para>
10269        This option can be used to ensure that idle sessions do not hold
10270        locks for an unreasonable amount of time.  Even when no significant
10271        locks are held, an open transaction prevents vacuuming away
10272        recently-dead tuples that may be visible only to this transaction;
10273        so remaining idle for a long time can contribute to table bloat.
10274        See <xref linkend="routine-vacuuming"/> for more details.
10275       </para>
10276      </listitem>
10277     </varlistentry>
10278
10279     <varlistentry id="guc-idle-session-timeout" xreflabel="idle_session_timeout">
10280      <term><varname>idle_session_timeout</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
10281      <indexterm>
10282       <primary><varname>idle_session_timeout</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10283      </indexterm>
10284      </term>
10285      <listitem>
10286       <para>
10287        Terminate any session that has been idle (that is, waiting for a
10288        client query), but not within an open transaction, for longer than
10289        the specified amount of time.
10290        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
10291        A value of zero (the default) disables the timeout.
10292       </para>
10293
10294       <para>
10295        Unlike the case with an open transaction, an idle session without a
10296        transaction imposes no large costs on the server, so there is less
10297        need to enable this timeout
10298        than <varname>idle_in_transaction_session_timeout</varname>.
10299       </para>
10300
10301       <para>
10302        Be wary of enforcing this timeout on connections made through
10303        connection-pooling software or other middleware, as such a layer
10304        may not react well to unexpected connection closure.  It may be
10305        helpful to enable this timeout only for interactive sessions,
10306        perhaps by applying it only to particular users.
10307       </para>
10308      </listitem>
10309     </varlistentry>
10310
10311     <varlistentry id="guc-bytea-output" xreflabel="bytea_output">
10312      <term><varname>bytea_output</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
10313      <indexterm>
10314       <primary><varname>bytea_output</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10315      </indexterm>
10316      </term>
10317      <listitem>
10318       <para>
10319        Sets the output format for values of type <type>bytea</type>.
10320        Valid values are <literal>hex</literal> (the default)
10321        and <literal>escape</literal> (the traditional PostgreSQL
10322        format).  See <xref linkend="datatype-binary"/> for more
10323        information.  The <type>bytea</type> type always
10324        accepts both formats on input, regardless of this setting.
10325       </para>
10326      </listitem>
10327     </varlistentry>
10328
10329     <varlistentry id="guc-xmlbinary" xreflabel="xmlbinary">
10330      <term><varname>xmlbinary</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
10331      <indexterm>
10332       <primary><varname>xmlbinary</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10333      </indexterm>
10334      </term>
10335      <listitem>
10336       <para>
10337        Sets how binary values are to be encoded in XML.  This applies
10338        for example when <type>bytea</type> values are converted to
10339        XML by the functions <function>xmlelement</function> or
10340        <function>xmlforest</function>.  Possible values are
10341        <literal>base64</literal> and <literal>hex</literal>, which
10342        are both defined in the XML Schema standard.  The default is
10343        <literal>base64</literal>.  For further information about
10344        XML-related functions, see <xref linkend="functions-xml"/>.
10345       </para>
10346
10347       <para>
10348        The actual choice here is mostly a matter of taste,
10349        constrained only by possible restrictions in client
10350        applications.  Both methods support all possible values,
10351        although the hex encoding will be somewhat larger than the
10352        base64 encoding.
10353       </para>
10354      </listitem>
10355     </varlistentry>
10356
10357     <varlistentry id="guc-xmloption" xreflabel="xmloption">
10358      <term><varname>xmloption</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
10359      <indexterm>
10360       <primary><varname>xmloption</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10361      </indexterm>
10362      <indexterm>
10363       <primary><varname>SET XML OPTION</varname></primary>
10364      </indexterm>
10365      <indexterm>
10366       <primary>XML option</primary>
10367      </indexterm>
10368      </term>
10369      <listitem>
10370       <para>
10371        Sets whether <literal>DOCUMENT</literal> or
10372        <literal>CONTENT</literal> is implicit when converting between
10373        XML and character string values.  See <xref
10374        linkend="datatype-xml"/> for a description of this.  Valid
10375        values are <literal>DOCUMENT</literal> and
10376        <literal>CONTENT</literal>.  The default is
10377        <literal>CONTENT</literal>.
10378       </para>
10379
10380       <para>
10381        According to the SQL standard, the command to set this option is
10382<synopsis>
10383SET XML OPTION { DOCUMENT | CONTENT };
10384</synopsis>
10385        This syntax is also available in PostgreSQL.
10386       </para>
10387      </listitem>
10388     </varlistentry>
10389
10390     <varlistentry id="guc-gin-pending-list-limit" xreflabel="gin_pending_list_limit">
10391      <term><varname>gin_pending_list_limit</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
10392      <indexterm>
10393       <primary><varname>gin_pending_list_limit</varname></primary>
10394       <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
10395      </indexterm>
10396      </term>
10397      <listitem>
10398       <para>
10399        Sets the maximum size of a GIN index's pending list, which is used
10400        when <literal>fastupdate</literal> is enabled. If the list grows
10401        larger than this maximum size, it is cleaned up by moving
10402        the entries in it to the index's main GIN data structure in bulk.
10403        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as kilobytes.
10404        The default is four megabytes (<literal>4MB</literal>). This setting
10405        can be overridden for individual GIN indexes by changing
10406        index storage parameters.
10407         See <xref linkend="gin-fast-update"/> and <xref linkend="gin-tips"/>
10408         for more information.
10409       </para>
10410      </listitem>
10411     </varlistentry>
10412
10413     <varlistentry id="guc-createrole-self-grant" xreflabel="createrole_self_grant">
10414      <term><varname>createrole_self_grant</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10415      <indexterm>
10416       <primary><varname>createrole_self_grant</varname></primary>
10417       <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
10418      </indexterm>
10419      </term>
10420      <listitem>
10421       <para>
10422        If a user who has <literal>CREATEROLE</literal> but not
10423        <literal>SUPERUSER</literal> creates a role, and if this
10424        is set to a non-empty value, the newly-created role will be granted
10425        to the creating user with the options specified. The value must be
10426        <literal>set</literal>, <literal>inherit</literal>, or a
10427        comma-separated list of these. The default value is an empty string,
10428        which disables the feature.
10429       </para>
10430       <para>
10431        The purpose of this option is to allow a <literal>CREATEROLE</literal>
10432        user who is not a superuser to automatically inherit, or automatically
10433        gain the ability to <literal>SET ROLE</literal> to, any created users.
10434        Since a <literal>CREATEROLE</literal> user is always implicitly granted
10435        <literal>ADMIN OPTION</literal> on created roles, that user could
10436        always execute a <literal>GRANT</literal> statement that would achieve
10437        the same effect as this setting. However, it can be convenient for
10438        usability reasons if the grant happens automatically. A superuser
10439        automatically inherits the privileges of every role and can always
10440        <literal>SET ROLE</literal> to any role, and this setting can be used
10441        to produce a similar behavior for <literal>CREATEROLE</literal> users
10442        for users which they create.
10443       </para>
10444      </listitem>
10445     </varlistentry>
10446
10447     <varlistentry id="guc-event-triggers" xreflabel="event_triggers">
10448      <term><varname>event_triggers</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
10449      <indexterm>
10450       <primary><varname>event_triggers</varname></primary>
10451       <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
10452     </indexterm>
10453     </term>
10454     <listitem>
10455      <para>
10456       Allow temporarily disabling execution of event triggers in order to
10457       troubleshoot and repair faulty event triggers. All event triggers will
10458       be disabled by setting it to <literal>false</literal>. Setting the value
10459       to <literal>true</literal> allows all event triggers to fire, this
10460       is the default value. Only superusers and users with the appropriate
10461       <literal>SET</literal> privilege can change this setting.
10462      </para>
10463     </listitem>
10464     </varlistentry>
10465
10466     <varlistentry id="guc-restrict-nonsystem-relation-kind" xreflabel="restrict_nonsystem_relation_kind">
10467      <term><varname>restrict_nonsystem_relation_kind</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10468      <indexterm>
10469       <primary><varname>restrict_nonsystem_relation_kind</varname></primary>
10470       <secondary>configuration parameter</secondary>
10471     </indexterm>
10472     </term>
10473     <listitem>
10474      <para>
10475       Set relation kinds for which access to non-system relations is prohibited.
10476       The value takes the form of a comma-separated list of relation kinds.
10477       Currently, the supported relation kinds are <literal>view</literal> and
10478       <literal>foreign-table</literal>.
10479      </para>
10480     </listitem>
10481     </varlistentry>
10482
10483     </variablelist>
10484    </sect2>
10485     <sect2 id="runtime-config-client-format">
10486     <title>Locale and Formatting</title>
10487
10488     <variablelist>
10489
10490     <varlistentry id="guc-datestyle" xreflabel="DateStyle">
10491      <term><varname>DateStyle</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10492      <indexterm>
10493       <primary><varname>DateStyle</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10494      </indexterm>
10495      </term>
10496      <listitem>
10497       <para>
10498        Sets the display format for date and time values, as well as the
10499        rules for interpreting ambiguous date input values. For
10500        historical reasons, this variable contains two independent
10501        components: the output format specification (<literal>ISO</literal>,
10502        <literal>Postgres</literal>, <literal>SQL</literal>, or <literal>German</literal>)
10503        and the input/output specification for year/month/day ordering
10504        (<literal>DMY</literal>, <literal>MDY</literal>, or <literal>YMD</literal>). These
10505        can be set separately or together. The keywords <literal>Euro</literal>
10506        and <literal>European</literal> are synonyms for <literal>DMY</literal>; the
10507        keywords <literal>US</literal>, <literal>NonEuro</literal>, and
10508        <literal>NonEuropean</literal> are synonyms for <literal>MDY</literal>. See
10509        <xref linkend="datatype-datetime"/> for more information. The
10510        built-in default is <literal>ISO, MDY</literal>, but
10511        <application>initdb</application> will initialize the
10512        configuration file with a setting that corresponds to the
10513        behavior of the chosen <varname>lc_time</varname> locale.
10514       </para>
10515      </listitem>
10516     </varlistentry>
10517
10518     <varlistentry id="guc-intervalstyle" xreflabel="IntervalStyle">
10519      <term><varname>IntervalStyle</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
10520      <indexterm>
10521       <primary><varname>IntervalStyle</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10522      </indexterm>
10523      </term>
10524      <listitem>
10525       <para>
10526        Sets the display format for interval values.
10527        The value <literal>sql_standard</literal> will produce
10528        output matching <acronym>SQL</acronym> standard interval literals.
10529        The value <literal>postgres</literal> (which is the default) will produce
10530        output matching <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> releases prior to 8.4
10531        when the <xref linkend="guc-datestyle"/>
10532        parameter was set to <literal>ISO</literal>.
10533        The value <literal>postgres_verbose</literal> will produce output
10534        matching <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> releases prior to 8.4
10535        when the <varname>DateStyle</varname>
10536        parameter was set to non-<literal>ISO</literal> output.
10537        The value <literal>iso_8601</literal> will produce output matching the time
10538        interval <quote>format with designators</quote> defined in section
10539        4.4.3.2 of ISO 8601.
10540       </para>
10541       <para>
10542        The <varname>IntervalStyle</varname> parameter also affects the
10543        interpretation of ambiguous interval input.  See
10544        <xref linkend="datatype-interval-input"/> for more information.
10545       </para>
10546      </listitem>
10547     </varlistentry>
10548
10549     <varlistentry id="guc-timezone" xreflabel="TimeZone">
10550      <term><varname>TimeZone</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10551      <indexterm>
10552       <primary><varname>TimeZone</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10553      </indexterm>
10554      <indexterm><primary>time zone</primary></indexterm>
10555      </term>
10556      <listitem>
10557       <para>
10558        Sets the time zone for displaying and interpreting time stamps.
10559        The built-in default is <literal>GMT</literal>, but that is typically
10560        overridden in <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>; <application>initdb</application>
10561        will install a setting there corresponding to its system environment.
10562        See <xref linkend="datatype-timezones"/> for more information.
10563       </para>
10564      </listitem>
10565     </varlistentry>
10566
10567     <varlistentry id="guc-timezone-abbreviations" xreflabel="timezone_abbreviations">
10568      <term><varname>timezone_abbreviations</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10569      <indexterm>
10570       <primary><varname>timezone_abbreviations</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10571      </indexterm>
10572      <indexterm><primary>time zone names</primary></indexterm>
10573      </term>
10574      <listitem>
10575       <para>
10576        Sets the collection of additional time zone abbreviations that
10577        will be accepted by the server for datetime input (beyond any
10578        abbreviations defined by the current <varname>TimeZone</varname>
10579        setting).  The default is <literal>'Default'</literal>,
10580        which is a collection that works in most of the world; there are
10581        also <literal>'Australia'</literal> and <literal>'India'</literal>,
10582        and other collections can be defined for a particular installation.
10583        See <xref linkend="datetime-config-files"/> for more information.
10584       </para>
10585      </listitem>
10586     </varlistentry>
10587
10588     <varlistentry id="guc-extra-float-digits" xreflabel="extra_float_digits">
10589      <term><varname>extra_float_digits</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
10590      <indexterm>
10591       <primary>significant digits</primary>
10592      </indexterm>
10593      <indexterm>
10594       <primary>floating-point</primary>
10595       <secondary>display</secondary>
10596      </indexterm>
10597      <indexterm>
10598       <primary><varname>extra_float_digits</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10599      </indexterm>
10600      </term>
10601      <listitem>
10602       <para>
10603        This parameter adjusts the number of digits used for textual output of
10604        floating-point values, including <type>float4</type>, <type>float8</type>,
10605        and geometric data types.
10606       </para>
10607       <para>
10608        If the value is 1 (the default) or above, float values are output in
10609        shortest-precise format; see <xref linkend="datatype-float"/>. The
10610        actual number of digits generated depends only on the value being
10611        output, not on the value of this parameter. At most 17 digits are
10612        required for <type>float8</type> values, and 9 for <type>float4</type>
10613        values. This format is both fast and precise, preserving the original
10614        binary float value exactly when correctly read. For historical
10615        compatibility, values up to 3 are permitted.
10616       </para>
10617       <para>
10618        If the value is zero or negative, then the output is rounded to a
10619        given decimal precision. The precision used is the standard number of
10620        digits for the type (<literal>FLT_DIG</literal>
10621        or <literal>DBL_DIG</literal> as appropriate) reduced according to the
10622        value of this parameter. (For example, specifying -1 will cause
10623        <type>float4</type> values to be output rounded to 5 significant
10624        digits, and <type>float8</type> values
10625        rounded to 14 digits.) This format is slower and does not preserve all
10626        the bits of the binary float value, but may be more human-readable.
10627       </para>
10628       <note>
10629        <para>
10630         The meaning of this parameter, and its default value, changed
10631         in <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> 12;
10632         see <xref linkend="datatype-float"/> for further discussion.
10633        </para>
10634       </note>
10635      </listitem>
10636     </varlistentry>
10637
10638     <varlistentry id="guc-client-encoding" xreflabel="client_encoding">
10639      <term><varname>client_encoding</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10640      <indexterm>
10641       <primary><varname>client_encoding</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10642      </indexterm>
10643      <indexterm><primary>character set</primary></indexterm>
10644      </term>
10645      <listitem>
10646       <para>
10647        Sets the client-side encoding (character set).
10648        The default is to use the database encoding.
10649        The character sets supported by the <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
10650        server are described in <xref linkend="multibyte-charset-supported"/>.
10651       </para>
10652      </listitem>
10653     </varlistentry>
10654
10655     <varlistentry id="guc-lc-messages" xreflabel="lc_messages">
10656      <term><varname>lc_messages</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10657      <indexterm>
10658       <primary><varname>lc_messages</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10659      </indexterm>
10660      </term>
10661      <listitem>
10662       <para>
10663        Sets the language in which messages are displayed.  Acceptable
10664        values are system-dependent; see <xref linkend="locale"/> for
10665        more information.  If this variable is set to the empty string
10666        (which is the default) then the value is inherited from the
10667        execution environment of the server in a system-dependent way.
10668       </para>
10669
10670       <para>
10671        On some systems, this locale category does not exist.  Setting
10672        this variable will still work, but there will be no effect.
10673        Also, there is a chance that no translated messages for the
10674        desired language exist.  In that case you will continue to see
10675        the English messages.
10676       </para>
10677
10678       <para>
10679        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
10680        privilege can change this setting.
10681       </para>
10682      </listitem>
10683     </varlistentry>
10684
10685     <varlistentry id="guc-lc-monetary" xreflabel="lc_monetary">
10686      <term><varname>lc_monetary</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10687      <indexterm>
10688       <primary><varname>lc_monetary</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10689      </indexterm>
10690      </term>
10691      <listitem>
10692       <para>
10693        Sets the locale to use for formatting monetary amounts, for
10694        example with the <function>to_char</function> family of
10695        functions.  Acceptable values are system-dependent; see <xref
10696        linkend="locale"/> for more information.  If this variable is
10697        set to the empty string (which is the default) then the value
10698        is inherited from the execution environment of the server in a
10699        system-dependent way.
10700       </para>
10701      </listitem>
10702     </varlistentry>
10703
10704     <varlistentry id="guc-lc-numeric" xreflabel="lc_numeric">
10705      <term><varname>lc_numeric</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10706      <indexterm>
10707       <primary><varname>lc_numeric</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10708      </indexterm>
10709      </term>
10710      <listitem>
10711       <para>
10712        Sets the locale to use for formatting numbers, for example
10713        with the <function>to_char</function> family of
10714        functions. Acceptable values are system-dependent; see <xref
10715        linkend="locale"/> for more information.  If this variable is
10716        set to the empty string (which is the default) then the value
10717        is inherited from the execution environment of the server in a
10718        system-dependent way.
10719       </para>
10720      </listitem>
10721     </varlistentry>
10722
10723     <varlistentry id="guc-lc-time" xreflabel="lc_time">
10724      <term><varname>lc_time</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10725      <indexterm>
10726       <primary><varname>lc_time</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10727      </indexterm>
10728      </term>
10729      <listitem>
10730       <para>
10731        Sets the locale to use for formatting dates and times, for example
10732        with the <function>to_char</function> family of
10733        functions. Acceptable values are system-dependent; see <xref
10734        linkend="locale"/> for more information.  If this variable is
10735        set to the empty string (which is the default) then the value
10736        is inherited from the execution environment of the server in a
10737        system-dependent way.
10738       </para>
10739      </listitem>
10740     </varlistentry>
10741
10742     <varlistentry id="guc-icu-validation-level" xreflabel="icu_validation_level">
10743      <term><varname>icu_validation_level</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
10744      <indexterm>
10745       <primary><varname>icu_validation_level</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10746      </indexterm>
10747      </term>
10748      <listitem>
10749       <para>
10750        When ICU locale validation problems are encountered, controls which
10751        <link linkend="runtime-config-severity-levels">message level</link> is
10752        used to report the problem. Valid values are
10753        <literal>DISABLED</literal>, <literal>DEBUG5</literal>,
10754        <literal>DEBUG4</literal>, <literal>DEBUG3</literal>,
10755        <literal>DEBUG2</literal>, <literal>DEBUG1</literal>,
10756        <literal>INFO</literal>, <literal>NOTICE</literal>,
10757        <literal>WARNING</literal>, <literal>ERROR</literal>, and
10758        <literal>LOG</literal>.
10759       </para>
10760       <para>
10761        If set to <literal>DISABLED</literal>, does not report validation
10762        problems at all. Otherwise reports problems at the given message
10763        level. The default is <literal>WARNING</literal>.
10764       </para>
10765      </listitem>
10766     </varlistentry>
10767
10768     <varlistentry id="guc-default-text-search-config" xreflabel="default_text_search_config">
10769      <term><varname>default_text_search_config</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10770      <indexterm>
10771       <primary><varname>default_text_search_config</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10772      </indexterm>
10773      </term>
10774      <listitem>
10775       <para>
10776        Selects the text search configuration that is used by those variants
10777        of the text search functions that do not have an explicit argument
10778        specifying the configuration.
10779        See <xref linkend="textsearch"/> for further information.
10780        The built-in default is <literal>pg_catalog.simple</literal>, but
10781        <application>initdb</application> will initialize the
10782        configuration file with a setting that corresponds to the
10783        chosen <varname>lc_ctype</varname> locale, if a configuration
10784        matching that locale can be identified.
10785       </para>
10786      </listitem>
10787     </varlistentry>
10788
10789     </variablelist>
10790
10791    </sect2>
10792
10793    <sect2 id="runtime-config-client-preload">
10794     <title>Shared Library Preloading</title>
10795
10796     <para>
10797      Several settings are available for preloading shared libraries into the
10798      server, in order to load additional functionality or achieve performance
10799      benefits.  For example, a setting of
10800      <literal>'$libdir/mylib'</literal> would cause
10801      <literal>mylib.so</literal> (or on some platforms,
10802      <literal>mylib.sl</literal>) to be preloaded from the installation's standard
10803      library directory.  The differences between the settings are when they
10804      take effect and what privileges are required to change them.
10805     </para>
10806
10807     <para>
10808      <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> procedural language libraries can
10809      be preloaded in this way, typically by using the
10810      syntax <literal>'$libdir/plXXX'</literal> where
10811      <literal>XXX</literal> is <literal>pgsql</literal>, <literal>perl</literal>,
10812      <literal>tcl</literal>, or <literal>python</literal>.
10813     </para>
10814
10815     <para>
10816      Only shared libraries specifically intended to be used with PostgreSQL
10817      can be loaded this way.  Every PostgreSQL-supported library has
10818      a <quote>magic block</quote> that is checked to guarantee compatibility.  For
10819      this reason, non-PostgreSQL libraries cannot be loaded in this way.  You
10820      might be able to use operating-system facilities such
10821      as <envar>LD_PRELOAD</envar> for that.
10822     </para>
10823
10824     <para>
10825      In general, refer to the documentation of a specific module for the
10826      recommended way to load that module.
10827     </para>
10828
10829     <variablelist>
10830     <varlistentry id="guc-local-preload-libraries" xreflabel="local_preload_libraries">
10831      <term><varname>local_preload_libraries</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10832      <indexterm>
10833       <primary><varname>local_preload_libraries</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10834      </indexterm>
10835      <indexterm>
10836       <primary><filename>$libdir/plugins</filename></primary>
10837      </indexterm>
10838      </term>
10839      <listitem>
10840       <para>
10841        This variable specifies one or more shared libraries that are to be
10842        preloaded at connection start.
10843        It contains a comma-separated list of library names, where each name
10844        is interpreted as for the <link linkend="sql-load"><command>LOAD</command></link> command.
10845        Whitespace between entries is ignored; surround a library name with
10846        double quotes if you need to include whitespace or commas in the name.
10847        The parameter value only takes effect at the start of the connection.
10848        Subsequent changes have no effect.  If a specified library is not
10849        found, the connection attempt will fail.
10850       </para>
10851
10852       <para>
10853        This option can be set by any user.  Because of that, the libraries
10854        that can be loaded are restricted to those appearing in the
10855        <filename>plugins</filename> subdirectory of the installation's
10856        standard library directory.  (It is the database administrator's
10857        responsibility to ensure that only <quote>safe</quote> libraries
10858        are installed there.)  Entries in <varname>local_preload_libraries</varname>
10859        can specify this directory explicitly, for example
10860        <literal>$libdir/plugins/mylib</literal>, or just specify
10861        the library name &mdash; <literal>mylib</literal> would have
10862        the same effect as <literal>$libdir/plugins/mylib</literal>.
10863       </para>
10864
10865       <para>
10866        The intent of this feature is to allow unprivileged users to load
10867        debugging or performance-measurement libraries into specific sessions
10868        without requiring an explicit <command>LOAD</command> command.  To that end,
10869        it would be typical to set this parameter using
10870        the <envar>PGOPTIONS</envar> environment variable on the client or by
10871        using
10872        <command>ALTER ROLE SET</command>.
10873       </para>
10874
10875       <para>
10876        However, unless a module is specifically designed to be used in this way by
10877        non-superusers, this is usually not the right setting to use.  Look
10878        at <xref linkend="guc-session-preload-libraries"/> instead.
10879       </para>
10880      </listitem>
10881     </varlistentry>
10882
10883
10884     <varlistentry id="guc-session-preload-libraries" xreflabel="session_preload_libraries">
10885      <term><varname>session_preload_libraries</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10886      <indexterm>
10887       <primary><varname>session_preload_libraries</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10888      </indexterm>
10889      </term>
10890      <listitem>
10891       <para>
10892        This variable specifies one or more shared libraries that are to be
10893        preloaded at connection start.
10894        It contains a comma-separated list of library names, where each name
10895        is interpreted as for the <link linkend="sql-load"><command>LOAD</command></link> command.
10896        Whitespace between entries is ignored; surround a library name with
10897        double quotes if you need to include whitespace or commas in the name.
10898        The parameter value only takes effect at the start of the connection.
10899        Subsequent changes have no effect.  If a specified library is not
10900        found, the connection attempt will fail.
10901        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
10902        privilege can change this setting.
10903       </para>
10904
10905       <para>
10906        The intent of this feature is to allow debugging or
10907        performance-measurement libraries to be loaded into specific sessions
10908        without an explicit
10909        <command>LOAD</command> command being given.  For
10910        example, <xref linkend="auto-explain"/> could be enabled for all
10911        sessions under a given user name by setting this parameter
10912        with <command>ALTER ROLE SET</command>.  Also, this parameter can be changed
10913        without restarting the server (but changes only take effect when a new
10914        session is started), so it is easier to add new modules this way, even
10915        if they should apply to all sessions.
10916       </para>
10917
10918       <para>
10919        Unlike <xref linkend="guc-shared-preload-libraries"/>, there is no large
10920        performance advantage to loading a library at session start rather than
10921        when it is first used.  There is some advantage, however, when
10922        connection pooling is used.
10923       </para>
10924      </listitem>
10925     </varlistentry>
10926
10927     <varlistentry id="guc-shared-preload-libraries" xreflabel="shared_preload_libraries">
10928      <term><varname>shared_preload_libraries</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10929      <indexterm>
10930       <primary><varname>shared_preload_libraries</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10931      </indexterm>
10932      </term>
10933      <listitem>
10934       <para>
10935        This variable specifies one or more shared libraries to be preloaded at
10936        server start.
10937        It contains a comma-separated list of library names, where each name
10938        is interpreted as for the <link linkend="sql-load"><command>LOAD</command></link> command.
10939        Whitespace between entries is ignored; surround a library name with
10940        double quotes if you need to include whitespace or commas in the name.
10941        This parameter can only be set at server start.  If a specified
10942        library is not found, the server will fail to start.
10943       </para>
10944
10945       <para>
10946        Some libraries need to perform certain operations that can only take
10947        place at postmaster start, such as allocating shared memory, reserving
10948        light-weight locks, or starting background workers.  Those libraries
10949        must be loaded at server start through this parameter.  See the
10950        documentation of each library for details.
10951       </para>
10952
10953       <para>
10954        Other libraries can also be preloaded.  By preloading a shared library,
10955        the library startup time is avoided when the library is first used.
10956        However, the time to start each new server process might increase
10957        slightly, even if that process never uses the library.  So this
10958        parameter is recommended only for libraries that will be used in most
10959        sessions.  Also, changing this parameter requires a server restart, so
10960        this is not the right setting to use for short-term debugging tasks,
10961        say.  Use <xref linkend="guc-session-preload-libraries"/> for that
10962        instead.
10963       </para>
10964
10965      <note>
10966       <para>
10967        On Windows hosts, preloading a library at server start will not reduce
10968        the time required to start each new server process; each server process
10969        will re-load all preload libraries.  However, <varname>shared_preload_libraries
10970        </varname> is still useful on Windows hosts for libraries that need to
10971        perform operations at postmaster start time.
10972       </para>
10973      </note>
10974      </listitem>
10975     </varlistentry>
10976
10977     <varlistentry id="guc-jit-provider" xreflabel="jit_provider">
10978      <term><varname>jit_provider</varname> (<type>string</type>)
10979       <indexterm>
10980        <primary><varname>jit_provider</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
10981       </indexterm>
10982      </term>
10983      <listitem>
10984       <para>
10985        This variable is the name of the JIT provider library to be used
10986        (see <xref linkend="jit-pluggable"/>).
10987        The default is <literal>llvmjit</literal>.
10988        This parameter can only be set at server start.
10989       </para>
10990
10991       <para>
10992        If set to a non-existent library, <acronym>JIT</acronym> will not be
10993        available, but no error will be raised. This allows JIT support to be
10994        installed separately from the main
10995        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> package.
10996       </para>
10997      </listitem>
10998     </varlistentry>
10999
11000    </variablelist>
11001   </sect2>
11002
11003     <sect2 id="runtime-config-client-other">
11004     <title>Other Defaults</title>
11005
11006     <variablelist>
11007
11008     <varlistentry id="guc-dynamic-library-path" xreflabel="dynamic_library_path">
11009      <term><varname>dynamic_library_path</varname> (<type>string</type>)
11010      <indexterm>
11011       <primary><varname>dynamic_library_path</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11012      </indexterm>
11013      <indexterm><primary>dynamic loading</primary></indexterm>
11014      </term>
11015      <listitem>
11016       <para>
11017        If a dynamically loadable module needs to be opened and the
11018        file name specified in the <command>CREATE FUNCTION</command> or
11019        <command>LOAD</command> command
11020        does not have a directory component (i.e., the
11021        name does not contain a slash), the system will search this
11022        path for the required file.
11023       </para>
11024
11025       <para>
11026        The value for <varname>dynamic_library_path</varname> must be a
11027        list of absolute directory paths separated by colons (or semi-colons
11028        on Windows).  If a list element starts
11029        with the special string <literal>$libdir</literal>, the
11030        compiled-in <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> package
11031        library directory is substituted for <literal>$libdir</literal>; this
11032        is where the modules provided by the standard
11033        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> distribution are installed.
11034        (Use <literal>pg_config --pkglibdir</literal> to find out the name of
11035        this directory.) For example:
11036<programlisting>
11037dynamic_library_path = '/usr/local/lib/postgresql:/home/my_project/lib:$libdir'
11038</programlisting>
11039        or, in a Windows environment:
11040<programlisting>
11041dynamic_library_path = 'C:\tools\postgresql;H:\my_project\lib;$libdir'
11042</programlisting>
11043       </para>
11044
11045       <para>
11046        The default value for this parameter is
11047        <literal>'$libdir'</literal>. If the value is set to an empty
11048        string, the automatic path search is turned off.
11049       </para>
11050
11051       <para>
11052        This parameter can be changed at run time by superusers and users
11053        with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal> privilege, but a
11054        setting done that way will only persist until the end of the
11055        client connection, so this method should be reserved for
11056        development purposes. The recommended way to set this parameter
11057        is in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> configuration
11058        file.
11059       </para>
11060      </listitem>
11061     </varlistentry>
11062
11063     <varlistentry id="guc-extension-control-path" xreflabel="extension_control_path">
11064      <term><varname>extension_control_path</varname> (<type>string</type>)
11065      <indexterm>
11066       <primary><varname>extension_control_path</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11067      </indexterm>
11068      </term>
11069      <listitem>
11070       <para>
11071        A path to search for extensions, specifically extension control files
11072        (<filename><replaceable>name</replaceable>.control</filename>).  The
11073        remaining extension script and secondary control files are then loaded
11074        from the same directory where the primary control file was found.
11075        See <xref linkend="extend-extensions-files"/> for details.
11076       </para>
11077
11078       <para>
11079        The value for <varname>extension_control_path</varname> must be a
11080        list of absolute directory paths separated by colons (or semi-colons
11081        on Windows).  If a list element starts
11082        with the special string <literal>$system</literal>, the
11083        compiled-in <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> extension
11084        directory is substituted for <literal>$system</literal>; this
11085        is where the extensions provided by the standard
11086        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> distribution are installed.
11087        (Use <literal>pg_config --sharedir</literal> to find out the name of
11088        this directory.) For example:
11089<programlisting>
11090extension_control_path = '/usr/local/share/postgresql:/home/my_project/share:$system'
11091</programlisting>
11092        or, in a Windows environment:
11093<programlisting>
11094extension_control_path = 'C:\tools\postgresql;H:\my_project\share;$system'
11095</programlisting>
11096        Note that the specified paths elements are expected to have a
11097        subdirectory <filename>extension</filename> which will contain the
11098        <filename>.control</filename> and <filename>.sql</filename> files; the
11099        <filename>extension</filename> suffix is automatically appended to
11100        each path element.
11101       </para>
11102
11103       <para>
11104        The default value for this parameter is
11105        <literal>'$system'</literal>. If the value is set to an empty
11106        string, the default <literal>'$system'</literal> is also assumed.
11107       </para>
11108
11109       <para>
11110        If extensions with equal names are present in multiple directories in
11111        the configured path, only the instance found first in the path will be
11112        used.
11113       </para>
11114
11115       <para>
11116        This parameter can be changed at run time by superusers and users
11117        with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal> privilege, but a
11118        setting done that way will only persist until the end of the
11119        client connection, so this method should be reserved for
11120        development purposes. The recommended way to set this parameter
11121        is in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> configuration
11122        file.
11123       </para>
11124
11125       <para>
11126        Note that if you set this parameter to be able to load extensions from
11127        nonstandard locations, you will most likely also need to set <xref
11128        linkend="guc-dynamic-library-path"/> to a correspondent location, for
11129        example,
11130<programlisting>
11131extension_control_path = '/usr/local/share/postgresql:$system'
11132dynamic_library_path = '/usr/local/lib/postgresql:$libdir'
11133</programlisting>
11134       </para>
11135      </listitem>
11136     </varlistentry>
11137
11138     <varlistentry id="guc-gin-fuzzy-search-limit" xreflabel="gin_fuzzy_search_limit">
11139      <term><varname>gin_fuzzy_search_limit</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11140      <indexterm>
11141       <primary><varname>gin_fuzzy_search_limit</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11142      </indexterm>
11143      </term>
11144      <listitem>
11145       <para>
11146        Soft upper limit of the size of the set returned by GIN index scans. For more
11147        information see <xref linkend="gin-tips"/>.
11148       </para>
11149      </listitem>
11150     </varlistentry>
11151
11152     </variablelist>
11153    </sect2>
11154   </sect1>
11155
11156   <sect1 id="runtime-config-locks">
11157    <title>Lock Management</title>
11158
11159     <variablelist>
11160
11161     <varlistentry id="guc-deadlock-timeout" xreflabel="deadlock_timeout">
11162      <term><varname>deadlock_timeout</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11163      <indexterm>
11164       <primary>deadlock</primary>
11165       <secondary>timeout during</secondary>
11166      </indexterm>
11167      <indexterm>
11168       <primary>timeout</primary>
11169       <secondary>deadlock</secondary>
11170      </indexterm>
11171      <indexterm>
11172       <primary><varname>deadlock_timeout</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11173      </indexterm>
11174      </term>
11175      <listitem>
11176       <para>
11177        This is the amount of time to wait on a lock
11178        before checking to see if there is a deadlock condition. The
11179        check for deadlock is relatively expensive, so the server doesn't run
11180        it every time it waits for a lock. We optimistically assume
11181        that deadlocks are not common in production applications and
11182        just wait on the lock for a while before checking for a
11183        deadlock. Increasing this value reduces the amount of time
11184        wasted in needless deadlock checks, but slows down reporting of
11185        real deadlock errors.
11186        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as milliseconds.
11187        The default is one second (<literal>1s</literal>),
11188        which is probably about the smallest value you would want in
11189        practice. On a heavily loaded server you might want to raise it.
11190        Ideally the setting should exceed your typical transaction time,
11191        so as to improve the odds that a lock will be released before
11192        the waiter decides to check for deadlock.
11193        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
11194        privilege can change this setting.
11195       </para>
11196
11197       <para>
11198        When <xref linkend="guc-log-lock-waits"/> is set,
11199        this parameter also determines the amount of time to wait before
11200        a log message is issued about the lock wait.  If you are trying
11201        to investigate locking delays you might want to set a shorter than
11202        normal <varname>deadlock_timeout</varname>.
11203       </para>
11204      </listitem>
11205     </varlistentry>
11206
11207     <varlistentry id="guc-max-locks-per-transaction" xreflabel="max_locks_per_transaction">
11208      <term><varname>max_locks_per_transaction</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11209      <indexterm>
11210       <primary><varname>max_locks_per_transaction</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11211      </indexterm>
11212      </term>
11213      <listitem>
11214       <para>
11215        The shared lock table has space for
11216        <varname>max_locks_per_transaction</varname> objects
11217        (e.g., tables) per server process or prepared transaction;
11218        hence, no more than this many distinct objects can be locked at
11219        any one time.  This parameter limits the average number of object
11220        locks used by each transaction; individual transactions
11221        can lock more objects as long as the locks of all transactions
11222        fit in the lock table.  This is <emphasis>not</emphasis> the number of
11223        rows that can be locked; that value is unlimited.  The default,
11224        64, has historically proven sufficient, but you might need to
11225        raise this value if you have queries that touch many different
11226        tables in a single transaction, e.g., query of a parent table with
11227        many children.  This parameter can only be set at server start.
11228       </para>
11229
11230       <para>
11231        When running a standby server, you must set this parameter to have the
11232        same or higher value as on the primary server. Otherwise, queries
11233        will not be allowed in the standby server.
11234       </para>
11235      </listitem>
11236     </varlistentry>
11237
11238     <varlistentry id="guc-max-pred-locks-per-transaction" xreflabel="max_pred_locks_per_transaction">
11239      <term><varname>max_pred_locks_per_transaction</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11240      <indexterm>
11241       <primary><varname>max_pred_locks_per_transaction</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11242      </indexterm>
11243      </term>
11244      <listitem>
11245       <para>
11246        The shared predicate lock table has space for
11247        <varname>max_pred_locks_per_transaction</varname> objects
11248        (e.g., tables) per server process or prepared transaction;
11249        hence, no more than this many distinct objects can be locked at
11250        any one time.  This parameter limits the average number of object
11251        locks used by each transaction; individual transactions
11252        can lock more objects as long as the locks of all transactions
11253        fit in the lock table.  This is <emphasis>not</emphasis> the number of
11254        rows that can be locked; that value is unlimited.  The default,
11255        64, has historically proven sufficient, but you might need to
11256        raise this value if you have clients that touch many different
11257        tables in a single serializable transaction. This parameter can
11258        only be set at server start.
11259       </para>
11260      </listitem>
11261     </varlistentry>
11262
11263     <varlistentry id="guc-max-pred-locks-per-relation" xreflabel="max_pred_locks_per_relation">
11264      <term><varname>max_pred_locks_per_relation</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11265      <indexterm>
11266       <primary><varname>max_pred_locks_per_relation</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11267      </indexterm>
11268      </term>
11269      <listitem>
11270       <para>
11271        This controls how many pages or tuples of a single relation can be
11272        predicate-locked before the lock is promoted to covering the whole
11273        relation.  Values greater than or equal to zero mean an absolute
11274        limit, while negative values
11275        mean <xref linkend="guc-max-pred-locks-per-transaction"/> divided by
11276        the absolute value of this setting.  The default is -2, which keeps
11277        the behavior from previous versions of <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>.
11278        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
11279        file or on the server command line.
11280       </para>
11281      </listitem>
11282     </varlistentry>
11283
11284     <varlistentry id="guc-max-pred-locks-per-page" xreflabel="max_pred_locks_per_page">
11285      <term><varname>max_pred_locks_per_page</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11286      <indexterm>
11287       <primary><varname>max_pred_locks_per_page</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11288      </indexterm>
11289      </term>
11290      <listitem>
11291       <para>
11292        This controls how many rows on a single page can be predicate-locked
11293        before the lock is promoted to covering the whole page.  The default
11294        is 2.  This parameter can only be set in
11295        the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command line.
11296       </para>
11297      </listitem>
11298     </varlistentry>
11299
11300     </variablelist>
11301   </sect1>
11302
11303   <sect1 id="runtime-config-compatible">
11304    <title>Version and Platform Compatibility</title>
11305
11306    <sect2 id="runtime-config-compatible-version">
11307     <title>Previous PostgreSQL Versions</title>
11308
11309     <variablelist>
11310
11311     <varlistentry id="guc-array-nulls" xreflabel="array_nulls">
11312      <term><varname>array_nulls</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11313      <indexterm>
11314       <primary><varname>array_nulls</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11315      </indexterm>
11316      </term>
11317      <listitem>
11318       <para>
11319        This controls whether the array input parser recognizes
11320        unquoted <literal>NULL</literal> as specifying a null array element.
11321        By default, this is <literal>on</literal>, allowing array values containing
11322        null values to be entered.  However, <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> versions
11323        before 8.2 did not support null values in arrays, and therefore would
11324        treat <literal>NULL</literal> as specifying a normal array element with
11325        the string value <quote>NULL</quote>.  For backward compatibility with
11326        applications that require the old behavior, this variable can be
11327        turned <literal>off</literal>.
11328       </para>
11329
11330       <para>
11331        Note that it is possible to create array values containing null values
11332        even when this variable is <literal>off</literal>.
11333       </para>
11334      </listitem>
11335     </varlistentry>
11336
11337     <varlistentry id="guc-backslash-quote" xreflabel="backslash_quote">
11338      <term><varname>backslash_quote</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
11339      <indexterm><primary>strings</primary><secondary>backslash quotes</secondary></indexterm>
11340      <indexterm>
11341       <primary><varname>backslash_quote</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11342      </indexterm>
11343      </term>
11344      <listitem>
11345       <para>
11346        This controls whether a quote mark can be represented by
11347        <literal>\'</literal> in a string literal.  The preferred, SQL-standard way
11348        to represent a quote mark is by doubling it (<literal>''</literal>) but
11349        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> has historically also accepted
11350        <literal>\'</literal>. However, use of <literal>\'</literal> creates security risks
11351        because in some client character set encodings, there are multibyte
11352        characters in which the last byte is numerically equivalent to ASCII
11353        <literal>\</literal>.  If client-side code does escaping incorrectly then an
11354        SQL-injection attack is possible.  This risk can be prevented by
11355        making the server reject queries in which a quote mark appears to be
11356        escaped by a backslash.
11357        The allowed values of <varname>backslash_quote</varname> are
11358        <literal>on</literal> (allow <literal>\'</literal> always),
11359        <literal>off</literal> (reject always), and
11360        <literal>safe_encoding</literal> (allow only if client encoding does not
11361        allow ASCII <literal>\</literal> within a multibyte character).
11362        <literal>safe_encoding</literal> is the default setting.
11363       </para>
11364
11365       <para>
11366        Note that in a standard-conforming string literal, <literal>\</literal> just
11367        means <literal>\</literal> anyway.  This parameter only affects the handling of
11368        non-standard-conforming literals, including
11369        escape string syntax (<literal>E'...'</literal>).
11370       </para>
11371      </listitem>
11372     </varlistentry>
11373
11374     <varlistentry id="guc-escape-string-warning" xreflabel="escape_string_warning">
11375      <term><varname>escape_string_warning</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11376      <indexterm><primary>strings</primary><secondary>escape warning</secondary></indexterm>
11377      <indexterm>
11378       <primary><varname>escape_string_warning</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11379      </indexterm>
11380      </term>
11381      <listitem>
11382       <para>
11383        When on, a warning is issued if a backslash (<literal>\</literal>)
11384        appears in an ordinary string literal (<literal>'...'</literal>
11385        syntax) and <varname>standard_conforming_strings</varname> is off.
11386        The default is <literal>on</literal>.
11387       </para>
11388       <para>
11389        Applications that wish to use backslash as escape should be
11390        modified to use escape string syntax (<literal>E'...'</literal>),
11391        because the default behavior of ordinary strings is now to treat
11392        backslash as an ordinary character, per SQL standard.  This variable
11393        can be enabled to help locate code that needs to be changed.
11394       </para>
11395      </listitem>
11396     </varlistentry>
11397
11398     <varlistentry id="guc-lo-compat-privileges" xreflabel="lo_compat_privileges">
11399      <term><varname>lo_compat_privileges</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11400      <indexterm>
11401       <primary><varname>lo_compat_privileges</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11402      </indexterm>
11403      </term>
11404      <listitem>
11405       <para>
11406        In <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> releases prior to 9.0, large objects
11407        did not have access privileges and were, therefore, always readable
11408        and writable by all users.  Setting this variable to <literal>on</literal>
11409        disables the new privilege checks, for compatibility with prior
11410        releases.  The default is <literal>off</literal>.
11411        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
11412        privilege can change this setting.
11413       </para>
11414       <para>
11415        Setting this variable does not disable all security checks related to
11416        large objects &mdash; only those for which the default behavior has
11417        changed in <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> 9.0.
11418       </para>
11419      </listitem>
11420     </varlistentry>
11421
11422    <varlistentry id="guc-quote-all-identifiers" xreflabel="quote-all-identifiers">
11423      <term><varname>quote_all_identifiers</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11424      <indexterm>
11425       <primary><varname>quote_all_identifiers</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11426      </indexterm>
11427      </term>
11428      <listitem>
11429       <para>
11430        When the database generates SQL, force all identifiers to be quoted,
11431        even if they are not (currently) keywords.  This will affect the
11432        output of <command>EXPLAIN</command> as well as the results of functions
11433        like <function>pg_get_viewdef</function>.  See also the
11434        <option>--quote-all-identifiers</option> option of
11435        <xref linkend="app-pgdump"/> and <xref linkend="app-pg-dumpall"/>.
11436       </para>
11437      </listitem>
11438     </varlistentry>
11439
11440     <varlistentry id="guc-standard-conforming-strings" xreflabel="standard_conforming_strings">
11441      <term><varname>standard_conforming_strings</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11442      <indexterm><primary>strings</primary><secondary>standard conforming</secondary></indexterm>
11443      <indexterm>
11444       <primary><varname>standard_conforming_strings</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11445      </indexterm>
11446      </term>
11447      <listitem>
11448       <para>
11449        This controls whether ordinary string literals
11450        (<literal>'...'</literal>) treat backslashes literally, as specified in
11451        the SQL standard.
11452        Beginning in <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> 9.1, the default is
11453        <literal>on</literal> (prior releases defaulted to <literal>off</literal>).
11454        Applications can check this
11455        parameter to determine how string literals will be processed.
11456        The presence of this parameter can also be taken as an indication
11457        that the escape string syntax (<literal>E'...'</literal>) is supported.
11458        Escape string syntax (<xref linkend="sql-syntax-strings-escape"/>)
11459        should be used if an application desires
11460        backslashes to be treated as escape characters.
11461       </para>
11462      </listitem>
11463     </varlistentry>
11464
11465     <varlistentry id="guc-synchronize-seqscans" xreflabel="synchronize_seqscans">
11466      <term><varname>synchronize_seqscans</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11467      <indexterm>
11468       <primary><varname>synchronize_seqscans</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11469      </indexterm>
11470      </term>
11471      <listitem>
11472       <para>
11473        This allows sequential scans of large tables to synchronize with each
11474        other, so that concurrent scans read the same block at about the
11475        same time and hence share the I/O workload.  When this is enabled,
11476        a scan might start in the middle of the table and then <quote>wrap
11477        around</quote> the end to cover all rows, so as to synchronize with the
11478        activity of scans already in progress.  This can result in
11479        unpredictable changes in the row ordering returned by queries that
11480        have no <literal>ORDER BY</literal> clause.  Setting this parameter to
11481        <literal>off</literal> ensures the pre-8.3 behavior in which a sequential
11482        scan always starts from the beginning of the table.  The default
11483        is <literal>on</literal>.
11484       </para>
11485      </listitem>
11486     </varlistentry>
11487
11488     </variablelist>
11489    </sect2>
11490
11491    <sect2 id="runtime-config-compatible-clients">
11492     <title>Platform and Client Compatibility</title>
11493     <variablelist>
11494
11495     <varlistentry id="guc-transform-null-equals" xreflabel="transform_null_equals">
11496      <term><varname>transform_null_equals</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11497      <indexterm><primary>IS NULL</primary></indexterm>
11498      <indexterm>
11499       <primary><varname>transform_null_equals</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11500      </indexterm>
11501      </term>
11502      <listitem>
11503       <para>
11504        When on, expressions of the form <literal><replaceable>expr</replaceable> =
11505        NULL</literal> (or <literal>NULL =
11506        <replaceable>expr</replaceable></literal>) are treated as
11507        <literal><replaceable>expr</replaceable> IS NULL</literal>, that is, they
11508        return true if <replaceable>expr</replaceable> evaluates to the null value,
11509        and false otherwise. The correct SQL-spec-compliant behavior of
11510        <literal><replaceable>expr</replaceable> = NULL</literal> is to always
11511        return null (unknown). Therefore this parameter defaults to
11512        <literal>off</literal>.
11513       </para>
11514
11515       <para>
11516        However, filtered forms in <productname>Microsoft
11517        Access</productname> generate queries that appear to use
11518        <literal><replaceable>expr</replaceable> = NULL</literal> to test for
11519        null values, so if you use that interface to access the database you
11520        might want to turn this option on.  Since expressions of the
11521        form <literal><replaceable>expr</replaceable> = NULL</literal> always
11522        return the null value (using the SQL standard interpretation), they are not
11523        very useful and do not appear often in normal applications so
11524        this option does little harm in practice.  But new users are
11525        frequently confused about the semantics of expressions
11526        involving null values, so this option is off by default.
11527       </para>
11528
11529       <para>
11530        Note that this option only affects the exact form <literal>= NULL</literal>,
11531        not other comparison operators or other expressions
11532        that are computationally equivalent to some expression
11533        involving the equals operator (such as <literal>IN</literal>).
11534        Thus, this option is not a general fix for bad programming.
11535       </para>
11536
11537       <para>
11538        Refer to <xref linkend="functions-comparison"/> for related information.
11539       </para>
11540      </listitem>
11541     </varlistentry>
11542
11543     <varlistentry id="guc-allow-alter-system" xreflabel="allow_alter_system">
11544      <term><varname>allow_alter_system</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11545      <indexterm>
11546       <primary><varname>allow_alter_system</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11547      </indexterm>
11548      </term>
11549      <listitem>
11550       <para>
11551        When <literal>allow_alter_system</literal> is set to
11552        <literal>off</literal>, an error is returned if the <command>ALTER
11553        SYSTEM</command> command is executed. This parameter can only be set in
11554        the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command
11555        line. The default value is <literal>on</literal>.
11556       </para>
11557
11558       <para>
11559        Note that this setting must not be regarded as a security feature. It
11560        only disables the <literal>ALTER SYSTEM</literal> command. It does not
11561        prevent a superuser from changing the configuration using other SQL
11562        commands. A superuser has many ways of executing shell commands at
