11563        the operating system level, and can therefore modify
11564        <literal>postgresql.auto.conf</literal> regardless of the value of
11565        this setting.
11566       </para>
11567
11568       <para>
11569        Turning this setting off is intended for environments where the
11570        configuration of <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> is managed by
11571        some external tool.
11572        In such environments, a well-intentioned superuser might
11573        <emphasis>mistakenly</emphasis> use <command>ALTER SYSTEM</command>
11574        to change the configuration instead of using the external tool.
11575        This might result in unintended behavior, such as the external tool
11576        overwriting the change at some later point in time when it updates the
11577        configuration.
11578        Setting this parameter to <literal>off</literal> can
11579        help avoid such mistakes.
11580       </para>
11581
11582       <para>
11583        This parameter only controls the use of <command>ALTER SYSTEM</command>.
11584        The settings stored in <filename>postgresql.auto.conf</filename>
11585        take effect even if <literal>allow_alter_system</literal> is set to
11586        <literal>off</literal>.
11587       </para>
11588      </listitem>
11589     </varlistentry>
11590
11591     </variablelist>
11592    </sect2>
11593   </sect1>
11594
11595   <sect1 id="runtime-config-error-handling">
11596    <title>Error Handling</title>
11597
11598    <variablelist>
11599
11600     <varlistentry id="guc-exit-on-error" xreflabel="exit_on_error">
11601      <term><varname>exit_on_error</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11602      <indexterm>
11603       <primary><varname>exit_on_error</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11604      </indexterm>
11605      </term>
11606      <listitem>
11607       <para>
11608        If on, any error will terminate the current session.  By default,
11609        this is set to off, so that only FATAL errors will terminate the
11610        session.
11611       </para>
11612      </listitem>
11613     </varlistentry>
11614
11615     <varlistentry id="guc-restart-after-crash" xreflabel="restart_after_crash">
11616      <term><varname>restart_after_crash</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11617      <indexterm>
11618       <primary><varname>restart_after_crash</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11619      </indexterm>
11620      </term>
11621      <listitem>
11622       <para>
11623        When set to on, which is the default, <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
11624        will automatically reinitialize after a backend crash.  Leaving this
11625        value set to on is normally the best way to maximize the availability
11626        of the database.  However, in some circumstances, such as when
11627        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> is being invoked by clusterware, it may be
11628        useful to disable the restart so that the clusterware can gain
11629        control and take any actions it deems appropriate.
11630       </para>
11631
11632       <para>
11633        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
11634        file or on the server command line.
11635       </para>
11636      </listitem>
11637     </varlistentry>
11638
11639     <varlistentry id="guc-data-sync-retry" xreflabel="data_sync_retry">
11640      <term><varname>data_sync_retry</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11641      <indexterm>
11642       <primary><varname>data_sync_retry</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11643      </indexterm>
11644      </term>
11645      <listitem>
11646       <para>
11647        When set to off, which is the default, <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
11648        will raise a PANIC-level error on failure to flush modified data files
11649        to the file system.  This causes the database server to crash.  This
11650        parameter can only be set at server start.
11651       </para>
11652       <para>
11653        On some operating systems, the status of data in the kernel's page
11654        cache is unknown after a write-back failure.  In some cases it might
11655        have been entirely forgotten, making it unsafe to retry; the second
11656        attempt may be reported as successful, when in fact the data has been
11657        lost.  In these circumstances, the only way to avoid data loss is to
11658        recover from the WAL after any failure is reported, preferably
11659        after investigating the root cause of the failure and replacing any
11660        faulty hardware.
11661       </para>
11662       <para>
11663        If set to on, <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> will instead
11664        report an error but continue to run so that the data flushing
11665        operation can be retried in a later checkpoint.  Only set it to on
11666        after investigating the operating system's treatment of buffered data
11667        in case of write-back failure.
11668       </para>
11669      </listitem>
11670     </varlistentry>
11671
11672     <varlistentry id="guc-recovery-init-sync-method" xreflabel="recovery_init_sync_method">
11673      <term><varname>recovery_init_sync_method</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
11674       <indexterm>
11675        <primary><varname>recovery_init_sync_method</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11676       </indexterm>
11677      </term>
11678      <listitem>
11679       <para>
11680        When set to <literal>fsync</literal>, which is the default,
11681        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> will recursively open and
11682        synchronize all files in the data directory before crash recovery
11683        begins.  The search for files will follow symbolic links for the WAL
11684        directory and each configured tablespace (but not any other symbolic
11685        links).  This is intended to make sure that all WAL and data files are
11686        durably stored on disk before replaying changes.  This applies whenever
11687        starting a database cluster that did not shut down cleanly, including
11688        copies created with <application>pg_basebackup</application>.
11689       </para>
11690       <para>
11691        On Linux, <literal>syncfs</literal> may be used instead, to ask the
11692        operating system to synchronize the file systems that contain the
11693        data directory, the WAL files and each tablespace (but not any other
11694        file systems that may be reachable through symbolic links).  This may
11695        be a lot faster than the <literal>fsync</literal> setting, because it
11696        doesn't need to open each file one by one.  On the other hand, it may
11697        be slower if a file system is shared by other applications that
11698        modify a lot of files, since those files will also be written to disk.
11699        Furthermore, on versions of Linux before 5.8, I/O errors encountered
11700        while writing data to disk may not be reported to
11701        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>, and relevant error messages may
11702        appear only in kernel logs.
11703       </para>
11704       <para>
11705        This parameter can only be set in the
11706        <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server command line.
11707       </para>
11708      </listitem>
11709     </varlistentry>
11710
11711    </variablelist>
11712
11713   </sect1>
11714
11715   <sect1 id="runtime-config-preset">
11716    <title>Preset Options</title>
11717
11718    <para>
11719     The following <quote>parameters</quote> are read-only.
11720     As such, they have been excluded from the sample
11721     <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file.  These options report
11722     various aspects of <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> behavior
11723     that might be of interest to certain applications, particularly
11724     administrative front-ends.
11725     Most of them are determined when <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
11726     is compiled or when it is installed.
11727    </para>
11728
11729    <variablelist>
11730
11731     <varlistentry id="guc-block-size" xreflabel="block_size">
11732      <term><varname>block_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11733      <indexterm>
11734       <primary><varname>block_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11735      </indexterm>
11736      </term>
11737      <listitem>
11738       <para>
11739        Reports the size of a disk block.  It is determined by the value
11740        of <literal>BLCKSZ</literal> when building the server. The default
11741        value is 8192 bytes.  The meaning of some configuration
11742        variables (such as <xref linkend="guc-shared-buffers"/>) is
11743        influenced by <varname>block_size</varname>. See <xref
11744        linkend="runtime-config-resource"/> for information.
11745       </para>
11746      </listitem>
11747     </varlistentry>
11748
11749     <varlistentry id="guc-data-checksums" xreflabel="data_checksums">
11750      <term><varname>data_checksums</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11751      <indexterm>
11752       <primary><varname>data_checksums</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11753      </indexterm>
11754      </term>
11755      <listitem>
11756       <para>
11757        Reports whether data checksums are enabled for this cluster.
11758        See <xref linkend="app-initdb-data-checksums"/> for more information.
11759       </para>
11760      </listitem>
11761     </varlistentry>
11762
11763     <varlistentry id="guc-data-directory-mode" xreflabel="data_directory_mode">
11764      <term><varname>data_directory_mode</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11765      <indexterm>
11766       <primary><varname>data_directory_mode</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11767      </indexterm>
11768      </term>
11769      <listitem>
11770       <para>
11771        On Unix systems this parameter reports the permissions the data
11772        directory (defined by <xref linkend="guc-data-directory"/>)
11773        had at server startup.
11774        (On Microsoft Windows this parameter will always display
11775        <literal>0700</literal>.) See
11776        <link linkend="app-initdb-allow-group-access">the
11777        <application>initdb</application> <option>-g</option> option</link>
11778        for more information.
11779       </para>
11780      </listitem>
11781     </varlistentry>
11782
11783     <varlistentry id="guc-debug-assertions" xreflabel="debug_assertions">
11784      <term><varname>debug_assertions</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11785      <indexterm>
11786       <primary><varname>debug_assertions</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11787      </indexterm>
11788      </term>
11789      <listitem>
11790       <para>
11791        Reports whether <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> has been built
11792        with assertions enabled. That is the case if the
11793        macro <symbol>USE_ASSERT_CHECKING</symbol> is defined
11794        when <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> is built (accomplished
11795        e.g., by the <command>configure</command> option
11796        <option>--enable-cassert</option>). By
11797        default <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> is built without
11798        assertions.
11799       </para>
11800      </listitem>
11801     </varlistentry>
11802
11803     <varlistentry id="guc-huge-pages-status" xreflabel="huge_pages_status">
11804      <term><varname>huge_pages_status</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
11805      <indexterm>
11806       <primary><varname>huge_pages_status</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11807      </indexterm>
11808      </term>
11809      <listitem>
11810       <para>
11811        Reports the state of huge pages in the current instance:
11812        <literal>on</literal>, <literal>off</literal>, or
11813        <literal>unknown</literal> (if displayed with
11814        <literal>postgres -C</literal>).
11815        This parameter is useful to determine whether allocation of huge pages
11816        was successful under <literal>huge_pages=try</literal>.
11817        See <xref linkend="guc-huge-pages"/> for more information.
11818       </para>
11819      </listitem>
11820     </varlistentry>
11821
11822     <varlistentry id="guc-integer-datetimes" xreflabel="integer_datetimes">
11823      <term><varname>integer_datetimes</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11824      <indexterm>
11825       <primary><varname>integer_datetimes</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11826      </indexterm>
11827      </term>
11828      <listitem>
11829       <para>
11830        Reports whether <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> was built with support for
11831        64-bit-integer dates and times.  As of <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> 10,
11832        this is always <literal>on</literal>.
11833       </para>
11834      </listitem>
11835     </varlistentry>
11836
11837     <varlistentry id="guc-in-hot-standby" xreflabel="in_hot_standby">
11838      <term><varname>in_hot_standby</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
11839      <indexterm>
11840       <primary><varname>in_hot_standby</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11841      </indexterm>
11842      </term>
11843      <listitem>
11844       <para>
11845        Reports whether the server is currently in hot standby mode.  When
11846        this is <literal>on</literal>, all transactions are forced to be
11847        read-only.  Within a session, this can change only if the server is
11848        promoted to be primary.  See <xref linkend="hot-standby"/> for more
11849        information.
11850       </para>
11851      </listitem>
11852     </varlistentry>
11853
11854     <varlistentry id="guc-max-function-args" xreflabel="max_function_args">
11855      <term><varname>max_function_args</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11856      <indexterm>
11857       <primary><varname>max_function_args</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11858      </indexterm>
11859      </term>
11860      <listitem>
11861       <para>
11862        Reports the maximum number of function arguments. It is determined by
11863        the value of <literal>FUNC_MAX_ARGS</literal> when building the server. The
11864        default value is 100 arguments.
11865       </para>
11866      </listitem>
11867     </varlistentry>
11868
11869     <varlistentry id="guc-max-identifier-length" xreflabel="max_identifier_length">
11870      <term><varname>max_identifier_length</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11871      <indexterm>
11872       <primary><varname>max_identifier_length</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11873      </indexterm>
11874      </term>
11875      <listitem>
11876       <para>
11877        Reports the maximum identifier length. It is determined as one
11878        less than the value of <literal>NAMEDATALEN</literal> when building
11879        the server. The default value of <literal>NAMEDATALEN</literal> is
11880        64; therefore the default
11881        <varname>max_identifier_length</varname> is 63 bytes, which
11882        can be less than 63 characters when using multibyte encodings.
11883       </para>
11884      </listitem>
11885     </varlistentry>
11886
11887     <varlistentry id="guc-max-index-keys" xreflabel="max_index_keys">
11888      <term><varname>max_index_keys</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11889      <indexterm>
11890       <primary><varname>max_index_keys</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11891      </indexterm>
11892      </term>
11893      <listitem>
11894       <para>
11895        Reports the maximum number of index keys. It is determined by
11896        the value of <literal>INDEX_MAX_KEYS</literal> when building the server. The
11897        default value is 32 keys.
11898       </para>
11899      </listitem>
11900     </varlistentry>
11901
11902     <varlistentry id="guc-num-os-semaphores" xreflabel="num_os_semaphores">
11903      <term><varname>num_os_semaphores</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11904      <indexterm>
11905       <primary><varname>num_os_semaphores</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11906      </indexterm>
11907      </term>
11908      <listitem>
11909       <para>
11910        Reports the number of semaphores that are needed for the server based
11911        on the configured number of allowed connections
11912        (<xref linkend="guc-max-connections"/>), allowed autovacuum worker
11913        processes (<xref linkend="guc-autovacuum-max-workers"/>), allowed WAL
11914        sender processes (<xref linkend="guc-max-wal-senders"/>), allowed
11915        background processes (<xref linkend="guc-max-worker-processes"/>), etc.
11916       </para>
11917      </listitem>
11918     </varlistentry>
11919
11920     <varlistentry id="guc-segment-size" xreflabel="segment_size">
11921      <term><varname>segment_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11922      <indexterm>
11923       <primary><varname>segment_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11924      </indexterm>
11925      </term>
11926      <listitem>
11927       <para>
11928        Reports the number of blocks (pages) that can be stored within a file
11929        segment.  It is determined by the value of <literal>RELSEG_SIZE</literal>
11930        when building the server.  The maximum size of a segment file in bytes
11931        is equal to <varname>segment_size</varname> multiplied by
11932        <varname>block_size</varname>; by default this is 1GB.
11933       </para>
11934      </listitem>
11935     </varlistentry>
11936
11937     <varlistentry id="guc-server-encoding" xreflabel="server_encoding">
11938      <term><varname>server_encoding</varname> (<type>string</type>)
11939      <indexterm>
11940       <primary><varname>server_encoding</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11941      </indexterm>
11942      <indexterm><primary>character set</primary></indexterm>
11943      </term>
11944      <listitem>
11945       <para>
11946        Reports the database encoding (character set).
11947        It is determined when the database is created.  Ordinarily,
11948        clients need only be concerned with the value of <xref
11949        linkend="guc-client-encoding"/>.
11950       </para>
11951      </listitem>
11952     </varlistentry>
11953
11954     <varlistentry id="guc-server-version" xreflabel="server_version">
11955      <term><varname>server_version</varname> (<type>string</type>)
11956      <indexterm>
11957       <primary><varname>server_version</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11958      </indexterm>
11959      </term>
11960      <listitem>
11961       <para>
11962        Reports the version number of the server. It is determined by the
11963        value of <literal>PG_VERSION</literal> when building the server.
11964       </para>
11965      </listitem>
11966     </varlistentry>
11967
11968     <varlistentry id="guc-server-version-num" xreflabel="server_version_num">
11969      <term><varname>server_version_num</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11970      <indexterm>
11971       <primary><varname>server_version_num</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11972      </indexterm>
11973      </term>
11974      <listitem>
11975       <para>
11976        Reports the version number of the server as an integer. It is determined
11977        by the value of <literal>PG_VERSION_NUM</literal> when building the server.
11978       </para>
11979      </listitem>
11980     </varlistentry>
11981
11982     <varlistentry id="guc-shared-memory-size" xreflabel="shared_memory_size">
11983      <term><varname>shared_memory_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11984      <indexterm>
11985       <primary><varname>shared_memory_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
11986      </indexterm>
11987      </term>
11988      <listitem>
11989       <para>
11990        Reports the size of the main shared memory area, rounded up to the
11991        nearest megabyte.
11992       </para>
11993      </listitem>
11994     </varlistentry>
11995
11996     <varlistentry id="guc-shared-memory-size-in-huge-pages" xreflabel="shared_memory_size_in_huge_pages">
11997      <term><varname>shared_memory_size_in_huge_pages</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
11998      <indexterm>
11999       <primary><varname>shared_memory_size_in_huge_pages</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12000      </indexterm>
12001      </term>
12002      <listitem>
12003       <para>
12004        Reports the number of huge pages that are needed for the main shared
12005        memory area based on the specified <xref linkend="guc-huge-page-size"/>.
12006        If huge pages are not supported, this will be <literal>-1</literal>.
12007       </para>
12008       <para>
12009        This setting is supported only on <productname>Linux</productname>.  It
12010        is always set to <literal>-1</literal> on other platforms.  For more
12011        details about using huge pages on <productname>Linux</productname>, see
12012        <xref linkend="linux-huge-pages"/>.
12013       </para>
12014      </listitem>
12015     </varlistentry>
12016
12017     <varlistentry id="guc-ssl-library" xreflabel="ssl_library">
12018      <term><varname>ssl_library</varname> (<type>string</type>)
12019      <indexterm>
12020       <primary><varname>ssl_library</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12021      </indexterm>
12022      </term>
12023      <listitem>
12024       <para>
12025        Reports the name of the SSL library that this
12026        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> server was built with (even if
12027        SSL is not currently configured or in use on this instance), for
12028        example <literal>OpenSSL</literal>, or an empty string if none.
12029       </para>
12030      </listitem>
12031     </varlistentry>
12032
12033     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-block-size" xreflabel="wal_block_size">
12034      <term><varname>wal_block_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
12035      <indexterm>
12036       <primary><varname>wal_block_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12037      </indexterm>
12038      </term>
12039      <listitem>
12040       <para>
12041        Reports the size of a WAL disk block.  It is determined by the value
12042        of <literal>XLOG_BLCKSZ</literal> when building the server. The default value
12043        is 8192 bytes.
12044       </para>
12045      </listitem>
12046     </varlistentry>
12047
12048     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-segment-size" xreflabel="wal_segment_size">
12049      <term><varname>wal_segment_size</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
12050      <indexterm>
12051       <primary><varname>wal_segment_size</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12052      </indexterm>
12053      </term>
12054      <listitem>
12055       <para>
12056        Reports the size of write ahead log segments.  The default value is
12057        16MB. See <xref linkend="wal-configuration"/> for more information.
12058       </para>
12059      </listitem>
12060     </varlistentry>
12061
12062    </variablelist>
12063   </sect1>
12064
12065   <sect1 id="runtime-config-custom">
12066    <title>Customized Options</title>
12067
12068    <para>
12069     This feature was designed to allow parameters not normally known to
12070     <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> to be added by add-on modules
12071     (such as procedural languages).  This allows extension modules to be
12072     configured in the standard ways.
12073    </para>
12074
12075    <para>
12076     Custom options have two-part names: an extension name, then a dot, then
12077     the parameter name proper, much like qualified names in SQL.  An example
12078     is <literal>plpgsql.variable_conflict</literal>.
12079    </para>
12080
12081    <para>
12082     Because custom options may need to be set in processes that have not
12083     loaded the relevant extension module, <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
12084     will accept a setting for any two-part parameter name.  Such variables
12085     are treated as placeholders and have no function until the module that
12086     defines them is loaded. When an extension module is loaded, it will add
12087     its variable definitions and convert any placeholder values according to
12088     those definitions.  If there are any unrecognized placeholders
12089     that begin with its extension name, warnings are issued and those
12090     placeholders are removed.
12091    </para>
12092   </sect1>
12093
12094   <sect1 id="runtime-config-developer">
12095    <title>Developer Options</title>
12096
12097    <para>
12098     The following parameters are intended for developer testing, and
12099     should never be used on a production database.  However, some of
12100     them can be used to assist with the recovery of severely damaged
12101     databases.  As such, they have been excluded from the sample
12102     <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file.  Note that many of these
12103     parameters require special source compilation flags to work at all.
12104    </para>
12105
12106    <variablelist>
12107     <varlistentry id="guc-allow-in-place-tablespaces" xreflabel="allow_in_place_tablespaces">
12108      <term><varname>allow_in_place_tablespaces</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12109      <indexterm>
12110        <primary><varname>allow_in_place_tablespaces</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12111      </indexterm>
12112      </term>
12113      <listitem>
12114       <para>
12115        Allows tablespaces to be created as directories inside
12116        <filename>pg_tblspc</filename>, when an empty location string
12117        is provided to the <command>CREATE TABLESPACE</command> command.  This
12118        is intended to allow testing replication scenarios where primary and
12119        standby servers are running on the same machine.  Such directories
12120        are likely to confuse backup tools that expect to find only symbolic
12121        links in that location.
12122        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
12123        privilege can change this setting.
12124       </para>
12125      </listitem>
12126     </varlistentry>
12127
12128     <varlistentry id="guc-allow-system-table-mods" xreflabel="allow_system_table_mods">
12129      <term><varname>allow_system_table_mods</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12130      <indexterm>
12131        <primary><varname>allow_system_table_mods</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12132      </indexterm>
12133      </term>
12134      <listitem>
12135       <para>
12136        Allows modification of the structure of system tables as well as
12137        certain other risky actions on system tables.  This is otherwise not
12138        allowed even for superusers.  Ill-advised use of this setting can
12139        cause irretrievable data loss or seriously corrupt the database
12140        system.
12141        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
12142        privilege can change this setting.
12143       </para>
12144      </listitem>
12145     </varlistentry>
12146
12147     <varlistentry id="guc-backtrace-functions" xreflabel="backtrace_functions">
12148      <term><varname>backtrace_functions</varname> (<type>string</type>)
12149      <indexterm>
12150        <primary><varname>backtrace_functions</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12151      </indexterm>
12152      </term>
12153      <listitem>
12154       <para>
12155        This parameter contains a comma-separated list of C function names.
12156        If an error is raised and the name of the internal C function where
12157        the error happens matches a value in the list, then a backtrace is
12158        written to the server log together with the error message.  This can
12159        be used to debug specific areas of the source code.
12160       </para>
12161
12162       <para>
12163        Backtrace support is not available on all platforms, and the quality
12164        of the backtraces depends on compilation options.
12165       </para>
12166
12167       <para>
12168        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
12169        privilege can change this setting.
12170       </para>
12171      </listitem>
12172     </varlistentry>
12173
12174     <varlistentry id="guc-debug-copy-parse-plan-trees" xreflabel="debug_copy_parse_plan_trees">
12175      <term><varname>debug_copy_parse_plan_trees</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12176      <indexterm>
12177       <primary><varname>debug_copy_parse_plan_trees</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12178      </indexterm>
12179      </term>
12180      <listitem>
12181       <para>
12182        Enabling this forces all parse and plan trees to be passed through
12183        <function>copyObject()</function>, to facilitate catching errors and
12184        omissions in <function>copyObject()</function>.  The default is off.
12185       </para>
12186
12187       <para>
12188        This parameter is only available when
12189        <symbol>DEBUG_NODE_TESTS_ENABLED</symbol> was defined at compile time
12190        (which happens automatically when using the
12191        <application>configure</application> option
12192        <option>--enable-cassert</option>).
12193       </para>
12194      </listitem>
12195     </varlistentry>
12196
12197     <varlistentry id="guc-debug-discard-caches" xreflabel="debug_discard_caches">
12198      <term><varname>debug_discard_caches</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
12199      <indexterm>
12200       <primary><varname>debug_discard_caches</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12201      </indexterm>
12202      </term>
12203      <listitem>
12204       <para>
12205        When set to <literal>1</literal>, each system catalog cache entry is
12206        invalidated at the first possible opportunity, whether or not
12207        anything that would render it invalid really occurred.  Caching of
12208        system catalogs is effectively disabled as a result, so the server
12209        will run extremely slowly.  Higher values run the cache invalidation
12210        recursively, which is even slower and only useful for testing
12211        the caching logic itself.  The default value of <literal>0</literal>
12212        selects normal catalog caching behavior.
12213       </para>
12214
12215       <para>
12216        This parameter can be very helpful when trying to trigger
12217        hard-to-reproduce bugs involving concurrent catalog changes, but it
12218        is otherwise rarely needed.  See the source code files
12219        <filename>inval.c</filename> and
12220        <filename>pg_config_manual.h</filename> for details.
12221       </para>
12222
12223       <para>
12224        This parameter is supported when
12225        <symbol>DISCARD_CACHES_ENABLED</symbol> was defined at compile time
12226        (which happens automatically when using the
12227        <application>configure</application> option
12228        <option>--enable-cassert</option>).  In production builds, its value
12229        will always be <literal>0</literal> and attempts to set it to another
12230        value will raise an error.
12231       </para>
12232      </listitem>
12233     </varlistentry>
12234
12235     <varlistentry id="guc-debug-io-direct" xreflabel="debug_io_direct">
12236      <term><varname>debug_io_direct</varname> (<type>string</type>)
12237      <indexterm>
12238        <primary><varname>debug_io_direct</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12239      </indexterm>
12240      </term>
12241      <listitem>
12242       <para>
12243        Ask the kernel to minimize caching effects for relation data and WAL
12244        files using <literal>O_DIRECT</literal> (most Unix-like systems),
12245        <literal>F_NOCACHE</literal> (macOS) or
12246        <literal>FILE_FLAG_NO_BUFFERING</literal> (Windows).
12247       </para>
12248       <para>
12249        May be set to an empty string (the default) to disable use of direct
12250        I/O, or a comma-separated list of operations that should use direct I/O.
12251        The valid options are <literal>data</literal> for
12252        main data files, <literal>wal</literal> for WAL files, and
12253        <literal>wal_init</literal> for WAL files when being initially
12254        allocated.
12255        This parameter can only be set at server start.
12256       </para>
12257       <para>
12258        Some operating systems and file systems do not support direct I/O, so
12259        non-default settings may be rejected at startup or cause errors.
12260       </para>
12261       <para>
12262        Currently this feature reduces performance, and is intended for
12263        developer testing only.
12264       </para>
12265      </listitem>
12266     </varlistentry>
12267
12268     <varlistentry id="guc-debug-parallel-query" xreflabel="debug_parallel_query">
12269      <term><varname>debug_parallel_query</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
12270      <indexterm>
12271       <primary><varname>debug_parallel_query</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12272      </indexterm>
12273      </term>
12274      <listitem>
12275       <para>
12276        Allows the use of parallel queries for testing purposes even in cases
12277        where no performance benefit is expected.
12278        The allowed values of <varname>debug_parallel_query</varname> are
12279        <literal>off</literal> (use parallel mode only when it is expected to improve
12280        performance), <literal>on</literal> (force parallel query for all queries
12281        for which it is thought to be safe), and <literal>regress</literal> (like
12282        <literal>on</literal>, but with additional behavior changes as explained
12283        below).
12284       </para>
12285
12286       <para>
12287        More specifically, setting this value to <literal>on</literal> will add
12288        a <literal>Gather</literal> node to the top of any query plan for which this
12289        appears to be safe, so that the query runs inside of a parallel worker.
12290        Even when a parallel worker is not available or cannot be used,
12291        operations such as starting a subtransaction that would be prohibited
12292        in a parallel query context will be prohibited unless the planner
12293        believes that this will cause the query to fail.  If failures or
12294        unexpected results occur when this option is set, some functions used
12295        by the query may need to be marked <literal>PARALLEL UNSAFE</literal>
12296        (or, possibly, <literal>PARALLEL RESTRICTED</literal>).
12297       </para>
12298
12299       <para>
12300        Setting this value to <literal>regress</literal> has all of the same effects
12301        as setting it to <literal>on</literal> plus some additional effects that are
12302        intended to facilitate automated regression testing.  Normally,
12303        messages from a parallel worker include a context line indicating that,
12304        but a setting of <literal>regress</literal> suppresses this line so that the
12305        output is the same as in non-parallel execution.  Also,
12306        the <literal>Gather</literal> nodes added to plans by this setting are hidden
12307        in <literal>EXPLAIN</literal> output so that the output matches what
12308        would be obtained if this setting were turned <literal>off</literal>.
12309       </para>
12310      </listitem>
12311     </varlistentry>
12312
12313     <varlistentry id="guc-debug-raw-expression-coverage-test" xreflabel="debug_raw_expression_coverage_test">
12314      <term><varname>debug_raw_expression_coverage_test</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12315      <indexterm>
12316       <primary><varname>debug_raw_expression_coverage_test</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12317      </indexterm>
12318      </term>
12319      <listitem>
12320       <para>
12321        Enabling this forces all raw parse trees for DML statements to be
12322        scanned by <function>raw_expression_tree_walker()</function>, to
12323        facilitate catching errors and omissions in that function.  The
12324        default is off.
12325       </para>
12326
12327       <para>
12328        This parameter is only available when
12329        <symbol>DEBUG_NODE_TESTS_ENABLED</symbol> was defined at compile time
12330        (which happens automatically when using the
12331        <application>configure</application> option
12332        <option>--enable-cassert</option>).
12333       </para>
12334      </listitem>
12335     </varlistentry>
12336
12337     <varlistentry id="guc-debug-write-read-parse-plan-trees" xreflabel="debug_write_read_parse_plan_trees">
12338      <term><varname>debug_write_read_parse_plan_trees</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12339      <indexterm>
12340       <primary><varname>debug_write_read_parse_plan_trees</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12341      </indexterm>
12342      </term>
12343      <listitem>
12344       <para>
12345        Enabling this forces all parse and plan trees to be passed through
12346        <filename>outfuncs.c</filename>/<filename>readfuncs.c</filename>, to
12347        facilitate catching errors and omissions in those modules.  The
12348        default is off.
12349       </para>
12350
12351       <para>
12352        This parameter is only available when
12353        <symbol>DEBUG_NODE_TESTS_ENABLED</symbol> was defined at compile time
12354        (which happens automatically when using the
12355        <application>configure</application> option
12356        <option>--enable-cassert</option>).
12357       </para>
12358      </listitem>
12359     </varlistentry>
12360
12361     <varlistentry id="guc-ignore-system-indexes" xreflabel="ignore_system_indexes">
12362      <term><varname>ignore_system_indexes</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12363      <indexterm>
12364        <primary><varname>ignore_system_indexes</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12365      </indexterm>
12366      </term>
12367      <listitem>
12368       <para>
12369        Ignore system indexes when reading system tables (but still
12370        update the indexes when modifying the tables).  This is useful
12371        when recovering from damaged system indexes.
12372        This parameter cannot be changed after session start.
12373       </para>
12374      </listitem>
12375     </varlistentry>
12376
12377     <varlistentry id="guc-post-auth-delay" xreflabel="post_auth_delay">
12378      <term><varname>post_auth_delay</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
12379      <indexterm>
12380       <primary><varname>post_auth_delay</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12381      </indexterm>
12382      </term>
12383      <listitem>
12384       <para>
12385        The amount of time to delay when a new
12386        server process is started, after it conducts the
12387        authentication procedure.  This is intended to give developers an
12388        opportunity to attach to the server process with a debugger.
12389        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as seconds.
12390        A value of zero (the default) disables the delay.
12391        This parameter cannot be changed after session start.
12392       </para>
12393      </listitem>
12394     </varlistentry>
12395
12396     <varlistentry id="guc-pre-auth-delay" xreflabel="pre_auth_delay">
12397      <term><varname>pre_auth_delay</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
12398      <indexterm>
12399       <primary><varname>pre_auth_delay</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12400      </indexterm>
12401      </term>
12402      <listitem>
12403       <para>
12404        The amount of time to delay just after a
12405        new server process is forked, before it conducts the
12406        authentication procedure.  This is intended to give developers an
12407        opportunity to attach to the server process with a debugger to
12408        trace down misbehavior in authentication.
12409        If this value is specified without units, it is taken as seconds.
12410        A value of zero (the default) disables the delay.
12411        This parameter can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename>
12412        file or on the server command line.
12413       </para>
12414      </listitem>
12415     </varlistentry>
12416
12417     <varlistentry id="guc-trace-notify" xreflabel="trace_notify">
12418      <term><varname>trace_notify</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12419      <indexterm>
12420       <primary><varname>trace_notify</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12421      </indexterm>
12422      </term>
12423      <listitem>
12424       <para>
12425        Generates a great amount of debugging output for the
12426        <command>LISTEN</command> and <command>NOTIFY</command>
12427        commands.  <xref linkend="guc-client-min-messages"/> or
12428        <xref linkend="guc-log-min-messages"/> must be
12429        <literal>DEBUG1</literal> or lower to send this output to the
12430        client or server logs, respectively.
12431       </para>
12432      </listitem>
12433     </varlistentry>
12434
12435     <varlistentry id="guc-trace-sort" xreflabel="trace_sort">
12436      <term><varname>trace_sort</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12437      <indexterm>
12438       <primary><varname>trace_sort</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12439      </indexterm>
12440      </term>
12441      <listitem>
12442       <para>
12443        If on, emit information about resource usage during sort operations.
12444       </para>
12445      </listitem>
12446     </varlistentry>
12447
12448     <varlistentry id="guc-trace-locks" xreflabel="trace_locks">
12449      <term><varname>trace_locks</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12450      <indexterm>
12451       <primary><varname>trace_locks</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12452      </indexterm>
12453      </term>
12454      <listitem>
12455       <para>
12456        If on, emit information about lock usage.  Information dumped
12457        includes the type of lock operation, the type of lock and the unique
12458        identifier of the object being locked or unlocked.  Also included
12459        are bit masks for the lock types already granted on this object as
12460        well as for the lock types awaited on this object.  For each lock
12461        type a count of the number of granted locks and waiting locks is
12462        also dumped as well as the totals.  An example of the log file output
12463        is shown here:
12464<screen>
12465LOG:  LockAcquire: new: lock(0xb7acd844) id(24688,24696,0,0,0,1)
12466      grantMask(0) req(0,0,0,0,0,0,0)=0 grant(0,0,0,0,0,0,0)=0
12467      wait(0) type(AccessShareLock)
12468LOG:  GrantLock: lock(0xb7acd844) id(24688,24696,0,0,0,1)
12469      grantMask(2) req(1,0,0,0,0,0,0)=1 grant(1,0,0,0,0,0,0)=1
12470      wait(0) type(AccessShareLock)
12471LOG:  UnGrantLock: updated: lock(0xb7acd844) id(24688,24696,0,0,0,1)
12472      grantMask(0) req(0,0,0,0,0,0,0)=0 grant(0,0,0,0,0,0,0)=0
12473      wait(0) type(AccessShareLock)
12474LOG:  CleanUpLock: deleting: lock(0xb7acd844) id(24688,24696,0,0,0,1)
12475      grantMask(0) req(0,0,0,0,0,0,0)=0 grant(0,0,0,0,0,0,0)=0
12476      wait(0) type(INVALID)
12477</screen>
12478        Details of the structure being dumped may be found in
12479        <filename>src/include/storage/lock.h</filename>.
12480       </para>
12481       <para>
12482        This parameter is only available if the <symbol>LOCK_DEBUG</symbol>
12483        macro was defined when <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> was
12484        compiled.
12485       </para>
12486      </listitem>
12487     </varlistentry>
12488
12489     <varlistentry id="guc-trace-lwlocks" xreflabel="trace_lwlocks">
12490      <term><varname>trace_lwlocks</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12491      <indexterm>
12492       <primary><varname>trace_lwlocks</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12493      </indexterm>
12494      </term>
12495      <listitem>
12496       <para>
12497        If on, emit information about lightweight lock usage.  Lightweight
12498        locks are intended primarily to provide mutual exclusion of access
12499        to shared-memory data structures.
12500       </para>
12501       <para>
12502        This parameter is only available if the <symbol>LOCK_DEBUG</symbol>
12503        macro was defined when <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> was
12504        compiled.
12505       </para>
12506      </listitem>
12507     </varlistentry>
12508
12509     <varlistentry id="guc-trace-userlocks" xreflabel="trace_userlocks">
12510      <term><varname>trace_userlocks</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12511      <indexterm>
12512       <primary><varname>trace_userlocks</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12513      </indexterm>
12514      </term>
12515      <listitem>
12516       <para>
12517        If on, emit information about user lock usage.  Output is the same
12518        as for <symbol>trace_locks</symbol>, only for advisory locks.
12519       </para>
12520       <para>
12521        This parameter is only available if the <symbol>LOCK_DEBUG</symbol>
12522        macro was defined when <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> was
12523        compiled.
12524       </para>
12525      </listitem>
12526     </varlistentry>
12527
12528     <varlistentry id="guc-trace-lock-oidmin" xreflabel="trace_lock_oidmin">
12529      <term><varname>trace_lock_oidmin</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
12530      <indexterm>
12531       <primary><varname>trace_lock_oidmin</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12532      </indexterm>
12533      </term>
12534      <listitem>
12535       <para>
12536        If set, do not trace locks for tables below this OID (used to avoid
12537        output on system tables).
12538       </para>
12539       <para>
12540        This parameter is only available if the <symbol>LOCK_DEBUG</symbol>
12541        macro was defined when <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> was
12542        compiled.
12543       </para>
12544      </listitem>
12545     </varlistentry>
12546
12547     <varlistentry id="guc-trace-lock-table" xreflabel="trace_lock_table">
12548      <term><varname>trace_lock_table</varname> (<type>integer</type>)
12549      <indexterm>
12550       <primary><varname>trace_lock_table</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12551      </indexterm>
12552      </term>
12553      <listitem>
12554       <para>
12555        Unconditionally trace locks on this table (OID).
12556       </para>
12557       <para>
12558        This parameter is only available if the <symbol>LOCK_DEBUG</symbol>
12559        macro was defined when <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> was
12560        compiled.
12561       </para>
12562      </listitem>
12563     </varlistentry>
12564
12565     <varlistentry id="guc-debug-deadlocks" xreflabel="debug_deadlocks">
12566      <term><varname>debug_deadlocks</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12567      <indexterm>
12568       <primary><varname>debug_deadlocks</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12569      </indexterm>
12570      </term>
12571      <listitem>
12572       <para>
12573        If set, dumps information about all current locks when a
12574        deadlock timeout occurs.
12575       </para>
12576       <para>
12577        This parameter is only available if the <symbol>LOCK_DEBUG</symbol>
12578        macro was defined when <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> was
12579        compiled.
12580       </para>
12581      </listitem>
12582     </varlistentry>
12583
12584     <varlistentry id="guc-log-btree-build-stats" xreflabel="log_btree_build_stats">
12585      <term><varname>log_btree_build_stats</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12586      <indexterm>
12587       <primary><varname>log_btree_build_stats</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12588      </indexterm>
12589      </term>
12590      <listitem>
12591       <para>
12592        If set, logs system resource usage statistics (memory and CPU) on
12593        various B-tree operations.
12594       </para>
12595       <para>
12596        This parameter is only available if the <symbol>BTREE_BUILD_STATS</symbol>
12597        macro was defined when <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> was
12598        compiled.
12599       </para>
12600      </listitem>
12601     </varlistentry>
12602
12603     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-consistency-checking" xreflabel="wal_consistency_checking">
12604      <term><varname>wal_consistency_checking</varname> (<type>string</type>)
12605      <indexterm>
12606       <primary><varname>wal_consistency_checking</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12607      </indexterm>
12608      </term>
12609      <listitem>
12610       <para>
12611        This parameter is intended to be used to check for bugs in the WAL
12612        redo routines.  When enabled, full-page images of any buffers modified
12613        in conjunction with the WAL record are added to the record.
12614        If the record is subsequently replayed, the system will first apply
12615        each record and then test whether the buffers modified by the record
12616        match the stored images.  In certain cases (such as hint bits), minor
12617        variations are acceptable, and will be ignored.  Any unexpected
12618        differences will result in a fatal error, terminating recovery.
12619       </para>
12620
12621       <para>
12622        The default value of this setting is the empty string, which disables
12623        the feature.  It can be set to <literal>all</literal> to check all
12624        records, or to a comma-separated list of resource managers to check
12625        only records originating from those resource managers.  Currently,
12626        the supported resource managers are <literal>heap</literal>,
12627        <literal>heap2</literal>, <literal>btree</literal>, <literal>hash</literal>,
12628        <literal>gin</literal>, <literal>gist</literal>, <literal>sequence</literal>,
12629        <literal>spgist</literal>, <literal>brin</literal>, and <literal>generic</literal>.
12630        Extensions may define additional resource managers. Only superusers and users with
12631        the appropriate <literal>SET</literal> privilege can change this setting.
12632       </para>
12633      </listitem>
12634     </varlistentry>
12635
12636     <varlistentry id="guc-wal-debug" xreflabel="wal_debug">
12637      <term><varname>wal_debug</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12638      <indexterm>
12639       <primary><varname>wal_debug</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12640      </indexterm>
12641      </term>
12642      <listitem>
12643       <para>
12644        If on, emit WAL-related debugging output. This parameter is
12645        only available if the <symbol>WAL_DEBUG</symbol> macro was
12646        defined when <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> was
12647        compiled.
12648       </para>
12649      </listitem>
12650     </varlistentry>
12651
12652    <varlistentry id="guc-ignore-checksum-failure" xreflabel="ignore_checksum_failure">
12653      <term><varname>ignore_checksum_failure</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12654      <indexterm>
12655       <primary><varname>ignore_checksum_failure</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12656      </indexterm>
12657      </term>
12658      <listitem>
12659       <para>
12660        Only has effect if <xref linkend="app-initdb-data-checksums"/> are enabled.
12661       </para>
12662       <para>
12663        Detection of a checksum failure during a read normally causes
12664        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> to report an error, aborting the current
12665        transaction.  Setting <varname>ignore_checksum_failure</varname> to on causes
12666        the system to ignore the failure (but still report a warning), and
12667        continue processing.  This behavior may <emphasis>cause crashes, propagate
12668        or hide corruption, or other serious problems</emphasis>.  However, it may allow
12669        you to get past the error and retrieve undamaged tuples that might still be
12670        present in the table if the block header is still sane. If the header is
12671        corrupt an error will be reported even if this option is enabled. The
12672        default setting is <literal>off</literal>.
12673        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
12674        privilege can change this setting.
12675       </para>
12676      </listitem>
12677     </varlistentry>
12678
12679    <varlistentry id="guc-zero-damaged-pages" xreflabel="zero_damaged_pages">
12680      <term><varname>zero_damaged_pages</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12681      <indexterm>
12682       <primary><varname>zero_damaged_pages</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12683      </indexterm>
12684      </term>
12685      <listitem>
12686       <para>
12687        Detection of a damaged page header normally causes
12688        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> to report an error, aborting the current
12689        transaction.  Setting <varname>zero_damaged_pages</varname> to on causes
12690        the system to instead report a warning, zero out the damaged
12691        page in memory, and continue processing.  This behavior <emphasis>will destroy data</emphasis>,
12692        namely all the rows on the damaged page.  However, it does allow you to get
12693        past the error and retrieve rows from any undamaged pages that might
12694        be present in the table.  It is useful for recovering data if
12695        corruption has occurred due to a hardware or software error.  You should
12696        generally not set this on until you have given up hope of recovering
12697        data from the damaged pages of a table.  Zeroed-out pages are not
12698        forced to disk so it is recommended to recreate the table or
12699        the index before turning this parameter off again.  The
12700        default setting is <literal>off</literal>.
12701        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
12702        privilege can change this setting.
12703       </para>
12704      </listitem>
12705     </varlistentry>
12706
12707     <varlistentry id="guc-ignore-invalid-pages" xreflabel="ignore_invalid_pages">
12708      <term><varname>ignore_invalid_pages</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12709      <indexterm>
12710       <primary><varname>ignore_invalid_pages</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12711      </indexterm>
12712      </term>
12713      <listitem>
12714       <para>
12715        If set to <literal>off</literal> (the default), detection of
12716        WAL records having references to invalid pages during
12717        recovery causes <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> to
12718        raise a PANIC-level error, aborting the recovery. Setting
12719        <varname>ignore_invalid_pages</varname> to <literal>on</literal>
12720        causes the system to ignore invalid page references in WAL records
12721        (but still report a warning), and continue the recovery.
12722        This behavior may <emphasis>cause crashes, data loss,
12723        propagate or hide corruption, or other serious problems</emphasis>.
12724        However, it may allow you to get past the PANIC-level error,
12725        to finish the recovery, and to cause the server to start up.
12726        The parameter can only be set at server start. It only has effect
12727        during recovery or in standby mode.
12728       </para>
12729      </listitem>
12730     </varlistentry>
12731
12732     <varlistentry id="guc-jit-debugging-support" xreflabel="jit_debugging_support">
12733      <term><varname>jit_debugging_support</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12734      <indexterm>
12735       <primary><varname>jit_debugging_support</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12736      </indexterm>
12737      </term>
12738      <listitem>
12739       <para>
12740        If LLVM has the required functionality, register generated functions
12741        with <productname>GDB</productname>.  This makes debugging easier.
12742        The default setting is <literal>off</literal>.
12743        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
12744        privilege can change this parameter at session start,
12745        and it cannot be changed at all within a session.
12746       </para>
12747      </listitem>
12748     </varlistentry>
12749
12750     <varlistentry id="guc-jit-dump-bitcode" xreflabel="jit_dump_bitcode">
12751      <term><varname>jit_dump_bitcode</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12752      <indexterm>
12753       <primary><varname>jit_dump_bitcode</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12754      </indexterm>
12755      </term>
12756      <listitem>
12757       <para>
12758        Writes the generated <productname>LLVM</productname> IR out to the
12759        file system, inside <xref linkend="guc-data-directory"/>. This is only
12760        useful for working on the internals of the JIT implementation.
12761        The default setting is <literal>off</literal>.
12762        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
12763        privilege can change this setting.
12764       </para>
12765      </listitem>
12766     </varlistentry>
12767
12768     <varlistentry id="guc-jit-expressions" xreflabel="jit_expressions">
12769      <term><varname>jit_expressions</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12770      <indexterm>
12771       <primary><varname>jit_expressions</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12772      </indexterm>
12773      </term>
12774      <listitem>
12775       <para>
12776        Determines whether expressions are JIT compiled, when JIT compilation
12777        is activated (see <xref linkend="jit-decision"/>).  The default is
12778        <literal>on</literal>.
12779       </para>
12780      </listitem>
12781     </varlistentry>
12782
12783     <varlistentry id="guc-jit-profiling-support" xreflabel="jit_profiling_support">
12784      <term><varname>jit_profiling_support</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12785      <indexterm>
12786       <primary><varname>jit_profiling_support</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12787      </indexterm>
12788      </term>
12789      <listitem>
12790       <para>
12791        If LLVM has the required functionality, emit the data needed to allow
12792        <productname>perf</productname> to profile functions generated by JIT.
12793        This writes out files to <filename>~/.debug/jit/</filename>; the
12794        user is responsible for performing cleanup when desired.
12795        The default setting is <literal>off</literal>.
12796        Only superusers and users with the appropriate <literal>SET</literal>
12797        privilege can change this parameter at session start,
12798        and it cannot be changed at all within a session.
12799       </para>
12800      </listitem>
12801     </varlistentry>
12802
12803     <varlistentry id="guc-jit-tuple-deforming" xreflabel="jit_tuple_deforming">
12804      <term><varname>jit_tuple_deforming</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12805      <indexterm>
12806       <primary><varname>jit_tuple_deforming</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12807      </indexterm>
12808      </term>
12809      <listitem>
12810       <para>
12811        Determines whether tuple deforming is JIT compiled, when JIT
12812        compilation is activated (see <xref linkend="jit-decision"/>).
12813        The default is <literal>on</literal>.
12814       </para>
12815      </listitem>
12816     </varlistentry>
12817
12818     <varlistentry id="guc-remove-temp-files-after-crash" xreflabel="remove_temp_files_after_crash">
12819      <term><varname>remove_temp_files_after_crash</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12820      <indexterm>
12821       <primary><varname>remove_temp_files_after_crash</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12822      </indexterm>
12823      </term>
12824      <listitem>
12825       <para>
12826        When set to <literal>on</literal>, which is the default,
12827        <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> will automatically remove
12828        temporary files after a backend crash. If disabled, the files will be
12829        retained and may be used for debugging, for example. Repeated crashes
12830        may however result in accumulation of useless files. This parameter
12831        can only be set in the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on
12832        the server command line.
12833       </para>
12834      </listitem>
12835     </varlistentry>
12836
12837     <varlistentry id="guc-send-abort-for-crash" xreflabel="send_abort_for_crash">
12838      <term><varname>send_abort_for_crash</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12839      <indexterm>
12840       <primary><varname>send_abort_for_crash</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12841      </indexterm>
12842      </term>
12843      <listitem>
12844       <para>
12845        By default, after a backend crash the postmaster will stop remaining
12846        child processes by sending them <systemitem>SIGQUIT</systemitem>
12847        signals, which permits them to exit more-or-less gracefully.  When
12848        this option is set to <literal>on</literal>,
12849        <systemitem>SIGABRT</systemitem> is sent instead.  That normally
12850        results in production of a core dump file for each such child
12851        process.
12852        This can be handy for investigating the states of other processes
12853        after a crash.  It can also consume lots of disk space in the event
12854        of repeated crashes, so do not enable this on systems you are not
12855        monitoring carefully.
12856        Beware that no support exists for cleaning up the core file(s)
12857        automatically.
12858        This parameter can only be set in
12859        the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server
12860        command line.
12861       </para>
12862      </listitem>
12863     </varlistentry>
12864
12865     <varlistentry id="guc-send-abort-for-kill" xreflabel="send_abort_for_kill">
12866      <term><varname>send_abort_for_kill</varname> (<type>boolean</type>)
12867      <indexterm>
12868       <primary><varname>send_abort_for_kill</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12869      </indexterm>
12870      </term>
12871      <listitem>
12872       <para>
12873        By default, after attempting to stop a child process with
12874        <systemitem>SIGQUIT</systemitem>, the postmaster will wait five
12875        seconds and then send <systemitem>SIGKILL</systemitem> to force
12876        immediate termination.  When this option is set
12877        to <literal>on</literal>, <systemitem>SIGABRT</systemitem> is sent
12878        instead of <systemitem>SIGKILL</systemitem>.  That normally results
12879        in production of a core dump file for each such child process.
12880        This can be handy for investigating the states
12881        of <quote>stuck</quote> child processes.  It can also consume lots
12882        of disk space in the event of repeated crashes, so do not enable
12883        this on systems you are not monitoring carefully.
12884        Beware that no support exists for cleaning up the core file(s)
12885        automatically.
12886        This parameter can only be set in
12887        the <filename>postgresql.conf</filename> file or on the server
12888        command line.
12889       </para>
12890      </listitem>
12891     </varlistentry>
12892
12893     <varlistentry id="guc-debug-logical-replication-streaming" xreflabel="debug_logical_replication_streaming">
12894      <term><varname>debug_logical_replication_streaming</varname> (<type>enum</type>)
12895      <indexterm>
12896       <primary><varname>debug_logical_replication_streaming</varname> configuration parameter</primary>
12897      </indexterm>
12898      </term>
12899      <listitem>
12900       <para>
12901        The allowed values are <literal>buffered</literal> and
12902        <literal>immediate</literal>. The default is <literal>buffered</literal>.
12903        This parameter is intended to be used to test logical decoding and
12904        replication of large transactions.  The effect of
12905        <varname>debug_logical_replication_streaming</varname> is different for the
12906        publisher and subscriber:
12907       </para>
12908
12909       <para>
12910        On the publisher side, <varname>debug_logical_replication_streaming</varname>
12911        allows streaming or serializing changes immediately in logical decoding.
12912        When set to <literal>immediate</literal>, stream each change if the
12913        <link linkend="sql-createsubscription-params-with-streaming"><literal>streaming</literal></link>
12914        option of
12915        <link linkend="sql-createsubscription"><command>CREATE SUBSCRIPTION</command></link>
12916        is enabled, otherwise, serialize each change.  When set to
12917        <literal>buffered</literal>, the decoding will stream or serialize
12918        changes when <varname>logical_decoding_work_mem</varname> is reached.
12919       </para>
12920
12921       <para>
12922        On the subscriber side, if the <literal>streaming</literal> option is set to
12923        <literal>parallel</literal>, <varname>debug_logical_replication_streaming</varname>
12924        can be used to direct the leader apply worker to send changes to the
12925        shared memory queue or to serialize all changes to the file.  When set to
12926        <literal>buffered</literal>, the leader sends changes to parallel apply
12927        workers via a shared memory queue.  When set to
12928        <literal>immediate</literal>, the leader serializes all changes to files
12929        and notifies the parallel apply workers to read and apply them at the
12930        end of the transaction.
12931       </para>
12932      </listitem>
12933     </varlistentry>
12934
12935    </variablelist>
12936  </sect1>
12937  <sect1 id="runtime-config-short">
12938   <title>Short Options</title>
12939
12940   <para>
12941    For convenience there are also single letter command-line option
12942    switches available for some parameters.  They are described in
12943    <xref linkend="runtime-config-short-table"/>.  Some of these
12944    options exist for historical reasons, and their presence as a
12945    single-letter option does not necessarily indicate an endorsement
12946    to use the option heavily.
12947   </para>
12948
12949    <table id="runtime-config-short-table">
12950     <title>Short Option Key</title>
12951     <tgroup cols="2">
12952      <colspec colname="col1" colwidth="1*"/>
12953      <colspec colname="col2" colwidth="2*"/>
12954      <thead>
12955       <row>
12956        <entry>Short Option</entry>
12957        <entry>Equivalent</entry>
12958       </row>
12959      </thead>
12960
12961      <tbody>
12962       <row>
12963        <entry><option>-B <replaceable>x</replaceable></option></entry>
12964        <entry><literal>shared_buffers = <replaceable>x</replaceable></literal></entry>
12965       </row>
12966       <row>
12967        <entry><option>-d <replaceable>x</replaceable></option></entry>
12968        <entry><literal>log_min_messages = DEBUG<replaceable>x</replaceable></literal></entry>
12969       </row>
12970       <row>
12971        <entry><option>-e</option></entry>
12972        <entry><literal>datestyle = euro</literal></entry>
12973       </row>
12974       <row>
12975        <entry>
12976          <option>-fb</option>, <option>-fh</option>, <option>-fi</option>,
12977          <option>-fm</option>, <option>-fn</option>, <option>-fo</option>,
12978          <option>-fs</option>, <option>-ft</option>
12979         </entry>
12980         <entry>
12981          <literal>enable_bitmapscan = off</literal>,
12982          <literal>enable_hashjoin = off</literal>,
12983          <literal>enable_indexscan = off</literal>,
12984          <literal>enable_mergejoin = off</literal>,
12985          <literal>enable_nestloop = off</literal>,
12986          <literal>enable_indexonlyscan = off</literal>,
12987          <literal>enable_seqscan = off</literal>,
12988          <literal>enable_tidscan = off</literal>
12989         </entry>
12990       </row>
12991       <row>
12992        <entry><option>-F</option></entry>
12993        <entry><literal>fsync = off</literal></entry>
12994       </row>
12995       <row>
12996        <entry><option>-h <replaceable>x</replaceable></option></entry>
12997        <entry><literal>listen_addresses = <replaceable>x</replaceable></literal></entry>
12998       </row>
12999       <row>
13000        <entry><option>-i</option></entry>
13001        <entry><literal>listen_addresses = '*'</literal></entry>
13002       </row>
13003       <row>
13004        <entry><option>-k <replaceable>x</replaceable></option></entry>
13005        <entry><literal>unix_socket_directories = <replaceable>x</replaceable></literal></entry>
13006       </row>
13007       <row>
13008        <entry><option>-l</option></entry>
13009        <entry><literal>ssl = on</literal></entry>
13010       </row>
13011       <row>
13012        <entry><option>-N <replaceable>x</replaceable></option></entry>
13013        <entry><literal>max_connections = <replaceable>x</replaceable></literal></entry>
13014       </row>
13015       <row>
13016        <entry><option>-O</option></entry>
13017        <entry><literal>allow_system_table_mods = on</literal></entry>
13018       </row>
13019       <row>
13020        <entry><option>-p <replaceable>x</replaceable></option></entry>
13021        <entry><literal>port = <replaceable>x</replaceable></literal></entry>
13022       </row>
13023       <row>
13024        <entry><option>-P</option></entry>
13025        <entry><literal>ignore_system_indexes = on</literal></entry>
13026       </row>
13027       <row>
13028        <entry><option>-s</option></entry>
13029        <entry><literal>log_statement_stats = on</literal></entry>
13030       </row>
13031       <row>
13032        <entry><option>-S <replaceable>x</replaceable></option></entry>
13033        <entry><literal>work_mem = <replaceable>x</replaceable></literal></entry>
13034       </row>
13035       <row>
13036        <entry><option>-tpa</option>, <option>-tpl</option>, <option>-te</option></entry>
13037        <entry><literal>log_parser_stats = on</literal>,
13038        <literal>log_planner_stats = on</literal>,
13039        <literal>log_executor_stats = on</literal></entry>
13040       </row>
13041       <row>
13042        <entry><option>-W <replaceable>x</replaceable></option></entry>
13043        <entry><literal>post_auth_delay = <replaceable>x</replaceable></literal></entry>
13044       </row>
13045      </tbody>
13046     </tgroup>
13047    </table>
13048
13049  </sect1>
13050</chapter>
